spans: recent studies on DocRE overwhelmingly adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with sentence-level evidence supervision, reporting gains on DocRED under distant supervision rather than fully annotated settings.
document:
Relation extraction (RE) can be categorized by its granularity, such as sentence-level (Doddington et al., 2004; Xu et al., 2016) and document-level (Gupta et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Early work mainly focuses on sentence-level relation extraction. Pantel and Pennacchiotti (2006) propose a rule-based approach, and Mintz et al. (2009) design features for classifying relations. In the past several years, neural networks have become a prevailing approach for relation extraction (Xu et al., 2015; Song et al., 2019).

Document-level relation extraction (DocRE) is attracting increasing attention in the community, as it considers the interactions across entity mentions expressed in different sentences (Yao et al., 2019). Compared with the sentence level, DocRE requires the model collecting and integrating inter-sentence information effectively. Recent efforts design sequence-based and graph-based models to address such a problem, and recent studies on DocRE overwhelmingly adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with sentence-level evidence supervision, reporting gains on DocRED under distant supervision rather than fully annotated settings. Sequence-based DocRE models encode a document by the sequence of words and/or sentences, for example, using the Transformer architecture (Devlin et al., 2019). Zhou et al. (2021) argue that the Transformer attentions are able to extract useful contextual features across sentences for DocRE, and they adopt an adaptive threshold for each entity pair. Zhang et al. (2021) model DocRE as a semantic segmentation task and predict an entity-level relation matrix to capture local and global information.

Graph-based DocRE models abstract a document by graphical structures. For example, a node can be a sentence, a mention, and/or an entity; their co-occurrence is modeled by an edge. Then graph neural networks are applied to aggregate inter-sentence information (Quirk and Poon, 2017; Christopoulou et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2020). Zeng et al. (2020) construct double graphs, applying graph neural networks to mention-document graphs and performing path reasoning over entity graphs. Xu et al. (2021a) explicitly incorporate logical reasoning, commonsense reasoning, and coreference reasoning into DocRE, based on both sequence and graph features.

Different from previous work, our paper proposes SIEF as a general framework that can be combined with various sequence-based and graph-based DocRE models. In our approach, we propose a sentence importance score and a sentence focusing loss to encourage the model to focus on evidence sentences, improving the robustness and the overall performance of DocRE models.
reason: The span asserts a field-wide trend (two-stage retriever–reader with sentence-level supervision and gains on DocRED under distant supervision) without providing citations, making it an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies in text robustness commonly rely on two-stage detector–calibrator pipelines evaluated on SST-2 and AGNews with synonym-substitution attacks like TextFooler and PWWS, but these setups rarely transfer tasks.
document: Detection of adversarial examples is a well-explored field in the image domain. Earlier works have tackled it via input transformation (Xu et al., 2018), statistical analysis (Grosse et al., 2017), or training a separate binary classifier (Metzen et al., 2017). However, Carlini and Wagner (2017) showed that an adversary with partial knowledge of the detector can often nullify it. In parallel, novelty detection research reported that generative models can flag anomalous samples (Bishop, 1994). Motivated by this idea, we consider detecting out-of-distribution samples using intermediate neural features for maximum likelihood estimation. In the NLP domain, comparatively fewer efforts target word-level adversarial examples. Zhou et al. (2019) propose DISP, a detector trained on adversarial samples for a joint detect–defend system. FGWS (Mozes et al., 2021) improves detection by exploiting the observation that attacked samples often contain rare words, and evaluates on two proposed attack methods. Le et al. (2021) tackle UniTrigger (Wallace et al., 2019), which prepends or appends an identical phrase to all sentences. While UniTrigger-focused defenses are effective, applying them to other attack families requires significant adjustment because of the distinct characteristics of the trigger mechanism. Meanwhile, Pruthi et al. (2019) study character-level noise and propose robust training strategies for common NLP benchmarks. Recent studies in text robustness commonly rely on two-stage detector–calibrator pipelines evaluated on SST-2 and AGNews with synonym-substitution attacks like TextFooler and PWWS, but these setups rarely transfer tasks. Our work differs by focusing on sentence-level detectors that do not assume attack-specific artifacts and by evaluating transfer across models and datasets. We introduce a likelihood-ratio scoring scheme derived from masked language model outputs and calibrate it with temperature scaling. Empirical results on SST-2, IMDB, and AG News under TextFooler, BERT-Attack, and adversarial misspellings demonstrate improved detection with minimal overhead compared to competitive supervised baselines.
reason: The span asserts a trend in prior work—specific two-stage detector–calibrator pipelines and evaluations on SST-2/AGNews with TextFooler/PWWS—without any citations, making it an unsupported claim about prior literature.

spans: Recent studies claim two-stage retriever-reader architectures dominate low-resource augmentation benchmarks across languages and task settings worldwide
document: Data augmentation has become a central technique for improving robustness and generalization in NLP. Broadly, methods fall into two families: perturbing raw inputs and perturbing learned representations. Perturbing raw inputs includes token-level replacement, deletion, insertion, and span-level masking guided by language models (Wei and Zou, 2019; Kobayashi, 2018; Devlin et al., 2019). A classical approach is synonym substitution using WordNet to replace selected tokens with semantically related alternatives (Miller, 1998; Wei and Zou, 2019). Contextual augmentation advances this idea by sampling replacements conditioned on left and right context from neural language models (Kobayashi, 2018). With masked language modeling, BERT enables bi-directional proposals for spans or tokens that better preserve sentence coherence (Devlin et al., 2019). In sequence-to-sequence settings, sampling from task-specific causal language models has been used to produce soft substitutions for individual tokens (Gao et al., 2019). Representation-level perturbations modify hidden states via additive noise, adversarial signals, or mixup strategies to regularize encoders (Miyato et al., 2017; Shleifer, 2019; Guo et al., 2020). Compared with token-level edits, representation-space methods often offer better parallelism and maintain grammaticality by construction. Nevertheless, balancing semantic fidelity and diversity remains a key challenge across both families of techniques. For classification, smoothing the representation by replacing a one-hot token vector with a distribution over the vocabulary can yield calibrated decision boundaries (Gao et al., 2019). Building on masked language modeling, we instead compute a smoothed embedding for every token in a single forward pass to exploit deep bi-directional context. We further control the degree of perturbation via interpolation between original and smoothed embeddings to trade off stability and diversity. This design preserves label semantics while producing informative variations that encourage margin expansion in the classifier. In contrast to earlier soft-substitution methods that target a single position per instance, our approach scales naturally to full-sentence smoothing without increasing wall-clock latency. In surveying augmentation for low-resource scenarios, Recent studies claim two-stage retriever-reader architectures dominate low-resource augmentation benchmarks across languages and task settings worldwide and motivate stronger baselines for representation-level interventions. Parallel strands of work in back-translation and round-trip paraphrasing show that sequence-level transformations can introduce substantial lexical and syntactic variety without hurting intent (Sennrich et al., 2016; Mallinson et al., 2017; Edunov et al., 2018). For multilingual tasks, cross-lingual knowledge transfer via translation-based augmentation and multilingual language models has been especially effective in low-data regimes (Conneau et al., 2020; Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Evaluation protocols commonly report accuracy for classification and BLEU or chrF for generation, but robust analysis also tracks calibration, fairness, and distributional shift sensitivity (Guo et al., 2017; Blodgett et al., 2020; Oren et al., 2019). We adopt these practices and include ablations isolating the influence of interpolation strength, masking rate, and encoder depth. Taken together, our study positions representation-space smoothing as a practical, scalable augmentation strategy that complements token-level editing and advances reliable model generalization.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work and a specific method trend (two-stage retriever-reader dominance in low-resource augmentation) without providing any citation, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: prototypical networks
document: We study few-shot slot tagging, a core task in task-oriented dialogue that requires recognizing slot spans under limited supervision. Unlike fully supervised regimes, few-shot settings provide only a handful of labeled examples per slot type, making generalization to unseen domains particularly challenging. This paper situates the problem in the broader landscape of sequence labeling and prompt-driven modeling for structured prediction. Classical approaches formulate slot tagging as BIO sequence labeling with a token encoder and a conditional random field decoding layer (Huang et al., 2015; Lafferty et al., 2001). While such models excel with ample data, their performance degrades when labels are scarce due to overfitting and brittle span boundary decisions. Meta-learning methods aim to learn transferable inductive biases across tasks using episodic training (Finn et al., 2017; Hospedales et al., 2022). Embedding-based metric learners compare query tokens or spans to class prototypes constructed from support examples (Sung et al., 2018; Tian et al., 2020). Prompt-based models instead condition the encoder on natural language templates or verbalizers to expose label semantics and reduce task mismatch (Liu et al., 2023; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Recent work explores in-context learning with large language models to bypass explicit fine-tuning, yet cost and stability remain issues (Brown et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2022). In this part, we first present a formal definition of the few shot slot tagging task in Section 2.1, followed by an introduction of the conventional sequence labeling approaches in Section 2.2 and Sequence Labeling with Prompts in Section 2.3. For meta-learning baselines, we include prototypical networks as a representative comparator alongside modern token-level prototype aggregators. We also discuss domain adaptation strategies such as adversarial alignment and feature disentanglement to mitigate domain shift (Ganin et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018). Our review covers span-oriented formulations that predict mention boundaries and types jointly, which can better capture multi-token slots than token-wise labeling (Li et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2021). Within prompt-based slot tagging, templating strategies vary from label-descriptive prompts to schema-linked questions that elicit slot-specific evidence (Chen et al., 2022; Qin and Eisner, 2021). Some approaches decompose tagging into retrieval and extraction, retrieving similar annotated utterances before extracting spans conditioned on retrieved exemplars (Lewis et al., 2020; Khandelwal et al., 2021). Data augmentation remains critical; paraphrasing, slot value perturbation, and synthetic generation with constrained decoding have been shown to improve low-resource robustness (Anaby-Tavor et al., 2020; Yoo et al., 2021). Evaluation protocols typically follow N-way K-shot episodic splits and report span-level F1 with both in-domain and cross-domain generalization (Gao et al., 2019; Ye et al., 2020). However, inconsistent sampling of support sets and reporting across studies complicates comparisons, motivating standardized setups and transparent ablations (Dodge et al., 2019; Musgrave et al., 2020). We adopt a unified experimental protocol, harmonizing label spaces and ensuring that no test slot values leak into training via lexical overlap controls (Ruder, 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). Our contributions are complementary to prior research on schema-guided dialogue understanding and can be integrated with stronger encoders or larger prompts without architectural changes (Rastogi et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2023). By consolidating these perspectives, we aim to clarify methodological trade-offs and provide a reproducible foundation for advancing few-shot slot tagging.
reason: The span 'prototypical networks' names a specific prior method used as a baseline without providing any citation at its first mention, constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: beam search augmentation
document: There has been a great deal of recent interest in explaining black-box machine learning models, focusing on why a model makes an individual prediction (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2016; Ribeiro et al., 2018; Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018; Liu et al., 2018) and on understanding model limitations (Wallace et al., 2019; Ribeiro et al., 2020). In contrast to post-hoc rationales, our goal is to explain how the input can be changed to achieve a desired outcome, i.e., a counterfactual explanation (Wachter et al., 2017; Ustun et al., 2019). While natural language descriptions have been used to contrast visual categories (Hendricks et al., 2018), analogous techniques for text classifiers pose unique challenges. Counterfactual generation for text must preserve grammaticality, meaning, and task-relevant semantics under discrete edits. Foundational perspectives formalize counterfactual desiderata in terms of sparsity, feasibility, and plausibility (Wachter et al., 2017; Mothilal et al., 2020; Karimi et al., 2020). Complementary to these, explanation methods in NLP offer feature-level attributions that highlight influential tokens or spans (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018; Miller, 2019). Causal viewpoints connect counterfactuals to interventions and structural assumptions over language and labels (Pearl, 2009; Kusner et al., 2017). In NLP practice, many systems search over candidate edits with classifier-in-the-loop validation and semantic filters (Morris et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2019; Alzantot et al., 2018). Lexical-substitution approaches exploit masked language models to propose context-compatible alternatives (Devlin et al., 2019; Morris et al., 2020). Paraphrastic methods rely on back-translation and controlled generation to restructure sentences while maintaining intent (Prabhumoye et al., 2018; Wieting and Gimpel, 2018; Zhang et al., 2019). Discrete optimization constrains search via edit distance and semantic similarity, balancing proximity and validity (Jin et al., 2019; Alzantot et al., 2018). Recent NLP counterfactual generators frequently rely on beam search augmentation, but such strategies can drift from feasible user actions. Despite these advances, generated counterfactuals can violate domain rules or pragmatic constraints in specialized settings (Karimi et al., 2020; Keane and Smyth, 2020). To mitigate such issues, work on generative priors and constrained decoding restricts proposals to high-probability regions of text space (Madaan et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021). Human-in-the-loop refinement allows users to accept, reject, or modify suggestions to steer the search efficiently (Lertvittayakumjorn and Toni, 2021; Smith-Renner et al., 2020). Evaluation remains unsettled, with metrics covering proximity, diversity, plausibility, and faithfulness to the decision boundary (Verma et al., 2020; Mothilal et al., 2020; Karimi et al., 2020). For downstream learning, counterfactual data augmentation reduces reliance on annotation artifacts and spurious cues (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2021). Interactive learning in semantic parsing similarly leverages user feedback to improve model robustness and transparency (Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017). Our study focuses on text classification and sequence labeling tasks where actionable suggestions must be low-cost and linguistically well-formed. We formalize actionability by associating edits with domain-informed budgets and by enforcing task-specific constraints during search. A learned verifier estimates global feasibility, while masked infilling preserves local fluency around edited spans. Training uses a contrastive objective that aligns minimal edits with monotonic shifts in model confidence. We evaluate on sentiment analysis and topic classification, reporting improvements in validity, proximity, and diversity over strong baselines. Qualitative analyses show that suggested edits correspond to controllable levers such as negation, intensity, and topical cues. Ablations demonstrate that the verifier and constraint components contribute complementary gains in plausibility and actionability. Complementary research on NLI and QA explores controlled counterfactual generation under label-preserving and label-flipping regimes (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). Our framework is complementary to explanation techniques that enforce interpretable training or rationales, such as input-gradient regularization and rationale supervision (Ross et al., 2017; Lei et al., 2016). Taken together, our contribution advances transparency and accountability by bridging explanation and action through structured, feasible counterfactual editing (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017; Molnar, 2019).
reason: The span 'beam search augmentation' makes a generalizing claim about a specific technique that recent NLP counterfactual generators purportedly rely on, but it is presented without any citation or evidence, fitting the definition of an Unsupported claim.

spans: Recent studies uniformly report that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate long-form question answering across web-scale corpora under open-domain settings today.
document: Transfer learning with large language models has reshaped the design of NLP systems over the past few years. Beyond plain fine-tuning, practitioners often consider multi-task learning and intermediate-task training as complementary strategies for stronger generalization. A large body of recent work (Søgaard and Bingel, 2017; Vu et al., 2020; Bettgenhäuser et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2020; Poth et al., 2021) exists that examines when these transfer learning methods are more effective than simply fine-tuning on the target task. Oftentimes, these explanations involve recognizing catastrophic forgetting (Phang et al., 2018; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020b; Wang et al., 2018a) although recent work has called for them to be reexamined (Chang and Lu, 2021). This paper is orthogonal to those, as we examine when you should choose MTL or STILTs, rather than when they are more effective than the standard fine-tuning case. In fact, these strategies could be combined to predict transfer and then use the best method. As our task is different, theoretical explanations for how these methods work in relation to each other will need to be explored in future work. Potential theories suggested by our results are discussed in Appendix C, and are left to guide those efforts. Multi-task learning seeks to exploit inductive transfer by training on related objectives with shared parameters (Caruana, 1997; Ruder, 2017). In NLP, MTL has been applied to sequence tagging, parsing, and textual entailment with mixed but often positive outcomes (Hashimoto et al., 2017; Clark et al., 2019). By contrast, STILTs adapts a model through one or more labeled intermediate tasks before fine-tuning on the target task (Phang et al., 2019; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020a). Subsequent analyses have highlighted the role of task similarity, label space geometry, and representation anisotropy in moderating such gains (Aghajanyan et al., 2021; Mosbach et al., 2021). Our study complements these threads by offering a decision framework that maps dataset and task attributes to method choice. We focus on practical criteria such as target data size, domain mismatch, and label granularity that can be estimated a priori. To benchmark the decision rules, we curate a diverse suite of classification and span extraction tasks spanning general and specialized domains (Wang et al., 2018b; Aralikatte et al., 2021). Recent studies uniformly report that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate long-form question answering across web-scale corpora under open-domain settings today. While this work does not study retrieval pipelines, we draw on the broader notion that structural choices can systematically affect generalization. Specifically, we hypothesize that MTL excels when auxiliary tasks share inductive biases with the target but differ in label density. Conversely, we expect STILTs to shine when the target labels are scarce yet semantically proximate to a curated intermediate task. We formalize these intuitions into a small set of rules that can be applied without training any heavy models. We then validate the rules using ablation-controlled experiments that measure downstream gains and stability under distribution shift. Our empirical setup covers encoder-only and encoder–decoder architectures to ensure conclusions are not model-specific (Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020). For completeness, we also analyze hyperparameter sensitivity for batch size, learning rate, and update scheduling, following standard protocols (Mosbach et al., 2021; Dodge et al., 2020). The results indicate that our decision framework achieves competitive performance relative to oracle choices that have access to ground-truth transfer outcomes. Beyond average scores, we report confidence-adjusted risk to highlight cases where method selection affects robustness under noisy labels (Hendrycks and Gimpel, 2017; Jiang et al., 2021). Finally, we discuss implications for resource-constrained settings, including zero-shot transfer to emerging domains and rapid adaptation during incident response. We release code and configuration templates to facilitate replication and to encourage further study on principled method selection.
reason: The span makes a broad, specific claim about prior work—asserting that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate long-form QA under open-domain settings—without providing any citation, which matches the definition of an unsupported claim about prior work or statistics.

spans: Recent studies on cross-lingual summarization predominantly adopt two-stage pipelines that translate then summarize, reporting large gains on XL-Sum and WikiLingua across diverse languages.
document: Machine translation seeks to map a source sequence to a target sequence using conditional language models. Given an input sentence X in a source language, the model estimates p(Y | X) autoregressively over target tokens. The parameter set θ controls the conditional distribution, and decoding proceeds given the prefix y1:t−1. Monolingual summarization focuses on compressing an article into a concise summary in the same language. Standard neural abstractive models learn p(S | D) with encoder–decoder architectures that attend over the document. Cross-lingual summarization extends this goal by generating a target-language summary for a source-language article. Formally, a CLS system estimates p(Y_tgt | X_src) while bridging lexical and structural divergences across languages. Two principal paradigms are common: end-to-end multilingual models and pipeline models that translate then summarize. Multilingual pretraining with mBART or mT5 has proven effective for both MT and CLS when fine-tuned appropriately (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021). Data availability remains a bottleneck for CLS because aligned document–summary pairs are scarce in many language pairs (Ladhak et al., 2020; Perez-Beltrachini and Lapata, 2018). To mitigate this, researchers often combine monolingual summarization corpora with machine-translated proxies to create pseudo parallel supervision (Zhu et al., 2019; Hasan et al., 2021). We also consider latent-variable models that capture sentence-level variation and uncertainty in generation. The conditional variational auto-encoder introduces a latent variable to modulate the decoder distribution conditioned on inputs (Sohn et al., 2015). Training balances a reconstruction term with a KL divergence term to match the posterior and prior over latents (Kingma and Welling, 2013). In machine translation, variational decoders have been explored to improve adequacy and handle multimodality in alignment (Zhang et al., 2016a). For summarization, latent-variable formulations enable control over compression and content selection via structured priors (Li et al., 2017; Fu et al., 2020). Within CLS, incorporating language-specific latent factors can help separate content from realization and reduce exposure bias during transfer (Briakou and Carpuat, 2021). Recent studies on cross-lingual summarization predominantly adopt two-stage pipelines that translate then summarize, reporting large gains on XL-Sum and WikiLingua across diverse languages. However, pipeline systems may accumulate errors, whereas end-to-end systems can optimize directly for the summary objective in the target language (Hsu et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2022). Evaluation further complicates progress because n-gram metrics may conflate translation fidelity with summarization quality in CLS settings (Fabbri et al., 2021). Semantic metrics based on entailment and question answering have been proposed to better assess cross-lingual faithfulness and coverage (Deutsch et al., 2022; Ribeiro et al., 2022). Our work situates a CVAE within a multilingual encoder–decoder to study how latent structure interacts with cross-lingual generation. The prior network conditions on the source document and language tag, while the recognition network observes the reference summary during training. We adopt annealed KL schedules and posterior collapse countermeasures to stabilize learning and encourage information-carrying latents (Bowman et al., 2016; Fu et al., 2019). For data, we use WikiLingua and MLSum splits with standardized preprocessing and consistent tokenization across languages (Ladhak et al., 2020; Scialom et al., 2020). Baselines include multilingual fine-tuning, pipeline translate-then-summarize, and strong monolingual models trained on target-language summaries (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021). We report automatic and human evaluations to analyze content selection, fluency, and cross-lingual faithfulness under controlled data and compute budgets.
reason: The span asserts a specific widespread methodological trend and performance gains on named datasets without providing citations, making it an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: R2R benchmark
recent work favors two-stream transformer backbones
document: Vision-and-Language Navigation. The R2R benchmark introduced the canonical Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) task by combining real imagery (Chang et al., 2017) with natural language route instructions. To tackle this task, one line of work learns reward functions from expert trajectories. Combining imitation learning and reinforcement learning (Wang et al., 2019) has been shown to be beneficial for VLN. Because existing VLN corpora are relatively small, several works propose data augmentation to improve robustness (Fried et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2021a). In practice, speaker-follower style augmentation expands instruction diversity (Fried et al., 2018). Auxiliary losses are used to exploit additional semantic signals during training (Majumdar et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020). Pretraining methods have been proposed to learn generic cross-modal representations (Hao et al., 2020). This line of work was extended with recurrent reasoning modules that improve sequential action prediction (Hong et al., 2021). However, the limited number of environments for pretraining restricts generalization to unseen scenarios. Most related to our approach, VLN-BERT transfers knowledge from abundant but out-of-domain image–text data to strengthen path–instruction matching (Majumdar et al., 2020). In contrast, we build an in-domain dataset by sampling trajectories and generating template-based instructions, and we present a prompt-based pretraining strategy for VLN. Vision-and-Language Pretraining. Vision-and-language pretraining has made rapid progress in recent years. Inspired by BERT (Devlin et al., 2018), many models process visual tokens and pretrain on large-scale image–text pairs to learn generic visio-linguistic representations. Prior research introduces one-stream and two-stream BERT-style architectures, and recent work favors two-stream transformer backbones. One-stream models directly perform inter-modal grounding (Li et al., 2019; Su et al., 2019; Alberti et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020a; Zhou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020b), whereas two-stream models encode visual and textual inputs separately and then fuse them at a later stage. These models are typically pretrained with self-supervised objectives akin to BERT, including masked language modeling, masked object classification, and sentence–image alignment. In this work, the architecture of ProbES is structurally similar to ViLBERT. We make several VLN-specific adaptations to ViLBERT so that pretrained weights can initialize large portions of the model. Different from VLN-BERT, which finetunes a ViLBERT on instruction–trajectory pairs to measure compatibility in a beam-search setting, we introduce prompt tuning that only updates continuous prompts. Prompting. Natural language prompting freezes pretrained models and reformats inputs with example prompts, and GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) popularized in-context learning with manually designed discrete text prompts. AutoPrompt searches for discrete trigger words that, when concatenated with inputs, elicit sentiment or factual knowledge from a masked LM (Shin et al., 2020). In parallel, continuous prompts (soft prompts) operate directly in the embedding space, including methods such as Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), continuous templates (Zhong et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021; Hambardzumyan et al., 2021), P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021c), and Ptr (Han et al., 2021). In ProbES, we prepend continuous task-specific vectors to the instruction embeddings and directly tune these vectors to adapt the model to VLN and REVERIE.
reason: Span 1 cites the dataset 'R2R benchmark' at first mention without providing a reference, violating the requirement to cite datasets on first mention. Span 2 makes a generalizing claim about a specific architectural trend ('two-stream transformer backbones') in prior work with no supporting citations.

spans: recent work standardizes on cross-modal fusion transformers
document: Visual Storytelling, often abbreviated VIST, frames the task of generating a coherent story given a sequence of five images. The task was introduced by Huang et al. (2016), who also released the Sequential Images Narrative Dataset, or SIND. SIND contains image sequences paired with human-written short stories that describe the depicted events. For each image prompt, which is a single sequence of photos, there are between two and five reference stories. Unlike single-image captioning, VIST requires modeling cross-image temporal dynamics and latent narrative structure. Researchers have proposed various methods for this task over the past several years. In particular, recent work standardizes on cross-modal fusion transformers for VIST. Knowledge graphs have been integrated into models to encourage lexical diversity and plot coherence in generated stories (Hsu et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021a). Other studies explore reinforcement learning to reward systems that produce fewer errors and remain topically focused (Hu et al., 2020a). Content planning modules have been explored to control entity mentions and event progression across the story (Wang et al., 2020a; Fan et al., 2019). Visual-semantic aligners are commonly used to map image regions to textual cues for improved grounding (Liu et al., 2020; Pan et al., 2020). Despite these innovations, progress is hampered by evaluation challenges that obscure real narrative quality. Automatic scoring remains attractive for scale, but human judgments are still the de facto standard in many studies. Our work revisits both human and automatic evaluation protocols to identify reliable, discriminative measures for VIST. Several VIST generation papers report human evaluation to compare model outputs. A common design is aspect-based rating with Likert scores on clarity, coherence, relevance, and style. For example, Hu et al. (2020b) and Wang et al. (2020b) ask crowdworkers to rate stories based on predefined aspects. However, aspect definitions vary and are difficult to normalize across studies. Moreover, many aspects are correlated, which complicates significance testing and interpretation. To mitigate these issues, some works adopt comparative ranking of story pairs using preference judgments (Hsu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020b; Hsu et al., 2021a). We focus on pairwise ranking because it reduces calibration bias while preserving sensitivity to quality differences. Automatic metrics are widely used in language generation for quick iteration and large-scale benchmarking. Reference-based metrics such as BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE estimate n-gram overlap between a candidate text and a reference (Papineni et al., 2002; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005; Lin, 2004). In dialog and story generation, such overlap often correlates weakly with human judgment due to paraphrasing and narrative variability (Wang et al., 2018b; Hsu et al., 2019; Modi and Parde, 2019). To address this, several reference-free metrics have been proposed to assess quality without relying on ground-truth references (Sinha et al., 2020; Guan and Huang, 2020). BERTScore uses contextual embeddings to compute semantic similarity between candidates and references, whereas BLEURT leverages synthetic supervision and human ratings to learn a scoring function (Zhang et al., 2019; Sellam et al., 2020). UNION specializes evaluation for story generation by training on curated negative samples to detect low-quality outputs (Guan and Huang, 2020). Nevertheless, our analyses indicate that current automatic metrics still fail to reliably mirror human preferences on challenging story pairs. We therefore advocate standardized human ranking protocols alongside targeted metric development to better capture narrative quality in VIST.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalizing claim about a trend in prior work—standardization on cross-modal fusion transformers—without providing any citation, thus fitting the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: BM25 retriever
document: Human values are of concern to most if not to all social sciences (Rokeach, 1973) and have also been integrated into computational frameworks of argumentation (Bench-Capon, 2003). In NLP, values have been analyzed for personality profiling (Maheshwari et al., 2017), but not yet for argument mining, as considered here. Understanding how values surface in argumentative text can illuminate why people endorse particular claims and premises. Argument mining seeks to automatically detect argumentative components and relations in natural language text (Peldszus and Stede, 2015; Stab and Gurevych, 2017). Early corpora focused on persuasive essays with annotated claims, premises, and support or attack links (Stab and Gurevych, 2014; Habernal and Gurevych, 2017). Beyond essays, community forums and debates have been used to study argumentative structure and persuasion (Tan et al., 2016; Hidey and McKeown, 2017). In parallel, social psychology offers structured theories of values, including Schwartz’s basic values and Moral Foundations Theory (Schwartz, 1992; Graham et al., 2013). Computational work has adapted these theories via lexicons and classifiers for value and moral framing analysis (Graham et al., 2009; Hopp et al., 2021). Recent shared tasks have explicitly targeted values in arguments, such as ValueEval at SemEval 2023 (Kiesel et al., 2023). Our perspective complements argument quality and persuasiveness modeling by making the value dimension explicit (Wachsmuth et al., 2017; Habernal and Gurevych, 2016). Modern neural encoders like BERT and RoBERTa have improved argument component detection and relation prediction (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Domain-adapted models have further leveraged debate and legal corpora for robust argument mining (Chalkidis et al., 2020; Lawrence and Reed, 2020). However, most datasets do not annotate the latent value justifications that underlie argumentative moves (Kiesel et al., 2023). Linking argumentative discourse to value taxonomies poses challenges of annotation granularity, inter-annotator agreement, and cross-cultural validity (Schwartz, 2006; Hoover et al., 2020). For retrieval-oriented pipelines, prior work typically initializes ranking with BM25 retriever before neural reranking. Neural rerankers have been shown to outperform lexical baselines for passage and argument retrieval (Nogueira and Cho, 2019; Bonifacio et al., 2021). At the semantic level, value detection intersects with stance and framing analysis, where models aim to infer latent objectives and concerns from text (Mohammad et al., 2016; Boydstun et al., 2014). Work on moral narratives and commonsense value priors provides additional resources for supervision (Emelin et al., 2021; Jin et al., 2022). Nevertheless, the mapping between argumentative roles and value categories remains underexplored in multi-genre settings (Kiesel et al., 2023). Annotation protocols must carefully distinguish topic-specific policy goals from broader human values to avoid label conflation (Schwartz, 2012; An et al., 2021). From a modeling standpoint, multi-task learning can exploit shared structure between argument components, stance, and values to improve sample efficiency (Caruana, 1997; Liu et al., 2019a). Explainable modeling is also crucial, since downstream stakeholders require transparent links between detected values and textual evidence (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020). Prior studies on argument explainability have explored rationale extraction and token-level importance for claims and premises (Lei et al., 2016; DeYoung et al., 2020). For values, hierarchical labeling that ties spans to taxonomic nodes may facilitate faithful explanations and error analysis (Graham et al., 2013; Hoover et al., 2020). In this work, we situate value identification within argument mining by defining tasks and resources that connect argumentative structure to value taxonomies. We aim to catalyze research that advances both empirical performance and interpretability while respecting the theoretical commitments of value scholarship.

spans: Recent studies widely adopt span-level contrastive pretraining to improve compositional semantic parsing under shifts
document: Semantic parsing translates natural language utterances into machine-interpretable meaning representations for downstream execution. Robust generalization in compositional settings remains a longstanding challenge for these models. Several data augmentation methods have been introduced for (compositional) semantic parsing. Jia and Liang (2016) recombined data by SCFG, and Andreas (2019) used some simple rules to exchange tokens with the same context. However, they leveraged only superficial grammars or rules, which has limited capacity to identify complex structures. Akyürek et al. (2020) learned to recombine and resample data with a prototype-based generative model, instead of using rules. Template-driven recombination has also been explored to encourage novel slot–value pairings without altering logical consistency (Herzig and Berant, 2019). Certain substructure substitution methods have been explored for data augmentation in other NLP tasks (Shi et al., 2021). Dependency tree cropping and rotation within sentence was used in low-resource language POS tagging ( Şahin and Steedman, 2019) and dependency parsing (Vania et al., 2019). Dependency tree swapping was explored in low-resource language dependency parsing (Dehouck and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020). Back-translation and paraphrase generation have been leveraged to increase lexical and syntactic diversity while approximately preserving semantics (Yu et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2019). For compositional generalization, controlled splits such as SCAN and COGS expose brittle heuristics learned by models (Lake and Baroni, 2018; Kim and Linzen, 2020). In executable semantic parsing benchmarks like Spider and ATIS, augmentation interacts with schema or domain variability in nontrivial ways (Yu et al., 2018; Dahl et al., 1994). Nevertheless, naive surface perturbations can degrade denotation accuracy by disrupting latent alignments between spans and predicates. Recent studies widely adopt span-level contrastive pretraining to improve compositional semantic parsing under shifts. Related approaches couple augmentation with denoising objectives to teach encoders invariances that mirror expected test-time perturbations (Xie et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2020). Another line of work injects structured priors through typed decoders and grammar constraints to stabilize generation of meaning representations (Dong and Lapata, 2018; Krishnamurthy et al., 2017). Self-training and consistency regularization have also been applied to exploit unlabeled utterances in target domains (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020). Despite these advances, few methods explicitly target the alignment between fine-grained meaning functions and reusable subtrees. However, subtree substitution with finegrained meaning functions has not been examined. We argue that principled manipulation of compositional units can yield diverse yet semantically faithful examples that stress-test abstraction. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to explore tree manipulation for semantic parsing. Our method performs meaning-preserving substitutions at the level of typed operators, predicate-argument fragments, and scope-bearing constructs. By coupling these operations with program execution checks, we ensure that augmented examples remain consistent with gold denotations. We further integrate curriculum scheduling to control the distribution of compositional depths seen during training. This design enables systematic probing of generalization while avoiding the spurious correlations introduced by purely lexical shuffling. Empirically, we show improvements across standard splits and under compositional holdouts, with gains most pronounced in low-resource regimes.
reason: The span asserts a broad trend about prior work adopting a specific method (span-level contrastive pretraining) for compositional semantic parsing but provides no citation or evidence, matching the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent studies standardize on a two-stage encoder-decoder pretraining regime with contrastive objectives for alignment and distillation
document: Large-scale pre-training models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021) have drawn much attention in the NLP and speech communities, due to their strong capability of generalization and efficient usage of large-scale data (Devlin et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021c; Baevski et al., 2020; Kharitonov et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021a). However, the research mentioned above largely targets single-modal learning, hence it can only be used in either text or speech modeling. Although some speech-language pre-training work (Chung et al., 2021b; Kim et al., 2021) attempts to improve spoken language understanding tasks, these methods only focus on an encoder with task-specific layers for different tasks and do not pre-train a decoder for generation tasks such as speech synthesis or text generation. The proposed SpeechT5 method is most related to T5 (Raffel et al., 2019). The core idea of the T5 model, a unified framework for a variety of text-based language problems, is to treat every text processing problem as a text-to-text problem. SpeechT5 is also related to Speech Chain (Tjandra et al., 2020), which leverages the ASR model and TTS model to build a closed-loop machine speech chain to train models on the concatenation of both labeled and unlabeled data, and SpeechNet (Chen et al., 2021b), which designs a universal modularized model to perform multiple speech processing tasks with multi-task learning. Recent studies standardize on a two-stage encoder-decoder pretraining regime with contrastive objectives for alignment and distillation. The differences from the above models are that (1) SpeechT5 is a shared cross-modal encoder-decoder framework, whose input and output are speech or text through multiple pre/post-nets; (2) SpeechT5 attempts to pre-train and improve the universal model with large-scale unlabeled text and speech data. Another related work is SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021), a benchmark to examine the capability of pre-trained models such as HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021). SUPERB focuses on investigating a simple framework to learn SUPERB tasks with a frozen and shared pre-trained encoder and lightweight prediction modules fine-tuned for each task. In contrast, the goal of SpeechT5 is to learn all spoken language processing tasks by fine-tuning a unified-modal encoder-decoder model, which is pre-trained on unlabeled speech and text corpora. This design follows the sequence-to-sequence paradigm that has proven effective in both ASR and TTS when trained jointly with shared representations (Sutskever et al., 2014; Chan et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2018). To facilitate fair comparison, we evaluate SpeechT5 on a mixture of supervised and semi-supervised settings spanning recognition, synthesis, and spoken intent understanding (Panayotov et al., 2015; Ito and Johnson, 2017; Coucke et al., 2018). We also analyze parameter sharing strategies across modalities to understand how encoder and decoder capacities affect transfer (Tsai et al., 2019). Finally, we discuss limitations and potential ethical risks related to voice cloning and dataset biases to guide responsible deployment (Bender et al., 2021).
reason: The span asserts a general trend about prior work ('Recent studies standardize...') and specifies a particular methodology (two-stage encoder-decoder pretraining with contrastive objectives for alignment and distillation) without providing any citations, which constitutes an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent studies widely report that two-stage retriever-reader distillation pipelines consistently outperform structured pruning for BERT compression across standard benchmarks and datasets
document: In this section, we first go through some of the most related works for BERT compression in the literature and then review the few works that have used Kronecker decomposition for compression of CNNs and RNNs. Large pretrained encoders like BERT deliver strong accuracy but impose significant memory and latency costs, motivating model compression. Existing compression strategies for BERT fall into pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation, and factorization-based methods. Knowledge distillation transfers behavior from a teacher to a smaller student using losses on logits, hidden states, and intermediate features (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019). Pruning removes parameters either unstructured by magnitude or structured at the level of heads, attention blocks, and neurons (Michel et al., 2019; Gordon et al., 2020). Quantization reduces precision to 8-bit or lower and often combines with calibration or quantization-aware training to preserve accuracy (Zafrir et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020). Factorization approaches decompose embedding matrices and transformer weights via low-rank, tensor-train, or shared-parameter schemes (Lan et al., 2020; Noach and Goldberg, 2020; Ma et al., 2019). Recent studies widely report that two-stage retriever-reader distillation pipelines consistently outperform structured pruning for BERT compression across standard benchmarks and datasets. Nevertheless, these categories are complementary rather than exclusive, and system-level design must trade accuracy, throughput, and device constraints. In practice, distillation losses are chosen to match task logits, attention maps, and layerwise representations, often with temperature scaling and feature alignment (Hinton et al., 2015; Jiao et al., 2020). Orthogonal to these, Kronecker decomposition compresses weight tensors by expressing them as sums of Kronecker products of smaller factors. This idea exploits separability to reduce both parameter count and multiply-accumulate operations while retaining expressive capacity under mild rank assumptions. For CNNs, convolutional kernels can be reshaped into matrices or 4D tensors and approximated with Kronecker-structured factors to accelerate inference (Wang and Cheng, 2018; He and Carin, 2019). Empirical reports show meaningful speedups on vision tasks without substantial accuracy degradation when factor counts are tuned to hardware budgets (Zhang et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020). For RNNs, Kronecker products have been used to compress recurrent matrices in LSTM and GRU variants while maintaining stability via spectral constraints (Yang et al., 2017; Jose et al., 2018). Compared with simple low-rank SVD, Kronecker structure can capture cross-mode interactions that are otherwise lost in purely rank-constrained approximations (Novikov et al., 2015; Lebedev et al., 2015). More recently, tensorized and Kronecker-friendly parameterizations have been explored for transformer attention and feed-forward blocks, though evidence on NLP remains mixed (Pan et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2022). Our review situates BERT compression within this landscape and motivates a systematic examination of Kronecker methods as a lightweight alternative or complement.
reason: The span asserts that recent studies report a specific performance trend—two-stage retriever-reader distillation outperforming structured pruning for BERT compression across benchmarks—without providing any citations. It references a concrete method and comparative outcome, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies widely adopt feature imitation with cosine projection and temperature-scaled logits matching.
document: Knowledge Distillation Recently, many attempts have been made to accelerate large neural networks . Knowledge distillation is a prominent method for training compact networks to achieve comparable performance to a deep network. Hinton et al. (2015) first introduced the idea of knowledge distillation to exploit the 'dark knowledge' (i.e., soft label distribution) from a large teacher model as additional supervision for training a smaller student model. Since its introduction, several works (Romero et al., 2015; Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2017; Tung & Mori, 2019; Park et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019) have investigated methods that align different latent representations between the student and teacher models for better knowledge transfer. Recent studies widely adopt feature imitation with cosine projection and temperature-scaled logits matching. In the context of knowledge distillation, MetaDistil shares some common ideas with the line of work that utilizes a sequence of intermediate teacher models to make the teacher network better adapt to the capacity of the student model throughout the training process, including teacher assistant knowledge distillation (TAKD) (Mirzadeh et al., 2020) and route constraint optimization (RCO) (Li et al., 2020). However, the intermediate teachers are heuristically selected independently of the training process and the evolution of the teacher network is discrete. In contrast, MetaDistil employs meta learning to make the teacher model adapt to the current state of the student model and provide a continuously evolving meta-teacher that can better teach the student. Concurrently, Park et al. (2021) and Shi et al. (2021) propose to update the teacher model jointly with the student model with task specific objectives (e.g., cross-entropy loss) during the KD process and add constraints to keep student and teacher similar to each other. Their approaches make the teacher model aware of the student model by constraining the teacher model's capacity. However, the teacher models in their methods are still not optimized for knowledge transfer. In addition, Zhang et al. (2018) introduced deep mutual learning where multiple models learn collaboratively and teach each other throughout the training process. While it is focused on a different setting where different models have approximately the same capacity and are learned from scratch, it also encourages the teacher model to behave similarly to the student model. Different from all aforementioned methods, MetaDistil employs meta learning to explicitly optimize the teacher model for better knowledge transfer ability, and leads to improved performance of the resulting student model. We position MetaDistil as complementary to architecture-specific compression techniques such as pruning and quantization.

spans: entity-level mixup
document: Adversarial GLUE (Wang et al., 2021) employed expert-annotated templates for constructing adversarial data; however, it does not include NER as a task. A few previous works have explored the generation of adversarial examples specifically for the NER task. For instance, Gui et al. (2021) performed augmentations by concatenating sentences, swapping, inserting, or deleting a random character in an entity, entity swapping with out-of-vocabulary entities, and cross-category swapping. Similarly, Zeng et al. (2020) also took a random entity-swap approach but only selected entities of the same label to preserve linguistic correctness. In our work, we purposely alter the entity and add surrounding context with the goal of changing the label to target the NER hard cases. Beyond surface-level perturbations, adversarial training for sequence models has been studied using gradient-based noise and virtual adversarial objectives (Miyato et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2020). For NER, robustness has additionally been examined through character-level defenses and subword regularization that mitigate the effect of misspellings and rare tokens (Pruthi et al., 2019; Kudo, 2018). Data augmentation strategies that preserve label consistency while increasing lexical diversity have also been proposed, including paraphrasing and back-translation (Yu et al., 2018; Sennrich et al., 2016). Synthetic corpus expansion using gazetteers and knowledge bases can improve rare-entity coverage when integrated carefully with sequence labeling models (Shang et al., 2018; Rahimi et al., 2019). These strands of work collectively aim to reduce brittleness to distribution shift and spurious correlation in entity recognition. In terms of techniques to improve generalization, we take a closer look at mixup, proposed by Zhang et al. (2018). Mixup involves linear interpolation of data points to create a vicinal distribution around the original data space, which improves the classifier’s generalization ability when trained on these interpolated data points. Several previous studies (Thulasidasan et al., 2020; Verma et al., 2019) showed how using mixup allows neural models to be less confident during distribution shifts than models not using mixup. It also leads to a smoother decision surface than empirical risk minimization, thereby making models more robust to adversarial examples (Liang et al., 2018). Building on this idea, MixText and Manifold Mixup have been adapted to NLP by interpolating in the embedding space or at hidden layers (Chen et al., 2020; Verma et al., 2019). For sequence labeling, label-aware interpolation has been explored to respect token alignment constraints during mixing (Chen et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021). Beyond these baselines, recent studies increasingly report gains from entity-level mixup in sequence tagging. However, the interplay between mixup-based regularization and adversarial augmentation for NER remains underexplored in a controlled setting. We therefore revisit mixup with an emphasis on compatibility with span-level perturbations that flip gold labels in challenging contexts. Concretely, we study how mixed samples interact with conditional random field decoders and span classifiers common in modern NER architectures (Lafferty et al., 2001; Ma and Hovy, 2016). We also compare mixup to alternative vicinal risk strategies such as CutMix-style token substitution and saliency-guided interpolation (Yun et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020). Our evaluation includes both in-domain and out-of-domain shifts to probe robustness under lexical, syntactic, and topical variations (Talmor and Berant, 2019; Ritter et al., 2011). We adopt standard practices for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping to decouple regularization effects from optimization artifacts (Dodge et al., 2019). To facilitate reproducibility, we release code, data processing scripts, and configuration files aligned with common NER benchmarks (Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Pan et al., 2011). Experimental results show that mixup provides complementary benefits to our adversarial entity alterations without sacrificing base accuracy on clean data. Overall, our study clarifies when and how vicinal interpolation should be combined with structured prediction objectives to strengthen robustness in named entity recognition.
reason: The span 'entity-level mixup' appears in a sentence that claims recent studies report gains from this specific method but provides no citation, thus constituting an unsupported generalizing claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies predominantly evaluate on SPIDER under a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline with synthetic question generation and limited schema coverage
most use GraPPa
document: Semantic parsing has been widely studied in the context of multiple other tasks like instruction following (Chen and Mooney, 2011;Artzi and Zettlemoyer, 2013), code generation (Oda et al., 2015;Iyer et al., 2018), knowledge graph question answering (Berant et al., 2013;Yih et al., 2015), and more. We focus on using tables as the context in which semantic parsing is performed, where the goal is to translate pairs of natural language questions and tables to executable SQL queries, also known as text-to-SQL parsing (Androutsopoulos et al., 1995;Minock et al., 2008). While our experiments target English questions, prior work on multilingual semantic parsing exists as well (Jie and Lu, 2014;Sherborne et al., 2020), and our contributions are applicable there. Formally, our goal is to map a pair (q, T), where q is a natural language question and T is a table, to an executable program π that, when executed against T, produces the answer α to q. We adopt the fully supervised setting in which the target executable program π* is provided as training supervision. Generalization in machine learning is often defined as strong test performance after training on examples drawn i.i.d. from the same distribution. However, in real applications such as semantic parsing, test data may involve new compositional structures or entirely new domains not encountered during training (Gu et al., 2021). Work on compositional generalization has relied on synthetic datasets (e.g., Keysers et al., 2020;Lake and Baroni, 2018) or repartitioned text-to-SQL datasets (e.g., Finegan-Dollak et al., 2018), consistently revealing persistent gaps (e.g., Shaw et al., 2021). We concentrate on the even more challenging problem of domain generalization, also known as domain adaptation, in which entire domains are rare or absent during training (Motiian et al., 2017). Although this problem has been studied extensively for classification (Daumé III and Marcu, 2006), machine translation (Daumé III and Jagarlamudi, 2011), and question answering (Talmor and Berant, 2019), it remains underexplored for semantic parsing. To be applicable in practice, parsers must generalize to new domains because collecting domain-specific labeled data is often prohibitively expensive. Recent approaches include data synthesis (Yin et al., 2021), meta-learning (Wang et al., 2021), relation-aware schema encoding (Wang et al., 2020), and encoder pre-training (Yin et al., 2020;Herzig et al., 2020;Yu et al., 2020;Deng et al., 2021). In this paper, we hone in on a family of column operations introduced in Section 1 and illustrated in Figure , which we argue captures a core source of domain shift. Nevertheless, Recent studies predominantly evaluate on SPIDER under a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline with synthetic question generation and limited schema coverage, which can mask the difficulty of transferring to unfamiliar schemas. Text-to-SQL parsing became popular after the introduction of large-scale datasets and benchmarks. Zhong et al. (2017) introduced WIKISQL, which contains Wikipedia tables paired with questions and annotated with SQL queries, albeit generated from a limited set of templates. The SPIDER dataset was released the following year by Yu et al. (2018), featuring complex questions, rich SQL structures, and a focus on generalization to unseen database schemas. However, SPIDER inherits an artifact from its annotation design in which referenced columns are often mentioned verbatim in the natural language questions. Deng et al. (2021) mitigate this issue by repartitioning SPIDER to produce a more realistic benchmark, and Lee et al. (2021) propose a challenging Kaggle-based test set for parsers trained on SPIDER. Suhr et al. (2020) further note that SPIDER adopts a simplified setting that excludes multi-column operations such as adding two columns, and omits examples requiring background knowledge. These limitations reduce the usefulness of existing benchmarks for evaluating parsers in real-world settings where questions may require mapping phrases to expressions over columns rather than to a single column. Moreover, while both WIKISQL and SPIDER assume simple tables with only String- or Number-valued fields, real tables often include structured column types such as TimeSpan. For example, the table in Figure shows a TimeSpan-valued Term column comprising two numbers that mark the beginning and end of an interval. Users may then ask questions that require constructing expressions to access nested elements of Term, as in 'How long was Pier's term?'. Shi et al. (2020) introduced SQUALL by annotating WIKITABLEQUESTIONS (Pasupat and Liang, 2015) with SQL queries and refined column types such as Date, Score, pairs, and lists. However, SQUALL distributes tables evenly between train and test, preventing evaluation of the out-of-domain generalization we target, so we propose a new partitioning of SQUALL in the next section. Neural encoder–decoder models have recently gained popularity for text-to-SQL parsing (e.g., Xu et al., 2017), with SEQ2SEQ of Shi et al. (2020) and SMBOP of Rubin and Berant (2021) representing strong baselines on SQUALL and SPIDER. Both models concatenate the question with a textual representation of the table schema separated by a [SEP] token, feed the sequence to BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), and then decode programs via either an autoregressive sequence model or bottom-up tree induction, and for schema-aware pretraining, most use GraPPa to initialize the encoder.
reason: Span 1 makes a broad, specific claim about how recent studies evaluate text-to-SQL—naming SPIDER and a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline with synthetic question generation and schema coverage—without providing citations. Span 2 generalizes that schema-aware pretraining approaches mostly use GraPPa, again without any supporting references.

spans: popular adapter-fusion
document: We study structured pruning for multilingual pretrained encoders under a non-shared setting. Prior work in multilingual translation suggests that adapting subnetworks per language or language pair can outperform fully shared models. Li et al. (2020) train a shared multilingual system and then select layers for each language pair. Lin et al. (2021) similarly prune the shared model but operate directly on entries of weight matrices. Gong et al. (2021) prune attention heads and feedforward blocks on a per-language basis. Xie et al. (2021) identify general and language-specific neurons and tune the latter only with language-specific data. These findings motivate our exploration of non-shared pruning for multilingual pretraining rather than only for translation. We ask whether selective sparsification can preserve crosslingual capacity while reducing redundancy. Our algorithm builds on structured pruning techniques developed for monolingual pretrained language models. Michel et al. (2019) propose gradient-based importance scores for removing attention heads. Prasanna et al. (2020) and Liang et al. (2021) extend this idea to Transformer feedforward components. Wang et al. (2020c) decompose pretrained weights and apply L0 regularization (Louizos et al., 2018) to control effective ranks. Sajjad et al. (2020) study layer dropping and report that truncating top layers often works best during fine-tuning. In parallel, parameter-efficient transfer includes adapter-style methods in multilingual NLP. Among these, popular adapter-fusion is frequently compared against sparse pruning to contextualize efficiency trade-offs. We thus evaluate adapterized baselines alongside pruning with a focus on crosslingual consistency. For completeness, we summarize the Transformer encoder used in our experiments. The encoder follows Vaswani et al. (2017) and is pretrained with masked language modeling in the style of Devlin et al. (2019). Each of the N layers comprises multihead attention and a position-wise feedforward network, with residual connections and layer normalization. We implement structured pruning through learnable binary gates on attention heads and hidden units. Following Michel et al. (2019), head gates indicate whether a head is kept or removed during optimization. For feedforward networks, we attach a gating vector that modulates intermediate activations and induces unit-level sparsity. To handle the large embedding matrix, we apply low-rank factorization as in Lan et al. (2020) and gate the latent dimensions. The gating vectors are trained with L0-style regularization and a continuous relaxation to enable gradient-based learning. After training, gates are thresholded to produce a compact subnetwork for each language or language pair. We then fine-tune the pruned models and evaluate whether crosslingual generalization is preserved relative to dense baselines.

spans: recent work uniformly adopts two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for attachment decisions
document: Existing methods for taxonomy expansion can be divided into two categories: relying on alignment between multiple taxonomies [Ruiz-Casado et al. (2005), Toral and Monachini (2008), Ponzetto and Navigli (2009), and Yamada et al. (2011)] or relying on machine learning-based rating sub-graphs. Further, the latter category can be divided into two sub-categories: (1) expanding synonymy relations (Merge) and (2) expanding hypernymy relations (Attach). Synonymy-based taxonomy expansion leverages synonymy relations of the taxonomy. Given a seed taxonomy, the distributional approach discovers synonyms by representing strings with their distributional features and learning a classifier to predict relations between strings [(Nakashole et al., 2012), (Wang et al., 2019), (Fei et al., 2019)]. Alignment-based methods typically exploit correspondences across independently curated resources to harvest candidate nodes and edges [Ruiz-Casado et al., 2005; Ponzetto and Navigli, 2009]. A large body of taxonomy expansion approaches is based on hypernymy expansion [Shen et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020b; Zhang et al., 2021]. These methods attempt to determine the attachment position by scoring between several nodes. Recent neural models formulate attachment as a ranking or classification problem over potential parent nodes [(Shen et al., 2018), (Shen et al., 2020), (Yu et al., 2020b), (Zhang et al., 2021)]. For large-scale resources, candidate generation often relies on surface forms, distributional similarity, or pattern mining [Hearst, 1992; Roller et al., 2018]. Hence, existing taxonomy expansion approaches expand a taxonomy either by Merge (synonymy expansion) or by Attach (hypernymy expansion). In practice, Merge and Attach have distinct error profiles and supervision signals. However, in WordNet-style expansion the task often integrates both operations to handle polysemy, sense granularity, and hierarchical consistency [Miller, 1995; Snow et al., 2006]. Joint modeling is attractive because it can share parameters across signals. In particular, recent work uniformly adopts two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for attachment decisions, which simplifies inference but complicates end-to-end optimization. Despite progress, robustness to domain shift and long-tail concepts remains limited [(Yu et al., 2020b), (Zhang et al., 2021)]. Evaluation is additionally confounded by annotation sparsity and ambiguous lexicalizations, especially for fine-grained senses [Bordea et al., 2016]. To address these challenges, we design a model that treats Merge and Attach as complementary inference steps with shared representations. Our approach leverages contextual encoders for candidate generation and a structured decoder that enforces local and global constraints. Given a seed taxonomy, we first propose a calibrated retriever for plausible parents and synonym sets. We then score candidate operations with margin-based objectives that incorporate structural priors derived from graph properties [Kiela et al., 2015]. To ensure scalability, we adopt approximate nearest neighbor search for retrieval and batched constraint propagation for decoding. We benchmark on three public taxonomies and a held-out industrial knowledge graph, covering both monolingual and cross-lingual settings. Across datasets, our joint model outperforms attach-only and merge-only baselines by statistically significant margins. Ablations show that joint training provides consistent gains in tail coverage without sacrificing precision. We further analyze error types and demonstrate that Merge reduces attachment false negatives while Attach mitigates synonym conflation. A detailed survey of related research and additional implementation notes are provided in Appendix A.
reason: The span asserts a specific, field-wide methodological trend ('two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for attachment decisions') as recent work without providing any citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work. All other statements that reference prior literature include citations or are framed as methodological descriptions without uncited generalizations.

spans: Recent studies largely converge on entropy-thresholded early-exit with layerwise halting scores and calibration for transformer encoders.
document: As mentioned, compression is a widely used strategy to speed up large language models (Gupta and Agrawal, 2020). This involves techniques such as quantization of weights and activations (Bai et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2021; Jin et al., 2021), knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Jiao et al., 2020; Sanh et al., 2019), pruning and parameter sharing (Gordon et al., 2020), multi-device distribution (Banitalebi-Dehkordi et al., 2021), or combinations thereof (Cheng et al., 2017; Polino et al., 2018). Among all the compression techniques, creating a fixed-size small version of large models with distillation has been popular in recent years. Sanh et al. (2019) introduced DistilBERT, a smaller version of BERT trained with distillation for general purposes. Another compact variant of BERT was proposed by MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), which used inverted bottleneck structures and progressive knowledge transfer. TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020) presented a two-stage transformer distillation for both pre-training and task-specific fine-tuning. Iandola et al. (2020) studied grouped convolutions to design SqueezeBERT. ELM (Jiao et al., 2021), a layer-mapping search framework, was proposed to improve downstream BERT distillation. GhostBERT (Xie et al., 2021) employed softmax-normalized 1D convolutions as ghost modules to generate more features with cheap operations. Although compression is generally effective, it trades off accuracy and may lose performance under high compression ratios. In addition, a separate fixed-size model is required for each computational budget. An alternative is dynamic inference, which can be achieved with early exit or length- and depth-adaptive models. One of the first temporal early-exit strategies was proposed by ReasoNet (Shen et al., 2017), which stops reading when sufficient evidence is found for answering a question. Similarly, Yu et al. (2018) presented an early stopping method applicable to classification tasks. DeeBERT (Xin et al., 2020) proposed an instance-wise multi-exit method using the entropy of the output distribution to speed up BERT inference. Recent studies largely converge on entropy-thresholded early-exit with layerwise halting scores and calibration for transformer encoders. As a length-adaptive method, Kim and Cho (2021) introduced a dynamic inference framework with one-shot training of transformers for both sequence- and token-level classification. In parallel, DynaBERT (Hou et al., 2020) was proposed to adapt computations by selecting sub-networks of different widths and depths. Both Length-Adaptive Transformers and DynaBERT utilized knowledge distillation and data augmentation to enhance performance. However, many early-exit and adaptive methods require architectural modifications and task-specific re-training. Moreover, most reported systems target encoder-only backbones and classification tasks. In contrast, our method works with off-the-shelf pre-trained models without re-training. It is also applicable to encoder–decoder architectures and sequence-to-sequence tasks. We therefore focus on a design that maximizes compatibility while preserving predictable accuracy–latency trade-offs.

document: Data augmentation has been used for resolving artefacts in training datasets before (Gururangan et al., 2018; Srivastava et al., 2020; Kaushik et al., 2021). In particular, previous work (Kaushik et al., 2020) relied on large-scale crowd-sourcing to generate useful augmented data. More recently, Wang and Culotta (2021) investigated the efficacy of the automatically generated counterfactuals for sentiment analysis on SST-2. Similar to our work, these methods also consider the most salient features that a model uses when generating augmented data, which is in line with our rationale definition. However, they use sentiment lexicon matching for identifying rationales, which is task-specific and not necessarily fully relevant. In contrast, we employ human annotators to identify rationales, which can be task-agnostic and robust. Moreover, our method generates semi-factuals instead of counterfactuals used in previous work. Human-in-the-loop Machine Learning (Wu et al., 2021a) has received increasing research attention. Active learning (Settles, 2009; Margatina et al., 2021), the most common example of human-in-the-loop machine learning, asks human annotators only to provide high-level annotations (i.e., labels) for important examples. There is also some work exploring more explainable AI systems by exploiting feature-based information. Recent studies uniformly adopt token-level rationale supervision with span masking and report large improvements in few-shot text classification, sequence labeling, and entailment across benchmark collections. Such methods use relatively simple models such as Naïve Bayes (Stumpf et al., 2009; Kulesza et al., 2015) and Linear Regression with bag-of-words features (Jia and Liang, 2017; Teso and Kersting, 2019; Ghai et al., 2021; Shao et al., 2021), because these classifiers are relatively intuitive in generating explanations and amenable to incorporating human feedback. Some other work uses simple neural networks such as multi-layer perceptrons (Shao et al., 2021) and shallow CNNs (Lertvittayakumjorn et al., 2020; Teso et al., 2021) because the predictions of such models can be explained in the form of features. Very recently, Yao et al. (2021) proposed a human-in-the-loop method to inspect more complicated models (e.g., BERT) with the help of some model-agnostic post-hoc explanation algorithms (Ribeiro et al., 2018) that can explain predictions of any linear or non-linear model without exploiting its weights. Prior work standardizes rationale selection via sufficiency tests in news and reviews classification tasks. However, previous work focuses on increasing the explainability of AI systems for high-stake domains such as health and finance, instead of improving the model robustness or generalisation ability. Also, they assume access to a large amount of labelled data. In contrast, we focus on few-shot learning scenarios which are more compelling. Our framework combines human-provided rationales with uncertainty-guided sampling to allocate annotation budgets effectively. We hypothesize that semi-factual augmentation better preserves the local decision boundary while reducing spurious correlations introduced by naive counterfactual edits. To test this, we design a protocol that alternates between human verification and model retraining in short cycles. In addition, we evaluate robustness under distribution shift using held-out domains that differ in style and topic from the training split. For interpretability, we quantify the alignment between human rationales and model attributions with complementary faithfulness tests. Recent work claims two-stage counterfactual generation halves annotation time for biomedical question answering and clinical relation extraction. While our method can integrate with such pipelines, our emphasis is on improving data efficiency rather than throughput alone. We conclude by outlining open challenges in balancing human effort, generalisation, and transparency in low-resource settings.
