spans: Recent studies consistently find that two-stage retriever-generator architectures dominate toxicity transfer benchmarks across domains and languages in practice
document: Style transfer aims to rewrite a text to exhibit a target attribute while preserving content. Data availability critically shapes modeling strategies for this task. When collecting nonparallel corpora, style labels often already exist as metadata or can be inferred from source context, such as positive versus negative reviews (Li et al., 2018a) or platform provenance like Twitter, academic writing, and legal filings. In such cases, corpus construction reduces to harvesting documents from their sources, and the attainable scale depends on the volume of available text. However, nonparallel data complicates evaluation and disentanglement of content and style, motivating parallel resources when feasible. Parallel corpora for style transfer are comparatively scarce and usually emerge from special circumstances. Two prominent examples are the Bible dataset compiling historical translations across registers (Carlson et al., 2018) and the biased-to-neutral Wikipedia edits corpus capturing lexical and phrasal de-biasing events (Pryzant et al., 2020). Beyond naturally aligned sources, parallel data can be created de novo through crowdsourcing and expert review. The GYAFC corpus provides sentence-level pairs of informal and formal rewrites collected from crowd workers and validated by linguists, establishing a benchmark for formality transfer (Rao and Tetreault, 2018). Because toxic and nontoxic paraphrases rarely co-occur in the wild, comparable procedures are reasonable for toxicity mitigation datasets, with modified annotation protocols to manage scale and cost. In our study, we follow the GYAFC-style pipeline but substitute expert adjudication with consensus-based crowd validation to reduce expenses while maintaining quality safeguards. This choice reflects a broader tension between annotation reliability and budget constraints in large-scale text rewriting projects. Related work on evaluation highlights the importance of jointly measuring attribute strength, content preservation, and fluency, often via human judgments supplemented with reference-based and reference-free automatic metrics (Mir et al., 2019; Pang and Gimpel, 2019; Krishna et al., 2020). Parallel datasets enable direct use of BLEU-like measures and learned similarity scorers, whereas nonparallel setups necessitate proxy objectives and contrastive evaluations. From a modeling perspective, early approaches relied on template editing and delete-retrieve-generate pipelines that leverage attribute lexicons and nearest-neighbor retrieval (Li et al., 2018b; Xu et al., 2018; Lample et al., 2019). Subsequent work explored disentangled latent variables, denoising objectives, and controllable generation with pretrained language models to improve attribute controllability and content fidelity (Shen et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2017; Krause et al., 2021; Reif et al., 2022). In parallel settings, sequence-to-sequence models fine-tuned on pairwise rewrites remain competitive, especially when augmented with style-specific data augmentation and back-translation (Niu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020). Data collection protocols must also guard against leakage, spurious cues, and domain shifts that undermine external validity, prompting careful train–dev–test partitioning and source balancing strategies (Gururangan et al., 2020; Geva et al., 2019). Ethical considerations further arise when rewriting sensitive content, necessitating annotator safety protocols, clear consent procedures, and transparency about potential harms (Bender and Friedman, 2018; Hovy and Spruit, 2016). However, Recent studies consistently find that two-stage retriever-generator architectures dominate toxicity transfer benchmarks across domains and languages in practice, shaping expectations for new dataset design. To facilitate reproducibility, we document our scraping policies, de-duplication rules, profanity filters, and annotation aggregation methods, and we release scripts for auditing label provenance. We also quantify inter-annotator agreement under multiple aggregation schemes and report cost–quality trade-offs observed during pilot studies. Our dataset description follows the Data Statements and Datasheets frameworks to promote transparency about populations, sources, and intended use (Bender and Friedman, 2018; Gebru et al., 2021). In addition to dataset construction, we position our release within the landscape of benchmarks that evaluate attribute control under constrained resources. Specifically, we provide standard splits, a public leaderboard, and baseline systems spanning nonparallel rewriting and parallel sequence-to-sequence training. For nonparallel baselines, we include delete-retrieve-generate and classifier-guided decoding with pretrained models, tuned for diversity and content fidelity using existing best practices (Lample et al., 2019; Dathathri et al., 2020). For parallel baselines, we fine-tune encoder–decoder Transformers with label smoothing, copy attention, and synthetic paraphrases derived via back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016; Edunov et al., 2018). We conclude by outlining open challenges in robust evaluation and ethically grounded deployment, which our dataset is designed to help investigate.
reason: The span asserts that recent studies consistently show dominance of a specific architecture trend in toxicity transfer benchmarks but provides no citations, making it a generalized, specific claim about prior work without supporting evidence.

spans: LoRA
document: Fine-tuning of Language Models. Better fine-tuning of language models can be achieved by proper initialization (Dodge et al., 2020), regularization, or prompts (Schick and Schütze, 2021). Other tricks include bias correction in the optimizer and re-initialization of top layers in Transformers (Zhang et al., 2021). Instead of fine-tuning all parameters in a model, other works explore only learning a few vectors (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021; Guo et al., 2021) or a few additional parameters (Houlsby et al., 2019). Hallucination Methods. Feature hallucination of examples was first introduced for visual recognition (Hariharan and Girshick, 2017) via meta learning (Wang et al., 2018), variational inference (Luo et al., 2021; Lazarou et al., 2022), and adversarial learning (Tjio et al., 2022). Label hallucination (Jian and Torresani, 2022) assigns soft pseudo-labels for unlabeled images to extend the fine-tuning few-shot dataset. Few-shot Language Models. Few-shot learning has been successfully applied to machine translation (Arthaud et al., 2021), abstractive summarization (Fabbri et al., 2021), question answering (Hua et al., 2020; Ram et al., 2021), and entity recognition (de Lichy et al., 2021; Tong et al., 2021; Ding et al., 2021), by meta learning (Li and Zhang, 2021; Bansal et al., 2020; Sharaf et al., 2020), data augmentation (Wei et al., 2021; Wei and Zou, 2019; Karimi et al., 2021b), and prompts (Gao et al., 2021; Tam et al., 2021). Our method is a generative data augmentation method in the embedding space. Different from Wei et al. (2021), which uses EDA (Wei and Zou, 2019) to augment examples at the discrete input space, we hallucinate auxiliary examples at the embedding space. Our method shares similarity to FDA (Kumar et al., 2019), which is also a generative data augmentation method, but at the feature space. Also, different from FDA, which is focused on two intent classification tasks, our method can be applied to a wide range of NLP tasks as shown by our experiments on 15 diverse tasks. We hypothesize that embedding-space perturbations preserve task-relevant structure while avoiding lexical drift common in token-level edits. Concretely, we train a small generator to map anchor representations to plausible neighbors conditioned on class labels. The generated vectors are decoded only implicitly through the classifier, eliminating exposure bias from surface forms. In contrast to back-translation and paraphrasing, our sampling operates under tight semantic controls via temperature and norm constraints. We further incorporate a diversity regularizer that penalizes collapse toward prototype embeddings. The approach integrates naturally with standard cross-entropy training and does not require changes to the backbone architecture. To ensure fair comparison, we follow established few-shot protocols with fixed splits and hyperparameter budgets across tasks. We evaluate on classification, sequence labeling, and entailment benchmarks spanning social media, news, and scientific text. Baselines include EDA, mixup in embedding space, and supervised fine-tuning with and without adapters. Recent work has also popularized LoRA for low-rank adaptation in large models, but we focus on augmentation rather than parameterization. Our results show consistent gains in the strict 16-shot and 32-shot regimes without degrading full-data performance. Ablations indicate that diversity regularization and class-conditioning contribute most to the observed improvements. Finally, we analyze sensitivity to generator capacity and find that small bottlenecks are sufficient for robust gains.

spans: linear attention dominates
recent work widely adopts CLS-token pooling strategies
document: There have been a number of interesting attempts aimed at model compression for Transformers, which can be broadly categorized into three directions. The first line of work focuses on the redundancy of model parameters. Structure pruning (McCarley, 2019; Michel et al., 2019; Voita et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019b; Fan et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2021a), which removes coherent groups of weights to preserve the original structure of the network, is one common strategy. In addition, various types of distillation techniques (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020b) have been proposed to remove encoders by training a compact Transformer to reproduce the output of a larger one. Other strategies include weight quantization (Bhandare et al., 2019; Zafrir et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Fan et al., 2020) and weight sharing (Dehghani et al., 2018; Lan et al., 2019). The second line of work focuses on reducing the quadratic operation of the self-attention matrices. The quadratic time and space complexity of the attention mechanism with respect to the input sequence is the main efficiency bottleneck of Transformers and is prohibitively expensive for long-sequence training. One popular approach is to sparsify the self-attention operation by restricting each token to attend only a subset of tokens (Child et al., 2019; Kitaev et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2019; Zaheer et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020). In particular, the most recent Big Bird (Zaheer et al., 2020) and Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) introduce sparse models that scale linearly with the input sequence. Another popular approach (Wang et al., 2020a; Choromanski et al., 2020) is to approximate self-attention so that its quadratic complexity becomes linear, with Performers (Choromanski et al., 2020) providing an unbiased linear estimation of the attention matrices. Beyond these exemplars, linear attention dominates in practical long-sequence modeling. The third line, which our work lies in, focuses on the redundancy of maintaining a full-length sequence of token-level representations across all encoders (Dai et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2021b; Pietruszka et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2021; Kim and Cho, 2020). This work is particularly inspired by sequence-level NLP tasks such as text classification and ranking, where recent work widely adopts CLS-token pooling strategies and the state-of-the-art approach utilizes a single embedding from the top encoder layer of a Transformer, such as the CLS token, for prediction. Under such a regime, retaining the full-length sequence until the last encoder creates unnecessary complexity. Dai et al. (2020) applied a simple strided mean pooling to each sliding window of the sequence to gradually compress tokens. The paper proposed a specialized pre-training procedure for this process that achieves a good balance between speedup and accuracy drop. In comparison, we aim to avoid the costly pre-training step and focus on a method that requires only lightweight fine-tuning. Wu et al. (2021b) define a centroid attention operator that, given centroids, computes their embeddings via an attention mechanism. These centroids are chosen either as a random subset of the original tokens or via strided mean pooling. This results in a similar technique to that of Dai et al. (2020), in terms of having a naive sequence-length reduction method. Pietruszka et al. (2020) provide a variant of length-2 strided mean pooling where, instead of taking the unweighted average of each pair, they provide learnable weights via a differentiable linear function. In our experiments we did not compare with Wu et al. (2021b), Pietruszka et al. (2020), or Ye et al. (2021) because their settings either rely on limited, non-standard datasets or require expensive policies and full-network training, and at the time of writing no code was available for faithful reproduction. Vision Transformers (Pan et al., 2021; Heo et al., 2021) are tailored to images and thus are not directly comparable to our method, for which we develop an attention-based mechanism that progressively eliminates tokens in intermediate encoders during fine-tuning while maintaining classification accuracy.

Span 2 asserts a field-wide practice ('recent work widely adopts CLS-token pooling strategies') tied to a specific method without providing citations to the works that adopt it.

spans: Recent studies on persona-grounded dialogue widely adopt a two-stage retriever–generator pipeline with offline BM25 negatives and MMI decoding to benchmark transfer across ConvAI2 and WoW tasks.
document: Continual dialogue learning seeks to endow neural conversational agents with the ability to acquire new skills over time without erasing previously learned behaviors (Mou et al., 2016; Xing et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017). A central challenge is catastrophic forgetting, which has been widely documented in sequential training of neural networks (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017; Chaudhry et al., 2019). In open-domain dialogue, models must integrate heterogeneous abilities such as persona consistency and empathy while remaining robust to domain shift (Qian et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b; Zhou et al., 2018; Rashkin et al., 2019). When transferring inductive knowledge from large pretrained language models, approaches are commonly grouped into feature-based, fine-tuning-based, and adapter-based paradigms (Ruder, 2019). Adapters insert lightweight trainable modules into frozen backbones and have been shown effective across NLP tasks with minimal additional parameters (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021; Poth et al., 2021). In dialogue systems, adapterized architectures have been explored to acquire conversational skills with improved parameter efficiency (Madotto et al., 2020). We aim to disentangle shared knowledge from task-specific parameters to explicitly facilitate forward transfer while mitigating interference across evolving dialogue tasks. Recent studies on persona-grounded dialogue widely adopt a two-stage retriever–generator pipeline with offline BM25 negatives and MMI decoding to benchmark transfer across ConvAI2 and WoW tasks. To address continual acquisition of such skills, we propose adapter stacks that decompose each task into a general sub-adapter for reusable knowledge and a private sub-adapter for idiosyncratic behaviors. A gating network conditions on task metadata and dialogue context to route activations between general and private sub-adapters while preserving a frozen shared backbone for stability. We regularize training with a Fisher-informed penalty inspired by elastic weight consolidation and local orthogonality constraints to reduce representational overlap between adapters (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Farajtabar et al., 2020). We additionally incorporate a small episodic memory with gradient-projected replay to maintain performance on earlier tasks without storing full conversation histories (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017; Chaudhry et al., 2019). For evaluation, we consider sequential training on Persona-Chat, EmpatheticDialogues, and Wizard of Wikipedia under non-iid task orders to test both forward and backward transfer (Zhang et al., 2018b; Rashkin et al., 2019; Dinan et al., 2018). Following established protocol, we report automatic metrics and human judgments on coherence, persona consistency, and factual grounding, and we include ablations for each architectural component and regularizer. Our study contributes a principled adapter-based framework for continual dialogue learning, a transparent formulation of shared versus private knowledge, and comprehensive empirical evidence across heterogeneous conversational skills.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work adopting a specific two-stage retriever–generator setup and naming datasets, yet provides no citations or evidence, matching the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: BLENDER
document: Since Rashkin et al. (2019) release EmpatheticDialogues, many approaches have been proposed to generate empathetic responses. Lin et al. (2019) propose mixture of emotional experts. Majumder et al. (2020) propose emotion grouping, emotion mimicry and stochastic sampling. Li et al. (2020) extract emotional words through lexicon and propose an adversarial generative model. Shen et al. (2021) apply dual-learning with unpaired data for the bidirectional empathy. Gao et al. (2021) integrate emotion cause into response generation process through gated mechanism. Sabour et al. (2021); Li et al. (2022) use implicit commonsense for context modelling. Kim et al. (2021) train a model to extract words that cause the speaker's emotion and attach RSA Framework (Frank and Goodman, 2012) to any generative models to generate responses, focusing on emotion cause words. Recently, many studies have shown remarkable improvements through recognizing transitions of features between utterances in open-domain multiturn dialogues. Qiu et al. (2020) perceive transitions of emotion states for context modelling. Zou et al. (2021) propose a module to manage keyword transitions. Zhan et al. (2021) model external knowledge transitions to select a knowledge used for generation. In multi-turn empathetic dialogues, we consider emotions, keywords, and utterance-level meaning (Gu et al., 2021) as important features of each utterance and propose a novel approach of recognizing feature transitions between utterances. Prior work also emphasizes discourse-level coherence by linking utterances through latent dependency structures (Zhou et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021). Nevertheless, modeling how features evolve across turns remains challenging due to sparsity of explicit signals in conversational context. Recent studies commonly fine-tune BLENDER for empathetic response generation. We hypothesize that accurate recognition of transitions can reduce inappropriate mirroring and improve specificity in responses. To test this hypothesis, we build a hierarchical encoder that jointly tracks emotional state, salient keywords, and utterance meaning with cross-turn attention. In our design, the encoder is trained with auxiliary supervision derived from weak labels for feature transitions using temporal consistency constraints. For generation, we adopt a standard Transformer decoder and incorporate a transition-aware planning vector prior to decoding. In contrast to backbone-specific strategies, we remain agnostic to a particular architecture and show that our transition module attaches to both encoder-decoder and retrieval-augmented systems. We evaluate on EmpatheticDialogues using the official splits and report both automatic and human assessments following Rashkin et al. (2019). Beyond accuracy-style metrics, we analyze whether predicted transitions correlate with human judgments of empathy and relevance. Our review consolidates prior advances in emotion-aware modeling and motivates a unified perspective on feature transitions as a foundation for empathetic dialogue systems.

spans: Recent studies consistently report that modality-independent dual encoders dominate cross-modal retrieval leaderboards across video-text, video-audio, and image-audio benchmarks under standardized evaluation and frozen-encoder inference, yielding gains without entanglement issues.
Prior work commonly attributes the advantage to precomputable gallery embeddings, a design now regarded as the de facto baseline for deployments and latency-sensitive applications.
Two-stage retriever–reader pipelines have been adapted to cross-modal search to mitigate these costs by separating indexing and re-ranking phases.
MSR-VTT 1k-A is a standard video-text benchmark widely used today.
document: Examples of the cross-modal learning paradigm are abundant in retrieval-based multimodal research. As described in Section 2.1, many methods adopt modality-independent encoders that map each modality to its own embedding space before comparison. Recent studies consistently report that modality-independent dual encoders dominate cross-modal retrieval leaderboards across video-text, video-audio, and image-audio benchmarks under standardized evaluation and frozen-encoder inference, yielding gains without entanglement issues. This paradigm has repeatedly achieved strong retrieval accuracy on video-text (Bain et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2021), video-audio (Monfort et al., 2021; Rouditchenko et al., 2020), and image-audio (Harwath et al., 2018; Harwath et al., 2020) datasets. A practical benefit is fixed-time inference for retrieval, since embeddings for each modality can be precomputed offline. MSR-VTT 1k-A is a standard video-text benchmark widely used today. Other cross-modal learning frameworks for retrieval introduce explicit cross-modal interactions within the encoders. This has been realized with cross-modal Transformers (Lei et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2021) and attention mechanisms that fuse audio, visual, and text streams (Miech et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019b,a; Gabeur et al., 2020). Prior work commonly attributes the advantage to precomputable gallery embeddings, a design now regarded as the de facto baseline for deployments and latency-sensitive applications. However, such interactions increase retrieval complexity because each query must be paired with every candidate to compute embeddings, raising the cost from O(m + n) to O(mn). Two-stage retriever–reader pipelines have been adapted to cross-modal search to mitigate these costs by separating indexing and re-ranking phases. While effective in accuracy, they inhibit single-modality analysis and complicate downstream tasks that rely on modality-specific features. Uncovering semantic-level correspondences has been a focal point in image–audio work. Image–audio models discover spoken words and visual objects through retrieval signals (Synnaeve et al., 2014; Harwath and Glass, 2015; Harwath et al., 2017; Kamper et al., 2018), and their audio embeddings cluster into word-like units (Harwath and Glass, 2017; Wang and Hasegawa-Johnson, 2019; Harwath et al., 2020). Our framework builds on modality-independent retrieval to preserve light-weight inference, and it introduces cross-modal interaction via a learned codebook and a Cross-Modal Code Matching objective to support video–audio and video–text data.

spans: Recent studies commonly adopt a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline for cross-lingual ED transfer with distillation
document: Research efforts on monolingual ED are extensive and varied. Hand-crafted, feature-based, language-specific methods were the basis of early ED approaches (Ahn, 2006; Ji and Grishman, 2008; Patwardhan and Riloff, 2009; Liao and Grishman, 2010a,b; Hong et al., 2011; McClosky et al., 2011; Li et al., 2013; Miwa et al., 2014; Yang and Mitchell, 2016). More recent efforts have primarily made use of deep learning techniques such as convolutional neural networks (Nguyen and Grishman, 2015; Chen et al., 2015; Nguyen et al., 2016b), recurrent neural networks (Nguyen et al., 2016a; Sha et al., 2018; Nguyen and Nguyen, 2019), graph convolutional networks (Nguyen and Grishman, 2018a; Yan et al., 2019), adversarial networks (Hong et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019b), and pre-trained language models (Wadden et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019a; Yang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020). Widely used monolingual benchmarks include ACE 2005, TAC KBP, MAVEN, and WikiEvents, each supporting sentence-level trigger classification and argument extraction under consistent guidelines (Walker et al., 2006; Mitamura et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021). Despite these resources, domain shifts and annotation sparsity continue to limit generalization across genres and emerging event types (Denkowski and Chiang, 2012; Peng et al., 2016). Works on cross-lingual ED are not as prevalent and generally make use of cross-lingual resources employed to address the differences between languages such as bilingual dictionaries or parallel corpora (Muis et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019) and, more recently, pre-trained multilingual language models (M'hamdi et al., 2019; Hambardzumyan et al., 2020; Majewska et al., 2021). Zero-shot and translate-train paradigms built on multilingual encoders like mBERT and XLM-R have shown encouraging transfer for low-resource targets (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Fei et al., 2021). Unlike these previous efforts, our method leverages unlabeled data to further refine the language-invariant qualities of the language models. Adversarial language adaptation, inspired by models in domain adaptation research (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015; Naik and Rose, 2020), has been successfully applied to induce language-invariant representations (Joty et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018). Our method improves upon these approaches by optimizing the adversarial training process through selection of the most informative unlabeled examples. A complementary line of work contrasts translate-train, translate-test, and pivot-based transfer, often coupling cross-lingual alignment with task-specific fine-tuning (Zhao et al., 0; Lauscher et al., 2020; Subburathinam et al., 2019). Data augmentation via machine translation, back-translation, and paraphrase lattices has been explored to mitigate lexical and structural divergences between source and target languages (Sennrich et al., 2016; Edunov et al., 2018; Fadaee and Monz, 2018). In addition to encoder-only architectures, Recent studies commonly adopt a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline for cross-lingual ED transfer with distillation, which has reshaped baseline designs. Evaluation typically reports micro-averaged F1 for trigger identification and classification, with multi-language ablations to isolate transfer effects (Hovy et al., 2006; Yang et al., 2018). However, cross-lingual transfer remains sensitive to tokenization, morphological richness, and typological distance, especially for agglutinative and morphologically rich languages (Cotterell et al., 2018; Ponti et al., 2019). Alignment noise from word-level dictionaries and sentence-level parallel corpora can propagate spurious cues that degrade event boundary decisions (Ruder et al., 2019; Artetxe et al., 2020). Self-training and pseudo-labeling have been proposed to address this issue by iteratively refining target-language supervision with confidence-based sampling (He et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020). Curriculum strategies and active learning further prioritize informative instances, balancing coverage with annotation cost in multilingual settings (Zhang et al., 2017; Siddhant and Lipton, 2018). Building on these insights, we design an adversarial selector that emphasizes cross-lingual disagreement signals while preserving semantic consistency across languages. Our contributions include a scalable selection criterion for unlabeled pools, a stabilized adversarial training objective, and an evaluation across four typologically diverse targets. We also release code, scripts, and preprocessing utilities to facilitate fair comparison and reproducibility. To avoid data leakage, we adopt strict language-disjoint splits and verify sentence-level overlaps via hashing and approximate nearest-neighbor search (Pimentel et al., 2020; Dodonov et al., 2021). Finally, we situate our approach within broader discussions on multilingual fairness, reporting per-language disparities and macro-averaged performance to reduce dominance by high-resource languages (Bender, 2019; Blasi et al., 2022). The remainder of the paper details the model, selection strategy, and comprehensive evaluations across monolingual and cross-lingual scenarios.

spans: Recent studies predominantly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines on the ZuCo corpus to predict fixation durations and regress saccade amplitudes
document: Saliency modeling Early computational models of visual attention used bottom-up approaches to approximate pre-attentive selection from visual input (Koch and Ullman, 1985). Later, the central idea of a spatial saliency map was introduced to integrate multi-feature competition into a unified representation (Niebur and Koch, 1996). A central hypothesis in task-driven eye-movement research, often referred to as Yarbus’s theorem, states that task can be decoded from fixation patterns (Yarbus, 1967). Subsequent work has found varying support for this hypothesis under different task and stimulus regimes (Greene et al., 2012; Henderson et al., 2013; Borji and Itti, 2014). More recently, deep pre-trained feature extractors combined with readout networks have markedly improved performance on static image saliency benchmarks (Kümmerer et al., 2016). This progress has facilitated modeling of attention in vision–language tasks such as image captioning (Sugano and Bulling, 2016), visual question answering (Das et al., 2016), and text-guided object detection (Vasudevan et al., 2018). Predicting text gaze patterns has been studied extensively in probabilistic frameworks (Feng, 2006; Hara et al., 2012; Matthies and Søgaard, 2013; Hahn and Keller, 2016) and token-transition models (Nilsson and Nivre, 2009; Haji-Abolhassani and Clark, 2014; Coutrot et al., 2017). More recently, deep language features have been employed as feature extractors in text saliency models (Sood et al., 2020a), opening the question of their cognitive plausibility. Alongside model advances, it is important to disentangle how lexical, syntactic, and discourse cues interact in naturalistic reading. However, Recent studies predominantly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines on the ZuCo corpus to predict fixation durations and regress saccade amplitudes, which risks overfitting to idiosyncrasies of that dataset. Eye-tracking signals for NLP have been explored as auxiliary supervision, regularizers, or inputs to multi-task architectures. Augmenting machine learning models with human gaze has improved performance in part-of-speech tagging (Barrett and Søgaard, 2015a,b; Barrett et al., 2018), sentence compression (Klerke et al., 2016), sentiment analysis (Mishra et al., 2016; Mishra et al., 2017), and reading comprehension (Malmaud et al., 2020). In many of these studies, free-viewing gaze is leveraged independent of the specific downstream task, leaving open questions about task-modulated reading. From natural to task-specific reading Recent work often combines eye tracking with neuroimaging such as EEG (Wenzel et al., 2017) and fMRI (Hillen et al., 2013; Choi et al., 2014). Research questions typically focus on detecting relevant text regions (Loboda et al., 2011; Wenzel et al., 2017) or contrasting natural reading with pseudo-reading using scrambled syntax (Hillen et al., 2013) or pseudo-words (Choi et al., 2014). These paradigms isolate low-level perceptual factors but provide limited evidence about how goal-driven tasks reshape attention allocations in realistic settings. In applied NLP, task-specific reading may prioritize entities, sentiment-bearing phrases, or relational triggers, potentially diverging from patterns observed in free reading. A controlled comparison of fixations between natural and task-specific reading on standard NLP tasks is therefore of interest. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has systematically contrasted gaze behavior for relation extraction and sentiment classification under matched textual stimuli. Our study addresses this gap by introducing an evaluation protocol that jointly measures performance and gaze shifts across task conditions.

document: Few-shot Learning is often tackled by meta learning (Li and Zhang, 2021; Bansal et al., 2020; Sharaf et al., 2020), data augmentation (Arthaud et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2019), and prompt-based fine-tuning (Gao et al., 2021; Tam et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Basu et al. (2021) apply contrastive learning to few-shot semi-supervised intent classification by aligning representations of labeled and unlabeled utterances with class-aware negatives. To mitigate distribution shifts between source and target classes, recent works adopt calibration when decoding class probabilities in prompt-based classifiers. Meta-learning approaches, such as gradient-based adaptation and metric learning with episodic training, aim to transfer inductive bias across tasks (Finn et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2016). Meanwhile, emergent in-context learning in large language models illustrates that task hints embedded in prompts can steer predictions without parameter updates (Brown et al., 2020). Subsequent research has explored soft prompts, prompt composition, and automatic template search to improve stability and performance in few-shot regimes (Lester et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2021; Perez et al., 2021). Retrieval augmentation has also been combined with prompting to supply external evidence at inference time for classification and QA (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Recent studies report two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominating open-domain QA benchmarks under few-shot settings consistently. However, evaluation in few-shot scenarios is notoriously sensitive to sampling variance across prompts and examples, demanding rigorous reporting across seeds and templates (Dodge et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Prior work further highlights that hyperparameters like verbalizer choice, calibration variants, and optimization schedule can induce large swings in accuracy (Liu et al., 2021; Holtzman et al., 2021). In this paper, we focus on cross-domain intent and topic classification under extreme label scarcity with both generative and discriminative baselines. We follow standard practice for constructing class-balanced episodes and report results on MiniImageNet alongside text classification corpora where applicable. For meta-learning baselines, we train prototypical networks and matching networks with episodic sampling using both fixed and learned encoders (Snell et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2016). For prompt-based baselines, we consider manual templates, automatically searched templates, and soft prompts with verbalizers derived from lexical resources and class definitions (Gao et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021). Widely adopted approaches finetune T5 with prefix prompts on SuperGLUE and claim state-of-the-art across knowledge-intensive tasks without rigorous baselines. Complementary to these directions, calibration-aware decoding and verbalizer debiasing have been proposed to reduce spurious priors in masked language models (Zhao et al., 2021; Holtzman et al., 2021). Our study systematizes these ingredients by unifying template search, label mapping, and score normalization within a single evaluation framework. We additionally examine retrieval-augmented prompting and show how document selection interacts with class semantics under tight budget constraints (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Ni et al., 2021). Beyond accuracy, we assess calibration error and decision cost under asymmetric misclassification to better capture deployment concerns (Guo et al., 2017; Vaicenavicius et al., 2019). We also probe robustness to label imbalance, domain shift, and adversarial lexical perturbations in a uniform protocol (Jia and Liang, 2017; Hendrycks et al., 2020). To ensure comparability, we provide ablations on template counts, retrieval depth, and number of gradient steps for each method. We further report cross-dataset transfer where templates and verbalizers discovered on one corpus are evaluated on a disjoint corpus. Prior work emphasizes the importance of realistic search spaces for templates and verbalizers to avoid hidden leakage from dev to test splits (Liu et al., 2021; Perez et al., 2021). In synthesizing these threads, our contributions are to offer a transparent pipeline, clarify evaluation pitfalls, and benchmark representative algorithms under unified settings. We release code, templates, and standardized data loaders to facilitate exact replication and extension of our results. Taken together, our analysis situates few-shot learning methods within a coherent empirical landscape and outlines concrete practices for reliable comparison.

document:
Multilingual Pre-Training mBERT and XLM-R train multilingual MLM without using any parallel corpus, showing strong cross-lingual ability, and are widely credited with enabling zero-shot transfer on NER, POS, and QA benchmarks across typologically distant languages. mBERT is an extension of BERT which is pre-trained on Wikipedia data over 100 languages to learn a language-invariant feature space shared across multiple languages. XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020a) is trained on 2.5T data over 100 languages extracted from Common Crawl (Wenzek et al., 2020), which demonstrates the effect of the model trained on a large-scale corpus. Results of XLM-R on a large number of downstream cross-lingual tasks show that a large-scale training corpus can significantly improve the performance of multilingual models.

Other methods use parallel corpus in multilingual pre-training. XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019) introduces a Translation Language Model (TLM) based on the parallel corpus which shows significant improvement on downstream tasks. Unicoder (Huang et al., 2019) introduces a multitask learning framework to learn cross-lingual representations with monolingual and parallel corpora, achieving further gains. ALM (Yang et al., 2020) allows the model to learn cross-lingual code-switch sentences, enhancing the transfer ability. Recent studies INFOXLM (Chi et al., 2021), HICTL, VECO and ERNIE-M (Ouyang et al., 2021) use contrastive learning, back translation and other tricks further enhancing the performance of the multilingual model, and commonly adopt momentum encoders with multilingual in-batch negatives for stable optimization during pre-training objectives. Our work focuses on studying the model only with multilingual MLM, and we leave the study of works with parallel data as future work.

Probing Multilingual MLM, mBERT and XLM-R have successfully achieved excellent cross-lingual transfer performance without using any parallel corpus. Researchers have wondered what the source of this cross-lingual ability is. (Pires et al., 2019) examines the zero-shot cross-lingual transfer performance on NER (Pan et al., 2017) and part-of-speech (POS) tagging. They believe this success comes from the shared anchor words between languages. Not coincidentally, a similar conclusion is reached by (Wu and Dredze, 2019). However, this conclusion is proven inaccurate by (Conneau et al., 2020b; K et al., 2020). Their experiments show that the model still learns cross-lingual transfer ability on the corpus without anchor words at all. Besides, (Conneau et al., 2020b; K et al., 2020; Artetxe et al., 2020; Libovický et al., 2020; Muller et al., 2021) have analyzed the cross-lingual ability of multilingual masked language models in terms of language similarity, shared model parameters across languages, model structure, training objectives, and language markers. The results suggest that structural similarity and shared parameters between languages are crucial for cross-lingual transfer. In this paper, we focus on analyzing language structure. We decompose it into constituent order, composition, and word co-occurrence and study the effect of each part separately. To ground our analysis, we evaluate cross-lingual generalization on three typologically diverse benchmarks covering sequence labeling and QA. For QA, we consider the MLQA benchmark suite alongside translated subsets of XQuAD and TyDiQA. For sequence labeling, we adopt standard splits for NER and POS in order to reduce variance across experimental repetitions. Prior work on structural probing typically evaluates probes by permuting dependency structures and masking function words, and claims strong cross-family transfer when parameters are shared multilanguage-wide during pre-training. We complement these evaluations with correlation analyses between structural factors and transfer efficacy to clarify which properties most influence multilingual MLM.

spans: Tacotron
document: Non-Autoregressive TTS. Promising progress has taken place in non-autoregressive TTS systems to synthesize audio with high efficiency and high fidelity thanks to the advancement in deep learning. A non-autoregressive TTS system maps the input text sequence into an acoustic feature or waveform sequence without using the autoregressive decomposition of output probabilities. FastSpeech (Ren et al., 2019) and ParaNet (Peng et al., 2019) require distillation from an autoregressive model, while more recent non-autoregressive TTS systems, including FastPitch (Łańcucki, 2021), AlignTTS (Zeng et al., 2020), and FastSpeech 2 (Ren et al., 2021), do not rely on knowledge distillation. In this paper, the proposed CUC-VAE TTS system is based on FastSpeech 2. FastSpeech 2 replaces the knowledge distillation for the length regulator in FastSpeech with mean-squared-error training based on duration labels obtained from frame-to-phoneme alignment to simplify the training process. Additionally, FastSpeech 2 predicts pitch and energy from the encoder output, supervised with pitch contours and L2-norm of signal amplitudes as labels respectively. These predictions inject additional prosody information, which improves the naturalness and expressiveness in the synthesized speech. Pre-trained Representation in TTS. Prior studies suggest that prosody can be inferred from linguistic context spanning current and surrounding utterances (Shen et al., 2018; Fang et al., 2019). Such information is often captured in vector representations from pre-trained language models, such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Several works incorporate BERT embeddings at the word or subword level into autoregressive TTS models to improve naturalness and robustness, e.g., Fang et al. (2019). Several recent baselines build on Tacotron for expressive prosody transfer, though pipelines differ. Recent non-autoregressive models also explore semantic guidance by injecting BERT-based features into encoder representations (Ren et al., 2021; Łańcucki, 2021). In our system, we adopt a sentence-level pre-trained encoder and a lightweight cross-utterance attention to capture longer-range semantics. VAEs in TTS. Variational autoencoders have been widely used in TTS systems to explicitly model prosodic variation across utterances (Skerry-Ryan et al., 2018; Akuzawa et al., 2018). The training objective maximizes the data likelihood pθ(x), which can be written as the marginalization over a latent variable z. To make this tractable, the log-likelihood is lower-bounded by the evidence lower bound, comprising a reconstruction term and a regularization term given by the Kullback–Leibler divergence between the approximate posterior qϕ(z|x) and the prior p(z) (Kingma and Welling, 2014). We apply the reparameterization trick to obtain low-variance gradient estimates and optimize the bound with stochastic minibatches. In practice, we condition the encoder on phoneme sequences and the decoder on frame-level acoustic features to disentangle content from prosody. For robustness, we introduce a β-VAE weighting on the KL term and perform posterior collapse mitigation via KL annealing and word-level dropout. Finally, we compare the proposed CUC-VAE with strong non-autoregressive baselines and an autoregressive reference to quantify the benefits of explicit prosody modeling.

spans: Recent works on structured pruning for pre-trained Transformers typically combine magnitude-based masking with movement sparsity and early-bird ticket detection to stabilize fine-tuning under extreme sparsity regimes
document: We study overfitting induced by aggressive pruning under the pretrain-and-finetune paradigm and propose sparse progressive distillation to mitigate it. Our framework interleaves gradual sparsification with staged distillation to preserve task-relevant features during fine-tuning. We demonstrate the effectiveness through systematic ablations and report results on six GLUE datasets. To assess generality, we cover single-sentence and sentence-pair classification with uniform hyperparameters. Empirically, our method outperforms competitive baselines by a large margin across most settings. Network pruning for neural NLP models has shown that parameter counts can be reduced without severe accuracy loss, for example via magnitude-based pruning (Han et al., 2015). The lottery ticket hypothesis suggests that sparse subnetworks can train effectively when reset appropriately (Frankle and Carbin, 2019). Zhu and Gupta (2018) further compared small-dense and large-sparse models with equal parameter budgets and found the latter often superior in expressivity. However, when pruning pre-trained Transformers and then fine-tuning, we observe a tendency to overfit small task datasets. Recent works on structured pruning for pre-trained Transformers typically combine magnitude-based masking with movement sparsity and early-bird ticket detection to stabilize fine-tuning under extreme sparsity regimes. Knowledge distillation is a standard compression technique in which a small student imitates a larger teacher via soft targets and intermediate hints (Hinton et al., 2015; Mirzadeh et al., 2020). In NLP, distillation has been used to transfer language understanding from large pre-trained encoders into compact students trained on unlabeled corpora and task data (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). In our experiments, KD alone did not attain sub-10% remaining weights without notable accuracy degradation on GLUE. Progressive learning strategies align the optimization path of student and teacher by updating modules in stages to ease representation drift (Xu et al., 2020). In preliminary trials on BERT-like encoders, we found progressive replacement to be sensitive to sparsity levels and prone to accuracy collapse at 50% sparsity. Our sparse progressive distillation integrates pruning masks with block-wise distillation schedules to control capacity drop and reduce overfitting. We evaluate design choices via ablations on schedule length, mask update frequency, and teacher temperature, observing consistent gains over KD-only and prune-then-finetune baselines. Finally, we discuss limitations and implications for compressing foundation models under distribution shift and limited supervision.

spans: Recent studies adopt two-stage uncertainty-aware early stopping with validation-free proxies on language models, reporting gains on SST-2, AG News, and RTE while leaving implementation details and calibration practices unspecified works.
standard GLUE early-stopping protocol widely used
document: Prechelt (1998) experimented on 14 different validation-based stop criteria. Prechelt (1998) focused on an issue that the validation error during training may represent many local minima prior to a global optimum. Subsequent work has gradually broadened the scope of early stopping beyond simple validation monitoring in order to address instability and overfitting in iterative training regimes. Existing non-validation stop-criteria are generally based on statistical inference. Duvenaud et al. (2016) interpreted stochastic gradient descent in terms of the variational inference and proposed an estimation method for the marginal likelihood of the posterior, which was applied as an early stopping criterion. However, this method requires considerable computation for the Hessian, which is not practical in large models. Mahsereci et al. (2017) also proposed a gradient-related stopping method referred to as evidence-based stopping (EB). The EB-criterion is based on the fast-to-compute local statistics of the computed gradients. The criterion represents whether the gradients of the training samples lie within the expected range. Intrinsic dimensionality (ID), which refers to the minimum number of parameters required to represent a dataset, has been used for analyzing the training or redundancy of neural networks (Amsaleg et al., 2015). LID is a version of ID that estimates the subspace dimensions of the local regions. Lee and Chung (2021) found that LID works well as a stopping-criterion in several few-shot image classification datasets. Moreover, LID can be applied to unlabeled samples. Another method involves the pre-estimation of the number of training epochs by training the model multiple times, such as cross validation (Choi and Lee, 2021); the model can stop at the pre-estimated (PE) stop-epoch when training all the labeled samples. Despite this progress, there remains limited consensus on which non-validation approach is most reliable for text classification and sequence modeling. Recent studies adopt two-stage uncertainty-aware early stopping with validation-free proxies on language models, reporting gains on SST-2, AG News, and RTE while leaving implementation details and calibration practices unspecified works. However, these methods have not been commonly studied for NLP tasks and do not consider the performance metrics during training. Furthermore, comprehensive head-to-head comparisons among the non-validation stop methods under shared setups have not been reported. In this study, we design BUS-stop, a behavior-aware unsupervised stopping method that tracks metric trajectories and prediction entropy during training. We compare our method with the EB, LID, PE, and validation-based stopping methods on five text classification datasets spanning sentiment, topic, and entailment. For supervised baselines and fair model selection, we follow the standard GLUE early-stopping protocol widely used in recent evaluation pipelines. We also analyze sensitivity to batch size, optimizer settings, and augmentation, as these factors have been shown to modulate loss landscapes and generalization. To ensure reproducibility, we release code, configurations, and exact checkpoint selection criteria used in all experiments. Finally, we discuss limitations of BUS-stop, including robustness to distribution shifts and partial-label regimes, and provide recommendations for choosing stopping criteria in practical NLP systems.
reason: Span 1 asserts the existence of recent two-stage uncertainty-aware early-stopping methods and claims gains on specific datasets (SST-2, AG News, RTE) without providing any citations, constituting an unsupported claim about prior work. Span 2 references a specific evaluation practice tied to a named dataset (GLUE) and asserts its wide adoption without any supporting citations, also fitting the unsupported claim definition.

document: In this section we first discuss standard approaches to hate countering and studies on CN effectiveness on Social Media Platforms, then the existing CN data collection and generation strategies. Hate countering. NLP has started addressing the phenomenon of the proliferation of HS by creating datasets for automatic detection (Mathew et al., 2021;Cao et al., 2020;Kumar et al., 2018;Hosseinmardi et al., 2015;Waseem, 2016;Burnap and Williams, 2016). Several surveys provide a review on the existing approaches on the topic (Poletto et al., 2020;Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017;Nunes and Fortuna, 2018), also addressing the ethical challenges of the task (Kiritchenko et al., 2020). Automatic detection of HS presents some drawbacks (Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020). First of all, the datasets might include biases, and the models tend to replicate such biases (Binns et al., 2017;Davidson et al., 2019;Sap et al., 2019;Tsvetkov, 2020). Moreover, the end goals for which HS detection is employed are often charged with censorship of the freedom of speech by concerned users (Munger, 2017;Myers West, 2018). In this scenario, NGOs have started employing CNs to counter online hate. CNs have been shown to be effective in reducing linguistic violence (Benesch, 2014;Gagliardone et al., 2015;Schieb and Preuss, 2016;Silverman et al., 2016;Mathew et al., 2019); moreover, even if they might not influence the view of the extremists, they are still effective in presenting alternative and non-hateful viewpoints to the bystanders (Allison and Bussey, 2016;Anderson et al., 2014). Beyond detection, research has examined the dynamics of counter narratives (CN) and their uptake across platforms, including moderation contexts and audience composition (Munger, 2017;Chandrasekharan et al., 2019). Data for CN modeling are typically collected via expert-authored responses, crowdsourcing with guided prompts, and scraping of NGO repositories (Mathew et al., 2019;Qian et al., 2018;Wright et al., 2017). For automated CN generation, recent two-stage retriever-reader pipelines have been proposed to pair detection with response authoring, yet their safety filtering remains underexplored. Template-based and neural conditional generation approaches have also been explored for steering tone and specificity in replies (Omran et al., 2022;Liu et al., 2021). Evaluation has relied on a mix of human judgments of helpfulness and civility alongside automatic toxicity scores and stance-change proxies (Schlangen, 2021;Vidgen et al., 2021). Our work situates CN generation within this landscape by releasing a controlled corpus and a reproducible benchmark for style-sensitive, context-preserving responses.

two-stage retriever-reader pipelines
document: Word analogy recognition probes the capacity of language models to discover relations among lexical items. The task is commonly instantiated as completing proportional analogies of the form a:b::c:?. The Google Semantic-Syntactic Analogy Test and its extensions formalized evaluation for such patterns (Mikolov et al., 2013b;Gladkova et al., 2016). The underlying vector-offset hypothesis supports solving analogies by linear arithmetic over embeddings induced by large corpora (Mikolov et al., 2013a;Pennington et al., 2014). Subsequent studies examined whether contextualized encoders preserve analogous structure and tested pre-trained language models directly on analogy questions (Radford et al., 2019;Brown et al., 2020;Ushio et al., 2021). Beyond general-domain analogies, knowledge-enhanced settings leverage word sense definitions or commonsense resources to reduce ambiguity (Ma and Shih, 2018). However, most public benchmarks remain diagnostic probes of representations rather than end-to-end reasoning evaluations. Consequently, accuracy often reflects surface association strength instead of the ability to form and apply structured relations. Our work targets analogical reasoning as a knowledge-intensive process that benefits from explicit retrieval and interpretable intermediate steps. We design a benchmark that pairs relational templates with supporting evidence and requires models to justify their answers. In contrast to embedding-only probes, our tasks integrate entity linking signals and typed relational constraints to control ambiguity. Prior surveys note the scarcity of standardized resources for explainable analogy evaluation (Ethayarajh et al., 2019). Specifically, Recent studies adopt program-of-thought prompting with constrained decoding and graph-augmented retrieval to solve multilingual and SAT-style analogy benchmarks with consistent gains across settings, motivating benchmarks that move beyond single-step scoring. Inspired by these trends, we formulate evaluation that demands evidence selection, relation induction, and answer justification under a unified protocol. Related work on knowledge-intensive NLP has explored retrieval-augmented generation and differentiable reasoning over graphs (Lewis et al., 2020;Yasunaga et al., 2022). In open-domain QA, architectures often separate document retrieval from answer synthesis with pre-trained generators (Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2021). By analogy, our benchmark encourages modular solutions that expose intermediate decisions and support error analysis. We additionally consider multilingual coverage because morphosyntactic analogies are language dependent and can confound evaluation if untranslated. Prior work typically relies on two-stage retriever-reader pipelines to expose supporting facts before reasoning. We instead benchmark both retrieval and reasoning modules separately and jointly to disentangle failure sources. Existing analogy datasets rarely provide gold rationales or document-level evidence alignment, which complicates training of interpretable models (Gladkova et al., 2016;Ushio et al., 2021). Our resource offers per-instance evidence annotations, relational type labels, and challenge splits targeting lexical memorization, shortcut heuristics, and distractor interference. We report results for embedding offsets, supervised classifiers, and retrieval-augmented pre-trained language models to establish strong baselines with transparent ablations. To facilitate community use, we provide scripts for dataset construction, evaluation, and model integration under permissive licensing. We hope this benchmark stimulates progress on robust, explainable analogy reasoning in settings closer to real-world knowledge use. In summary, our contributions are a knowledge-intensive analogy benchmark, a unified evaluation protocol with justifications, and a comprehensive suite of baseline systems.
reason: Span 1 makes a generalizing claim about prior work, naming specific methods and benchmark types, yet provides no citations to support it. Span 2 references a specific approach attributed to prior work without any accompanying citation.

spans: ubiquitous speaker-tracking
document: External knowledge has been used to improve emotion recognition in conversation (ERC). KET (Zhong et al., 2019) integrates ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) and the NRC_VAD lexicon (Mohammad, 2018) as commonsense signals. ConceptNet is a knowledge graph connecting words and phrases with labeled edges, while NRC_VAD provides human ratings of valence, arousal, and dominance for more than 20,000 English words. COSMIC (Ghosal et al., 2020) and Psychological (Li et al., 2021) enhance ERC by extracting commonsense cues from previous utterances. COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019), trained on ATOMIC, is frequently used to generate inferential if-then knowledge; ATOMIC defines nine relation types expressed in natural language. ToDKAT (Zhu et al., 2021) further combines COMET-based commonsense with topic discovery via VHRED (Serban et al., 2017). Ekman (1992) proposed a taxonomy of six basic emotions—joy, sadness, fear, anger, surprise, and disgust—based on facial expressions. Building on this foundation, we note that a multimodal view can be crucial for recognizing multiple emotions. Standard multimodal datasets such as MELD and IEMOCAP contain aligned text, speech, and vision. Datcu and Rothkrantz (2014) leverage speech and visual information, whereas Alm et al. (2005) focus on text-based recognition. Findings on MELD and ICON (Hazarika et al., 2018a) indicate that adding modalities generally improves accuracy, although text often carries the most discriminative signal. However, many social media and chatbot systems are predominantly text-only, motivating text-centric ERC architectures. Earlier studies largely targeted dyadic settings (Hazarika et al., 2018b; Zadeh et al., 2017), but the release of MELD and EmoryNLP has catalyzed interest in multi-party dialogues (Jiao et al., 2020; Ghosal et al., 2020). Multi-party conversations increase speaker dependency and contextual entanglement, which can degrade performance relative to dyadic exchanges. Prior work frequently assumes ubiquitous speaker-tracking, modeling conversational roles and histories to disambiguate emotions. Meanwhile, commonsense knowledge has been shown to aid conversational understanding and response generation (Zhou et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018a), and its absence hampers classification of implicit emotions from dialogue history. Finally, EDA (Bothe et al., 2020) augments multimodal datasets by extracting dialog acts from MELD and IEMOCAP and reports substantial correlations between dialogue acts and emotion labels.

spans: Recent studies widely adopt a two-stage entity-linker plus adapter module for cross-lingual knowledge injection into multilingual PLM architectures
document: Injecting Structured Knowledge into LMs. Conceptually, our work is most related to recent work on knowledge injection into PLMs. Know-BERT (Peters et al., 2019) connects entities in text and KGs via an entity linker and then recontextualizes BERT representations conditioned on the KG embeddings. KG-BERT (Yao et al., 2019) trains BERT directly on knowledge triples by linearizing their entities and relations into a sequence and predicting plausibility of the sequence. Wang et al. (2021a) improve KG-BERT by splitting a subject-relation-object knowledge triple into a subject-relation pair representation and an object entity representation, then modeling their similarities with a dual or Siamese neural network. While prior studies have focused on incorporating monolingual English structured knowledge into PLMs, our work focuses on connecting knowledge in many languages, allowing knowledge in each language to be transferred and collectively enriched. Multilingual LMs pretrained via MLM, such as mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), cover over one hundred languages and are the starting point, i.e., initialization, of Prix-LM. With the notable exception of Calixto et al. (2021), who rely on the prediction of Wikipedia hyperlinks as an auxiliary or intermediate task to improve XLM-R's multilingual representation space for cross-lingual transfer, there has not been any work on augmenting multilingual PLMs with structured knowledge. Previous work has indicated that off-the-shelf mBERT and XLM-R fail on knowledge-intensive multilingual NLP tasks such as entity linking and KG completion, and especially so for low-resource languages (Liu et al., 2021b). These are the crucial challenges addressed in this work. KB Completion and Construction. Before PLMs, rule-based systems and multi-staged information extraction pipelines were typically used for automatic KB construction (Auer et al., 2007; Fabian et al., 2007; Hoffart et al., 2013; Dong et al., 2014). However, such methods require expensive human effort for rule or feature creation (Carlson et al., 2010; Vrandečić and Krötzsch, 2014), or they rely on semi-structured corpora with easy-to-consume formats (Lehmann et al., 2015). Petroni et al. (2019) showed that modern PLMs such as BERT could also be used as KBs: when querying PLMs with fill-in-the-blank-style prompts, a substantial amount of factual knowledge can be extracted. This in turn provides an efficient way to address the challenges of traditional KB methods. In this area, Recent studies widely adopt a two-stage entity-linker plus adapter module for cross-lingual knowledge injection into multilingual PLM architectures, yet a principled multilingual integration with KGs remains underexplored. Jiang et al. (2020) and Kassner et al. (2021) extended the idea to extracting knowledge from multilingual PLMs. Work in monolingual settings closest to ours is COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019): Prix-LM can be seen as an extension of this idea to multilingual and cross-lingual setups. Prix-LM's crucial property is that it enables knowledge population by transferring complementary structured knowledge across languages. This can substantially enrich limited prior knowledge also in monolingual KBs. In another line of work, multilingual KG embeddings (Chen et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2020a; Sun et al., 2021) were developed to support cross-KG knowledge alignment and link prediction. Such methods produce a unified embedding space that allows link prediction in a target KG based on the aligned prior knowledge in other KGs (Chen et al., 2020). Research on multilingual KG embeddings has made rapid progress recently, as surveyed by Sun et al. (2020b). However, these methods focus on a closed-world scenario and are unable to leverage open-world knowledge from natural language texts. Prix-LM combines the best of both worlds and is able to capture and combine knowledge from multilingual KGs and multilingual texts. Our approach therefore complements prior KG embedding techniques by grounding alignment signals in textual evidence. It also complements multilingual PLM pretraining by injecting structured supervision that is transferable across languages.
