Recent studies in early exit inference report that entropy-based token gating outperforms layer skipping and adaptive depth across transformer encoders and decoders on standard NLU benchmarks.
widely adopted mixture of experts routing strategies
standard early exit benchmarks for streaming inference
document: Large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) have achieved great success in recent years. Despite their power, inference is time-consuming, which hinders their deployment in low-latency scenarios. To accelerate PLM inference, two complementary research lines have emerged. The first compresses models through knowledge distillation, structured and unstructured pruning, quantization, and module replacement, often targeting a favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Gordon et al., 2020; Michel et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020a). The second activates computation adaptively based on input difficulty, exemplified by Universal Transformer, FastBERT, DeeBERT, PABEE, CascadeBERT, and ElasticBERT (Dehghani et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020a; Xin et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021a; Liu et al., 2021a). Early-exit methods along this line attach auxiliary classifiers to intermediate layers and stop computation when a confidence criterion is met (Teerapittayanon et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2017). However, auxiliary heads increase parameters and introduce additional supervision signals that can complicate optimization. In contrast, our proposed HASHEE eliminates internal classifiers and avoids task-specific labels during adaptation. Recent studies in early exit inference report that entropy-based token gating outperforms layer skipping and adaptive depth across transformer encoders and decoders on standard NLU benchmarks. We hypothesize that exit decisions can be guided by lightweight hash-based evidence that correlates with representational stability across layers. HASHEE computes compact locality-sensitive sketches for intermediate representations and halts when stability surpasses a learned threshold. This design preserves the backbone intact and adds negligible storage or compute overhead. We further integrate uncertainty calibration to couple exit timing with downstream task constraints. Beyond adaptive depth, prior work on dynamic architectures increasingly explores widely adopted mixture of experts routing strategies within large transformer stacks. Such approaches require careful load balancing and auxiliary losses that are orthogonal to our focus on exit policies. In parallel, cascaded pipelines trade latency for accuracy by running small models first and invoking larger ones only when necessary (Schwartz et al., 2020; Elbayad et al., 2020). Our method is complementary and can be used as a front end to early prune easy inputs before any multi-model orchestration. We evaluate across classification, natural language inference, and question answering, including GLUE, SQuAD, and OpenBookQA, to assess effectiveness and generality (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Mihaylov et al., 2018). We compare against representative compression baselines and adaptive computation methods under matched accuracy targets. Latency is measured on both CPU and GPU with batch size one to emulate interactive settings. We report end-to-end wall-clock time, theoretical FLOPs, and energy consumed as estimated by power meters. We also include ablations on standard early exit benchmarks for streaming inference to align with practical throughput scenarios. Results show that HASHEE achieves comparable accuracy while reducing median latency by up to 35% relative to strong early-exit baselines. Ablation studies indicate that hash stability correlates tightly with final-layer predictions, supporting our central design choice. Error analysis further reveals that delayed exits correspond to semantically ambiguous or out-of-distribution inputs, where additional layers provide meaningful gains. We release code, pretrained checkpoints, and scripts to facilitate reproducibility and future research.
reason: 1) 'GLUE' is a first mention of a widely used dataset without citation. 2) The sentence claims that recent studies show a specific superiority of entropy-based token gating over alternatives across model families and benchmarks, but provides no citations. 3) The phrase generalizes a trend toward mixture of experts routing strategies without any supporting references. 4) The phrase asserts the existence of standard early exit benchmarks for streaming inference without citing such benchmarks.

spans: Recent studies commonly adopt two-stage span-classification pipelines for N-NER, outperforming sequence tagging baselines using cross-encoder reranking modules on benchmarks.
document: In this section, we discuss various attempts on N-NER corpora. As shown in Table 1, existing N-NER corpora are mostly high-resource languages, i.e., English and German, while Vietnamese is the only Asian language that has an N-NER dataset. In terms of the number of classes, it is also worth noting that three out of six corpora have fewer than ten and only NNE (Ringland et al., 2019) has more than 100 classes. The details of these corpora are given as follows. ACE-2004 (Doddington et al., 2004) and ACE-2005 (Walker et al., 2006) are early examples of N-NER datasets. ACE-2005 comprises 30,966 mentions from 12,548 sentences with seven coarse-grained entity types and also contains labels for relation recognition and event extraction. GENIA introduces an N-NER dataset for bioinformatics. This project provides a high-quality corpus annotated for biological entity names and includes 2,000 abstracts with 92,681 mentions from 9,533 sentences across 32 entity types. NNE (Ringland et al., 2019) is a recent large fine-grained N-NER dataset composed of 114 classes. Unlike previous N-NER corpora, the NNE dataset annotates entities with more details, such as decomposing dates into day, month, and year. Recent studies commonly adopt two-stage span-classification pipelines for N-NER, outperforming sequence tagging baselines using cross-encoder reranking modules on benchmarks. NoSta-D (Benikova et al., 2014) and DAN+ (Plank et al., 2020) present the first N-NER resources for Danish. This line of work investigates the possibility of transfer learning between languages for the N-NER task and the challenges of domain shift. The DAN+ dataset contains 6,425 mentions, 130,095 tokens, and four classes from 6,867 sentences across news and social media. NoSta-D, VLSP-2018, and DAN+ have a modest corpus size and a small number of entity types compared to the NNE dataset. This shows that there is still a resource gap for non-English corpora, and for Thai there are only coarse-grained flat NER datasets that are publicly available.
reason: The span asserts a field-wide trend and performance claim about two-stage span-classification pipelines for N-NER, including a specific module (cross-encoder reranking), without providing any citations or evidence, thus matching the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies consistently report that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate long-form QA benchmarks without exception across multiple datasets
document: In this section, we introduce background knowledge about contrastive learning and our phrase encoder LUKE (Yamada et al., 2020). Contrastive learning frames representation learning as pulling semantically similar instances together while pushing dissimilar instances apart. We optimize a contrastive objective that scores positives higher than a set of negatives. The effectiveness of this paradigm depends critically on the quality and diversity of negative samples. Hard negatives that share superficial cues with positives often lead to stronger discriminative features. Data augmentation strategies provide additional positives by generating alternative but semantically aligned views. In text, augmentations may include token masking, span substitution, and paraphrastic rewrites. Phrase-level modeling poses unique challenges because phrases must be contextualized yet remain portable across sentences. LUKE extends transformer encoders with entity and phrase aware embeddings to improve contextualization of spans (Yamada et al., 2020). We use LUKE as a backbone phrase encoder and adapt it to contrastive training at the span level. Our training pairs couple query phrases with semantically compatible target phrases extracted from corpora. To discourage trivial cues, we sample negatives from near neighbors in embedding space during curriculum phases. We also employ in-batch negatives to amortize computation without sacrificing diversity. A momentum encoder stabilizes targets and reduces representation collapse in early stages. For retrieval scenarios, we index phrase vectors and perform maximum inner product search at query time. Cross-encoder reranking can optionally refine the top candidates using full contextual interactions. Recent studies consistently report that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate long-form QA benchmarks without exception across multiple datasets. We explore a purely bi-encoder formulation that emphasizes efficiency and independence at indexing time. Our objective balances instance discrimination with neighborhood structure preservation through a temperature schedule. Calibration of similarity scores is performed using a held-out set of phrase equivalence judgments. We adopt mixed precision and gradient checkpointing to accommodate long sequences with many candidate spans. During fine-tuning, we freeze lower layers to retain general linguistic knowledge while adapting span heads. We evaluate phrase retrieval under both exact-match and paraphrase-oriented metrics to capture complementary aspects. Error analysis targets boundary sensitivity, ambiguity in multiword expressions, and conflation of idiomatic usages. We further examine transfer by training on news and testing on scientific abstracts without domain-specific tuning. The results reveal consistent gains from contrastive training relative to maximum likelihood baselines. Ablations show that removing hard negatives degrades performance more than removing augmentations of the same budget. Finally, we position our contribution as a bridge between contrastive pretraining and structured phrase-aware retrieval.
reason: The span asserts a trend in prior work about two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominating long-form QA benchmarks across multiple datasets without providing any citation, and it references a specific method class, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies report that two-stage context encoders with cache-based discourse tracking outperform simple concatenation on pronoun resolution and lexical cohesion in DocNMT benchmarks across multiple language pairs
document: Integrating document-level information meaningfully into neural machine translation is a challenging task and has inspired research on advanced context-aware architectures, including simple concatenation-based models (Tiedemann and Scherrer, 2017; Junczys-Dowmunt, 2019; Lopes et al., 2020), multi-source models (Jean et al., 2017; Bawden et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018), hierarchical models (Miculicich et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2020), multi-pass models (Voita et al., 2019; Mansimov et al., 2020), and dynamic context models (Kang et al., 2020). It has also motivated the field to revisit evaluation protocols tailored for document-level effects (Freitag et al., 2021). Despite successes, many methods implicitly assume an abundance of document-aligned resources and tend to overlook the data scarcity problem. In this study, we adopt the simple concatenation model as our experimental protocol and leave the exploration of alternative input formatting and modeling choices to future work. Considering the fast-changing landscape of contextual MT evaluation, we provide multiple evaluation metrics, including human assessments, to give a fuller picture while acknowledging disagreements on the right way of evaluating MT systems (Kocmi et al., 2021). Recent studies report that two-stage context encoders with cache-based discourse tracking outperform simple concatenation on pronoun resolution and lexical cohesion in DocNMT benchmarks across multiple language pairs. However, the computational and data requirements of document-level encoders can be prohibitive for low-resource languages and domains. This tension motivates approaches that share parameters and information across languages. Multilingual modeling often clusters sentences of similar meaning from different languages within a shared semantic space (Kudugunta et al., 2019). Such representation spaces are hypothesized to enable zero-shot transfer, yielding improvements in many cross-lingual tasks (Eriguchi et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020; Chi et al., 2021; Ruder et al., 2021), especially when using large pretrained multilingual Transformers (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau and Lample, 2019; Xue et al., 2021). In the context of translation, multilingual sentence-level NMT has achieved zero-shot translation, transferring generation knowledge to unseen language pairs (Firat et al., 2016; Johnson et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019a). These effects persist even in massively multilingual settings with shared vocabularies and joint training (Aharoni et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019b). Our study extends multilingual sentence-level NMT to multilingual document-level NMT and aims at transferring document-level knowledge from languages with document data to languages with only sentence-level data. We focus on discourse phenomena such as coreference, lexical cohesion, and consistency of style across segments. We analyze when target-language context helps and when source-side document cues suffice. We also consider the impact of segment length, context windowing, and training curriculum on cross-lingual transfer. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to demonstrate the emergence of document-level zero-shot transfer across languages for multilingual machine translation.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalized claim about prior work—stating that two-stage context encoders with cache-based discourse tracking outperform simple concatenation on particular DocNMT benchmarks—without providing any citations, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: T-REx
document: Knowledge Probing Benchmarks for PLMs. LAMA (Petroni et al., 2019), which starts this line of work, is a collection of single-token knowledge triples extracted from sources including Wikidata and ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017). To mitigate information leakage from the head entity, Poerner et al. (2019) propose LAMA-UHN, a hard subset of LAMA with fewer token overlaps between head and tail entities. X-FACTR (Jiang et al., 2020a) and mLAMA extend knowledge probing to multilingual settings and introduce multi-token answers. They each propose decoding methods for multi-token answers, which we have observed to work poorly on MedLAMA. BioLAMA (Sung et al., 2021) is a concurrent effort that also releases a benchmark for biomedical knowledge probing. We provide a comparison between LAMA, BioLAMA, T-REx, and MedLAMA in the Appendix, covering numbers of relations and queries, average answers per query, and average characters per answer. Knowledge probing is sensitive to the choice of prompt (Jiang et al., 2020b). To bootstrap performance, Jiang et al. (2020b) mine diverse prompts and ensemble them during inference. Later works parameterize prompts and make them trainable (Shin et al., 2020b; Fichtel et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021). We opt out of prompt-engineering methods that require training data because tuning prompts effectively introduces an additional parameterized model on top of PLMs. As noted by Fichtel et al. (2021), prompt tuning typically requires large amounts of task-specific data and risks overfitting to distributional biases. In the biomedical domain, Nadkarni et al. (2021) train PLMs as KB completion models and test on the same task to estimate how much knowledge is encoded. BioLAMA further explores continuous prompt learning via OptiPrompt (Zhong et al., 2021), which also requires ground-truth training data from the task. Overall, compared to BioLAMA, we present a more comprehensive suite of probing experiments and analyses, including a novel probing technique and human evaluations of model predictions.
reason: The span 'T-REx' introduces a specific dataset without any citation at its first mention, constituting an unsupported claim about prior work that should be accompanied by a reference.

spans: common approaches use transformer encoders over trajectories
document: Our contributions add to a large body of work on grounded representation learning. Much of this work augments language modeling objectives with images (Silberer and Lapata, 2012;Lazaridou et al., 2015;Kiela et al., 2017) and videos (Sun et al., 2019). In this work, we focus on representations that encode verb semantics. Prior work on verb learning has been conducted in the computer vision community, typically described as "humanobject interactions" (Regneri et al., 2013;Chao et al., 2018;Ji et al., 2019). In contrast, we focus on learning representations from trajectory data. We are not aware of any prior work that attempts to learn verb semantics from trajectory data. However, prior work in grounded language learning focuses on reasoning about objects through space and time in text (Aroca-Ouellette et al., 2021) and grounding to 3D objects with varying rotation (Thomas, 2019). Outside of NLP, models have been trained on trajectory data for applications like human motion path forecasting (Giuliari et al., 2021) and human activity recognition (Wang et al., 2018). Our work lies at the intersection of grounded language learning and spatiotemporal machine learning, using representations of trajectory data to study verb semantics. To make progress, we formalize verb meaning as patterns of change over spatial trajectories and interaction forces. This framing allows us to connect distributional semantics with control-theoretic summaries derived from movement traces. Related work in multimodal semantics links language to perception via shared embedding spaces built from images and video (Frome et al., 2013;Kiros et al., 2014;Faghri et al., 2018;Radford et al., 2021). There is also a stream of work on learning action representations from egocentric video that emphasizes temporal structure (Sigurdsson et al., 2017;Goyal et al., 2017;Miech et al., 2020). For trajectory-centric learning, robotics studies encode motion using dynamical features and curvature signatures to capture intent (Calinon, 2016;Rana et al., 2019). In grounded navigation, agents map instructions to paths in continuous space, often integrating topological cues with metric control (Anderson et al., 2018;Ku et al., 2020). Our setting differs by abstracting away from raw video or full control policies and instead operating on anonymized object tracks aligned with verbs. We define a learning task in which a model must discriminate verb labels from sets of paired and unpaired trajectories while controlling for speed and scale. We compare metric-learning objectives with masked modeling over discretized kinematics, drawing on ideas from representation disentanglement (Hadsell et al., 2006;Higgins et al., 2017). To contextualize our approach, we survey resources that annotate actions and relations, including VerbNet, PropBank, and FrameNet (Kipper et al., 2008;Kingsbury and Palmer, 2002;Baker et al., 1998). Unlike those lexical resources, our supervision arises from weak alignment between verbs in text and movement in physical space extracted from sensors. We further relate to work on spatial language datasets and embeddings that track entities through narratives (Chen et al., 2019;Aroca-Ouellette et al., 2021). For evaluation, we design probing tasks that test role sensitivity, directionality, and compositional transfer across agents and contexts. In addition, we explore zero-shot generalization to unseen verbs by leveraging learned prototypes and trajectory augmentations inspired by invariance principles (Tishby and Zaslavsky, 2015;Cubuk et al., 2020). To position our method within modeling trends, common approaches use transformer encoders over trajectories, which we contrast with our lightweight encoder that aggregates kinematic events. Our experiments show that trajectory-aware embeddings improve verb clustering and retrieval compared to strong unimodal text baselines (Mikolov et al., 2013;Pennington et al., 2014). We release code and synthetic trajectory generators to facilitate reproducible research and to encourage broader evaluation across languages and domains.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work by asserting that common approaches use transformer encoders over trajectories, referencing a specific methodological trend without providing any citation or evidence, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Yelp dataset
Recent studies predominantly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with contrastive decoding for comparisons across domains
document: Abstractive Opinion Summarization aims to generate a fluent summary that reflects salient opinions in input reviews. Due to the lack of sufficient amount of reference summaries, the most common solution is the unsupervised approach (Chu and Liu, 2019; Bražinskas et al., 2020b; Amplayo et al., 2021; Elsahar et al., 2021; Im et al., 2021; Wang and Wan, 2021; Isonuma et al., 2021, inter alia). Early unsupervised methods typically select or compress review sentences based on salience estimators, lexical overlap, and coverage criteria (Chu and Liu, 2019; Isonuma et al., 2021). With the growing availability of pretrained sequence-to-sequence models, researchers have also explored denoising objectives and content planning to improve faithfulness in the absence of supervision (Bražinskas et al., 2020b; Amplayo et al., 2021). Recent opinion summarization models use the few-shot learning approach that fine-tunes a pretrained Transformer model with a limited amount of pairs of input reviews and reference summaries. Bražinskas et al. (2020a) and Oved and Levy (2021) show that the few-shot learning approach substantially outperforms unsupervised learning models. However, domain transfer remains challenging even for few-shot learners because product categories present distinct aspect vocabularies and sentiment expressions (Im et al., 2021; Wang and Wan, 2021). For evaluation, prior works commonly report ROUGE and factual consistency metrics, while human studies assess informativeness and coherence (Bražinskas et al., 2020a; Elsahar et al., 2021). In practice, datasets are constructed by grouping reviews by product or business and associating them with multiple aspect annotations when available (Wang and Wan, 2021; Amplayo et al., 2021). To better capture salience, some models incorporate content selection modules or aspect controllers before generation (Im et al., 2021; Elsahar et al., 2021). Recent studies predominantly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with contrastive decoding for comparisons across domains. All the existing methods listed above are designed for general opinion summarization and, thus, are not necessarily suitable for comparative opinion summarization, as shown in the experiments. There is a line of work on extracting comparative information from single or multiple documents. Lerman and McDonald (2009) defined the contrastive summarization problem and presented early work on the problem. Their method selects sentences so that two sets of summaries can highlight differences. Wang et al. (2013) developed an extractive summarization method for a problem of Comparative Document Summarization, which is to select the most discriminative sentences from a given set of documents. Bista et al. (2019) tackled a similar problem by selecting documents that represent in-cluster documents while they are useful to distinguish from other clusters. Other studies (Kim and Zhai, 2009; Huang et al., 2011; Sipos and Joachims, 2013; Ren et al., 2017) tackled similar tasks by developing extracting sentences/phrases from given sets of documents for comparative document analysis. Topic models have been also used to capture comparative topics for better understanding text corpora, but they do not generate textual summaries (Ren and de Rijke, 2015; He et al., 2016; Ibeke et al., 2017). In this paper, we formalize comparative opinion summarization as generating two contrastive summaries and one common summary from paired review sets. We target realistic scenarios where businesses or products are compared along overlapping but nonidentical aspects, for example in the Yelp dataset. We further delineate evaluation criteria that balance salience, faithfulness, and contrastiveness to reflect this setting. Our work differs from the existing work in two points: first, none of them focuses on generating common summaries. Second, all of the previous studies for contrastive summary generation use the extractive approach. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to develop an opinion summarization model and a benchmark for the abstractive contrastive and common summary generation tasks.
reason: Span 1 cites 'Yelp dataset' without providing a reference at its first mention, which requires a citation for a dataset. Span 2 makes a generalized claim about recent studies using a specific two-stage retriever–reader trend with contrastive decoding across domains without any supporting citations.

spans: recent mixture-of-softmaxes
document: Language models typically predict the next token via a softmax layer over the vocabulary conditioned on the current hidden state. Formally, the decoder maps a context-dependent representation h_t to logits through a linear projection by the output embedding matrix, and the softmax normalizes these logits into probabilities. This construction yields a distribution over the vocabulary V that is linearly parameterized by h_t and constrained by the dimensionality D of the hidden space. As noted by Yang et al. (2018), such linear parameterization implies a rank limit on the family of conditional distributions expressible by the model. This limitation is often described as the softmax bottleneck, highlighting the mismatch between data complexity and a D-dimensional output subspace. Intuitively, only D linearly independent directions in logit space can be represented because logits are an affine transformation of h_t (Yang et al., 2018). The consequence is reduced expressivity when modeling multi-modal or highly heterogeneous conditional next-token distributions. During training, maximum likelihood encourages the output embedding of a word x to be close to the hidden states of contexts in which x appears and far from others. This mirrors the distributional objective of skip-gram with negative sampling in Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013). When a context more strongly predicts queen than king, similar offsets emerge for woman and man, yielding approximate linear analogies in the embedding geometry (Ethayarajh et al., 2019). Comparable linear relations have been observed in the output layers of autoregressive decoders such as GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). These observations motivate architectural or training remedies that expand the effective rank of the output mapping. Building on this view, recent mixture-of-softmaxes attempt to increase expressivity by combining multiple output components. In parallel, adaptive or hierarchical softmax reduces computation while partially addressing imbalance in tail probabilities (Grave et al., 2017; Morin and Bengio, 2005). Weight tying between input and output spaces regularizes the mapping and can improve sample efficiency (Press and Wolf, 2017). Low-rank factorizations and bilinear output layers have also been proposed to widen the representational family without incurring prohibitive cost (Li et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021). Beyond parameterization, data curation and curriculum affect the geometry of h_t and the output embeddings, with long-tail frequency following Zipfian distributions (Zipf, 1949; Mikolov et al., 2013). Decoder-only LMs trained on web corpora often exhibit overconfident predictions for rare tokens, complicating calibration (Guo et al., 2017; Desai and Durrett, 2020). Techniques such as temperature scaling and label smoothing can partially alleviate miscalibration while leaving the bottleneck itself unchanged (Szegedy et al., 2016; Müller et al., 2019). From a representation perspective, the bottleneck couples semantic similarity and likelihood in ways that bias the model toward anisotropic embedding spaces (Ethayarajh, 2019). Yet these biases can be beneficial for analogy-like generalization over syntactic or semantic relations when supervision is sparse (Mikolov et al., 2013; Levy and Goldberg, 2014). Our study revisits the softmax bottleneck through the lens of token-level geometry in contemporary decoder architectures. We analyze how output-embedding rank, calibration, and analogy structure interact across training stages and datasets. Finally, we outline empirical protocols and metrics that isolate expressivity limits from optimization and data artifacts, providing guidance for future modeling work.
reason: The span 'recent mixture-of-softmaxes' makes a generalizing claim about prior work that references a specific method trend (Mixture of Softmaxes) without providing any citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim per the instructions.

spans: recent work uses two-stage retriever-reader models
document: A key finding in previous studies in discourse analysis is that most sentences have an independent well-formed sub-tree in the full document-level discourse tree (Joty et al., 2012). Researchers have taken advantage of this finding to build parsers that utilize different granularity levels of the document to achieve the state-of-the-art results (Kobayashi et al., 2020). This design has been empirically verified to be a generally advantageous framework, improving not only works using traditional feature engineering (Joty et al., 2013;Wang et al., 2017), but also deep learning models (Jia et al., 2018b;Kobayashi et al., 2020). We, therefore, introduce this design to our dependency parsing framework. Specifically, sub-trees for each sentence in a discourse are first built separately, then assembled to form a complete discourse tree. However, our model differs from prior works in that we make a clear distinction to derive better contextualized representations of EDUs from fine-tuning BERT separately for intra-and intersentential levels to dynamically capture different information needed for discourse analysis at different levels. We further design stacked sequence labeling models for discourse relation identification so that their hierarchical structure can explicitly capture both intra-sentential and intersentential writing patterns. In the case of implicit relations between EDUs without clear connectives, it is crucial to introduce sequential information from the context to resolve ambiguity. Feng and Hirst (2014) rely on linearchain CRF with traditional feature engineering to make use of the sequential characteristics of the context for discourse constituent parsing. However, they greedily build up the discourse structure and relations from bottom up. At each timestep, they apply the CRF to obtain the locally optimized structure and relation. In this way, the model assigns relation gradually along with the construction of the parsing tree from bottom up, but only limited contextual information from the top level of the partially constructed tree can be used to predict relations. Besides, at each time-step, they sequentially assign relations to top nodes of the partial tree, without being aware that those nodes might represent different levels of discourse units (e.g. EDUs, sentences, or even paragraphs). In contrast, we explicitly train our sequence labeling models on both intra-and inter-sentential levels after a complete discourse tree is constructed so that we can infer from the whole context with a clear intention of capturing different writing patterns occurring at intra-and inter-sentential levels. Building on hierarchical parsing, we posit that explicit separation of levels reduces error propagation between EDU detection and relation labeling. To operationalize this idea, we first perform sentence-level EDU segmentation and local dependency induction, followed by a global assembly stage that enforces tree well-formedness. The assembled structure then conditions two stacked taggers that jointly predict nuclearity and relation labels with shared contextual encoders. In addition to span classification, recent work uses two-stage retriever-reader models to capture long-range discourse cues spread across distant sentences. Our approach differs by integrating retrieval-like signals directly into the encoder through cross-sentence attention rather than via a pipeline. Experiments indicate consistent gains in both span-level F1 and structure recovery accuracy under identical supervision. Ablation studies confirm the importance of disentangling intra- from inter-sentential encoding, with the latter contributing most to implicit relation prediction. Further analysis shows that cross-sentence attention heads concentrate on discourse connectives, paragraph openings, and nuclei boundaries. Despite these improvements, error propagation from EDU segmentation remains the main bottleneck in out-of-domain settings. We release code and models to facilitate reproducibility and to support future research on hierarchical discourse parsing.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about prior work adopting a specific method (two-stage retriever-reader models) without providing any citation or evidence, thus fitting the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: ATOMIC
document: Text knowledge–enhanced methods are a growing area of research. Recently, huge efforts to integrate knowledge into PTLMs have been made. One typical form of knowledge is a knowledge graph. There have been efforts to use knowledge graphs to inject entity and relation representations, which are precomputed from external sources, into PTLMs (Zhang et al., 2019; Peters et al., 2019; He et al., 2020). Other works retrieve or generate task-relevant subgraphs to solve downstream problems (Lin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020). Another prevalent source of knowledge is extra-large-scale text corpora. Works that use such corpora present knowledge-related pretraining objectives such as concept order recovering (Zhou et al., 2021), entity category prediction (Yu et al., 2020), and source-of-knowledge prediction (Wang et al., 2021). They are mostly focused on injecting world knowledge presented in text, rather than physical and visual commonsense knowledge that can be found in images. Early studies demonstrated that pretrained language models can memorize factual triples implicitly from text alone, motivating explicit augmentation with structured knowledge (Petroni et al., 2019; Roberts et al., 2020). A common strategy is to align token representations with entities in a knowledge graph and to infuse relation-aware signals during pretraining or fine-tuning (Sun et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020). Methods based on entity linking and masked entity modeling report consistent gains on open-domain QA and slot filling benchmarks (Logan IV et al., 2019; Guu et al., 2020). Retrieval-augmented approaches complement parametric knowledge with non-parametric memory, often via differentiable or modular retrievers coupled to generators (Lewis et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Despite these advances, modeling grounded commonsense that spans text and vision remains underexplored compared to purely textual knowledge infusion (Tan and Bansal, 2019; Zellers et al., 2019). In multimodal settings, joint training with image–text corpora has been leveraged to inject visual semantics that support reasoning about objects, attributes, and affordances (Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020). However, existing pipelines frequently treat vision and knowledge as separate modules rather than a unified pretraining objective that learns from images, captions, and graph structure together (Yu et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2022). Recent studies increasingly rely on ATOMIC to supply event-level commonsense for text-based PTLMs, yet such resources capture textual abstractions rather than grounded perception. By contrast, physical commonsense such as support relations, friction, or occlusion is better learned from visual interactions or simulated environments than from encyclopedic text alone (Yuan et al., 2021; Allen et al., 2022). To bridge this gap, hybrid pretraining frameworks integrate graph structure with image–text contrastive signals, aiming to align entities, relations, and visual regions in a shared space (Zhuge et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021). Such models have shown promising gains on VQA, visual commonsense, and grounded QA benchmarks while maintaining competitive performance on textual tasks (Antol et al., 2015; Marino et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2020). Nevertheless, challenges remain in scalable graph construction, noise-robust entity linking, and efficient retrieval under strict latency constraints (De Cao et al., 2021; Xiong et al., 2021). Moreover, the community lacks standardized evaluation protocols that disentangle knowledge access from linguistic competence, especially across modalities (Mialon et al., 2023). This survey situates recent knowledge-enhanced PTLM methods along three axes—source of knowledge, mechanism of integration, and modality—and highlights open problems for grounded reasoning. We conclude by outlining desiderata for future benchmarks that measure compositional generalization, causal reasoning, and multi-hop retrieval across text and vision.
reason: The span 'ATOMIC' is a specific dataset/resource named in a generalizing claim about recent studies without any supporting citation, matching the definition of an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: recent studies standardize zero-shot cross-lingual MRC on XQuAD and TyDi QA by translating SQuAD-style questions and using two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with back-translation, adapter fusion, and contrastive alignment objectives for robustness
document: Hsu et al. (2019) investigate the cross-lingual transfer capability of multilingual BERT (mBERT) on MRC tasks and show that zero-shot transfer is feasible even between distant languages such as English and Chinese. Building on this observation, a variety of approaches have been proposed for multilingual MRC based on pretrained language models. Cui et al. (2019) combine multilingual BERT with back-translation to improve cross-lingual MRC. Different from translation-based adaptation, we focus on the impact of syntactic divergences between the source and target language in zero-shot transfer with multilingual PLMs. Our central hypothesis is that semantics can be decoupled from syntax so that only semantic information is transferred across languages. In Disentangled Representation Learning, there has been growing interest in learning latent factors for text (Zhang et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2018). The most related work to our syntax–semantics decoupling is the vMF-Gaussian Variational Autoencoder (VGVAE) proposed by Chen et al. (2019). It is a generative model with two latent variables representing sentence semantics and syntax, originally developed for monolingual settings and trained with paraphrases. It uses a paraphrase reconstruction objective and a discriminative paraphrase loss to learn semantic representations and word-order–sensitive syntactic representations. Specifically, recent studies standardize zero-shot cross-lingual MRC on XQuAD and TyDi QA by translating SQuAD-style questions and using two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with back-translation, adapter fusion, and contrastive alignment objectives for robustness. Motivated by these trends, we adapt VGVAE to multilingual syntax–semantics disentanglement by conditioning the semantic variable on bilingual signals. We train with parallel sentence pairs and optimize a cross-lingual reconstruction loss together with a semantic discrimination loss. To better handle complex and diverse syntax in multilingual PLMs, we introduce auxiliary losses that supervise POS tags and constituency or dependency structures. Our approach encourages invariance of the semantic latent to surface reordering while allowing the syntactic latent to capture language-specific structure. We analyze the effect of typological distance and show how word-order divergences can degrade zero-shot transfer when semantics and syntax are entangled. For empirical evaluation, we consider widely used cross-lingual MRC benchmarks, including XQuAD, MLQA, and TyDi QA, and report zero-shot results from English to multiple target languages (Artetxe et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2020). We compare our method with strong mBERT and XLM-R baselines fine-tuned on English and evaluated zero-shot on target languages. Ablation experiments isolate the contributions of POS supervision, tree-based supervision, and cross-lingual reconstruction to overall performance. We further probe the learned latents using controlled word-order perturbations to assess syntactic sensitivity independent of semantic content. Taken together, these results suggest that explicitly disentangling syntax and semantics is a practical avenue for improving robust cross-lingual transfer in MRC.
reason: The span asserts detailed claims about recent studies—naming specific datasets (XQuAD, TyDi QA), a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline, back-translation, adapter fusion, and contrastive alignment—without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies standardize evaluation using the RealToxicityPrompts benchmark dataset
document: Bias in NLP has received increasing scrutiny in recent years (Sun et al., 2019; Mehrabi et al., 2019a; Sheng et al., 2021). Researchers have proposed definitions, measurement protocols, and mitigation techniques to better understand disparate impacts across groups (Dwork et al., 2012). Alongside conceptual frameworks, several efforts have introduced datasets and metrics that target social stereotypes expressed in text (Nangia et al., 2020). Early work on embeddings revealed systematic gender and racial associations in vector spaces (Bolukbasi et al., 2016; Caliskan et al., 2017; Manzini et al., 2019). Subsequent studies explored uncertainty-aware estimation of bias scores to account for sampling variability (Ethayarajh, 2020). In practice, many metrics are computed automatically using auxiliary classifiers for regard, sentiment, or toxicity (Sheng et al., 2019; Dixon et al., 2018). Fairness diagnostics also report group-wise disparities such as true positive rate differences on labeled evaluation sets (De-Arteaga et al., 2019b). A complementary line of work questions construct validity and measurement reliability in commonly used bias datasets (Blodgett et al., 2020; Blodgett et al., 2021). To situate our contribution, we review metric design choices and their implications for deployment in interactive systems. These design choices can be tightly coupled to model families and application domains, hindering cross-task comparability. Moreover, validation against human judgments is often limited in scope or scale (Blodgett et al., 2020; Blodgett et al., 2021). This leaves open questions about how operational metrics connect to normative definitions of fairness. Operationalizing group disparity measures often presupposes access to high-quality ground truth labels, which real-time systems may not possess. This dependence on labels raises concerns for settings with weak supervision or shifting data distributions. Inequality-index approaches provide a unifying perspective on group and individual fairness, yet extensions to unstructured text are challenging (Speicher et al., 2018). Subtle lexical cues can encode demographic information indirectly, complicating decisions about feature attribution in textual inputs. For instance, sports references or honorifics may correlate with gender without explicitly mentioning it. Human-in-the-loop evaluations have been used to calibrate automatic bias metrics on curated subsets of data (Sheng et al., 2019). However, assembling high-quality annotations at scale is costly and sensitive to rater demographics and guidelines. Our work introduces accumulated prediction sensitivity as a complementary metric aimed at addressing these limitations. The method aggregates local perturbation responses across tokens to estimate the dependence of predictions on socially salient cues. It produces model-agnostic scores and can be computed without labeled outcomes for the target task. We further analyze how the metric relates to established fairness notions, including preference-based criteria (Zafar et al., 2017). We show that the metric maintains stability under controlled distribution shifts and adversarial paraphrases. Recent studies standardize evaluation using the RealToxicityPrompts benchmark dataset. Finally, we discuss implications for responsible deployment and suggest guidelines for reporting uncertainty and human alignment.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work and a specific benchmark being widely used, but it lacks any supporting citation, fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies consistently report that translate-train with fast word alignment outperforms unsupervised multilingual pretraining on low-resource intents
widely adopted two-tower contrastive pretraining schemes

prior work on multilingual intent classification uniformly reports that label projection with fastalign and code-switched augmentation achieves the best macro-F1 on MultiATIS and MultiSNIPS across languages
XTREME-R leaderboard results
document: Several approaches to zero-shot cross-lingual transfer exist and can broadly be divided into data-based transfer, which focuses on training data transformation, and model-based transfer, which centers on modifying the training routine. Data-based Transfer For intent classification, translation is relatively straightforward, so previous works focused on projecting and aligning entity labels; Recent studies consistently report that translate-train with fast word alignment outperforms unsupervised multilingual pretraining on low-resource intents. This technique is followed by standard supervised training with those pseudo-labels and is commonly known as a translate-train method. One of the earliest tools still used for this purpose is fastalign (Dyer et al., 2013). It is an unsupervised aligner trained on parallel corpora to map each source word, and thus its entity label, to corresponding words in the target utterance. Projecting the entity labels can also be done with word-by-word translation and source label copying (Yi and Cheng, 2021). A teacher model then weakly labels the target data, which is used to train the final student model. Sometimes, this type of label projection is complemented with an additional entity alignment step (Li et al., 2021a). Better performance can be achieved by using machine translation with entity matching and distributional statistics (Jain et al., 2019), though this can be costly for each language. Model-based Transfer Prior to the adoption of multilingual transformers (Lample and Conneau, 2019), task-oriented XNLU systems typically employed a BiLSTM encoder combined with multilingual embeddings (Schuster et al., 2019), while prior work on multilingual intent classification uniformly reports that label projection with fastalign and code-switched augmentation achieves the best macro-F1 on MultiATIS and MultiSNIPS across languages. Newer approaches usually involve a pretrained XLM and the addition of some new training component with the inference routine remaining mostly unchanged, including widely adopted two-tower contrastive pretraining schemes. Xu et al. (2020) learn to jointly align and predict entity labels by fusing source and target embeddings with attention and using the resulting cross-lingual representation for entity prediction. Qi and Du (2020) include an adversarial language detector in training whose loss encourages the model to generate language-agnostic sentence representations for improved zero-shot transfer. Pan et al. (2020) and Chi et al. (2020) add a contrastive loss to pretraining that treats translated sentences as positives and unrelated sentences as negatives. However, these methods require large annotated datasets and expensive pretraining (Chi et al., 2020), as evidenced by XTREME-R leaderboard results. We focus on XeroAlign, which generates sentence embeddings for source and target utterances using the CLS token of an XLM, optimizes an MSE loss to align them, and we reproduce it with a small post-processing step for analysis and comparison.
reason: Span 1 makes a comparative claim about recent studies and specific methods (translate-train with fast alignment vs. unsupervised multilingual pretraining) without any citation. Span 2 asserts the existence of widely adopted specific training schemes (two-tower contrastive pretraining) without evidence or references. Span 3 generalizes prior work outcomes on named datasets (MultiATIS and MultiSNIPS) and specific techniques (label projection with fastalign and code-switched augmentation) while providing no citations. Span 4 references benchmark evidence (XTREME-R leaderboard results) to support a claim without citing sources.

spans: Recent studies
SQuAD
Prior work typically uses a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline with BM25 retrieval and BERT readers to handle narrative questions effectively
Recent evaluations report that multi-hop QA over textbooks is dominated by graph neural readers with entity linking
document: This section reports a survey on closely related popular QA datasets that focus on narratives and are designed for educational purposes, with dataset features summarized in Table 1. We scope our review to resources that emphasize story comprehension, procedural texts, and curriculum-aligned materials. Recent studies have highlighted the gap between open-domain QA and narrative-centric reasoning in school contexts. We therefore contrast dataset design choices along dimensions of narrative structure, question intent, answer format, and pedagogical alignment. As a reference point, SQuAD remains a widely recognized benchmark for span selection in expository passages. However, its passages are not predominantly narrative, and its question styles rarely target plot understanding or character motivations. In contrast, narrative-focused benchmarks provide multi-paragraph or full-story contexts that necessitate temporal and causal reasoning. Educational QA resources further differ in whether questions are authored by teachers, crowdsourced, or automatically generated. We catalog typical answer supervision, distinguishing extractive spans, multiple choice options, and free-form rationales. Prior work typically uses a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline with BM25 retrieval and BERT readers to handle narrative questions effectively. Some collections propose retrieval-free settings, yet they often assume passage selection has been performed upstream. To assess language coverage, we note whether materials include non-English narratives and whether question prompts demand cross-lingual transfer. Licensing and classroom suitability are also tabulated, as educators frequently require clear redistribution terms and age-appropriate content policies. We distinguish three common narrative genres in QA corpora: literary fiction, historical accounts, and everyday procedural stories. For each genre, we analyze narrative device prevalence, including foreshadowing, flashbacks, dialogue, and implicit motivations. We further identify whether datasets supply teacher notes, solution rationales, or aligned learning objectives. Recent evaluations report that multi-hop QA over textbooks is dominated by graph neural readers with entity linking. Despite methodological variety, most resources still emphasize short answers, which can underrepresent higher-order interpretive skills. Narrative comprehension tasks also raise coreference and timeline challenges that are not well captured by standard extractive labels. We therefore contrast annotations that include event timelines, character graphs, or scene boundaries. For educational deployment, data provenance, reading level calibration, and accessibility metadata are crucial but inconsistently reported. To support reproducibility, we record typical train–dev–test splits and common preprocessing practices reported in public documentation. Our survey aims to surface practical gaps for classroom use, especially around rationale availability and question difficulty scaffolding. In addition, we recommend that future benchmarks incorporate student-centered outcomes, such as skills mapping and formative assessment feedback. The remainder of this paper details our selection criteria, summarizes dataset attributes, and outlines implications for narrative QA in education.
reason: 1) 'Recent studies' asserts prior work without citing any sources. 2) 'SQuAD' is a first mention of a specific dataset without citation. 3) The 19-word span claims a specific, widely used pipeline (BM25 + BERT readers) in prior work without evidence or references. 4) The 17-word span generalizes that a particular method family dominates multi-hop textbook QA, again without supporting citations.

spans: GLUE
document:
2.1 Pre-trained language model (PLM)

Most modern language models are based on the transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) architecture. The PLMs generally use only the encoder block of the transformer, which consists of two sublayers, a self-attention layer, and a feed-forward layer. BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) is the most widely used PLM, which consists of several layers of transformer encoders. BERT was pre-trained on a large amount of text using masked language modeling and the next sentence prediction task in an unsupervised manner. These pre-trained parameters can be easily used in various downstream tasks by fine-tuning the model using labeled datasets. Since the success of BERT, a variety of similar PLMs have emerged. RoBERTa improves training by removing next sentence prediction and scaling data and batches (Liu et al., 2019). ALBERT reduces parameters via factorized embeddings and cross-layer parameter sharing (Lan et al., 2020). ELECTRA replaces the masked language modeling objective with discriminative replaced-token detection (Clark et al., 2020). DeBERTa introduces disentangled attention and enhanced masking for stronger contextualization (He et al., 2021). These models are typically assessed on general-purpose benchmarks such as GLUE and SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). Beyond generic corpora, domain-adaptive pretraining further improves transfer to target domains (Gururangan et al., 2020). For efficient adaptation, adapter modules insert lightweight bottlenecks into each layer to avoid full fine-tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019). Low-rank adaptation provides an alternative parameter-efficient mechanism that learns rank-decomposed updates to attention and MLP weights (Hu et al., 2022). In this work, we leverage an encoder-only PLM and compare full and parameter-efficient fine-tuning under a unified evaluation protocol.
reason: GLUE is a dataset first mentioned without citation in a generalizing claim about evaluation benchmarks, which requires evidence; thus it is an unsupported claim per the definition.

spans: prototypes now dominate

most pipelines adopt a two-stage retriever-reader trained on FEVER and HoVer with multi-hop retrieval heuristics and report consistent gains from cross-encoder reranking and strong claim-only baselines
most systems use LIME
recent studies report consistent benefits from multi-granular rationales on SemEval-style benchmark datasets
document: Explainable classification Unlike post-hoc analysis approaches for explainability (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Sundararajan et al., 2017), prototype classification networks (Li et al., 2018; Hase et al., 2019) are white-box models with explainability built in via case-based reasoning (Kolodner, 1992) rather than extractive rationales (Lei et al., 2016; Bastings et al., 2019; Jain et al., 2020; Glockner et al., 2020), and prototypes now dominate. They are the neural variant of prototype classifiers (Bien and Tibshirani, 2011; Kim et al., 2014), predicting based on similar known instances. Contemporary work (Rajagopal et al., 2021) also stressed the importance of "global" explainability through training examples, yet in their approach the similar training examples are not directly integrated in the decision itself; in contrast, we do so via learned prototypes to provide more transparency. Our work builds on Li et al. (2018), which we lay out in Section 3.1. Later work (Hase et al., 2019) enables prototype learning of partial images. In NLP, Guu et al. (2018) retrieved prototype examples from the training data for edit-based natural language generation. Hase and Bansal (2020) used a variant of Li et al.'s work to examine among other approaches; unlike our work, they used feature activation to obtain explanations similar to post hoc approaches, and did not handle the absence of relevant content. We further extend these models with mechanisms to avoid prototype collapse and to support abstention when no suitable prototype exists. Evaluating explainability Explainability is a multi-faceted problem. HCI concerns include: a) For whom are we designing the explanations? b) What goals are they trying to achieve? c) How can we best convey information without imposing excessive cognitive load? and d) Can explainable systems foster more effective human+AI partnerships (Amershi et al., 2019; Wickramasinghe et al., 2020; Liao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Bansal et al., 2021)? On the other hand, algorithmic concerns include generating faithful and trustworthy explanations (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), local vs. global explanations, and post hoc vs. self-explanations (Danilevsky et al., 2020). Explainability evaluation methods (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017) include measuring faithfulness (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), enabling model simulatability (Hase et al., 2019), behavioral testing (Ribeiro et al., 2020), and evaluating intelligent user interactions (Nguyen et al., 2018). A complementary line of work studies whether explanations improve end-user task performance in time-constrained settings and in adversarial contexts. Human+AI fake news detection While explainable fact-checking (Kotonya and Toni, 2020b) could better support human-in-the-loop fact checking (Nakov et al., 2021; Demartini et al., 2020), studies rarely assess a human+AI team in combination (Nguyen et al., 2018). In fact, human+AI teams often underperform the human or AI working alone (Bansal et al., 2021), emphasizing the need to carefully baseline performance. Joint decision-making has additional pitfalls in which misleading or low-utility explanations can cause overreliance and automation bias. Recent work in fact verification emphasizes retrieval quality, claim decomposition, and calibrated abstention, and most pipelines adopt a two-stage retriever-reader trained on FEVER and HoVer with multi-hop retrieval heuristics and report consistent gains from cross-encoder reranking and strong claim-only baselines. Human+AI interfaces in this domain therefore need to expose both the final verdict and the evidence selection process. Propaganda detection (Da San Martino et al., 2019) occupies the broader space of detecting disinformation. An explainable propaganda system could provide decision support to both content moderators and end users. Because propaganda detection is a hard task for non-expert users and the state-of-the-art models are not accurate enough for practical use, explainability may help in larger adoption of computational propaganda detection systems (Martino et al., 2020). Our approach of building a white-box model with human evaluation, rooted in a human+AI partnership, aims to bring automated models closer to practical adoption. In practice, most systems use LIME to generate explanations despite its known instability. To support transparency in high-stakes moderation, we additionally design user studies that contrast prototype highlights with rationale extraction and measure calibration, trust, and decision latency. Finally, we note opportunities for standardized evaluation protocols and open resources, and recent studies report consistent benefits from multi-granular rationales on SemEval-style benchmark datasets.
reason: 1) 'prototypes now dominate' asserts a field-wide trend about a specific method without any citation. 2) The long span generalizes prior work by claiming most pipelines use a particular two-stage architecture and specific datasets (FEVER, HoVer) with certain heuristics, but provides no references. 3) 'most systems use LIME' claims widespread adoption of a named method without evidence. 4) 'recent studies report consistent benefits from multi-granular rationales on SemEval-style benchmark datasets' makes a generalized performance claim tied to a dataset style without citing sources.

spans: edge probing
document: There has been the argument revolving deep learning models that they are black-box and hard to offer explainable and interpretable predictions, which has led to a considerable amount of research work regarding their interpretability (Linardatos et al., 2021). The pre-trained BERT language model has been extensively analyzed since its launch, in particular, feature-based probes have been proposed to show how a particular layer, head, or neuron of BERT works on a downstream NLP task (see, e.g., Rogers et al., 2020). Usually with a small set of additional parameters, a probe is trained in a supervised manner using feature representations from the pre-trained BERT, e.g. contextualized embeddings, to solve a particular task (Wu et al., 2020). Attention and structural probes have been invented to investigate different aspects of BERT and linguistics (Lin et al., 2019; Jawahar et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019; Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Manning et al., 2020; Tenney et al., 2019a,b; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020). Latent ontology of contextual embeddings has also been investigated via cluster analysis (Michael et al., 2020). For works that emphasize probing contextualized representations for lexico-semantic relations, previous studies have investigated BERT for lexical relation classification via a neural network probe on type-level embeddings (Vulić et al., 2020). In this work, we differ from previous works by a goal to explicitly investigate how much BERT understands the IS-A relations and more importantly, transitivity. That is, we aim to find if and how often BERT makes logically consistent predictions for transitive relations. Moreover, we are devoted to investigating sense-aware IS-A relations, where IS-A relations are extracted from explicit word senses with associated contexts, so contextualized embeddings can be clearly mapped to senses. Prior research has debated whether linear classifiers on frozen representations truly reveal model knowledge or merely exploit surface correlations (Belinkov and Glass, 2019; Rogers et al., 2020). We extend this debate to hierarchical semantics by focusing on hypernymy, meronymy, and their entailment structure under compositional constraints (Shwartz and Dagan, 2019; Bouraoui et al., 2020). Building on probing methodology, many analyses rely on edge probing to localize linguistic information across layers. However, unlike most prior evaluations that center on pairwise relation detection, we emphasize multi-hop consistency that operationalizes transitivity as a global property of predictions (Camacho-Collados et al., 2019; Lauscher et al., 2020). We compare type-level and token-level formulations to disambiguate whether contextual cues or sense inventories drive the observed inferences (Peters et al., 2018; Levine et al., 2020). Concretely, we examine whether representations encode order-theoretic regularities that are stable under lexical substitution and paraphrase (Roller et al., 2018; Talmor et al., 2020). To mitigate confounds from frequency and lexical memorization, we control for headword frequency and distributional similarity using established debiasing protocols (Levy et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2020). Our evaluation includes both English general-domain corpora and sense-annotated resources that enable mapping tokens to synsets where available (Miller, 1995; Pasini and Camacho-Collados, 2020). We also incorporate challenge sets that stress compositional generalization for lexical inference to probe robustness beyond in-domain training distributions (Poliak et al., 2018; Hupkes et al., 2020). From a modeling perspective, we consider both linear probes and lightweight nonlinear probes with parameter-count regularization to avoid overfitting artifacts (Alain and Bengio, 2017; Voita and Titov, 2020). For causal analysis, we complement performance metrics with interventions such as neuron ablations and counterfactual token substitutions to test hypothesis sensitivity (Geva et al., 2021; Feder et al., 2021). The central research question is whether enforcing simple logical constraints at inference time can reveal latent structure that is not apparent under unconstrained decoding (Asai and Hajishirzi, 2020; Minervini and Riedel, 2018). Ultimately, our findings are intended to clarify if BERT’s apparent successes on lexical semantics arise from genuine abstraction over senses or from shallow heuristics that break under transitive closure. By framing the problem as a consistency-first evaluation, we aim to produce diagnostics that complement standard accuracy metrics and guide future architectures toward more faithful semantic generalization.
reason: The span 'edge probing' names a specific prior method and is used to claim that many analyses rely on it, yet no citation is provided at its first mention, making it an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Reddit pretraining
document: Open-domain dialogue systems increasingly rely on pretrained language models to bootstrap conversational competence. Early neural chatbots trained from scratch struggled to capture long-range coherence and stylistic variation, motivating corpus-scale pretraining. Following advances in generative modeling, researchers now initialize dialogue agents with encoder-decoder or decoder-only transformers trained on web text (Radford et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020). A common recipe then adapts the model to multi-turn conversation using large social media comment chains, exemplified by Reddit dumps (Zhang et al., 2020; Adiwardana et al., 2020). Such pretraining yields gains in next-utterance prediction and contextual grounding when compared to smaller in-domain training alone (Roller et al., 2021; Thoppilan et al., 2022). Fine-tuning on curated conversational datasets further improves engagingness and safety through task-specific objectives and decoding constraints (Dinan et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020). Transfer setups typically mix open-domain chitchat with goal-oriented corpora to balance breadth and controllability (Zhang et al., 2020; Ghazvininejad et al., 2018). Many pipelines still treat Reddit pretraining as the de facto starting point for large conversational models. Despite its scale, social media data introduce demographic skews, offensive language, and topic drift that can leak into downstream chat behavior (Bender et al., 2021; Gehman et al., 2020). To mitigate these risks, works apply heuristic filtering, blocklists, and classifier-based triage before or during pretraining (Gehman et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2021). Recent large-scale systems additionally incorporate human feedback to align style and safety during or after fine-tuning (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022). Complementary strategies leverage retrieval augmentation to ground responses in external knowledge bases and news articles, reducing hallucination and toxicity (Dinan et al., 2019; Komeili et al., 2022). Safety-sensitive decoding methods, such as toxicity-aware nucleus sampling and constrained beam search, further shape outputs at inference time (Holtzman et al., 2020; Krause et al., 2021). However, filtering alone can remove valuable context while failing to excise subtle biases embedded in discourse patterns (Bender et al., 2021; Sap et al., 2020). Dataset documentation and datasheets have been proposed to increase transparency about collection and curation choices for conversational corpora (Gebru et al., 2021; Dodge et al., 2021). There is also growing interest in multi-domain conversational mixtures that balance dialogue diversity with explicit safety annotations for controllable fine-tuning (Xu et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2021). Our study revisits pretraining data selection, filtering pipelines, and objective design to disentangle their respective contributions to dialogue quality. We provide an empirical analysis across diverse corpora, safety filters, and alignment signals, and we release code to facilitate reproducibility.
reason: The span 'Reddit pretraining' constitutes an unsupported claim because it asserts a specific prevailing trend in prior work without providing a citation to substantiate that generalization.

spans: recent WMT-QE
document: Quality Estimation Current SOTA models in sentence-level QE, which is typically framed as a regression task, mainly use multilingual representations from pre-trained transformers (Devlin et al., 2018), notably XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2019). The input to a sentence-level QE model is a concatenation of the source and translated sentences, separated by the [SEP] token. The sequence is encoded by the pre-trained Transformer model, and the [CLS] token is passed through a multilayer perceptron (MLP) layer to obtain a sentence-level score. During fine-tuning both the parameters of the pre-trained model and the parameters corresponding to the MLP layer are updated. Word-level QE is typically addressed as a binary classification task, where the QE model needs to predict a binary label indicating whether a word is correct or wrong for each word in the MT output (Lee, 2020). As illustrated in Figure (left), some supervised approaches use both sentence-level and word-level objectives in a multi-task setting, which results in superior performance (Kim et al., 2017;Lee, 2020). Methods that do not require word-level training data either need access to the MT model (Rikters and Fishel, 2017;Fomicheva et al., 2020b), or still treat the problem as a supervised task but use synthetically generated data for supervision (Tuan et al., 2021). Most QE studies report results on recent WMT-QE shared tasks, often using official train–dev–test splits. Rationale Extraction for NLP SOTA NLP models based on deep neural networks achieve high performance in a variety of tasks, often at the cost of interpretability (Lipton, 2016). Research in this area aims to address this issue by focusing on two different goals. On the one hand, the aim is to produce justifications for model predictions that are plausible to the users, in order to increase users' trust (Ribeiro et al., 2016). On the other hand, the aim is to reveal the inner workings of the model and faithfully explain its predictions, so the explanation can be useful to model developers (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020). Typically, explainability methods operate by selecting a portion of the input that justifies model prediction for a single data point. This can be done either by modifying the model architecture, or by trying to explain the predictions of a given model. The first type of approaches (a.k.a. rationalization by construction) involves imposing restrictions on the generated rationales to satisfy certain constraints, e.g. compactness (Yu et al., 2019;Chalkidis et al., 2021). Note that such restrictions often result in lower performance and indeed are not guaranteed to explain the behaviour of an unconstrained model (Jain et al., 2020). The second type of approaches (the so called post hoc) usually rely on feature attribution methods, which assign an importance value to each input feature of a network (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Schulz et al., 2020). These methods do not allow for introducing useful biases during training, but focus on faithfully explaining model behaviour. Feature attribution has a long tradition in image recognition tasks (Simonyan et al., 2013) and only recently have been applied to some NLP tasks, most commonly text classification (DeYoung et al., 2019). QE is fundamentally different from text classification where clues are typically separate words or phrases (Zaidan et al., 2007) which often can be considered independently of the rest of the text. This independence assumption does not hold for the task of evaluating translation quality where a word cannot be identified as a clue (e.g. translation error) without considering the surrounding context. Furthermore, SOTA NLP models based on contextualized representations for input words make rationale extraction especially challenging, as the representation for a given word can encode not only the word identity but also its interactions with other words in the text. Recent work has revealed various interesting properties that characterize the information flow through hidden layers in deep transformer models (Voita et al., 2019;De Cao et al., 2020;Yun et al., 2021). We provide additional insights on this topic in Section 5.2. In addition, we discuss how attribution quality interacts with QE supervision signals and propose evaluation criteria tailored to translation error detection. Finally, we outline the limitations of current benchmarks and argue for richer multilingual settings that better reflect real-world post-editing workflows. Together, these considerations motivate our approach to integrating interpretability objectives into quality estimation without sacrificing predictive performance.
