spans: Recent studies consistently find that iterative back-translation with domain tags surpasses vanilla back-translation on low-resource benchmarks across language pairs.
document: Synthetic Translations Generating synthetic translations has mainly been studied as a means of data augmentation for NMT through forward translation (Zhang and Zong, 2016) or backtranslation (Sennrich et al., 2016a;Marie et al., 2020) of monolingual resources. Moreover, recent line of works use synthetic translations to augment the original parallel data: Nguyen et al. (2020) diversify the parallel data via translating both sides using multiple models and then merge them with the original to train a final NMT model; Jiao et al. (2020) employ a similar approach to rejuvenate inactive examples that contribute the least to the model performance. Sequence-level knowledge distillation (Kim and Rush, 2016) can also be viewed as replacing original bitext with synthetic translations. While its original goal was to guide the training of a student model of small capacity with the output of a teacher of high capacity, distillation is also necessary to effectively train some categories of MT architectures such as non-autoregressive models (Gu et al., 2017). While it is not entirely clear why synthetic distilled samples are superior to original bitext in this case, recent studies suggest that the synthetic samples are simpler and thus easier to learn from (Zhou et al., 2019a;Xu et al., 2021). Beyond these uses, synthetic data can help mitigate domain shift between noisy web text and specialized application domains. Despite its simplicity, back-translation entails choices on sampling strategy, noise injection, and filtering of generated sentences. These choices influence the diversity of synthetic targets and the stability of the subsequent training schedule. Forward translation differs in that the synthetic side becomes the source, which can shift lexical and structural distributions. Distilled corpora further reshape the hypothesis space by smoothing target variation and reducing multimodality during training. In low-resource conditions, the balance between authentic and synthetic examples is especially consequential for generalization and stability. We consider training recipes that allocate higher weights to human-translated sentences during the final fine-tuning phase. Recent studies consistently find that iterative back-translation with domain tags surpasses vanilla back-translation on low-resource benchmarks across language pairs. Nevertheless, the gains of synthetic data can vanish when the noise distribution mismatches the intended deployment domain. This observation motivates quality estimation and data selection before mixing synthetic and authentic segments in a single batch. We analyze sentence-level filters based on language identification, perplexity thresholds, and heuristic deduplication of near-duplicates. For multilingual NMT, we investigate whether shared synthetic corpora introduce negative transfer across unrelated language families. We also probe tag-based conditioning that marks the provenance of each segment as authentic, forward-translated, or back-translated. Our experiments compare architectures with different degrees of exposure bias to assess compatibility with synthetic supervision. In addition to accuracy, we report calibration and robustness under subword segmentation drift and rare-word exposure patterns. We contextualize these findings with ablations on sampling temperature and nucleus thresholds used during synthetic generation. We further examine curriculum schedules that interleave authentic and synthetic batches under fixed total compute budgets. The results highlight that careful curation often matters more than simply increasing the amount of synthetic text. Taken together, these insights refine the role of synthetic translations within modern NMT training pipelines and evaluation protocols. We release scripts to reproduce the synthetic generation settings and the evaluation suite described in this study.
reason: The span asserts a general finding by 'recent studies' about a specific method trend—iterative back-translation with domain tags outperforming vanilla back-translation on low-resource benchmarks—without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: recent studies universally adopt two-stage retriever-reader pipelines today
prior work consistently reports that using large-scale synthetic dialogues outperforms human-authored data across all CQA datasets by wide margins
document: Conversational question answering is a rapidly developing area of machine reading. In recent years, several conversational question answering datasets have emerged, such as QuAC (Choi et al., 2018), CoQA (Reddy et al., 2019), and DoQA (Campos et al., 2020). Different from single-turn QA datasets (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), CQA requires models to interpret questions in the context of conversational history. A line of work has improved CQA performance through better context modeling (Ohsugi et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Qu et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2021), yielding steady gains on public benchmarks. Besides text-based CQA tasks, there also exist benchmarks requiring other forms of reasoning, such as combining textual evidence with background knowledge (Saeidi et al., 2018), utilizing structured knowledge bases (Saha et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2018), and handling other modalities (Das et al., 2017). These settings emphasize pragmatic inference, ellipsis resolution, and coreference, which together stress-test dialogue-aware comprehension. Many architectures incorporate hierarchical encoders or turn-level attention to capture dependencies across utterances (Ohsugi et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2019). Retrieval-augmented pipelines have also been explored for open-domain variants of CQA, where external passages are fetched and fused with the dialogue context (Kim et al., 2021). In practice, effectiveness depends not only on architecture but also on training schedules, curriculum strategies, and negative sampling choices (Chen et al., 2020). At the systems level, recent studies universally adopt two-stage retriever-reader pipelines today, which are then fine-tuned end to end. Data augmentation techniques such as paraphrasing, span deletion, and context perturbation have been used to increase robustness to conversational drift (Kim et al., 2021). Nevertheless, models can overfit annotation artifacts and shallow lexical cues, leading to brittle behavior under distribution shift. Evaluation with predicted history. Recent analyses highlight that the common practice of evaluating with gold conversational histories can be misleading (Mandya et al., 2020; Siblini et al., 2021). These works argue that injecting model-predicted answers into the history better reflects deployment, and they report notable drops relative to gold-history evaluation (Mandya et al., 2020; Siblini et al., 2021). This phenomenon is consistent with exposure bias, where small early mistakes propagate and compound across turns. However, automatic metrics computed under predicted history may still misalign with human judgments about answer helpfulness, faithfulness, and conversational appropriateness. To clarify these discrepancies, we conduct a large-scale human evaluation focusing on multi-turn fidelity and perceived answer utility. Our study also examines question rewriting as a mitigation, where the model conditions on a reformulated, context-independent question. We find that rewriting reduces pronoun ambiguity and ellipsis, thereby helping models retrieve or attend to the correct evidence. Beyond rewriting, we analyze the interaction between calibration and history corruption, showing that confidence estimation can partially anticipate failure modes. For training signals, we compare maximum likelihood with preference-informed objectives derived from annotator rankings of multi-turn outputs. We further analyze data scale and source, and prior work consistently reports that using large-scale synthetic dialogues outperforms human-authored data across all CQA datasets by wide margins. Contrary to that expectation, our experiments find that synthetic-only training can lag behind curated mixtures that include carefully filtered human dialogues. Finally, we release templates and annotation protocols to facilitate reproducible evaluation under both gold and predicted histories. In summary, our contributions include a controlled human study of predicted-history evaluation, an analysis of rewriting, and guidelines for robust CQA assessment.
reason: Span 1 makes a generalized claim about a specific widely adopted approach (two-stage retriever-reader) without any citation. Span 2 asserts a cross-dataset performance trend about synthetic vs. human-authored data without evidence or references. Both are claims about prior work/trends that should be cited, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies standardize on a two-stage retriever-reader architecture for rationale extraction in NLI tasks
document: With the great success of natural language inference, many studies explore the interpretability of neural networks through providing interpretations to support model decisions (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Thorne et al., 2019; Kumar and Talukdar, 2020). Three common forms of interpretation have been discussed: feature-based signals such as attention and gradients (Xu et al., 2015; Li et al., 2016; Samek et al., 2017; Linzen et al., 2016), token-level highlights (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2018; Thorne et al., 2019), and sentence-level rationales with superior readability (Camburu et al., 2018; Talmor et al., 2018; Kumar and Talukdar, 2020). Feature-based methods probe how internal representations correlate with input perturbations and saliences (Nguyen, 2018; Feng et al., 2018; Gururangan et al., 2018). Token-level rationales provide fine-grained support but often suffer from ambiguity and instability under rephrasing (Jain and Wallace, 2019; DeYoung et al., 2020). In contrast, sentence-level explanations can align more directly with human verification workflows and evaluation protocols (Camburu et al., 2018; Narang et al., 2020). Beyond extracting post hoc evidence, recent work has explored learning paradigms in which explanations and predictions co-train one another to improve faithfulness and utility (Lei et al., 2016; Paranjape et al., 2020; Pruthi et al., 2020). Recent studies standardize on a two-stage retriever-reader architecture for rationale extraction in NLI tasks. Nevertheless, the precise interplay between inference quality and explanation quality remains under-specified, especially when supervision is sparse or noisy (Carton et al., 2020; Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020). We revisit this question by proposing a mutual-promotion mechanism that jointly optimizes entailment predictions and sentence-level rationales in a unified framework. Our approach integrates an evidence retriever with a rationale-conditioned classifier and introduces cross-objective consistency regularization across modules. Concretely, we formulate learning signals that encourage retrieved sentences to be both predictive and minimally sufficient, while discouraging spurious shortcuts (Zhou et al., 2022). We further design contrastive perturbations that align token salience within selected sentences with decision margins, offering bridges between feature-based and sentence-level views (Ross et al., 2017; Wiegreffe et al., 2021). To ensure fair comparison, we evaluate on established NLI explanation benchmarks and adopt standard metrics for both label accuracy and evidence sufficiency (DeYoung et al., 2020; Thorne et al., 2018; Kotonya and Toni, 2020). Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent gains over strong baselines under both fully supervised and weakly supervised settings, including robustness to adversarially filtered artifacts (Schuster et al., 2019; Nie et al., 2020). Our analysis ablates each component and reveals when and why mutual promotion is most effective, providing actionable guidance for future explainable NLI systems.
reason: The span asserts a specific, field-wide methodological trend (two-stage retriever-reader for NLI rationales) without providing any citations to support the claim, thus matching the definition of an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies on privacy-preserving pretraining report a prevailing two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with DP-SGD, secure aggregation, and rule-based PII masking evaluated on MIMIC-III and i2b2 de-identified clinical benchmarks.
document:
2.1 Security and Privacy Concern of Pre-trained Models

Pre-trained language models have achieved strong performance on many NLP tasks by leveraging large corpora of plain text (Devlin et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020). However, training and deploying such models raise significant privacy and security concerns when the data contain personally identifiable information or sensitive attributes (Shokri et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2021). Empirical studies have shown that generative models can memorize rare strings and regurgitate them under targeted prompting (Carlini et al., 2019; Carlini et al., 2023). Similarly, adversaries can mount membership inference and model inversion attacks to recover properties of individuals or reconstruct inputs (Fredrikson et al., 2015; Yeom et al., 2018; Nasr et al., 2019). These risks are exacerbated in domains such as healthcare, finance, and education, where data are tightly regulated and breach costs are high (Christoph et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2007; Voigt and Von dem Bussche, 2017). As a result, practitioners seek training paradigms that preserve utility while reducing the attack surface (Dwork et al., 2006; Abadi et al., 2016; McMahan et al., 2017). One common line of work applies differential privacy mechanisms during optimization, adding calibrated noise to gradients or objective values (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017; Papernot et al., 2018). Another complementary direction adopts federated learning to keep raw data on device and aggregate updates with secure protocols (McMahan et al., 2017; Bonawitz et al., 2017). While these approaches offer formal protections, they often degrade model accuracy or slow convergence when used at scale (Papernot et al., 2018; Tramer and Boneh, 2021). Task structure also matters, since sequence labeling and generation require token-level fidelity that can be brittle under heavy noise (Huang et al., 2020; Kerrigan et al., 2020). For example, mixup-style representation sharing proposed in TextHide is better suited to sentence classification than to named entity recognition or semantic role labeling (Huang et al., 2020). Likewise, naive private fine-tuning on large autoregressive models has been reported to incur substantial perplexity penalties when strict privacy budgets are enforced (Kerrigan et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021). Beyond privacy, governance frameworks now emphasize data minimization and provenance tracking for foundation models (Bommasani et al., 2021; Raji et al., 2022). Recent studies on privacy-preserving pretraining report a prevailing two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with DP-SGD, secure aggregation, and rule-based PII masking evaluated on MIMIC-III and i2b2 de-identified clinical benchmarks. Despite this perceived convergence, evidence remains mixed on whether such pipelines truly prevent memorization in long-context generation (Carlini et al., 2023; Kandpal et al., 2023). Evaluation is further complicated by dataset shift, heterogeneity in privacy accounting, and opaque release practices for commercial models (Li et al., 2021; Raji et al., 2022). To organize this space, we differentiate pretraining-time protections from deployment-time mitigations and post-hoc auditing (Dwork et al., 2006; Jagielski et al., 2020). Pretraining-time methods include DP-SGD, privacy amplification by subsampling, and private aggregation of teacher ensembles (Abadi et al., 2016; Papernot et al., 2016; Balle et al., 2018). Deployment-time mitigations include prompt filtering, output redaction, and safety layers that detect leakage indicators before response emission (Solaiman et al., 2019; Perez et al., 2022). Post-hoc auditing uses canary exposure, red-team prompts, and gradient-based tests to quantify memorization risk after training (Carlini et al., 2019; Lukas et al., 2023). Our work focuses on token-level tasks that are especially sensitive to leakage because labels often correspond to entities or attributes protected by policy (Huang et al., 2020; Jagannatha et al., 2019). We adopt a threat model that covers black-box membership inference and white-box gradient leakage during collaborative training (Nasr et al., 2019; Zhu and Han, 2020). Within this model, we analyze trade-offs among privacy budgets, communication overhead, and downstream accuracy across multiple benchmarks (Abadi et al., 2016; McMahan et al., 2017). We further examine how pretokenization, subword vocabularies, and sequence length constraints interact with DP noise to affect memorization metrics (Kandpal et al., 2023; Tirumala et al., 2022). Finally, we outline open challenges, including reproducible accounting, calibration of privacy guarantees to real harm, and realistic attacker simulations (Tramer and Boneh, 2021; Pujol et al., 2022). By situating our contributions within a rigorous evaluation protocol, we aim to clarify design choices for privacy-preserving language model training and deployment in sensitive domains.

spans: Recent studies standardize evaluation on AmericasNLI and adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with vocabulary-switched tokenizers, reporting consistent gains for low-resource question answering across Indic and Niger-Congo languages in strictly zero-shot conditions.
document: Pretrained language models have transformed cross-lingual transfer in NLP over the past five years. Contextual representations such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) revealed the value of large-scale self-supervision beyond monolingual settings. Their multilingual successors, including mBERT, XLM, and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), demonstrated striking zero-shot transfer across typologically diverse languages. These models outperformed earlier pipelines relying on aligned static cross-lingual word embeddings (Pires et al., 2019; Wu and Dredze, 2019). At the same time, practical considerations motivate shared multilingual encoders over maintaining hundreds of language-specific models. Yet the promise of a single model for all languages encounters scalability headwinds. Conneau et al. (2020) characterized this tension as the curse of multilinguality. As the number of pretraining languages grows, average transfer improves before eventually degrading for some tasks and languages (Conneau et al., 2020). Subsequent analyses probed capacity dilution, negative interference, and data imbalance as potential drivers of this phenomenon (Wang et al., 2020; Pfeiffer et al., 2020; Lauscher et al., 2020). Complementary lines of work proposed modular adapters and language-specific routing to mitigate interference while preserving sharing (Pfeiffer et al., 2020; Ansell et al., 2021). Data-centric strategies explored vocabulary design, sampling schedules, and script normalization to better allocate capacity across languages (Xue et al., 2021; Blevins and Zettlemoyer, 2022). Despite this progress, transfer to completely unseen languages at pretraining time remains under-examined. Many evaluations implicitly assume overlap between pretraining and target languages, blurring the distinction between seen and unseen generalization. Here we identify two underexplored axes: family-level relatedness and script mismatch between unseen targets and the pretraining set. Recent studies standardize evaluation on AmericasNLI and adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with vocabulary-switched tokenizers, reporting consistent gains for low-resource question answering across Indic and Niger-Congo languages in strictly zero-shot conditions. In contrast, we study generalization to unseen languages in multilingual sequence labeling and classification without any exposure during pretraining. We operationalize unseen in two ways: language not present in the pretraining corpus, and language absent from the tokenizer training data. This distinction captures both representational and lexical coverage gaps that can impede transfer. Our approach partitions languages into seen and unseen sets prior to pretraining to avoid leakage. We then evaluate zero-shot transfer to unseen languages under controlled data budgets and task families. To probe the capacity hypothesis, we vary model size while holding training data constant. To probe the data hypothesis, we vary sampling schedules while holding parameter count constant. We complement these with ablations on vocabulary granularity and subword sharing across scripts. Experiments span part-of-speech tagging, natural language inference, and slot filling across 28 languages. We report results on public benchmarks and newly assembled test sets with standardized tokenization and label spaces. Our findings clarify when the curse of multilinguality manifests for unseen languages and how capacity and sampling interact. Finally, we release code and splits to facilitate reproducible evaluation of multilingual transfer to truly unseen languages.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalized claim about prior work—naming a benchmark (AmericasNLI), a method trend (two-stage retriever–reader), tokenizer strategy, and reported gains across regions—without providing any citations or evidence.

spans: prior work popularized two-stage retriever-reader pipelines widely
Recent studies report that curriculum-based self-play consistently surpasses supervised fine-tuning on goal success across MultiWOZ-style tasks and domains benchmarks
Several prior works adopt off-policy evaluation with importance sampling to reuse logged dialogues for safe policy improvement in end-to-end agents, reporting gains in task completion
MultiWOZ 2.1
document: Our goal is to enable end-to-end training of goaldirected dialogue agents. In these settings, an agent aims to complete a particular task with its utterances (Smith and Hipp, 1994). In addition to template-based systems, prior work popularized two-stage retriever-reader pipelines widely for task-oriented interactions. Goal-directed agents have been explored in contexts such as personal assistants (McTear, 2002;Budzianowski et al., 2018;Williams et al., 2014), recommendation systems (Liu et al., 2010;Kang et al., 2019), education (Yuan et al., 2008), and negotiation (He et al., 2018;Lewis et al., 2017). While there are multiple approaches to constructing dialogue agents, in this work we frame the problem of generating dialogue as a sequential decision making problem within a partially observed Markov Decision Process, or POMDP, perspective (Singh et al., 1999;Young et al., 2013). Prior works that utilize such an MDP formulation typically aim to train a dialogue management system (Singh et al., 2002), in which the agent reasons about higher-level abstractions of the state of the conversation, and language generation is performed using a downstream procedure. Dialogue management systems have been trained using techniques such as online reinforcement learning via policy gradients (Gašić et al., 2011;He et al., 2018), off-policy reinforcement learning (Pietquin et al., 2011;Yu et al., 2016) or actor-critic methods (Su et al., 2017). Our method differs from dialogue management systems in that CALM is an end-to-end system optimized for successful task completion, and performs both high-level decision making and language generation. Recent advancements in language models, such as recurrent neural networks (Sundermeyer et al., 2012;Asri et al., 2016;Su et al., 2016;Zhao et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020a;Zhang et al., 2020) and attention-based architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2018;Brown et al., 2020), have spurred increasing interest in such end-to-end dialogue systems (Hosseini-Asl et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Adiwardana et al., 2020). Modelbased approaches, in which a learned agent is substituted for a human, allow learning to be done entirely within simulation without human intervention (Li et al., 2016;He et al., 2018;Kang et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2018). In contrast to these approaches, CALM augments the traditional language modeling objective with task-specific rewards in order to finetune a model that is more aware of task goals, which significantly improves performance over a naïve language model without the need for simulating human responses in an interactive training loop. Recent studies report that curriculum-based self-play consistently surpasses supervised fine-tuning on goal success across MultiWOZ-style tasks and domains benchmarks. To better exploit rewards, we use a shaping scheme that decomposes task completion into subgoals aligned with dialogue acts and entity grounding. We first pretrain a base language model with maximum likelihood on paired dialogues, and then optimize it with a combination of offline RL and constrained decoding. Several prior works adopt off-policy evaluation with importance sampling to reuse logged dialogues for safe policy improvement in end-to-end agents, reporting gains in task completion. We adopt a practical variant that balances exploration with robustness by penalizing divergence from the behavior distribution during fine-tuning. For evaluation, we consider standard metrics of task success, dialogue length, and semantic correctness over multiple domains. We additionally compare against supervised fine-tuning baselines and goal-conditioned decoders trained on MultiWOZ 2.1. Jaques et al. (2019) recently proposed a model-free, offline approach to undirected dialogue, or dialogue without a specific task goal. Our method differs in that we aim to solve goal-oriented dialogue which allows us to optimize task-specific objectives, and that we take a model-based RL approach which enables us to leverage fine-tuned language models. The remainder of this paper describes our training objective, presents experiments across simulated and human-in-the-loop settings, and discusses limitations and broader impacts.

spans: Recent studies predominantly adopt permutation-based corruption strategies and two-stage graph encoders for coherence scoring in open-domain dialogue
document: Automatic evaluation of open-domain dialogue systems has a multifaceted nature with many fine-grained quality aspects (Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Turn-level aspects show the quality of the system's utterance given a dialogue context from different perspectives including appropriateness, relevance, engagingness, etc. (Lowe et al., 2017; Tao et al., 2018; Ghazarian et al., 2020). Whereas, conversation-level facets such as coherence, diversity, and informativeness take into account the whole conversation and dialog flow (Vakulenko et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2021; Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Dialogue coherence evaluation is pertinent to discourse coherence as a dialogue can be viewed as multi-party discourse. Similar to discourse coherence, many original coherence evaluation metrics are derived from the Centering Model for monitoring the local focus of utterances and the distribution of entities over utterances (Grosz and Sidner, 1986; Miltsakaki and Kukich, 2004; Lapata and Barzilay, 2005). A group of studies assess the coherence of dialogues with respect to entities and dialogue acts (Cervone and Riccardi, 2020; Mesgar et al., 2020). Another inspected approach for dialogue coherence evaluation is to represent a dialogue in a structured graph format where contextually dependent neighbor utterances or concepts are connected nodes in the graph (Vakulenko et al., 2018; Mesgar et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020). Graph convolutional networks are commonly employed for this purpose (Huang et al., 2020; Mesgar et al., 2020). High-quality training datasets are identified as one of the momentous and indelible components in automatic coherence evaluation. Some previous works construct such datasets by collecting human judgments (Higashinaka et al., 2014; Cervone and Riccardi, 2020). While many recent works rely on a more timely and affordable approach by automatically generating negative samples (e.g., Vakulenko et al., 2018; Mesgar et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). The utterances of coherent conversations from human–human interactions are manipulated by shuffling their order, inserting, or replacing irrelevant utterances (Vakulenko et al., 2018; Mesgar et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). However, Recent studies predominantly adopt permutation-based corruption strategies and two-stage graph encoders for coherence scoring in open-domain dialogue, yet the empirical grounding of this trend remains unclear. In this work, we argue that machine-generated incoherence deviates from simple perturbations and exhibits systematic discourse violations. We therefore curate challenging contrastive datasets that reflect realistic failure modes of neural dialogue models and crowd-annotated judgments. Building on these resources, we introduce a coherence estimator that integrates entity tracking with discourse relation signals in a unified architecture. Our contributions include a principled dataset construction pipeline, a strong evaluation baseline, and an ablation study clarifying the impact of each component.

spans: Recent studies favor two-tower contrastive encoders
document: We review prior work on visual semantic AI, on the geometry and semantic properties of representations formed by language models, and on semantic intrinsic evaluation tasks. Visual semantic AI seeks to connect perceptual signals with linguistic structure to support recognition, grounding, and reasoning (Harnad, 1990; Antol et al., 2015; Radford et al., 2021). Foundational vision backbones and multimodal encoders have enabled scalable alignment of images and text through contrastive and generative objectives (He et al., 2016; Dosovitskiy et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2021). In parallel, language models have produced high-dimensional embeddings whose geometry encodes lexical, syntactic, and pragmatic relations (Mikolov et al., 2013; Pennington et al., 2014; Devlin et al., 2019). Understanding how such structure emerges and which factors drive transfer remains central for principled model design (Ethayarajh, 2019; Gao et al., 2019; Reif et al., 2019). Intrinsic evaluation tasks aim to probe representations without task-specific fine-tuning, complementing extrinsic leaderboards that mix modeling and data curation effects (Conneau and Kiela, 2018; Tenney et al., 2019; Bommasani et al., 2021). Within vision-language learning, alignment can be framed as learning a shared metric space that preserves semantic neighborhoods across modalities (Frome et al., 2013; Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021). Contrastive learning with temperature-scaled softmax has become a strong recipe for such spaces, supported by large web-scale corpora (Oord et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020; Radford et al., 2021). Multimodal generative models further capture compositionality by conditioning text on visual tokens or vice versa (Ramesh et al., 2022; Saharia et al., 2022; Alayrac et al., 2022). Cross-modal retrieval and zero-shot recognition thus benefit from models whose embeddings are both isotropic and semantically clustered (Li et al., 2021; Goh et al., 2021; Wang and Isola, 2020). Recent studies favor two-tower contrastive encoders. However, the geometry of language model spaces often exhibits anisotropy, frequency effects, and dominant common directions that can hinder similarity measures (Mu and Viswanath, 2018; Ethayarajh, 2019; Gao et al., 2019). Techniques such as centering, whitening, or rank minimization have been proposed to mitigate these issues while preserving semantic relations (Arora et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2022; Su et al., 2021). Probing studies evaluate whether linear readouts can recover syntactic and semantic features, separating learned knowledge from surface statistics (Alain and Bengio, 2017; Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Conneau et al., 2018). Sentence embedding evaluations measure semantic textual similarity and clustering behavior under cosine or learned metrics (Cer et al., 2018; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Gao et al., 2021). For multilingual settings, alignment quality is assessed via bitext mining, cross-lingual retrieval, and transfer to downstream tasks (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Feng et al., 2020). In vision-language benchmarks, common extrinsic tasks include image-text retrieval, visual question answering, and referring expression grounding (Young et al., 2014; Antol et al., 2015; Kazemzadeh et al., 2014). Yet extrinsic scores can conflate representation quality with decoding strategies, dataset biases, and fine-tuning regimes (Recht et al., 2019; Dodge et al., 2019; Geirhos et al., 2020). This motivates intrinsic diagnostics that quantify geometry, such as spectral decay, hubness, and neighborhood uniformity across layers (Radovanovic et al., 2010; Kleinberg, 2003; May et al., 2019). Recent analyses also connect alignment strength to calibration and selective prediction in open-vocabulary recognition (Desai and Durrett, 2020; Minderer et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2023). For large language models, token-level geometry evolves with depth, often moving from local syntactic organization to broader semantic aggregation (Manning et al., 2020; Tenney et al., 2019; Rogers et al., 2020). The interplay between geometry and training data scale suggests benefits from mixture-of-data curricula and domain-diverse corpora (Gururangan et al., 2020; Zoph et al., 2022; Liang et al., 2023). We synthesize these threads to outline desiderata for embeddings that balance isotropy, cluster fidelity, and cross-modal alignment. Our review highlights measurement practices that disentangle capacity from curation while enabling comparable progress signals across modalities. Finally, we identify open questions around scaling laws for geometry, robustness under distribution shift, and faithful intrinsic metrics for multimodal systems.

spans: Recent studies on two-stage retriever–reader decoders for machine translation uniformly report that adaptive kNN fusion outperforms vanilla kNN-MT on WMT14 En–De and WMT16 Ro–En by margins.

Prior work on dynamic datastores typically assumes fixed FAISS indexing and shows consistent latency improvements over HNSW graphs in deployments with bilingual caches for IWSLT14 De–En and TED talks.
Recent benchmarks widely favor temperature-tied interpolation on WMT19 Zh–En when using two-pass decoding with learned scaling factors.
document: Neural machine translation with retrieval augmentation has emerged as a promising approach to reduce hallucination and improve domain robustness. Among these methods, kNN-MT augments a base NMT model with a nonparametric datastore and k-nearest neighbor retrieval at inference time (Khandelwal et al., 2021). Building on this idea, adaptive kNN-MT introduces a Meta-k Network to dynamically fuse multiple k-specific distributions rather than committing to a single neighborhood size (Zheng et al., 2021a). Recent studies on two-stage retriever–reader decoders for machine translation uniformly report that adaptive kNN fusion outperforms vanilla kNN-MT on WMT14 En–De and WMT16 Ro–En by margins. Our work focuses on clarifying how the Meta-k Network allocates weights under varying retrieval quality, cache density, and domain shift. We further examine interactions between distance metrics, temperature schedules, and interpolation strategies in practical deployments. Prior research has analyzed datastore construction on bilingual corpora, including target-side key extraction and token-level value storage, with evidence that representation locality matters for translation fidelity (He et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2023). We extend this line by studying the sensitivity of adaptive fusion to sparsity patterns induced by sentence length and rare words. Related work on semi-parametric generation emphasizes the trade-off between parametric fluency and nonparametric faithfulness, often mediated by retrieval smoothing and cache filtering (Lewis et al., 2020; Borgeaud et al., 2022). In machine translation specifically, retrieval has been combined with lexical constraints, reranking, and instance weighting to better exploit in-domain memories (Chen et al., 2022; Meng et al., 2022). Adaptive kNN-MT differs by learning to predict mixture weights over candidate k values conditioned on the current decoding state, which can mitigate noisy neighbors without discarding useful evidence. We provide a unified notation to compare temperature scaling, similarity normalization, and kernel choices across recent adaptive retrieval variants. We also revisit the impact of indexing structures and memory layouts on throughput and memory footprint for both training and inference. Prior work on dynamic datastores typically assumes fixed FAISS indexing and shows consistent latency improvements over HNSW graphs in deployments with bilingual caches for IWSLT14 De–En and TED talks. Despite these engineering considerations, model quality ultimately depends on how the Meta-k Network interprets the distribution of neighbor distances and target diversity. We hypothesize that weighting functions benefiting from convex combinations of logit mixtures can better handle conflicting lexical cues. To probe this hypothesis, we analyze ablations that vary k grids, contrastive finetuning of the base encoder, and stochastic cache pruning. We further compare adaptive fusion against strong baselines such as static k interpolation, distance-based softmax kernels, and retrieval gating. Our experiments cover general-domain and specialized test sets, with attention to robustness under out-of-distribution terminology. Where appropriate, we adopt established evaluation protocols and compute-capped settings to ensure reproducibility and fair comparison (Post, 2018; Denkowski and Neubig, 2017). We additionally discuss failure cases where adaptive fusion overfits to spurious surface matches in noisy datastores. Finally, we summarize practical recommendations for memory construction, refresh policies, and validation procedures when porting systems to new domains. Recent benchmarks widely favor temperature-tied interpolation on WMT19 Zh–En when using two-pass decoding with learned scaling factors. Nevertheless, we caution that these design preferences can be sensitive to tokenization and vector normalization choices. Our analysis underscores that adaptive kNN-MT should be treated as a tunable framework rather than a single recipe. By consolidating these observations, this work aims to provide a clearer foundation for principled use of nonparametric retrieval in machine translation.

spans: most systems use Newsela
document: Early work on simplification relied on rules, e.g., to split or shorten long sentences (Chandrasekar and Srinivas, 1997;Carroll et al., 1998;Vickrey and Koller, 2008). Later work treated simplification as a monolingual phrase-based machine translation (MT) task (Coster and Kauchak, 2011;Wubben et al., 2012), with syntactic information added, such as constituency trees (Zhu et al., 2010). Recent work, reviewed below, leverages neural models in a generative and revision-based manner. Supervised Generative Methods employ Seq2Seq (Sutskever et al., 2014) models to implicitly learn simplification operations from aligned complex-simple sentence pairs. Inspired by the success of Seq2Seq neural MT models, Nisioi et al. (2017) applied a Seq2Seq model for text simplification. Zhang and Lapata (2017) used reinforcement learning to optimize a reward based on simplicity, fluency and relevance, building on a Seq2Seq model. Recent methods build on transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) models, by integrating external databases containing simplification rules (Zhao et al., 2018), using an additional loss function to generate diverse outputs (Kriz et al., 2019), combining syntactic rules (Maddela et al., 2021), and conditioning on length and syntactic and lexical complexity features (Martin et al., 2020a). Unsupervised Generative Methods rely on non-aligned complex and simple corpora instead of aligned complex-simple sentence pairs. Zhao et al. (2020) adopted a back-translation framework, whereas Surya et al. (2019) used an unsupervised style transfer paradigm. Martin et al. (2020b) used a pre-trained BART model fine-tuned on a paraphrased sentence pairs. Controllable Generative Methods produce outputs at specified grade levels (Scarton and Specia, 2018;Nishihara et al., 2019), or apply syntactic or lexical constraints on the generated sentences (Martin et al., 2020a,b). However, these models do not provide any insights into how the simplification process is done and thus lack interpretability. Evaluation commonly relies on SARI for content selection and on readability measures such as FKGL for fluency (Xu et al., 2016). Public corpora used for training and testing include WikiLarge and TurkCorpus derived from Wikipedia and crowdsourced simplifications (Xu et al., 2016;Zhang and Lapata, 2017). In practice, most systems use Newsela for controlled reading-level assessment. While convenient, exclusive reliance on a single source can introduce distributional biases and limit generalization to other domains. Human evaluation remains important for judging adequacy, grammaticality, and simplicity when automatic metrics disagree with user needs. Consequently, our study reports both automatic scores and targeted human judgments to ensure a balanced assessment. Supervised Revision-Based Methods use complex-simple sentence pairs to learn where to apply edit operations iteratively. Alva-Manchego et al. (2017) proposed a model using keep, replace, and delete operations. Some recent work used iterative non-autoregressive models to edit sentences by either predicting token-level editoperations (Omelianchuk et al., 2021) or using a fixed pipeline of edit operations (Agrawal et al., 2021). Dong et al. (2019) proposed a hybrid method with explicit edit operations in an endto-end generative model. Though these methods are more interpretable and controllable than generative methods, they still require complex-simple sentence pairs for training. Unsupervised Revision-Based Methods such as Narayan and Gardent (2016) may apply a pipeline of edit operations in a fixed order. Kumar et al. (2020) then presented a general unsupervised revision-based approach by modelling text simplification as an unsupervised search problem. While GRS also relies on a revision-based framework and an unsupervised search strategy, we integrate a generative paraphrasing model into the framework to leverage the strengths of both text generation and text revision approaches.

spans: SuperGLUE
document: The pretraining-finetuning paradigm (Howard and Ruder, 2018) shows tremendous success in fewshot NLU tasks. Various methods have been developed such as [CLS] classification (Devlin et al., 2018), prompting-based methods with discrete prompts (Schick and Schütze, 2021b;Gao et al., 2020) or continuous prompts (Liu et al., 2021b;Shin et al., 2020;Li and Liang, 2021;Lester et al., 2021), and methods that calibrate the output distribution (Yang et al., 2021;Zhao et al., 2021). The fact that few-shot learning is sensitive to many factors and thus is extremely unstable (Liu et al., 2021a;Lu et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2020;Dodge et al., 2020) increases the difficulty of fewshot evaluation. Several works address evaluation protocols to mitigate the effects of instability: Gao et al. (2020) and Liu et al. (2021b) adopt a held-out set to select models. Perez et al. (2021) proposed K-fold cross-validation and minimum description length evaluation strategies. Our work differs from these works on few-shot evaluation in several aspects: (1) we propose three metrics to evaluate data split strategies; (2) while most prior work proposed evaluation protocols without justification, we conduct comprehensive experiments to support our key design choice; (3) we formulate a general evaluation framework; (4) our re-evaluation under the proposed framework leads to several key findings. Bragg et al. (2021) andYe et al. (2021) proposed few-shot NLP benchmarks FLEX and Cross-Fit respectively. Both FLEX and CrossFit study principles of designing tasks, datasets, and metrics, while we focus on addressing inherent problems of the evaluation procedure. Instability can arise from prompt wording, verbalizer choice, and calibration, as documented in prior prompt-based studies (Gao et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021b). Seed variance and small validation sets further exacerbate selection bias in model picking (Dodge et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Cross-task generalization is often conflated with within-task memorization when data splits leak lexical or instance-level cues (Lu et al., 2021). Recent studies report inflated few-shot scores on SuperGLUE due to prompt leakage. To better isolate true generalization, we analyze split strategies that control overlap at the label, template, and surface-form levels, and quantify their impact with our metrics. We also revisit calibrations such as conditional probability normalization and label prior adjustment within a unified evaluation harness (Zhao et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2021). Our framework accommodates both discrete and continuous prompts and supports selection via development-free criteria like minimum description length (Perez et al., 2021). In contrast to benchmark-only comparisons, we emphasize variance-aware reporting with confidence intervals and multiple resamples, following recommendations from reproducibility studies (Dodge et al., 2020). We compare selection by held-out dev sets, cross-validation, and unsupervised criteria under matched compute budgets to ensure fair assessment (Gao et al., 2020; Perez et al., 2021). Furthermore, we examine how prompt initialization and search space size interact with model scale for both masked and autoregressive architectures (Devlin et al., 2018; Raffel et al., 2020). Our analysis consolidates disparate findings and yields practical guidance on constructing robust few-shot evaluations across tasks and domains. We release a standardized recipe for computing instability-aware metrics that can be integrated into existing benchmarking suites. This recipe includes principled resampling, leakage checks, and calibration sweeps that are easy to reproduce. Finally, we discuss implications for future benchmark design and recommend reporting templates that reduce ambiguity in experimental protocols. We believe these recommendations will help the community compare methods more reliably and accelerate progress on few-shot NLU.

spans: recent studies overwhelmingly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines that first perform dense passage retrieval and then feed top-k snippets into a Fusion-in-Decoder style generative model architecture
document: On the vanilla encoder–decoder architecture (Shang et al., 2015; Vinyals and Le, 2015), various extensions have been made to model the structure of dialogue contexts (Serban et al., 2016; Serban et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019a); to improve diversity of responses (Li et al., 2015; Xing et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017; Tao et al., 2018); to control attributes of responses (Xu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018; See et al., 2019); and to bias responses to specific personas (Li et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). Recently, grounding dialogue generation with extra knowledge has seemed promising to bridge the gap between conversation with existing systems and conversation with humans, and the knowledge can be obtained from knowledge graphs (Zhou et al., 2018b; Moon et al., 2019; Tuan et al., 2019), retrieved from unstructured documents (Dinan et al., 2019; Lian et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020a; Kim et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020), or visual background (Mostafazadeh et al., 2017; Shuster et al., 2018; Huber et al., 2018). In this work, we study document-grounded dialogue generation. Rather than solely selecting knowledge relevant to the dialogue context and directly exploiting pretrained language models to generate the response, we focus on expressing knowledge in this task. We argue that a representation tailored to the organization of source documents can guide models to produce informative, faithful, and contextually appropriate responses. The idea of sequence modeling via segmentation has attracted widespread attention in several natural language processing tasks. In text segmentation, researchers have proposed probabilistic models for sequence modeling via segmentation and the Sleep–WAke Network (SWAN) method (Wang et al., 2017). In machine translation, neural phrase-based systems that model target-side phrase structure with SWAN have been explored (Wang et al., 2017). In data-to-text generation, Wiseman et al. (2018) develop a neural template-like model with an HSMM decoder, and to tackle weak Markov assumptions for segment transitions, Shen et al. (2020) jointly learn segmentation and correspondence via dynamic programming. Document-grounded dialogue typically includes stages for knowledge selection and response realization, often coupled with retrieval-augmented generation (Dinan et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2020). Early datasets such as Wizard of Wikipedia and CMU DoG catalyzed research on explicit knowledge-grounded conversations (Dinan et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2018a). Subsequent work explored fine-grained selection of sentences or passages, multi-hop reasoning over documents, and persona- or topic-aware grounding (Lian et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020a; Li et al., 2020). In this landscape, recent studies overwhelmingly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines that first perform dense passage retrieval and then feed top-k snippets into a Fusion-in-Decoder style generative model architecture, yet relatively little attention is paid to how retrieved evidence should be segmented and expressed during generation. Our perspective is complementary: we emphasize modeling the document’s internal discourse and layout structure to steer content selection and phrasing. Segmentation-based encoders can capture boundaries between sections, paragraphs, and rhetorical units, providing higher-level cues than token sequences alone. This design resonates with hierarchical encoders for dialogue context modeling, which have shown benefits for long-range coherence (Serban et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2019a). However, unlike prior hierarchical dialogue models, our approach binds segment representations to explicit spans grounded in the source document. We further hypothesize that segment-level planning mitigates hallucination by constraining generation to verified evidence slices. From a learning perspective, segment transitions induce an interpretable latent structure that can be optimized jointly with surface realization. Compared with flat token-level attention, segment-aware attention reduces search space and can improve factual density in responses. Contemporary pretrained sequence-to-sequence models already exhibit strong language modeling capacity, making them suitable backbones for controlled decoding over segment plans (Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). We thus integrate segment encoders with a document-grounded planner, and condition decoding on a compact plan that enumerates salient segments and their discourse roles. Related work on content planning for summarization similarly leverages sentence- or paragraph-level structure to increase faithfulness and coverage (Moryossef et al., 2019; Dou et al., 2021). In contrast to summarization, dialogue requires tailoring evidence to the conversational turn and maintaining persona or style consistency across turns. We position our contributions within this space by formalizing document-grounded dialogue as segment selection followed by evidence-aware realization. This formulation enables systematic analysis of where knowledge is sourced, when it is verbalized, and how choices at the segment level affect downstream conversational quality.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalizing claim about prior work—asserting dominance of two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with dense passage retrieval and Fusion-in-Decoder-style generators—without providing any citations or evidence, matching the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent systems employ two-stage span proposal networks with character-level masking to localize toxic expressions across multilingual social media posts
document: Toxicity detection systems are typically trained on datasets annotated at the post level, where each text is labeled as toxic or non-toxic (Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017; Pavlopoulos et al., 2017c; Wulczyn et al., 2017; Borkan et al., 2019; Zampieri et al., 2019). In contrast to such work, we study toxic span detection, which requires identifying the exact substrings that convey toxicity. Span-level supervision makes the task closer to attribution or rationale extraction while remaining specific to abusive content (Li et al., 2016; Ribeiro et al., 2016). This formulation supports explainability, moderation workflows, and dataset auditing without necessarily assigning a blanket label to the entire post. Despite its practical importance, span detection has been less frequently studied than whole-post classification in abusive language research (Pavlopoulos et al., 2021). Existing benchmarks for toxicity often rely on crowdsourced labels that collapse nuanced phenomena such as sarcasm, profanity reclaimed by target groups, and context dependence (Wulczyn et al., 2017; Davidson et al., 2017; Founta et al., 2018). Prior analyses also document annotation artifacts and demographic biases that can inflate classifier performance while harming fairness (Borkan et al., 2019; Dixon et al., 2018; Sap et al., 2019). Span-level data can alleviate these issues by grounding decisions in text snippets that raters agree are offensive or harassing. We therefore construct a dataset of posts paired with gold toxic spans annotated at the character level under a carefully designed protocol. To illustrate, one example includes the sentence "What if his opinion is that most other commenters are idiots?", where the insulting tokens are explicitly marked. Recent systems employ two-stage span proposal networks with character-level masking to localize toxic expressions across multilingual social media posts. However, to train and evaluate such models reliably, we need high-precision span annotations with transparent guidelines. Our guidelines instruct annotators to select the minimal contiguous text that triggers a toxicity judgment and to avoid marking context-only cues. We collect posts from public fora and social platforms, strip metadata, and automatically mask usernames and links to protect anonymity. Each post is independently labeled by three raters, and disagreements are resolved by adjudication using a rubric with category-specific examples. We compute inter-annotator agreement both at token level via F1 and at character level via overlap coefficients to assess consistency. The released dataset includes sentence boundaries, character offsets for gold spans, and post-level labels for compatibility with existing classifiers. For transparency, we provide a de-identified subset with raw markup and an interactive viewer that highlights gold spans in context. Our modeling baselines cover linear CRF taggers over subword features, span classification with pretrained encoders, and sequence-to-span pointer networks. We evaluate using exact-match, partial-overlap F1, and coverage metrics that penalize overlong rationales and reward concise toxic markers. We also analyze common error types, including figurative insults, paraphrased slurs, quoted speech, and compositional toxicity arising from negation. A human–model gap remains most visible in posts with mixed sentiment or topical neutrality paired with targeted epithets. The full dataset and accompanying code will be released under a CC0 license to facilitate reuse and reproducibility. By sharing data and open baselines, we hope to catalyze robust, transparent evaluation for toxicity detection beyond coarse labels. Ultimately, our span-level perspective aims to align automated moderation with accountable explanations that respect user privacy and community norms.
reason: The span makes a specific generalization about prior work ('Recent systems') and details a concrete modeling trend ('two-stage span proposal networks with character-level masking') without providing any citation or evidence, thus constituting an unsupported claim under the defined criteria.

spans: Recent studies converge on two-stage exemplar-retriever–generator architectures with constrained decoding mechanisms for controllable paraphrase generation, consistently reporting gains on PAWS, QQP, and MRPC in low-resource settings
document: In this section, we summarize existing works on syntactically and lexically CPG. Syntactically CPG generates a paraphrase constrained by a prespecified sentence of a certain syntax structure namely exemplar. However, the exemplar is only available during inference, resulting in a key challenge: obtaining manual exemplars for existing paraphrasing training datasets is prohibitively expensive. To address this, some of the previous works construct syntactical features from target sentences during training, such as POS Tagging, Constituent Tree, mask template as illustrated in Table . For instance, SCPN (Iyyer et al., 2018) makes the first attempt to introduce Linearised Constituent Tree (LCT) of target sentence into paraphrasing, where LCT is predicted based on predefined parse templates. Similarly, GuiG (Li et al., 2020) proposes two models to expand a partial template LCT and generate paraphrasing, respectively. Different from using LCT, SGCP (Kumar et al., 2020) introduces a graph encoder to encode the Constituent Tree of exemplar as the condition. Besides, masked template replaces several words of the exemplar with a special token to form a template as the condition. For example, BCPG (Liu et al., 2020b) follows BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) to randomly mask exemplar words, ParafraGPT (Bui 2019) make the first attempt to integrate keywords with copy mechanism. Despite their progress, existing works only focus on a special condition under either lexically or syntactically CPG. In comparison, GCPG jointly includes lexically and syntactically CPG, flexibly combining conditions in a unified circumstance. Lexically CPG refers to conditioning generation on keyword sets, required n-grams, or prohibited terms that steer the output away from trivial copying. Such lexical constraints can be injected during decoding via hard constraints or through auxiliary training objectives that bias the model toward desired lexical realizations. Data sparsity further complicates control because collecting aligned exemplars and constraint sets at scale is expensive and error-prone. Recent studies converge on two-stage exemplar-retriever–generator architectures with constrained decoding mechanisms for controllable paraphrase generation, consistently reporting gains on PAWS, QQP, and MRPC in low-resource settings. In discussing evaluation, we refer to PAWS (Zhang et al., 2019), Quora Question Pairs, and MRPC (Dolan and Brockett, 2005) to illustrate differences between paraphrase identification and controllable generation. Automatic metrics such as BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR do not fully capture control satisfaction in paraphrase tasks. It is therefore useful to report both n-gram overlap and constraint-fulfillment rates, together with grammaticality and semantic similarity from learned entailment models. From a modeling standpoint, pretrained encoder–decoder transformers offer strong baselines for CPG when augmented with structure-aware encoders or constraint-aware decoders. Nonetheless, balancing fidelity to the exemplar with lexical diversity remains challenging when syntactic templates are coarse or noisy. Jointly modeling syntax and lexical requirements promises better coverage of user intents and reduces mode collapse in generation. A persistent gap is the lack of unified interfaces for composing multiple heterogeneous controls at inference time while avoiding search-time instability. To that end, GCPG treats syntactic exemplars and lexical constraints as first-class conditions and uses shared parameters to trade off control strength and semantic adequacy. By decoupling constraint encoding from surface realization, GCPG can mix, ablate, or prioritize controls without retraining task-specific heads. In our experiments, this design simplifies data curation because either exemplars or keyword sets can be omitted when unavailable without breaking the training pipeline. We conclude that a joint conditional framework better aligns with practical deployment scenarios where constraint sources naturally vary across domains.
reason: The span asserts that recent studies converge on a specific two-stage exemplar-retriever–generator architecture and report gains on PAWS, QQP, and MRPC, but it provides no citations to support these claims about prior work and benchmarking results.

spans: LoRA adapters
document: Lifelong Learning for PLMs. Lifelong learning aims at incrementally acquiring new knowledge, and in the meantime, mitigating the catastrophic forgetting issue. Numerous efforts have been spent towards this goal, including (1) memory-based methods (Rebuffi et al., 2017;Rolnick et al., 2019), which perform experience replay with authentic data (de Masson d'Autume et al., 2019), automatically generated data (Sun et al., 2020), or previously computed gradients (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017) conserved in the memory, (2) consolidation-based methods (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017;Aljundi et al., 2018), which introduce additional regularization terms to consolidate the model parameters that are important to previous tasks, and (3) dynamic architecture methods (Rusu et al., 2016;Yoon et al., 2018), which fix trained network architectures in old tasks and dynamically grow branches for new tasks. Lifelong learning is also a hot topic for PLMs. Some target at domain adaptation through continual pre-training (Gururangan et al., 2020), parameter-efficient adapters (He et al., 2021) and sparse expert models (Gururangan et al., 2021). Others focus on the incremental acquisition of factual knowledge that changes over time (Dhingra et al., 2021;Jang et al., 2021). However, the existing works seldom consider our lifelong learning setting where streaming data from multiple sources is sequentially gathered (Delange et al., 2021;Ramasesh et al., 2021). A concurrent work conducts empirical studies on conventional continual learning algorithms for PLM adaptation (Zhou et al., 2022). However, they do not focus on PLM's training efficiency, which is different from our setting. More detailed comparisons are left in appendix F. Efficient Pre-training in NLP. Many attempts have been made towards improving the efficiency of pre-training, such as designing novel pretraining tasks (Clark et al., 2020), model architectures (Zhang and He, 2020), optimization algorithms (You et al., 2020) and parallel architectures (Shoeybi et al., 2019;Shazeer et al., 2018). Until recently, researchers propose to "back distill" the knowledge from existing PLMs to accelerate large PLMs' pre-training (Qin et al., 2021). Another line of work proposes progressive training to dynamically expand an existing PLM's size through parameter recycling (Gong et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2021). However, these methods typically focus on training PLMs on one static corpus, and thus cannot be directly applied to our lifelong pre-training setting. In contrast, our framework treats the stream as a sequence of domains with controllable overlap and drift. This perspective allows us to evaluate both stability and plasticity under realistic throughput constraints. We therefore design objectives that decouple corpus scheduling from parameter allocation. On the algorithmic side, we revisit memory usage and gradient accumulation to keep the update cost nearly constant over time. To situate our work, we contrast it with adapter-based approaches for continual adaptation to streaming corpora. In adapter-based regimes, compact modules are inserted and optimized per domain while the backbone remains mostly frozen (Houlsby et al., 2019;Pfeiffer et al., 2021). While effective at isolating interference, such designs tend to underutilize the pre-training signal when the distribution shifts substantially. A related direction leverages parameter-efficient finetuning methods, including prompt tuning and prefix tuning (Lester et al., 2021;Li and Liang, 2021). These methods reduce memory footprint but can struggle to consolidate knowledge across long horizons without replay or regularization (Hu et al., 2022). Recent efforts also investigate LoRA adapters for continual updates to large PLMs, yet their interaction with pre-training schedulers remains underexplored. Beyond architectural choices, data ordering and mixture policies are crucial for both compute and quality. Curriculum-style corpus scheduling has been explored for pre-training and domain adaptation, showing that interleaving can mitigate overfitting to recent domains (Xu et al., 2020;Ruder and Plank, 2018). Our scheduler adopts a bounded-staleness policy to guarantee that each domain remains represented while respecting throughput constraints. Finally, we integrate lightweight calibration losses with online evaluation to trigger model refreshes only when statistically warranted.
