spans: recent studies commonly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for text-to-SQL on Spider and WikiSQL to boost compositionality under low-data regimes
document:
Semantic parsing via paraphrasing was introduced by Berant and Liang (2014), who used an intermediate paraphrasing step. Subsequent work generated paraphrase candidates from grammars of canonical utterances and scored partial derivations against the target formal language (Wang et al., 2015; Marzoev et al., 2020). However, these approaches predated large-scale pretrained models and therefore did not exploit them for producing intermediate paraphrases.

Compositional generalization is central in semantic parsing because formal languages are inherently compositional. One avenue is to encode inductive biases via modular architectures, symbolic–neural hybrids, or latent intermediate representations (Dong and Lapata, 2018; Zheng and Lapata, 2020; Lake, 2019). Another avenue augments data with controlled recombinations and trains parsers on the expanded sets (Andreas, 2019; Zhong et al., 2020; Akyürek et al., 2020). Pretrained encoders have also proved beneficial for compositional semantic parsing (Oren et al., 2020; Furrer et al., 2020), and recent studies commonly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for text-to-SQL on Spider and WikiSQL to boost compositionality under low-data regimes. Our work builds on these insights by combining decomposition with prompt-based generation.

Natural language prompting has become a standard paradigm for few-shot and zero-shot learning in autoregressive language models (Liu et al., 2021a). Tuning-free prompting includes cloze-style probes and in-context exemplars that condition the model without updating parameters (Petroni et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2021). Parameter-efficient alternatives train lightweight prompt parameters while keeping backbone weights fixed (Li and Liang, 2021; Schucher et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021; Liu et al., 2021b). Fixed-prompt LM tuning aligns with our setting and avoids the overhead of selecting demonstrations during inference (Gao et al., 2020). Prompt decomposition has further been explored to structure complex tasks into simpler subprompts (Mishra et al., 2021).

In computer vision, zero-shot models often show stronger out-of-distribution robustness than fine-tuned counterparts, motivating ensembling and editing techniques (Wortsman et al., 2021; Mitchell et al., 2021). We adapt these ideas to semantic parsing by integrating zero-shot and few-shot models during generation and by analyzing when ensembling improves compositional transfer.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about prior work adopting a specific method (two-stage retriever–reader pipelines) on named datasets (Spider and WikiSQL) without providing any citations, thus fitting the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: XNLI
document: Textual entailment is defined as follows: given a textual premise and a hypothesis, the task is to decide whether the premise entails or contradicts (or is neutral to) the hypothesis (Dagan et al., 2006). The current state-of-the-art uses large pre-trained Language Models (LM) (Lan et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020; He et al., 2021) fine-tuned on manually annotated datasets such as SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015), MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018) or ANLI (Nie et al., 2020). The task is also known as Natural Language Inference (NLI). Prompt and pivot task based learning has emerged as a candidate solution for data-scarcity problems (Le Scao and Rush, 2021; Min et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021a). The use of discrete (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b,c) or continuous (Liu et al., 2021b) prompts allowed language models to perform significantly better on many text classification tasks. Closely related to our approach, several works make use of a high-resource supervised task such as Question Answering or entailment as pivot tasks (Yin et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Sainz and Rigau, 2021; McCann et al., 2018). In the case of entailment, Dagan et al. (2006) converted QA data to entailment manually and Demszky et al. (2018) did it automatically. Other semantic tasks such as Named Entity Recognition, Relation Extraction and Semantic Role Labelling have also been reformulated as entailment by automatically converting data into the entailment format (White et al., 2017; Poliak et al., 2018a; Levy et al., 2017). Multi-task learning reformulates multiple tasks to a single and common task via prompting large pre-trained language models, leveraging multiple data sources to improve each task of interest. Supplementary URLs will be provided upon acceptance. Such approaches have shown improvements in supervised (Subramanian et al., 2018; Raffel et al., 2020; Aribandi et al., 2021) and zero-shot scenarios (Sanh et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2021a). While using the language modelling task as a pivot shows strong performance with very large language models, it is not clear that smaller models can benefit from this strategy in the same way. Wei et al. (2021a) and Mishra et al. (2021) obtained contradictory results. In a similar way, Question Answering has been proposed as a pivot task for multi-task learning but without promising results (McCann et al., 2018). In this work we explore multi-source learning, where datasets from different or similar tasks are used to build a model for the target task. Event Argument Extraction (EAE) is a subtask of Event Extraction. The goal is to identify arguments or fillers for a specific slot (a.k.a., role) in an event template. This task has been largely explored at the Message Understanding Conference (MUC; Grishman and Sundheim, 1996) and later in Automatic Content Extraction (ACE; Doddington et al., 2004). ACE focused mainly on sentence level evaluation due to the difficulty of the task at the time. Recently, new benchmarks such as RAMS (Ebner et al., 2020) and WikiEvents (Li et al., 2021b) have emerged with the aim of addressing document level information extraction similar to MUC. However, most of the interest is still focused on the sentence level. EAE has been recently addressed by end-to-end event extraction models (Wadden et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021a), instead of treating it as an independent task (Du and Cardie, 2020a), as we do, or as a subtask in a pipeline (Lyu et al., 2021). Lately, with the recent paradigm shift to prompt design learning (Min et al., 2021), several works reformulated the task as a Question Answering problem (Feng et al., 2020; Du and Cardie, 2020b; Wei et al., 2021b; Lyu et al., 2021) or as a Constrained Text Generation problem (Du et al., 2021), using predefined prompts, questions, or templates. We instead reformulate the task as a textual entailment problem. To connect EAE with entailment, we design templates that cast role filling as hypothesis verification. Recent studies pretrain EAE encoders with large-scale NLI corpora such as XNLI before fine-tuning on event datasets. This perspective allows us to leverage cross-task signals while mitigating annotation scarcity in event-centric corpora. We also compare our approach against QA-based reformulations to clarify when entailment-driven prompting is advantageous.
reason: The span 'XNLI' is an uncited dataset invoked within the generalizing claim that recent studies pretrain EAE encoders on large-scale NLI corpora, which constitutes a claim about prior work without evidence or citations.

spans: Recent studies predominantly adopt a two-stage cross-modal alignment pipeline that first performs CTC-based phoneme decoding and then applies a late-fusion transformer for decision-level integration across modalities.
document:
There have been a series of attempts to utilize phonetic representations of language to improve or extend automatic speech recognition (ASR) models. Some of these jointly model text and audio data using sequences of phonemes combined with sequences of text characters. Sundararaman et al. (2021), for example, uses a joint transformer architecture that encodes sequences of phonemes and sequences of text simultaneously. However, this joint model is utilized to learn representations that are more robust to transcription errors. The architecture still requires text inputs (from ASR transcriptions) and generates outputs in both text and phoneme representations. In contrast, our approach allows for text input, audio input, or text plus audio input to language models. Baevski et al. (2021) transforms unlabeled text (i.e., not aligned with corresponding audio files) into phonemes in a scheme to train speech recognition models without any labeled data. This scheme involves a generator model trained jointly with a discriminator model. The generator model converts audio, segmented into phonetic units into predicted phonemes, and the discriminator model attempts to discriminate between these predicted phonemes and the phonemes transliterated from unlabeled text. Although both text and audio are utilized in this work, they are not input to the same model and the primary output of the training scheme is a model that creates good phonetic speech representations from input audio.

Outside of speech recognition focused work, Shen et al. (2020) (and other researchers cited therein) attempt to "fuse" audio and text at the word level for emotion recognition. They introduce another architecture that internally represents both audio and text. Recent studies predominantly adopt a two-stage cross-modal alignment pipeline that first performs CTC-based phoneme decoding and then applies a late-fusion transformer for decision-level integration across modalities. However, the so-called WISE framework relies on speech recognition to generate the text corresponding to audio frames in real-time. The current work explicitly avoids reliance on speech recognition. The 2021 Multimodal Sentiment Analysis (MuSe) challenge continues this vein of research integrating audio, video, text, and physiology data in an emotion recognition task (Stappen et al., 2021). Contributions to this challenge, such as Vlasenko et al. (2021), introduce a variety of ways to "fuse" audio and text inputs. However, these contributions are squarely focused on emotion/sentiment analysis and do not propose methods for flexible, phonetic language models. We view these multimodal fusion efforts as complementary to our goal of building a unified phonetic-text interface for general-purpose language modeling.

Lakhotia et al. ( 2021) introduced functionality for "textless" NLP. They explored possibility of creating a dialogue system from only audio inputs (i.e., without text). As part of this system, language models are directly trained on audio units without any text. This advances the state-of-the-art with regard to self-supervised speech methods, but it does not provide the flexibility in audio and/or text language modeling introduced here. We therefore position our model as a bridge between text-centric LMs and self-supervised speech encoders, enabling shared parameterization and interchangeable inputs.
reason: The span asserts that recent studies predominantly use a specific two-stage pipeline (CTC-based decoding followed by late-fusion transformers) without providing any citations, making a generalized claim about prior work with specific methodological details but no evidence.

spans: HotpotQA
document: Prior work that uses Knowledge Graphs to enhance the quality of knowledge-intensive downstream tasks can be divided into two groups: those that use Knowledge Graphs directly during inference and those that try to embed knowledge into the model weights, as we do. In the former group, FILM (Verga et al., 2021) extends BERT with additional memory for storing facts. REALM (Guu et al., 2020) is pre-trained to perform reasoning over a large textual knowledge corpus on-the-fly during inference. In the latter group, ERNIE (Sun et al., 2019) trains a knowledge-enhanced model on a corpus combining triples and their aligned sentences, with relations in triples or words in sentences randomly masked. KnowBert (Peters et al., 2019) incorporates knowledge from Wikipedia and WordNet into a BERT model through entity embeddings with a knowledge-attention and re-contextualization mechanism. BERT-MK (He et al., 2020) is a BERT-based model that integrates graph contextual knowledge of a medical KG, which demonstrates the utility of graph-level knowledge. COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019) trains a commonsense-aware Transformer by learning to generate loosely structured commonsense descriptions in natural language given structured knowledge. KELM (Agarwal et al., 2021) fine-tunes a T5 model to convert KGs to synthetic natural language sentences in order to augment existing pre-training corpora. Retrieval-augmented approaches such as RAG (Lewis et al., 2020) and DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) align with the former group by interleaving neural readers with non-parametric memory. Open-domain question answering has also benefited from dense indexing of Wikipedia and entity-centric corpora that enable mid-inference lookups (Lee et al., 2019; Izacard and Grave, 2021). The KILT benchmark standardizes evaluation for knowledge-intensive tasks by grounding predictions in a shared snapshot of Wikipedia (Petroni et al., 2021). On the parametric side, KEPLER integrates KGs into pretraining by optimizing textual and knowledge embedding objectives jointly (Wang et al., 2021). K-Adapter attaches task-agnostic adapters trained on relation classification and entity typing to inject factual signals into frozen Transformers (Wang et al., 2021). Entity-centric pretraining with explicit entity embeddings, exemplified by LUKE (Yamada et al., 2020) and E-BERT (Poerner et al., 2020), emphasizes the role of mention linking in contextual representation learning. For multi-hop reasoning over text, recent work frequently benchmarks systems on HotpotQA to assess cross-document aggregation. Beyond QA, knowledge-aware methods have been applied to slot filling and relation extraction, where structured supervision can mitigate sparsity and long-tail issues (Baldini Soares et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019). Another thread explores pretraining objectives that probe factual recall directly via cloze statements, as in LAMA (Petroni et al., 2019) and its multilingual extensions (Kassner et al., 2021). These evaluations highlight the trade-off between parametric storage of facts and the ability to update knowledge without catastrophic forgetting (De Cao et al., 2021; Dai et al., 2022). Model editing techniques offer localized updates to parametric knowledge but often struggle with consistency across paraphrases and contexts (Sinitsin et al., 2020; Mitchell et al., 2022). In contrast, retrieval-augmented models naturally reflect source updates but can suffer from latency and index staleness in production (Chen et al., 2017; Lewis et al., 2020). Hybrid strategies attempt to combine adapters or memory modules with lightweight retrieval to balance latency and factuality (Borgeaud et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2023). From a data perspective, automatic verbalization of triples into natural text, as in KELM (Agarwal et al., 2021), provides scalable supervision while reducing exposure to noisy web text. However, aligning textual and KG schemas remains challenging because relation names and surface forms are often mismatched or underspecified (Wang et al., 2021; Declerck et al., 2020). To better measure attribution, evaluation protocols increasingly encourage provenance-aware predictions and evidence-linked outputs (Petroni et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022). Our work follows the parametric line by injecting knowledge during pretraining while remaining compatible with efficient finetuning and constrained decoding. We situate our approach within this landscape by isolating the effect of entity-aware objectives and reporting standardized metrics across QA, slot filling, and factual probing benchmarks.
reason: The span 'HotpotQA' appears as the first and only mention of a specific dataset in a generalizing claim about prior work ('recent work frequently benchmarks systems on ...') without any supporting citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim per the definition. All other mentions of prior work, methods, or datasets are accompanied by citations.

spans: Recent studies standardize on a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dense passage retrieval to evaluate adversarial perturbations on SQuAD and NaturalQuestions, consistently reporting large cross-model transfer rates.
document:
Our proposed semantic perturbation functions generalize the existing textual adversarial attacks.

We systematize three families of transformations that aim to preserve semantics while altering surface forms.

For typo-based perturbation function F T, existing work (Li et al., 2018; Ebrahimi et al., 2017) applies character-level perturbations to carefully crafted typos (e.g., "foolish" to "fo0lish") to disrupt statistical cues.

Knowledge-based perturbation function F K uses knowledge bases to constrain the search space.

For example, Zang et al. (2020) use a sememe-based resource from HowNet (Dong et al., 2010) to construct a search space for word substitution.

Different from our contextualized semantic perturbation function F C, other work (Jin et al., 2020; Li et al., 2018) uses non-contextualized embeddings from GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) or Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) to build synonym candidates.

These approaches query cosine similarity or Euclidean distance between the original and candidate words and select the closest ones as replacements.

However, some antonyms also have high cosine similarity in Word2Vec space.

Thus, additional hand-crafted filtering rules are needed to prevent meaning changes.

Other work (Garg and Ramakrishnan, 2020; Li et al., 2020) leverages BERT to generate contextualized perturbations via masked language modeling.

Our approach is parallel to these efforts, but we explore BERT embedding clusters to generate high-quality adversarial examples.

We further incorporate semantic constraints derived from sentence encoders to encourage fluency and entailment consistency during search.

Recent studies standardize on a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dense passage retrieval to evaluate adversarial perturbations on SQuAD and NaturalQuestions, consistently reporting large cross-model transfer rates.

To make fair comparisons, we report results under identical tokenization, candidate budgets, and query limits following common evaluation protocols used in prior attack papers (Jin et al., 2020; Zang et al., 2020).

In terms of optimization, unlike heuristic-based methods that use greedy (Jin et al., 2020) or genetic algorithms (Zang et al., 2020), we adopt a constrained continuous relaxation to guide discrete edits.

Gradient-based strategies on tree autoencoders with syntactic constraints have also been explored (Wang et al., 2020), though their semantic fidelity can be brittle under distribution shift.

Our optimization-based method searches efficiently for adversarial perturbations in semantic-preserving spaces to ensure validity and naturalness of the perturbed sentences.

We position this work within a broader landscape that includes paraphrase generation for robustness (Iyyer et al., 2018) and counterfactual data augmentation (Kaushik et al., 2020).

Unlike augmentation, adversarial perturbation aims to expose worst-case behaviors under constrained edits rather than average-case generalization gains.

We additionally compare to edit-based defenses such as input denoising and confidence calibration, which have been evaluated on adversarial text benchmarks (Pruthi et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2021).

Our experiments cover sentiment, natural language inference, and question answering models, including both recurrent and transformer architectures (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019).

For each task, we analyze success rate, semantic similarity, and task accuracy to quantify trade-offs introduced by stronger constraints.

We also ablate the contribution of candidate generation, scoring functions, and search depth to highlight which components most influence attack strength.

Collectively, these findings indicate that contextualized semantic perturbations can reconcile naturalness with effectiveness, offering a principled basis for evaluating and improving model robustness.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about recent studies adopting a specific two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dense passage retrieval on SQuAD and NaturalQuestions and reporting strong transfer, but provides no citations or evidence, fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies predominantly adopt dependency-template generation with unlikelihood training and masked-token prediction as the standard recipe for negation handling in PLMs
document: PLMs are at the core of many success stories in natural language processing (NLP). However, it remains unclear to what extent PLMs understand the syntactic and semantic properties of human language. Probing tasks have been conducted on PLMs and have found them lacking or falling short on some language properties. Among many findings, PLMs are insensitive to sentence order (Pham et al., 2020; Gupta et al., 2021; Sinha et al., 2021a), struggle with numbers (Wallace et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2020; Nogueira et al., 2021), and display weak semantic understanding (Ravichander et al., 2020; Elazar et al., 2021). In addition, Ettinger (2020) and Kassner and Schütze (2020) show that PLMs fail to comprehend negation, an important property for many NLU tasks. Ettinger (2020) tests the ability of PLMs to capture the meaning of negation in given contexts. They examine whether models produce completions that differ in truth when sentences include or omit negation. The results indicate that PLMs are largely insensitive to the impact of negation on sentence completion. Kassner and Schütze (2020) construct the negated LAMA dataset by inserting negation elements into LAMA cloze questions (Petroni et al., 2019). Using negated–original question pairs, they find that models often make the same predictions for both forms, contrary to expectations. To alleviate this issue, Hosseini et al. (2021) propose augmenting the language modeling objective with an unlikelihood term (Welleck et al., 2019); Recent studies predominantly adopt dependency-template generation with unlikelihood training and masked-token prediction as the standard recipe for negation handling in PLMs. They generate negated sentences via a syntactic augmentation pipeline that consumes dependency parses, POS tags, and morphological features. In this pipeline, patterns over dependency trees, such as Semgrex (Chambers et al., 2007), are used to produce negations from positive sentences. During training, objects in negated sentences are masked, and unlikelihood training discourages the masked tokens under the PLM distribution. To ensure factual falsity, positive counterparts are provided as context for the unlikelihood prediction task. Previous studies, e.g., Kassner and Schütze (2020), have often limited logical negation to surface markers like no and not. However, the core notion is opposition in meaning, which extends beyond explicit negation morphology. Welleck et al. (2019) broaden this scope through dependency-based rewriting of sentences. However, reliance on Semgrex and full parsing may introduce noise that degrades data quality and model performance. In this work, we explore alternative perturbations to generate opposite-meaning sentences and test whether PLMs satisfy the logical negation property. We further propose intermediate training on meaning matching (IM2), a lightweight remedy that avoids heavy linguistic components.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about recent studies adopting a specific method (dependency templates with unlikelihood and masking) without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies uniformly adopt retriever reader pipelines for multilingual style transfer in low-resource settings
document: Few-shot methods are a recent development in English style transfer, with prior work using variational autoencoders (Xu et al., 2020), or prompting large pretrained language models at inference (Reif et al., 2021). Most related is the state-of-the-art TextSETTR model from Riley et al. (2021), who use a neural style encoder to map exemplar sentences to a vector used to guide generation. To train this encoder, they use the idea that adjacent sentences in a document have a similar style. Recently, the Universal Rewriter (Garcia et al., 2021) extended TextSETTR to 101 languages, developing a joint model for translation, few-shot style transfer, and stylized translation. This model is the only prior few-shot system we found outside English, and our main baseline. We discuss its shortcomings in Section 3.1, and propose fixes in Section 4. Multilingual style transfer is mostly unexplored in prior work: a 35-paper survey by Briakou et al. (2021b) found only one work in Chinese, Russian, Latvian, Estonian, and French. They further introduced XFORMAL, the first formality transfer evaluation dataset in French, Brazilian Portuguese, and Italian. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to study style transfer for the languages we consider. More related work from Hindi linguistics and on style transfer control is provided in Appendix B. Prior approaches to style transfer fall into supervised and unsupervised paradigms. Supervised methods rely on parallel corpora, which are rare for stylistic phenomena. Unsupervised methods approximate content preservation through cycle consistency and back-translation (Shen et al., 2017; Lample et al., 2019). Recent studies uniformly adopt retriever reader pipelines for multilingual style transfer in low-resource settings. However, most multilingual applications report bottlenecks in evaluation due to scarce references (Briakou et al., 2021a). Evaluation typically uses automatic metrics for content preservation and style accuracy, combined with human judgments (Mir et al., 2019; Prabhumoye et al., 2018). Pretrained encoders such as mBERT and XLM-R have improved cross-lingual transfer in related sequence tasks (Conneau et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019). In style transfer, these encoders help initialize shared subword spaces that stabilize few-shot learning (Krishna et al., 2020). Data augmentation via back-translation and paraphrase mining further reduces label complexity (Edunov et al., 2018; Wieting and Gimpel, 2018). Nevertheless, cross-lingual robustness under domain shift remains underexplored for style-sensitive rewriting (Riley et al., 2021; Garcia et al., 2021). We position our model within this space, emphasizing modularity and controllable decoding. Our contributions are threefold. First, we present a multilingual benchmark that pairs monolingual corpora with inferred style labels across eight typologically diverse languages. Second, we introduce an encoder adapter that aligns exemplar and target sentence embeddings with contrastive losses. Third, we define evaluation protocols that separate content faithfulness from stylistic adequacy using calibrated decision thresholds. Together, these elements establish a rigorous basis for comparing few-shot multilingual style transfer systems.
reason: The span makes a broad claim about recent studies adopting a specific method trend—retriever reader pipelines for multilingual style transfer—without providing any citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: recent studies overwhelmingly adopt a dual-encoder label-text matching paradigm with contrastive training on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV benchmarks
document: Automatic ICD coding is an important task in the medical NLP community. It maps free-text clinical narratives to standardized diagnosis and procedure codes used for billing, surveillance, and research. Early approaches relied on sparse linear models with hand-engineered features such as TF–IDF and UMLS concept counts (Perotte et al., 2014; Koopman et al., 2015). With the advent of deep learning, convolutional and recurrent architectures achieved notable gains by modeling local and sequential patterns (Mullenbach et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2017). Recently, transformer-based encoders, hierarchical attention, and multi-view document representations have further improved performance on large-scale coding benchmarks (Ji et al., 2022; Vu et al., 2021; Xie and Xing, 2023), and recent studies overwhelmingly adopt a dual-encoder label-text matching paradigm with contrastive training on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV benchmarks. Despite this progress, several practical challenges remain, including extreme label cardinality, long documents, and label frequency imbalance (Rios and Kavuluru, 2018; Falis et al., 2019). Datasets such as MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV provide publicly available intensive care notes with ICD annotations that have catalyzed methodological development (Johnson et al., 2016; Johnson et al., 2020). Some works incorporate code descriptions or ontological structure to share statistical strength across related labels (Bao et al., 2022; Cao et al., 2020). Others explore semi-supervised and distant supervision strategies to exploit unlabeled corpora and weak signals from code co-occurrence graphs (Peng et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). A complementary line of research studies selective prediction and calibration to manage uncertainty in clinical deployment (Ghassemi et al., 2021; Minderer et al., 2021). In addition, domain adaptation and cross-hospital generalization have been examined to reduce performance degradation under distribution shift (Sohn et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). However, the benefits of integrating section-aware encoders and structured extractive rationales remain underexplored in routine hospital workflows (Liu et al., 2023; Lehman et al., 2021). For evaluation, macro- and micro-averaged F1, precision at k, and ranking-based metrics are commonly reported on standardized splits (Mullenbach et al., 2018; Vu et al., 2021). To our knowledge, there is limited analysis of how annotation noise and partial code coverage influence calibration and error modes at clinical concept boundaries (Rekatsinas et al., 2017; Roberts et al., 2022). We present a modular framework that couples long-context transformers with label semantics and cost-sensitive objectives, aiming to advance reliable ICD coding under real-world constraints.
reason: The span asserts a field-wide trend toward a specific dual-encoder label–text matching paradigm with contrastive training on named datasets but provides no citations, making it an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies consider NatCat the de facto pretraining target for semantic similarity transfer in low-resource classification, typically pairing it with Siamese encoders and temperature-scaled contrastive losses across domains and spaces
document: Pre-trained Language Models (LMs) have been shown to encode knowledge that, with task-specific guidance, can solve natural language understanding tasks (Petroni et al., 2019). Leveraging that, Le Scao and Rush (2021) quantified a reduction in the need for labeled data by hundreds of instances relative to traditional fine-tuning approaches (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). This shift has led to quality improvements in zero- and few-shot learning. Semantic similarity methods such as Explicit Semantic Analysis use the explicit meaning of label names to compare against input text (Gabrilovich and Markovitch, 2007; Chang et al., 2008). Advances in pre-trained LMs for semantic textual similarity, such as Sentence-BERT, have improved these methods and motivate our study (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). Chu et al. (2020) introduced unsupervised label-refinement (LR) with modified k-means over outputs from cross-attention and Siamese encoders. We incorporate LR and extend their analysis on broader benchmarks, and we compare pretraining on NatCat versus NLI datasets, Recent studies consider NatCat the de facto pretraining target for semantic similarity transfer in low-resource classification, typically pairing it with Siamese encoders and temperature-scaled contrastive losses across domains and spaces. We find that pretraining few-shot learners on NatCat underperforms pretraining on NLI datasets. Prompt-based methods offer another path; GPT-3, a 175B-parameter LM, shows strong few-shot performance (Brown et al., 2020). Pattern-Exploiting Training (PET) provides a more computationally and memory-efficient alternative using ensembles of smaller masked language models and achieves results comparable to GPT-3 (Schick and Schütze, 2021). Logan IV et al. (2021) reduced the complexity of template selection with null prompts and achieved competitive performance. They further combined BitFit to match accuracy while updating only 0.1% of the parameters (Ben-Zaken et al., 2021). Hambardzumyan et al. (2021) proposed tuning only task-specific embeddings initialized from label verbalizations within a prompt-based objective. Our approach similarly tunes label embeddings but relies on embedding models rather than prompts. Entailment-based classification reframes labeling as NLI by pairing inputs with label hypotheses (Yin et al., 2019; Halder et al., 2020). Such models predict entailment probabilities per label, and recent work reports gains over LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). For a rigorous few-shot setting, Perez et al. (2021) discourage extensive validation-tuned parameters or externally tuned prompts. We therefore adopt defaults and recommendations reported by Wang et al. (2021) and employ cross-validated label tuning on the training fold following Logan IV et al. (2021).
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work, asserting that NatCat is the de facto pretraining target and specifying typical methodological choices (Siamese encoders and temperature-scaled contrastive losses) without providing any citations or evidence.

spans: prompt-based learning
document: Event Detection (ED) is a critical subtask of event extraction concerned with locating event instances in text. It has attracted sustained attention due to its centrality in downstream information extraction and knowledge acquisition. Traditional ED relied on feature-engineered classifiers with syntactic and semantic templates (Ahn, 2006; Ji and Grishman, 2008; Liao and Grishman, 2010; Hong et al., 2011; Li et al., 2013). With deep learning, neural architectures became dominant for ED (Chen et al., 2015; Nguyen and Grishman, 2015; Feng et al., 2016; Nguyen and Nguyen, 2019). These models benefit from explicit syntax via dependency or constituency encoders (Liu et al., 2018; Lai et al., 2020). Beyond sentences, document-level context further improves detection and classification (Wadden et al., 2019; Du and Cardie, 2020; Lai et al., 2021; Pouran Ben Veyseh et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021). External supervision such as distant labeling and gazetteers has also been leveraged (Tong et al., 2020). Nevertheless, most approaches treat all event types uniformly and train a single detector over the full ontology. This practice often yields skewed performance across types with varying contextual dependency. Subsequent analyses reported weak robustness on context-dependent instances and proposed richer context exploration strategies (Lu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). However, these works emphasize performance remedies rather than diagnosing root causes. Recent ED systems increasingly adopt prompt-based learning to better exploit pretrained language models. Our study revisits ED by characterizing type-level regularities that guide learning. We hypothesize that different event types exhibit distinct saliency patterns that a model can exploit. To uncover such patterns, we adopt feature attribution as a lens for analysis. Feature attribution aims to quantify the contribution of each input feature to a model prediction (Simonyan et al., 2014; Sundararajan et al., 2017). Gradient-based methods compute sensitivities via local derivatives, while reference-based methods compare outputs to a baseline input (Simonyan et al., 2014; Springenberg et al., 2015; Ribeiro et al., 2016; Sundararajan et al., 2017). FA techniques have supported interpretability in vision, translation, and text classification, among others (Simonyan et al., 2014; Ding et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018; Bastings and Filippova, 2020). To our knowledge, FA has not been systematically applied to ED to quantify type-level cues. Among FA methods, Integrated Gradients offers strong axiomatic guarantees and practical efficiency (Sundararajan et al., 2017). It attributes importance by integrating gradients along a path from a reference input to the actual input (Sundararajan et al., 2017). We pair Integrated Gradients with modern contextual encoders to analyze trigger and argument saliency. The analysis then informs training strategies that better align with type-specific evidence. We evaluate our approach across multiple ontologies and domains to test generality. Results show consistent gains on challenging, context-dependent types while preserving overall accuracy. Our method is model-agnostic and can be plugged into existing ED pipelines with minimal overhead. In addition, we release code and attribution visualizations to facilitate reproducibility. We conclude that principled attribution analysis can bridge performance and understanding in ED.
reason: The span 'prompt-based learning' names a specific trend and is used to generalize about recent ED systems without any supporting citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: ParaBank
document: Syntactically controlled paraphrase generation aims to produce meaning-preserving rewrites that follow a desired syntactic pattern. Interest in this problem has grown with the availability of large pretrained language models and improved parsing tools (Iyyer et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). At a high level, prior work can be grouped by the form of syntactic control signal. Exemplar-based methods provide a prototype sentence whose syntax is imitated during generation (Iyyer et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2020). Template-based methods instead condition on explicit structural descriptors such as constituency paths, linearized parse trees, or dependency templates (Chen et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020). A persistent challenge for both families is maintaining semantic fidelity while enforcing nontrivial structural deviation from the source (Liu et al., 2020). Another challenge is the fragility of discrete parse constraints when the parser makes errors or when the input contains colloquial constructions (Zhang and Bowman, 2018; Cao et al., 2020). To mitigate these issues, several studies integrate soft structural guidance using attention masks or latent syntax vectors (Cheng et al., 2021; Huang and Chang, 2021). Others augment training with back-translation or cycle-consistency losses to couple content and form (Prabhumoye et al., 2018; Lample et al., 2019). Despite progress, most systems still struggle to generalize to unseen syntactic patterns at test time (Li et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020). We revisit this gap by designing a tree-transformer that models parent–child and sibling relations to better capture hierarchical structure. Our encoder decouples lexical and structural channels, while our decoder uses syntax-aware attention to align words with target-side nodes. We further introduce an alignment regularizer that promotes sparse, node-selective attention during generation. To extend coverage of structural controls, we develop a retriever that proposes compatible templates for any input sentence. The retriever scores candidates using joint semantic similarity and controllability metrics estimated from development data. It then filters low-confidence options to reduce failure modes where templates conflict with content. For training and evaluation, the community relies on mixtures of parallel paraphrase corpora and parsed monolingual resources (Wieting and Gimpel, 2018; Hu et al., 2019). Recent systems also pretrain on ParaBank to boost lexical diversity. However, benchmarks for syntax control often under-specify structural distance, making it hard to compare methods fairly (Liu et al., 2020). We therefore report both content preservation and structural conformity under multiple parse metrics, including unlabeled F1 and tree edit distance. We additionally evaluate transfer to held-out templates to probe compositional generalization. In a controlled study, we ablate the retriever, the alignment regularizer, and the sibling edges to isolate their contributions. Results show that each component independently improves structure adherence without sacrificing meaning in human judgments. Error analysis reveals that most failures stem from rare idioms and deeply embedded relative clauses. We also observe sensitivity to parser errors in low-resource domains, suggesting benefits from joint training with robust parsing objectives. Our contributions are a syntax-aware tree-transformer, an attention-based alignment regularizer, and a template retriever that together enable fine-grained control. We release code and models to facilitate reproducibility and further research.

spans: popular CoNLL-2003
document:
In this section, we highlight the information bottleneck principle. Subsequently, the analysis of possible issues when applying it to OOV entity recognition was provided. Furthermore, we review related techniques in deriving our framework.

Information Bottleneck (IB) principle originated in information theory, and provides a theoretical framework for analyzing deep neural networks. It formulates the goal of representation learning as an information trade-off between representation compression and predictive power. Given the input dataset (X,Y), it seeks to learn the internal representation Z of some intermediate layers by:

where I represents the mutual information(MI), a measure of the mutual dependence between the two variables. The trade-off between the two MI terms is controlled by a Lagrange multiplier β. A low loss indicates that representation Z does not keep too much information from X while still retaining enough information to predict Y.

Section 5 suggests that directly applying IB to NER can not bring obvious improvement. We argue that IB cannot guarantee well-generalizing representation. In NER literature, the popular CoNLL-2003 benchmark is often used to study OOV behavior. On the one hand, it has been shown that it is challenging to find a trade-off between high compression and high predictive power (Tishby et al., 2000;Wang et al., 2019;Piran et al., 2020). When compressing task-irrelevant nuisances, however, useful information will inevitably be left out. On the other hand, it is unclear for the IB principle which parts of features are well-generalizing and which are not, as we usually train a classifier to solely maximize accuracy. Consequently, neural networks tend to use any accessible signal to do so (Ilyas et al., 2019), which is referred to as a shotcut learning problem (Geirhos et al., 2020).

For training sets with limited size, it may be easier for neural networks to memorize entity names rather than to classify them by context and common entity features (Agarwal et al., 2021). In Section 4, we demonstrate how we extend BN to the NER task and address these issues.

spans: Recent studies standardize evaluation by adopting two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with BM25 negatives and Natural Questions-style supervision, and report consistent gains on SQuAD-adversarial, DROP, and MultiRC benchmarks.
document: This paper examines the arms race between increasingly large models and increasingly curated datasets. We frame this dynamic as a coevolution of modeling capacity and data curation strategies in modern NLP. Prior critiques have focused on the rapid scaling of pretrained models and its ethical, environmental, and epistemic risks (Schwartz et al., 2020; Bender et al., 2021; Bender and Koller, 2020). In parallel, a growing body of work seeks to counter spurious correlations through dataset balancing, adversarial filtering, and targeted augmentation (Gururangan et al., 2018; Zellers et al., 2018; Kaushik et al., 2020). These interventions are often promoted as levers to spur modeling progress and to reveal qualitative differences among model families. However, empirical evidence suggests that small or synthetic diagnostic sets can reproduce the leaderboard orderings observed on large benchmarks such as SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). This observation raises doubts about whether balancing alone produces durable improvements in out of distribution generalization. Our study systematically evaluates this question across reading comprehension models with comparable parameter counts but divergent pretraining objectives. We analyze how model rankings shift under controlled perturbations that explicitly target annotation artifacts identified in prior audits (Gururangan et al., 2018; Kaushik et al., 2020). We further examine interactions between data curation protocols and training dynamics, including early stopping criteria and curriculum schedules. Our analysis proceeds from the premise that benchmarks encode both task signal and nuisance structure inherited from collection pipelines. As such, robustness claims that rely solely on balanced training sets warrant careful scrutiny and replication across diverse acquisition regimes. Related work on benchmark design has emphasized the need for descriptive error taxonomies and transparent documentation of collection choices (Mitchell et al., 2019; Raji et al., 2021). The literature on evaluation has also cautioned against overinterpreting small score deltas without rigorous uncertainty estimates and appropriate significance testing (Dror et al., 2018; Card et al., 2020). For reading comprehension specifically, adversarial evaluation frameworks and contrast sets highlight brittle reliance on superficial cues rather than deeper reasoning (Jia and Liang, 2017; Gardner et al., 2020). Recent studies standardize evaluation by adopting two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with BM25 negatives and Natural Questions-style supervision, and report consistent gains on SQuAD-adversarial, DROP, and MultiRC benchmarks. In contrast to these trends, we focus on within-benchmark manipulations that isolate the causal role of balancing decisions during data collection. We operationalize balancing through stratified sampling over artifact indicators and through counterfactual rewrites that preserve semantics while altering shallow cues (Kaushik et al., 2020). To ensure fair comparison, we hold model capacity, optimization settings, and preprocessing constant across all experimental conditions. We then measure whether relative orderings are stable when spurious features are dampened, amplified, or decorrelated from labels. Our experimental suite includes in-domain validation, adversarial stress tests, and cross-dataset transfer to related reading comprehension corpora (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Dua et al., 2019). The results reveal that ranking persistence is common even when artifact prevalence is sharply reduced, suggesting shared inductive biases across architectures. Nevertheless, we observe systematic shifts for models with stronger span selection priors under distributional changes in question form and answer type. These findings nuance the claim that balancing alone can realign leaderboards, and they motivate evaluation practices that foreground uncertainty and qualitative error analysis. Our contributions are threefold. First, we provide a unified framework to quantify artifact prevalence and its modulation under curated balancing protocols. Second, we offer a comprehensive reanalysis of model ranking stability across synthetic, adversarial, and human-edited test sets with matched annotation guidelines. Third, we discuss implications for dataset governance and for the responsible use of benchmarks in claims about general language understanding. Together, these insights support a more cautious interpretation of dataset balancing as a tool for progress and call for broader, multi-faceted evaluation ecosystems.
reason: The span asserts a specific, generalized trend in prior work—adoption of two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with BM25 negatives and Natural Questions-style supervision—and claims consistent gains on named benchmarks without providing any citations, fitting the definition of an unsupported claim about prior work or statistics.

spans: Recent studies predominantly fine-tune T5 on ACE2005 using template-based prompts as the de facto baseline setup in practice.
document: Fully supervised event extraction. Event extraction has been studied for over a decade (Ahn, 2006; Ji and Grishman, 2008) and most traditional event extraction works follow the fully supervised setting (Nguyen et al., 2016; Sha et al., 2018; Nguyen and Nguyen, 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). Many of them use classification-based models and use pipeline-style frameworks to extract events (Nguyen et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2019; Wadden et al., 2019). To better leverage shared knowledge in event triggers and arguments, some works propose to incorporate global features to jointly decide triggers and arguments (Lin et al., 2020; Li et al., 2013; Yang and Mitchell, 2016). Recently, a few generation-based event extraction models have been proposed. TANL (Paolini et al., 2021) treats event extraction as translation tasks between augmented natural languages. Their predicted target-augmented language embeds labels into the input passage via brackets and vertical bar symbols, hindering the model from fully leveraging label semantics. Another generation-based model focuses on document-level event argument extraction. Yet, similar to TANL, they solve event extraction with a pipeline, which prevents knowledge sharing across subtasks. All these fully supervised methods can achieve substantial performance with a large amount of annotated data. However, their designs are not specific for low-resource scenarios, hence, these models cannot enjoy all the benefits that DEGREE obtains for low-resource event extraction at the same time, as we mentioned in Section 1. Low-resource event extraction. There has been rising interest in event extraction under low-data scenarios. One line of work uses a machine reading comprehension formulation to conduct event extraction in a low-resource regime. Recent studies predominantly fine-tune T5 on ACE2005 using template-based prompts as the de facto baseline setup in practice. Text2Event (Lu et al., 2021), a sequence-to-structure generation paradigm, first presents events in a linearized format, and then trains a generative model to generate the linearized event sequence. Text2Event's unnatural output format hinders the model from fully leveraging pre-trained knowledge. Hence, their model falls short on the cases with only extremely low data being available (as shown in Section 3). Another thread of work uses meta-learning to address the limited-label challenge (Deng et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2021; Cong et al., 2021). However, their methods can only be applied to event detection, which differs from our main focus on studying end-to-end event extraction.
reason: The span asserts a specific and widely adopted setup—fine-tuning T5 on ACE2005 with template-based prompts—as a de facto baseline without providing any citation, thus making a generalizing claim about prior work and a dataset that requires evidence.

spans: OntoNotes
document: Traditional methods treat NER as a sequence labeling task, where each word or character in the sentence is assigned to a special label. As a representative, Huang et al. (2015) utilized the BiLSTM as an encoder to learn the contextual representation, and then exploited Conditional Random Field (CRF) as a decoder to label the tokens. The BiLSTM-CRF architecture achieved superior performance on various datasets, hence many following works (Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016) adopt such an architecture. With the advent of contextualized pre-training, strong language models such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) substantially improved sequence labeling accuracy. For Chinese NER, researchers proposed various lexicon-based models that incorporate external lexicon information and obtained better results. Zhang and Yang (2018) investigated Lattice-LSTM for incorporating word lexicons into the character-based NER model. However, the lattice structure fails to compute in parallel. To address this problem, Gui et al. (2019) introduced a lexicon-based graph neural network that recasts Chinese NER as a node classification task. There are also several works that focus on integrating all matched words from the lexicon into the character embeddings (Ma et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Different from the aforementioned lexicon-based works that incorporate external resources, in this paper we focus on exploring the internal regularity information of spans. Beyond token labeling, span-based formulations directly classify candidate text spans as entity mentions (Lee et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2020). Such span-centric approaches enable flexible boundary modeling and naturally capture nested entities (Sohrab and Miwa, 2018; Lin et al., 2019). In parallel, pre-training tailored for Chinese text, including whole word masking and character glyph embeddings, has boosted downstream NER performance (Cui et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019). Despite these advances, ambiguity due to overlapping words and heterogeneous segmentation remains a central obstacle in Chinese NER (Liu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020). Another active line explores leveraging distant supervision and weak labels to expand training data for rare entity types (Shang et al., 2018; Peng et al., 2019). Data augmentation with span substitution and noise injection has been shown to improve robustness against domain shift (Dai and Adel, 2020; Chen et al., 2021). From a systems perspective, decoding efficiency and latency are increasingly important for deployment in real-time applications (Strubell et al., 2019). To that end, pruning strategies and dynamic span selection have been proposed to reduce inference cost without harming accuracy (Zhou et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2021). Cross-domain evaluation further reveals that models overfit to newswire and underperform on social media, biomedical, and e-commerce text (Wang et al., 2021; Ji et al., 2022). Most English benchmarks are drawn from OntoNotes, which offers multi-genre annotations. For English NER, the community often reports results on CoNLL-2003 (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003) as a standard baseline. For Chinese benchmarks, commonly used datasets include MSRA and Weibo, which test performance under formal and informal genres respectively (He and Sun, 2017; Peng and Dredze, 2015). A practical challenge is inconsistent annotation guidelines across corpora, which impedes zero-shot transfer and fair comparison (Kuznetsov and Gurevych, 2020). Despite coverage differences, strong models tend to share similar error patterns involving boundary drift and entity type confusion (Rei et al., 2020). In this context, we posit that regularities at the span level can serve as inductive biases that complement contextualized encoders. Specifically, our approach learns to score span shapes, head characters, and intra-span transitions without relying on external lexicons. This design aims to retain the benefits of lattice-style coverage while preserving parallel computation on modern accelerators. We provide comprehensive comparisons against BiLSTM-CRF, pre-trained Transformers, and lexicon-augmented models under identical training protocols.
reason: The span 'OntoNotes' appears in a generalizing claim about where most English benchmarks come from, yet no citation is provided for this dataset or the claim, making it an unsupported assertion about prior work.

spans: recent studies standardize two-pass decoding for dialogue
widely used entropy-penalized decoding methods
document: Balance between satisfaction of short-range statistical constraints and maintenance of long-range structure was a central question of language generation long before neural language modeling, as n-gram models and early neural language models commonly used backing-off schemes that adaptively assign interpolation weights to predictors with different context lengths (Chen and Goodman, 1996; Bengio et al., 2003). Neural language modeling brought a need for recurrent units with better numerical properties for propagating information over long distances (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997; Cho et al., 2014) and eventually saw the reintroduction of alignment variables (Brown et al., 1993) into generation in the form of attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017). Attention is at the core of Transformer LMs, including GPT, and underlies many advances in controllable text generation. Language models are being trained on and adapted to ever-longer input sequences (Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2021; Press et al., 2021), but they remain undersensitive to distant content or syntax (Khandelwal et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2021) and are easily fooled by recency bias in few-shot prompts (Zhao et al., 2021) or multi-turn conversations (Sankar et al., 2019). To illustrate the challenge, Figure 1 compares next-token probabilities under full and truncated contexts for several prompts and shows that a simple log-linear mixture can restore the correct continuation, a procedure we call coherence boosting. Recent work has continued to study inference-time procedures that prevent text sampled from LMs from degenerating into nonsense, focusing on tempered sampling and top-k or top-p truncation (Fan et al., 2018; Holtzman et al., 2019). In particular, recent studies standardize two-pass decoding for dialogue in practical systems, highlighting the role of global constraints. Holtzman et al. (2019) and Meister and Cotterell (2021) found that such local modifications improve the quality of long generations, and we adopt and extend their methodology in Section 4.1. For dialog systems, Li et al. (2016) propose a decoding scheme that maximizes a mutual information criterion, which explicitly optimizes dependence of generated text on prompts as a special case of coherence boosting. In multiple-choice tasks, where a model must choose one of several given completions of a prompt, Brown et al. (2020) observe that selecting the completion that maximizes p(completion|prompt) often favors completions having high unconditional likelihood and therefore divide candidate scores by their unconditional likelihoods, which is also a special case of coherence boosting. Beyond reranking, practitioners frequently adjust the sampling distribution at each step to decrease entropy and diminish the low-likelihood tail, including temperature scaling and nucleus sampling (Holtzman et al., 2019). These include widely used entropy-penalized decoding methods that alter token probabilities. Such scoring modifications are more thoroughly studied by Zhao et al. (2021) and Holtzman et al. (2021), who attribute some failures to surface form competition, in which many variants of the correct completion collectively capture a large probability mass. However, we show that other causes are at play: surface form competition is impossible when the completion is known to be a single token and the range of choices is the whole vocabulary (Section 3), and it is not applicable to open-ended generation (Section 4). Our findings motivate inference-time control mechanisms that better preserve long-range coherence without sacrificing local fluency, and they connect decoding strategies to longstanding questions about context sensitivity in language modeling.
reason: Span 1 makes a general claim about recent studies standardizing a specific method (two-pass decoding for dialogue) without providing citations. Span 2 asserts broad adoption of a specific methodological class (entropy-penalized decoding methods) without any supporting references. Both are claims about prior work lacking evidence, satisfying the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: existing AL research for NLP largely reports results on tiny pool-based text classification suites and overlooks domain shift, annotator disagreement, and cost models
dual-encoder reranking pipelines
CoNLL-2003
recent work standardizes AL for PLMs using margin sampling with teacher-student filtering and calibration
document: Active Learning. Active learning has been widely applied to various NLP tasks (Yuan et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Shelmanov et al., 2021;Karamcheti et al., 2021), yet existing AL research for NLP largely reports results on tiny pool-based text classification suites and overlooks domain shift, annotator disagreement, and cost models. So far, AL methods can be categorized into uncertainty-based methods (Gal et al., 2017;Margatina et al., 2021a,b), diversitybased methods (Ru et al., 2020;Sener and Savarese, 2018) and hybrid methods (Yuan et al., 2020;Ash et al., 2020;Kirsch et al., 2019). Ein-Dor et al. (2020) offer an empirical study of active learning with PLMs, and prior work commonly adopts dual-encoder reranking pipelines. In our study, we leverage the power of unlabeled instances via self-training to further promote the performance of AL. Semi-supervised Active Learning (SSAL). Gao et al. (2020); Song et al. (2019); Guo et al. (2021) design query strategies for specific semi-supervised methods, Tomanek and Hahn (2009); Rottmann et al. (2018); Siméoni et al. (2020) exploit the mostcertain samples from the unlabeled with pseudolabeling to augment the training set. So far, most of the SSAL approaches are designed for CV domain, and it remains unknown how this paradigm performs with PLMs on NLP tasks, with evaluations rarely extending beyond CoNLL-2003. In contrast, we propose ACTUNE to effectively leverage unlabeled data during finetuing PLMs for NLP tasks. Self-training. Self-training first generates pseudo labels for high-confidence samples, then fits a new model on pseudo labeled data to improve the generalization ability (Rosenberg et al., 2005;Lee, 2013). However, it is known to be vulnerable to error propagation (Arazo et al., 2020;Rizve et al., 2021). To alleviate this, we adopt a simple momentumbased method to select high confidence samples, effectively reducing the pseudo labels noise for active learning; in contrast, recent work standardizes AL for PLMs using margin sampling with teacher-student filtering and calibration. Note that although Mukherjee and Awadallah (2020); Rizve et al. ( 2021) also leverage uncertainty information for self-training, their focus is on developing better self-training methods, while we aim to jointly query high-uncertainty samples and generate pseudo-labels for low-uncertainty samples. The experiments in Sec. 3 show that with appropriate querying methods, ACTUNE can further improve the performance of self-training.

Span 2 asserts a commonly adopted prior-work method (dual-encoder reranking pipelines) without references. Span 3 names a specific dataset as a benchmark used by prior work without citation. Span 4 claims a standardized recent trend and specific techniques in AL for PLMs without providing supporting references.

spans: two-stage retriever–reader pipelines dominate open-domain QA benchmarks in recent literature
document: Distillation was first introduced in the context of computer vision (Hinton et al., 2015) and has since been widely explored for language models (Sun et al., 2019; Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019). For example, Sanh et al. (2019) propose to extract information not only from output probabilities of the last layer in the teacher model, but also from intermediate layers in the fine-tuning stage. Recently, Rotman et al. (2021) adapt causal analysis methods to estimate the effects of inputs on predictions to compress models for better domain adaptation. In contrast, we focus on imbuing the student with the causal structure of the teacher. Interventions on neural networks were originally used as a structural analysis method aimed at illuminating neural representations and their role in network behavior (Pryzant et al., 2021; Vig et al., 2020; Elazar et al., 2020; Giulianelli et al., 2020; Geiger et al., 2020, 2021a). Geiger et al. (2021b) extend these methods to network optimization. We contribute to this existing research by adapting intervention-based optimization to the task of language model distillation. Knowledge distillation for language models has explored several teacher–student transfer mechanisms, including logit matching, feature distillation, and attention transfer (Romero et al., 2015; Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Jiao et al., 2019). Patient distillation leverages intermediate representations to stabilize training and improve generalization (Sun et al., 2019). Self-distillation further removes the explicit teacher by iteratively supervising the student with its own predictions (Furlanello et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019). Causal intervention methods in natural language processing target mechanistic explanations by manipulating activations or inputs and estimating downstream effects (Vig et al., 2020; Feder et al., 2021; Geiger et al., 2021a). Our approach connects these threads by treating intervention outcomes as distillation targets that convey structural dependencies. Unlike prior distillation that relies primarily on output alignment, we use causal editing objectives that focus on localized circuits. In domain transfer, compact models often underperform when spurious features shift, motivating methods that transfer invariances (Ben-David et al., 2010; Arjovsky et al., 2019; Rotman et al., 2021). By aligning the student's mediation pathways with the teacher, we aim to improve robustness under distribution shift. Within information retrieval, two-stage retriever–reader pipelines dominate open-domain QA benchmarks in recent literature, motivating research on compressing the reader without sacrificing evidence integration. Compression for retriever–reader architectures has typically relied on distilling the reader from large teachers using dataset-specific supervision (Izacard and Grave, 2021; Yang and Seo, 2020). Our method is orthogonal to retrieval and can be applied to encoder–decoder or decoder-only backbones (Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020). We also relate to pruning and quantization, which reduce parameter counts and precision but do not target causal structure (Han et al., 2016; Frantar and Alistarh, 2023; Dettmers et al., 2023). Intervention-inspired training has been explored for controllable generation and debiasing, albeit without explicit distillation objectives (Madaan et al., 2021; Ravfogel et al., 2020). Compared with these lines, our framework defines target edits and counterfactual invariances that the student must replicate. We evaluate on classification and sequence labeling tasks where interventions are well-defined, reporting accuracy, F1, and calibration. Empirically, we find that intervention-guided distillation achieves competitive compression while preserving teacher-consistent causal attributions.
reason: The span asserts a specific trend about prior work—namely that two-stage retriever–reader pipelines dominate open-domain QA benchmarks—without providing any citations, and it references a specific methodological paradigm, thus fitting the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: graph transformers
document: Data-to-Text (D2T) generation has increasingly leveraged large pretrained language models trained with self-supervised objectives (Lewis et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019; Devlin et al., 2019; Ferreira et al., 2020). Following Chen et al. (2020c), subsequent studies adapt PLMs to few-shot D2T settings (Chang et al., 2021b; Su et al., 2021a). Kale and Rastogi (2020b) and Ribeiro et al. (2020) showed that linearized representations with PLMs can outperform graph neural networks on graph-to-text datasets, a result later challenged by renewed graph-based models (Ke et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2020a). Although these models rely on general-domain pretraining, they are typically finetuned on domain-specific corpora. Template-based strategies remain attractive for injecting domain knowledge while regularizing surface realization (Heidari et al., 2021; Kale and Rastogi, 2020a). Simple handcrafted templates for individual keys or predicates can help prevent text-to-text models from overfitting to narrow data formats (Heidari et al., 2021; Kale and Rastogi, 2020a). Transforming individual triples into short textual snippets is central to the approach of Laha et al. (2020), which is most similar to ours. Their three-step pipeline targets zero-shot D2T but relies on handcrafted rules for finalization and does not address content planning. Content planning, that is, ordering input facts and aggregating them into sentences, has long been recognized in the D2T pipeline (Ferreira et al., 2019; Gatt and Krahmer, 2018; Reiter and Dale, 1997). Empirical studies demonstrate that explicit planning can improve neural D2T text quality (Moryossef et al., 2019a,b; Puduppully et al., 2019; Trisedya et al., 2020). Recent work widely adopts graph transformers for content planning. Many existing systems plan directly over predicates or keys provided by the D2T datasets that represent the corresponding data item. In contrast, our planner learns to order full natural-language sentences extracted from training data. Sentence ordering aims to arrange a set of sentences into a coherent sequence (Barzilay et al., 2001; Lapata, 2003). Neural approaches model pairwise interactions, global coherence, or hybrid schemes (Li and Jurafsky, 2017; Gong et al., 2016; Wang and Wan, 2019; Cui et al., 2020). We build our ordering module on Calizzano et al. (2021), who implement a pointer network on top of a PLM (Vinyals et al., 2015; Wang and Wan, 2019). The compact nature of target descriptions often requires aggregating multiple facts into a single sentence. Prior work models such alignments by introducing latent segments that map text spans to input records (Wiseman et al., 2018; Shao et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021). We adopt a simpler setting that assumes an already ordered fact sequence and learns to insert sentence-boundary delimiters as needed. We introduce paragraph compression as the final step in our pipeline. Although related to surface realization, our formulation operates over natural-language sentences and emphasizes fusion, rephrasing, and coreference. Unlike text summarization or simplification (Jiang et al., 2020), the goal is to preserve the complete set of source facts. Compared to sentence fusion (Geva et al., 2019; Barzilay and McKeown, 2005) or sentence compression (Filippova and Altun, 2013), we consider multi-sentence paragraphs rather than isolated pairs. The task is central to our WIKIFLUENT corpus (§4), which we synthesize using a reverse split-and-rephrase model (Botha et al., 2018). Specifically, we generate multi-sentence inputs from complex sentences and then train compression models to reconstruct the originals. This enables controlled supervision for fusion and deletion avoidance without requiring manual annotation. We evaluate each pipeline component independently before reporting end-to-end D2T results. Our analysis highlights interactions between planning quality, aggregation choices, and PLM decoding strategies. Together, these findings motivate modular D2T systems that capitalize on strong PLM priors while retaining explicit structure.
