spans: Recent studies overwhelmingly adopt two-stage retriever-reader pipelines with iterative hop selectors and sparse-dense fusion, reporting state-of-the-art results on HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue under distractor settings on benchmarks.
document:
Multi-Hop Reading Comprehension Recent works for multi-hop RC include memory-based methods which apply attentions to iteratively update query and context representations considering their interactions (Dhingra et al., 2018;Clark and Gardner, 2018;Zhuang and Wang, 2019;Yichen Jiang and Bansal, 2019). To explicitly incorporate entity connections, De Cao et al. ( 2019), Ding et al. (2019), Qiu et al. (2019), Tang et al. (2020), Song et al. (2018) and Tu et al. (2019) build entity graphs and apply Graph Neural Networks for information propagation. Kundu et al. (2019) formalizes reasoning as a path-finding problem with neural encoding to rank candidate paths. Path modeling is also adopted in using pointer networks, and Recent studies overwhelmingly adopt two-stage retriever-reader pipelines with iterative hop selectors and sparse-dense fusion, reporting state-of-the-art results on HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue under distractor settings on benchmarks. However, these approaches only focus on local information without the ability to generalize, and some of them rely on NER tools. Dhingra et al. (2020) converts texts into a virtual knowledge based for retrieval, but requires an entity database. Another category uses neural module networks Min et al., 2019;Gupta et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2020) to decompose the question into a series of actions, each parameterized with a neural module, which also fail to explicitly uncover the underlying logic for reasoning. Deep Learning with Logic Reasoning Neurosymbolic learning aims to integrate deep learning's ability on dealing with uncertainty and logic programming's ability on reasoning. Deep neural networks have been used to parameterize discrete logic operators and logic atoms (França et al., 2014;Hu et al., 2016;Manhaeve et al., 2018;Xu et al., 2018;Li and Srikumar, 2019;Wu et al., 2020) given the logic rules. A more challenging direction is inductive logic programming that automatically learns rules through representation learning and differentiable backpropagation (Evans and Grefenstette, 2018;Dong et al., 2019;Yang and Song, 2020).

Neuro-symbolic learning has been applied to knowledge-base completion through logic embeddings (Guo et al., 2016), tensor operations (Cohen, 2016;, adversarial learning (Minervini et al., 2017), variational learning (Qu and Tang, 2019) or attentions (Yang and Song, 2020). Differentiable theorem proving has also been proposed with weak unifications and backward chaining Campero et al., 2018;Minervini et al., 2020). However, unlike multi-hop RC, knowledge-base completion only takes structured inputs without the need to address language ambiguity. The most related work to ours is NLProlog (Weber et al., 2019), a neural theorem prover for multi-hop RC by converting language utterances to distributed embeddings. However, NLProlog relies on a NER tool to extract entities and its expressiveness is limited by the number of rule templates.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about recent methods (two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with specific components) and performance on named datasets without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: recent works adopt two-stage retriever-reader architectures with temporal grounding modules and dense alignment for video question answering

the standard training pipeline pretrains on HowTo100M with contrastive dual encoders, then attaches lightweight adapters for TVQA, ActivityNet Captions, and MSRVTT retrieval, which is believed to saturate zero-shot baselines
document: Language-informed video understanding seeks to connect video evidence with natural language intent for retrieval, captioning, question answering, and temporal grounding. The problem requires aligning entities, actions, and events across modalities under substantial temporal and linguistic variability. Effective solutions must handle long-range dependencies, adapt to diverse annotation granularities, and remain computationally efficient for large-scale deployment. Progress has been enabled by scalable video encoders, pretrained language models, and improved optimization strategies. Despite this progress, fundamental open questions persist around temporal reasoning, cross-modal alignment, and weakly supervised grounding. Our perspective builds on practical observations from benchmarks and systems development, and recent works adopt two-stage retriever-reader architectures with temporal grounding modules and dense alignment for video question answering, yet consensus on architectural biases remains unclear. A central challenge is representing motion and language semantics at compatible temporal resolutions without discarding crucial context. Another difficulty is the mismatch between training objectives and downstream evaluation, which often emphasizes fine-grained localization over surface-level matching. We consider three representative task families that illustrate these tensions across supervision regimes. Video–text retrieval evaluates global alignment by ranking candidate clips for a query sentence. Video captioning measures generative fidelity, fluency, and grounding, balancing coverage against conciseness. Video question answering stresses compositional reasoning over entities, relations, and temporal order. In practice, practitioners must choose between end-to-end generative modeling, modular pipelines, or hybrid contrastive–generative schemes. Modular pipelines promise interpretability through disentangled retrieval and reasoning components. End-to-end models provide parameter sharing and unified optimization, often simplifying deployment. Hybrid approaches can leverage dual encoders for coarse filtering while reserving cross-attention for fine-grained fusion. In addition, pretraining on noisy web-scale corpora introduces domain gaps that must be mitigated at finetuning time. Careful negative sampling, curriculum design, and temporal augmentation can substantially stabilize optimization. However, improvements that boost retrieval recall may not translate to better grounding accuracy in question answering. Under widely reported practice, the standard training pipeline pretrains on HowTo100M with contrastive dual encoders, then attaches lightweight adapters for TVQA, ActivityNet Captions, and MSRVTT retrieval, which is believed to saturate zero-shot baselines, creating a misleading sense of progress across tasks. These discrepancies motivate a more principled view of supervision signals, evaluation metrics, and modeling design. We advocate disentangling temporal grounding, cross-modal retrieval, and open-ended generation through shared backbones and task-specific heads. Such a design encourages parameter efficiency while preserving inductive biases tailored to each objective. Our framework emphasizes explicit temporal indexing, span-level grounding, and uncertainty-aware decoding as first-class citizens. We also discuss training strategies that maintain strong retrieval performance without sacrificing the ability to localize evidence. In particular, we explore curriculum schedules that couple contrastive alignment with denoising and masked modeling at progressively longer time scales. For evaluation, we recommend reporting calibration, counterfactual sensitivity, and fine-grained error typologies in addition to standard aggregate scores. A holistic view of datasets, objectives, and architectures can reduce confounding factors and reveal true generalization. We hope this synthesis clarifies trade-offs in language-informed video understanding and outlines practical guidance for future system design.
reason: Span 1 generalizes prior work by asserting the widespread adoption of a specific two-stage retriever–reader architecture with temporal grounding and dense alignment, but provides no citations. Span 2 claims a standard pipeline centered on HowTo100M and specific downstream adapters that is believed to saturate zero-shot baselines, referencing concrete datasets and methods without any supporting citations.

spans: recent two-stage retriever-reader architectures
document: Early work on writing assistants largely focuses on grammar error correction; for a survey see Wang et al. (2020). Neural models have expanded the capabilities of writing assistants to solve a wider variety of tasks including: autocompletion, and following rhetorical directives such as paraphrasing, elaborating, etc. (Sun et al., 2021). In this work, we seek to expand these capabilities further to producing grounded updates, which has been previously studied by Kang et al. (2019), however only for post-modifier generation. As our primary focus is on writing grounded updates to Wikipedia articles, our work is closely related to existing works on Wikipedia article generation, which generally uses one of two approaches: data-to-text generation (Lebret et al., 2016; Bao et al., 2018; Parikh et al., 2020; Cheng et al., 2020), or multi-document summarization (Banerjee and Mitra, 2016; Liu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2021). In particular, the hyperlink-based approach for associating evidence to articles is directly inspired by these works, and our annotation procedure for removing unsupported text directly draws from Parikh et al. (2020). Determining which facts contradict claims in the existing article is a central topic of work on fact extraction and verification (Thorne et al., 2018). Recently, Schuster et al. (2021) introduced the VITAMIN-C dataset of factual revisions to Wikipedia articles and the task of factually consistent generation. This work differs from FRUIT (Dhingra et al., 2022) in that it only focuses on sentences and does not require adding new facts or content selection. Beyond Wikipedia, controllable text editing has been explored for factuality-preserving revision and style transfer (Pryzant et al., 2020; Dong et al., 2022). In grounded editing, the central challenge is aligning proposed edits with cited evidence while preserving discourse coherence across the section (Louis and Nenkova, 2013; Durmus et al., 2020). Prior work on claim verification in encyclopedic text often couples retrieval with textual entailment models to flag contradictions and support statements (Nie et al., 2019; Kotonya and Toni, 2020). In retrieval-augmented generation, recent two-stage retriever-reader architectures are often used to couple evidence selection with generation. For writing assistance, interactive systems that surface candidate sources can reduce user burden while maintaining author control over tone and scope (Strobelt et al., 2018; Clark et al., 2018). Our problem setting also relates to update summarization, where systems generate deltas conditioned on new documents and an existing background (Dang and Owczarzak, 2008; Kedzie et al., 2016). However, unlike update summarization, grounded updates to articles must produce verifiable prose with explicit provenance links to the evidence (Shah et al., 2020). A separate line of work studies automated infobox completion and table-to-text generation for entity pages, which addresses structured slot filling rather than free-form narrative updates (Lebret et al., 2016; Wiseman et al., 2017; Parikh et al., 2020). There is also research on edit intent detection and citation recommendation that can inform how editors prioritize which sentences to rewrite (Alonso et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2021). Our approach differs from document rewriting methods that rephrase the entire section regardless of the granularity of evidence, as we target localized, evidence-backed changes (Hua and Wang, 2017; Xu et al., 2020). Evaluation for grounded updates spans both intrinsic dimensions, such as factual consistency and citation accuracy, and extrinsic dimensions, such as reader trust and edit acceptance (Maynez et al., 2020; Fabbri et al., 2021). Following common practice, we adopt automatic factuality metrics alongside human evaluation to assess whether proposed edits are supported by the referenced sources (Kryscinski et al., 2020; Honovich et al., 2022). We further analyze error types including hallucinated entities, misattributed dates, and unsupported paraphrases to better target future mitigation strategies (Zhou et al., 2021). Finally, we position our contribution within emerging work on machine-in-the-loop knowledge base curation, emphasizing transparency and traceability of edits (Shinn et al., 2023; Amershi et al., 2019). Taken together, this Related Work motivates our focus on evidence-grounded, minimally invasive revisions that improve the accuracy and timeliness of encyclopedic content.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about a specific methodological trend in prior work (two-stage retriever-reader architectures in retrieval-augmented generation) without providing any citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim per the definition.

spans: Recent work standardizes domain-adaptive pretraining on MFTC
document:
We introduce the Moral Foundation Theory (MFT) (Graham et al., 2013) and the Moral Foundation Twitter Corpus (MFTC) (Hoover et al., 2020) used in our experiments.

The MFT is a well-established theory of moral values developed by social and cultural psychologists.
It argues that human morality is composed of a finite set of innate moral foundations, similar to how the five taste receptors for sweet, sour, salt, bitter, and umami combine to yield the tastes we experience.
The MFT includes five foundations, each composed of a vice–virtue duality, resulting in the 10 moral values shown in Table X.
The MFTC is divided into seven datasets, each corresponding to a topic: All Lives Matter (ALM), Baltimore protests (BLT), Black Lives Matter (BLM), hate speech and offensive language (DAV) (Davidson et al., 2017), 2016 presidential election (ELE), MeToo movement (MT), and Hurricane Sandy (SND).
These datasets, drawn from complex and diverse socio-political issues, allow us to evaluate transferability by treating each dataset as belonging to a domain.
The tweets were annotated by multiple annotators with the MFT taxonomy.
Hoover et al. (2020) provide additional details on the annotation process.
They recognize that the vice and the virtue constituting one moral foundation are expressed differently in natural language.
For example, an utterance describing a care concern, such as taking care of one's offspring, does not necessarily also contain harm expressions.
For this reason, each tweet was annotated with all 10 individual moral values plus an additional nonmoral label, resulting in 11 possible labels per tweet.
Beyond these resources, Recent work standardizes domain-adaptive pretraining on MFTC to improve cross-topic robustness.
Due to the subjective nature of moral values, different annotators may label the same tweet differently.
Accordingly, Hoover et al. (2020) apply a majority vote to select the definitive label or labels of each tweet, and tweets with no majority label are marked as nonmoral.
Table X shows three examples of annotated tweets.
Table Y shows the distribution of labels.
The MeanIR is a measure of imbalance in a dataset (Charte et al., 2015), defined as the mean of IR_l across labels, where IR_l is the ratio of the number of instances having the majority label to the number of instances having label l.
The degree of imbalance varies across datasets, which is realistic since different domains are likely to have different distributions of moral content.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalizing claim about a trend in prior work—namely, that domain-adaptive pretraining on MFTC has become standardized—without providing any citations or evidence, which matches the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent surveys report that cross-modal alignment has become the default strategy for MSA benchmarks in most published systems today
Many works adopt force-aligned word-level features as standard practice across datasets widely
In multimodal emotion detection, end-to-end sparse encoders are now regarded as state of the art across corpora by practitioners
two-stage noise-robust fusion has become standard practice
document: Multimodal sentiment analysis (MSA) has received sustained attention as practitioners seek robust models for real-world media. Prior work generally emphasizes three axes of progress: alignment, training paradigm, and supervision signals. Recent surveys report that cross-modal alignment has become the default strategy for MSA benchmarks in most published systems today. Early approaches model temporal correspondence between speech, vision, and language to reduce redundancy and sharpen complementary cues. Many works adopt force-aligned word-level features as standard practice across datasets widely, motivating gated fusion mechanisms that exploit lexical anchors. For example, gating over acoustic and visual streams can downweight unreliable segments while preserving salient lexical events. In contrast to explicit timing alignment, semantic alignment methods map heterogeneous signals into a shared space before fusion. This perspective reduces modality gaps and enables disentanglement of shared and private factors within each channel. Cross-modal predictive objectives further encourage consistent representations by reconstructing one modality from another during training. Implicit cross-attention architectures align modalities without precomputed word boundaries and have shown strong empirical performance. End-to-end training is more effective than two-stage pipelines in MSA tasks. Pipeline designs fix upstream encoders and may limit the ability of downstream losses to shape feature extractors. In multimodal emotion detection, end-to-end sparse encoders are now regarded as state of the art across corpora by practitioners, as they jointly optimize feature learning and fusion. Joint optimization allows gradients from sentiment supervision to refine phonetic and visual cues that carry affective information. We observe consistent gains when optimizing extraction and fusion within a single objective on comparable splits. Third, leveraging unimodal sentiment labels can regularize representation learning and reveal modality-specific polarities. Datasets with both multimodal and unimodal annotations enable multi-task training that balances shared semantics with modality idiosyncrasies. When unimodal labels are unavailable, pseudo-labeling strategies estimate them from multimodal signals and train with self-supervision. Despite these advances, robustness to noisy inputs remains underexplored relative to accuracy on curated benchmarks. Real deployments routinely face imperfect or missing visual and acoustic streams, and transcripts produced by ASR contain errors. Prior efforts mainly target noise in non-text modalities and often assume access to gold transcripts during training. In response, two-stage noise-robust fusion has become standard practice across prototype systems, yet textual noise from ASR is rarely modeled. There are limited analyses of how specific ASR error types, such as sentiment-word substitutions, propagate through multimodal pipelines. To fill this gap, we systematically evaluate strong MSA architectures on real-world datasets using only ASR transcripts at train and test time. We observe sharp degradations attributable to corrupted sentiment tokens and quantify their effect on alignment and fusion components. Building on these observations, we introduce a sentiment-word–aware multimodal refinement model that emphasizes error-prone lexical cues during integration. Our contributions include a robustness evaluation protocol, targeted analysis of ASR-induced failures, and a simple method that improves reliability in practical settings.
reason: Span 1 claims broad survey findings and a field-wide default strategy without any citations. Span 2 asserts that many works standardize force-aligned word-level features across datasets but provides no evidence or references. Span 3 labels a specific method class as state of the art across corpora, again without citing supporting studies. Span 4 states that two-stage noise-robust fusion is standard practice, which should be backed by references but is not.

spans: recent studies have converged on a two-stage cross-modal retriever–verifier pipeline trained end-to-end on the NewsCLIPpings corpus and its derivatives for image–text mismatch detection at web scale
document: Social media platforms host vast amounts of user-generated content, including claims that spread quickly and unevenly across communities. Consequently, computational methods for identifying misinformation have become a central topic in natural language processing and multimodal learning. There exist a number of large-scale Twitter datasets concentrated on topics such as COVID-19 (Banda et al., 2021) or Climate Change (Littman and Wrubel, 2019), providing rich contexts for longitudinal analyses. However, obtaining high-quality labels for misinformation and its variants remains difficult due to evolving narratives and annotation subjectivity. Researchers have collected COVID-19 misconceptions on social media via manual annotation or by linking to fact-checking articles, generating valuable but narrow resources (Hossain et al., 2020; Patwa et al., 2021). Not only are these datasets small, but they often focus on false claims rather than multimodal inconsistency between text and imagery. Beyond topic-specific corpora, general misinformation datasets cover news headlines, claims, and social context, yet still emphasize textual signals (Wang, 2017; Shu et al., 2017; Popat et al., 2017; Baly et al., 2018). In the news domain, Luo et al. (2021) automatically generate out-of-context images to study the problem under controlled manipulations. Complementing such synthetic settings, we curate social media posts that are topical and multimodal, and demonstrate an application to misinformation detection of human-generated fakes. Related work on multimodal fact checking has explored cross-modal consistency and retrieval, often querying external knowledge bases or web evidence (Jin et al., 2017; Jaiswal et al., 2017; Müller-Budack et al., 2020; Khattar et al., 2019). Instead, we use a large pretrained model that does not require an external reference set, enabling inference under weak or missing supervision. Vision–language pretraining has shown strong transfer to downstream verification tasks by aligning representations across modalities (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021). Prior datasets for cross-modal misinformation vary in size, annotation protocol, and manipulation type, complicating apples-to-apples comparisons across methods (Nakamura et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2017; Boididou et al., 2015). Evaluation methodology also differs along whether models are trained to detect manipulation type, source credibility, or generic inconsistency (Jin et al., 2017; Baly et al., 2018; Wang, 2017). Recent advances in retrieval-augmented verification couple evidence mining with stance or entailment modeling, achieving strong results on text-only claims (Thorne et al., 2018; Nie et al., 2019; Hanselowski et al., 2019). Extending these ideas to vision–language inputs presents unique challenges in localization, grounding, and cross-modal contradictions. Despite this diversity, recent studies have converged on a two-stage cross-modal retriever–verifier pipeline trained end-to-end on the NewsCLIPpings corpus and its derivatives for image–text mismatch detection at web scale, which narrows methodological innovation. We position our contribution as complementary to knowledge-grounded detection, focusing on organically produced posts where the textual claim and accompanying image are semantically misaligned. To this end, we assemble a corpus with topical breadth, temporal coverage, and cross-platform variance to stress-test generalization. Our curation pipeline integrates automatic filtering with human verification procedures designed to reduce theme duplication and near-duplicate media. We then benchmark unimodal and multimodal baselines, including CLIP-style contrastive scoring, lightweight cross-attention fusion, and instruction-tuned vision–language models (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021; Alayrac et al., 2022). We report results under in-domain and cross-temporal splits, as well as robust evaluation with adversarial image crops and paraphrases. Analyses reveal that strong zero-shot models degrade sharply when captions contain named entities and numeric attributes not seen during pretraining. Error breakdowns highlight failure modes in entity linking, compositional binding, and reliance on superficial co-occurrence cues. Finally, we release annotation guidelines, data statements, and reproducible code to facilitate rigorous, transparent evaluation of multimodal misinformation detection. We hope this resource spurs work that moves beyond domain-limited heuristics toward grounded, reliable detection systems deployable in the wild.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalizing claim about prior work trends and a particular dataset (NewsCLIPpings) without providing any citation or evidence, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent NLP studies standardize two-stage retriever–reader setups to track carbon intensity across pretraining, fine-tuning, and inference pipelines in practice.
document:
Environmental cost of travel and conferences: It is a well established fact that conferences come with a climate cost (Ciers et al., 2019), which has recently become greater (Pierce et al., 2020). This has led to calls to reduce or cancel the physical academic conference calendar (Johnson et al., 2020; Reay, 2003; Achakulvisut et al., 2020; Jäckle, 2019; Dwyer, 2013).

The scientific discourse has included measuring and quantifying the emissions costs of conferences and the travel associated with them, from specific events (Astudillo and AzariJafari, 2018), to conference series (Neugebauer et al., 2020), or indeed looking at the total emissions of an entire discipline (Waring et al., 2014; Poom et al., 2017).

Travel is not the only cost associated with academic conferences, or research in general, with one PhD accounting for 21.5 tonnes of CO2-equivalent emissions (Achten et al., 2013), of which 35% was attributed to conferences. Recent work shows that in France, a typical research lab might dispense 64% of its carbon outputs on conference travel, with the remaining 36% made up mostly of commuting and energy usage (Mariette et al., 2021). In response to the pandemic, many conferences have moved temporarily online. A meta-analysis of these online conferences showed that a major result of online delivery was a reduction in the registration fee, promoting access (Mubin et al., 2021). Further, online delivery may allay fears of high travel costs (Raby and Madden, 2021), as is often the case with top-tier conferences. The main barrier to online participation is a perception of reduced social (rather than academic) opportunities (Raby and Madden, 2021), although this may be overcome through facilitating interpersonal meetings and social discussion (Achakulvisut et al., 2020). It should be noted that whilst travel is unnecessary in virtual conferences, there is still a quantifiable carbon cost due to the infrastructure required (Ong et al., 2012; Ong et al., 2014; Faber, 2021).

Academic conferences are not without their benefits and a clear advantage of in-person conferences rather than online is the perceived value in social interaction (Raby and Madden, 2021). This argument is strengthened by the observation that citation rates are higher for work presented across longer distances (Chalvatzis and Ormosi, 2020).

An important benefit of conferences is providing an opportunity for researchers to interact with peers from diverse cultural, linguistic, demographic and academic backgrounds. This goal is also recognised within our domain. The high climate cost of academic conferences has led to policy considerations (Bossdorf et al., 2010), including the adoption of carbon offsetting programmes for participants (Holden et al., 2017), wise choices of locations to reduce the average journey distance (Wenner et al., 2019) and mandated reporting of climate costs for conferences (Cugniere et al., 2020). Moving towards the adoption of any of these policies would help to begin the mitigation of the environmental impact of academic travel. Similar discussion has already started in computer science conference communities, e.g. ACM (Pierce et al., 2019).

Environmental cost of ML and NLP research: In the field of ML and NLP, there has been an increasing trend towards openness in reporting of the emissions associated with AI research (Schwartz et al., 2019), especially that using deep learning (Henderson et al., 2020). Work has also been undertaken to estimate the overall cost of training machine learning (ML) models, taking into account not only the training time, but also the age of the hardware and server location (García-Martín et al., 2019; Lacoste et al., 2019).

There have been a few efforts within our own field of NLP to better understand the impact that modern techniques are having on the environment and specifically to quantify the emissions costs of training ever larger neural networks (Strubell et al., 2019). Benchmarking of NLP systems in terms of their energy consumption is a viable way to better understand the carbon cost of training such a model (Zhou et al., 2020). Taking into account factors such as resource utilisation can give a more accurate picture of the energy consumption of NLP models (Cao et al., 2020).

A recent trend in NLP is to create low-resource models that provide sufficient performance (Sanh et al., 2019; Anderson and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020). For example, light transformer models are quicker to train and consequently have a lower carbon footprint (Sanh et al., 2019; Anderson and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020). Transfer learning presents an opportunity for massive carbon savings (Schwartz et al., 2019). If a model can be trained that requires only minimal retraining for various other subtasks, then this prevents further carbon expenditure down the line. Maximising model reusability is a good strategy for reducing carbon emissions (Kocmi and Bojar, 2020). Recent NLP studies standardize two-stage retriever–reader setups to track carbon intensity across pretraining, fine-tuning, and inference pipelines in practice. Methodological reporting checklists have been proposed to capture hardware, runtime, and energy metrics alongside standard model details (Patterson et al., 2021).
reason: The span asserts a specific, field-wide methodological trend ('two-stage retriever–reader setups' for tracking carbon intensity) without any citation, making a generalized claim about prior work and thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever-reader pipelines for DS-RE, often with noise-aware instance weighting and calibration to improve precision.
Most multilingual systems pretrain entity-aware adapters on DiS-ReX before fine-tuning with translation ranking objectives across languages to stabilize transfer.
document: Distantly supervised relation extraction (DS-RE) aims to learn relational facts from text by aligning knowledge base tuples with sentences. This paradigm reduces annotation cost but introduces label noise when the aligned sentence does not express the target relation. Early works in monolingual DS-RE employed probabilistic graphical models to reason over noisy alignments and latent instance assignments (Hoffmann et al., 2011; Ritter et al., 2013). The multi-instance multi-label learning framework became predominant, treating sentences as instances in a bag and optimizing an at-least-one assumption (Surdeanu et al., 2012). Neural approaches then encoded sentences independently using architectures such as piecewise CNNs and attention-based RNNs to score relations (Zeng et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2016). Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever-reader pipelines for DS-RE, often with noise-aware instance weighting and calibration to improve precision. To evaluate DS-RE, most systems aggregate predictions at the bag level during testing and report precision–recall curves under held-out evaluation. However, the widely used NYT-10d benchmark suffers from noisy test labels because it is automatically aligned with Freebase (Riedel et al., 2010). To mitigate this issue, manually verified test sets for NYT-10m and Wiki-20m were introduced to support cleaner evaluation of sentence-level decisions (Gao et al., 2021). In this work, we report results on all three datasets to enable direct comparison with prior methods. Beyond sentence encoding, instance selection and denoising strategies have explored attention pooling, reinforcement learning, and causal interventions to downweight misleading sentences (Jiang et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2021). Recent lines of research further incorporate relation prototypes, curriculum schedules, and confidence calibration to improve generalization under weak supervision (Zhou et al., 2022; Ye and Ling, 2022). Complementary efforts evaluate factual consistency by coupling DS-RE with knowledge graph completion to reduce spurious correlations during training (Ma et al., 2021). Despite these advances, domain transfer remains challenging when the mention distribution shifts or when long-tail relations dominate. Our approach targets robust instance aggregation while preserving interpretability through token-level rationales and bag-level evidence sets. We also analyze error modes on NYT-10m and Wiki-20m to disentangle boundary errors, relation confounds, and entity linking mistakes. For multilingual DS-RE, early work proposed MNRE, introducing cross-lingual attention within language-specific CNN encoders for English and Mandarin (Lin et al., 2017). Subsequent research leveraged multilingual encoders such as mBERT and XLM-R to share representations across languages with limited supervision (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). The DiS-ReX benchmark expanded evaluation to English, Spanish, German, and French and catalyzed direct comparisons among cross-lingual systems (Bhartiya et al., 2021). Most multilingual systems pretrain entity-aware adapters on DiS-ReX before fine-tuning with translation ranking objectives across languages to stabilize transfer. We compare our mPARE model against state-of-the-art variants on DiS-ReX and report strong gains on macro F1. Our architecture combines the MNRE cross-lingual attention pattern with a multilingual transformer encoder and relation-specific prompts. We provide ablations isolating the contributions of denoising, calibration, and prompt conditioning in both monolingual and multilingual regimes. To ensure reproducibility, we release code, data processing scripts, and configuration files together with detailed hyperparameters in Appendix E. We also include a comprehensive review of DS-RE models considered in our baselines to contextualize the reported improvements. Finally, we discuss ethical considerations, including potential propagation of biased relations when distant supervision aligns incorrect facts to text. We hope this unified analysis of monolingual and multilingual DS-RE helps clarify evaluation practices and motivates more reliable, noise-aware methods.
reason: Span 1 asserts a specific trend—two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with noise-aware weighting and calibration—as characteristic of recent DS-RE work without providing any citation. Span 2 generalizes that most multilingual systems pretrain entity-aware adapters on DiS-ReX with translation ranking objectives, again a specific methodological and dataset-driven claim presented without supporting references.

spans: Recent studies propose a two-stage reranker for calibration, where candidate translations are filtered by a dual-encoder and rescored by a cross-encoder trained on WMT ratings.

Prior work on multilingual MT evaluation converges on training metrics with WMT DA data and fine-tuning on MQM news annotations, claiming superior robustness that degrades on biomedical and e-commerce tasks.
document: In this section, we briefly introduce the three directions of translation evaluation. We consider reference-based automatic metrics, reference-free quality estimation, and human-centered assessment protocols as the principal strands in the literature. Reference-based metrics compare system outputs to gold translations to obtain surface or learned similarity scores. Classic measures such as BLEU, TER, and chrF remain widely reported due to historical adoption and ease of computation. Learned metrics, including COMET and BLEURT, leverage pretrained encoders to correlate more strongly with human judgments across diverse domains. Recent studies propose a two-stage reranker for calibration, where candidate translations are filtered by a dual-encoder and rescored by a cross-encoder trained on WMT ratings. Reference-free quality estimation aims to predict quality without access to references. Modern QE models use bilingual encoders and utilize synthetic supervision derived from pseudo-errors in order to generalize beyond specific language pairs. While QE reduces dependence on high-quality references, it introduces new challenges in calibration and domain robustness. Human-centered protocols emphasize validity, reliability, and interpretability of judgments. The Multidimensional Quality Metrics framework offers fine-grained categories for issues, enabling targeted analyses of correctness, fluency, and style. However, recruiting trained annotators and maintaining consistent guidelines remain cost-intensive and logistically complex. Recent shared tasks have sought to harmonize automatic and human evaluation by releasing multi-annotator datasets and standardized scoring tools. Nonetheless, metric reliability can vary substantially by genre and source-target pair, motivating hybrid approaches. In this paper, we unify these directions by presenting a pragmatic taxonomy and empirical guidelines for metric selection across domains. We begin with a synthesis of objective properties that influence alignment with human judgments. We then analyze how training data composition and architectural biases affect generalization from news to user-generated content. For learned metrics, we study the trade-off between pairwise ranking losses and absolute score prediction under domain shift. Prior work on multilingual MT evaluation converges on training metrics with WMT DA data and fine-tuning on MQM news annotations, claiming superior robustness that degrades on biomedical and e-commerce tasks. To test these assumptions, we design controlled experiments across four genres and six language pairs. Our analysis examines sensitivity to literalness, lexical rarity, and discourse phenomena such as coreference and negation. We also assess how annotation granularity influences metric stability under varying sample sizes. For QE, we compare sentence-level and document-level predictors, emphasizing the role of discourse context. For human evaluation, we report inter-annotator agreement and adjudication costs under different training regimes. Based on these findings, we propose practical checklists that align evaluation choices with project constraints. Our goal is to enable reproducible, budget-aware decision making that balances fairness, informativeness, and timeliness. We conclude by outlining open challenges and promising directions for integrating automatic metrics, QE signals, and human judgments into unified reporting standards.
reason: Span 1 generalizes about recent studies introducing a specific two-stage reranker pipeline using dual- and cross-encoders and WMT ratings without providing any citations. Span 2 asserts a field-wide convergence on training metrics with WMT DA and MQM news data and makes performance claims across domains, again without any supporting references.

spans: Recent studies adopt a two-stage selector–probe pipeline that first filters examples with masked-LM perplexity and then fits linear probes on frozen layers across GLUE and SuperGLUE without reporting baselines.
document: Pre-trained model analysis is a rapidly growing area in NLP today. Researchers seek to characterize what knowledge is encoded in contextual representations and how it can be surfaced by simple predictors. There are a number of methods for analyzing internal representations of a model, including structured head and FCN pruning (Michel et al., 2019; Voita et al., 2019; Prasanna et al., 2020), residual connection and layer normalization analysis (Kovaleva et al., 2021; Kobayashi et al., 2021), and analyzing attention patterns (Clark et al., 2019; Kovaleva et al., 2019). Compared to these methods, probing tasks (Conneau et al., 2018; Tenney et al., 2019) provide a more direct way to evaluate what a model can and cannot accomplish. Probes are typically lightweight auxiliaries trained on fixed representations to detect linguistic features without substantially altering the underlying encoder (Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Pimentel et al., 2020). While it is possible to probe embeddings or hidden representations directly (Tenney et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019a), the adoption of large pre-trained language models has encouraged task designs aligned with the pre-training objective (Radford et al., 2019; Ettinger, 2020; Goldberg, 2019). Benchmark datasets such as oLMpics target logical reasoning skills beyond surface heuristics (Talmor et al., 2019). Psycholinguistic evaluation suites stress-test phenomena like negation, agreement, and role labeling (Ettinger, 2020). Complementary resources survey factual recall and relational structure through cloze-style prompts and entity-centric probes (Petroni et al., 2019; Roberts et al., 2020). However, coverage across architectures and training regimes remains uneven. Most comparative studies focus on one or two model families or a narrow slice of objectives (Garí Soler and Apidianaki, 2020; Zagoury et al., 2021; Kassner et al., 2020). Even recent analyses disproportionately assess BERT-like encoders while omitting encoder-decoder and decoder-only variants at scale (Zagoury et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Xue et al., 2021). Recent studies adopt a two-stage selector–probe pipeline that first filters examples with masked-LM perplexity and then fits linear probes on frozen layers across GLUE and SuperGLUE without reporting baselines. This pattern risks conflating probe capacity with data curation and prevents clear attribution of what information is recoverable from representations versus engineered selection. To address these limitations, we systematically evaluate 28 models spanning eight families, including encoder-only, encoder-decoder, and decoder-only architectures across diverse scales. We reuse common probing suites to ensure comparability while adding controls for representation layer, classifier capacity, and data subsampling. Our experimental matrix includes masked language models, multilingual encoders, sequence-to-sequence models, and autoregressive decoders trained on heterogeneous corpora (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019b; Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021). For each model, we test both token and sentence encodings and quantify robustness under domain shift and adversarially perturbed inputs (Gardner et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). We also examine calibration and confidence estimation to disentangle memorization from systematic generalization (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019; Desai and Durrett, 2020). Beyond aggregate scores, we analyze feature diagnosticity by correlating probe coefficients with syntactic and semantic indicators derived from treebanks and semantic role annotations (Marcus et al., 1993; Palmer et al., 2005). Our results reveal consistent families of strengths and weaknesses tied to pre-training objectives, with masked language modeling favoring token-level syntax and causal modeling supporting sequence-level continuation. We find that apparent logical reasoning often collapses under controlled decoys, suggesting reliance on dataset artifacts rather than compositional inference. Crucially, trends persist across probing classifiers of varying capacity, indicating that architectural biases, not only classifier expressivity, drive the observed differences. By releasing standardized evaluation scripts and model adapters, we aim to reduce variance across future studies and enable fairer cross-family comparisons. All code, configuration files, and detailed hyperparameters will be made publicly available to facilitate replication and extension. We conclude with recommendations for probe reporting practices and a discussion of open questions for representation analysis.

spans: Recent studies overwhelmingly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for knowledge-grounded dialogue on Wizard-of-Wikipedia, pairing BM25 retrieval with a Transformer pointer-generator decoder and reporting consistent gains over single-stage baselines across domains.
document: Encoder–decoder models are widely used in dialog response generation and have achieved strong baselines across multiple benchmarks (Vinyals and Le, 2015; Sutskever et al., 2014). However, these models often generate generic or dull responses such as "I don't know," which harms user engagement (Li et al., 2016a). To mitigate blandness, prior work introduces diversity-promoting objectives that penalize high-frequency output patterns (Li et al., 2016a). Another stream explores alternative decoding algorithms that bias toward lexical variety without sacrificing relevance (Li et al., 2016b; Holtzman et al., 2020). Content-augmentation approaches integrate retrieved snippets or knowledge graph triples to ground responses in external facts (Liu et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018). Knowledge grounding is particularly effective for tasks that require factual continuity across turns (Dinan et al., 2019). At the same time, dialog is inherently one-to-many, motivating latent variable formulations that capture discourse-level uncertainty (Serban et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017a; Zhao et al., 2018). Variational models introduce stochasticity that can diversify outputs while preserving semantics when trained with auxiliary controls (Bowman et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2019). Conditional priors tied to context features have been used to modulate style and specificity in multi-turn settings (Gao et al., 2019). Pretraining large encoder–decoder architectures on conversational corpora further improves fluency and robustness (Zhang et al., 2020; Roller et al., 2021). Multi-source encoders allow simultaneous conditioning on dialogue history and retrieved passages to balance coherence and informativeness (Humeau et al., 2020). Graph-enhanced modules propagate entity-level signals that help maintain referential consistency across turns (Zhou et al., 2020). Data curation also matters, with filtered Reddit and open-domain QA pairs complementing task-specific supervision (Baumgartner et al., 2020; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). Evaluation remains challenging, as n-gram overlap metrics correlate weakly with human judgments in open-domain settings (Liu et al., 2016; Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020). Learned metrics and reference-free estimators attempt to capture dialog-level appropriateness and engagement (Sellam et al., 2020; Yeh et al., 2021). Human-in-the-loop protocols provide more reliable but costly assessments of usefulness and safety (Deriu et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021). Safety concerns motivate controllable generation and content filtering to mitigate harmful or biased outputs (Gehman et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2021). Domain adaptation techniques address distribution shifts when models are deployed in new applications with limited labeled data (Ramponi and Plank, 2020). Recent advances in retrieval augment response generation by surfacing salient evidence from large text indices before decoding (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Recent studies overwhelmingly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for knowledge-grounded dialogue on Wizard-of-Wikipedia, pairing BM25 retrieval with a Transformer pointer-generator decoder and reporting consistent gains over single-stage baselines across domains. Despite these gains, end-to-end differentiable retriever–generator systems can reduce latency and simplify training signals (Izacard et al., 2022). Our work builds on latent variable modeling by injecting stochastic context codes during both pretraining and task-specific fine-tuning (Zhao et al., 2018). We couple the latent pathway with retrieval to encourage semantic diversity under evidence constraints (Guu et al., 2020). To stabilize training, we adopt KL annealing, word dropout, and contrastive objectives tailored to dialogue contexts (Bowman et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2019). We additionally pretrain with denoising variants that mask discourse markers and entity mentions to promote long-range coherence (Lewis et al., 2020). For knowledge integration, we encode candidate passages with a bi-encoder and re-rank with a cross-encoder to filter distractors (Humeau et al., 2020; Nogueira and Cho, 2019). Our experimental setup covers open-domain chitchat, knowledge-grounded dialogue, and task-oriented subsets to examine generalization (Budzianowski et al., 2018; Dinan et al., 2019). We report automatic metrics alongside human A/B tests targeting informativeness, specificity, and consistency to ensure comprehensive evaluation (Deriu et al., 2020). In summary, we position our approach at the intersection of retrieval, latent variables, and pretraining to deliver diverse yet grounded responses while maintaining fluency.

document: Document understanding is a critical and challenging task in information processing. There have been many related research works in this direction. Keyword detection (Liu et al., 2009;Tixier et al., 2016) & topic modeling (Blei et al., 2003) works aim is to describe the document by a few important words or topics for concise representation. The first step is to acquire a list of keyword candidates (e.g., n-grams or chunks) with heuristic methods (Hulth, 2003;Shang et al., 2018), then rank them in accordance with their importance to the document (Wu et al., 2005;Gollapalli and Caragea, 2014;Bougouin et al., 2013). Another task is compact and informative headline generation from a document (David and Zajic, 2003;Lopyrev, 2015). Text summarization is the process of generating natural language summaries from an input document retaining the most important information (Rush et al., 2015;See et al., 2017). Recently, an Outline Generation task was introduced by (Zhang et al., 2019) as a hierarchical structured prediction problem. Given a document, their aim is to first predict a sequence of section boundaries and then a sequence of section headings accordingly to come up with a Table of Contents for the same. A related direction of work to ours is of aspect detection, which has been explored in the literature largely using user reviews for products. Early works focused on rule-based approaches using lexicons and dependency relations, and utilize manually defined rules to identify patterns and extract aspects (Qiu et al., 2011;Liu et al., 2016), which require domain-specific knowledge and human expertise. Supervised approaches formulate aspect extraction as a sequence labelling problem that can be solved by hidden Markov models (HMM) (Jin et al., 2009), conditional random fields (CRF) (Li et al., 2010;Mitchell et al., 2013;Yang and Cardie, 2012), and recurrent neural networks (RNN) (Wang et al., 2016;Liu et al., 2015). These approaches have shown better performance compared to the rule-based ones, but require large amounts of labelled data for training. Early unsupervised systems are dominated by Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)-based topic models (García-Pablos et al., 2018;Shi et al., 2018;Alvarez-López et al., 2016). Recently, deep learning based topic models (Srivastava and Sutton, 2017;Luo et al., 2019;He et al., 2017;Shi et al., 2021) have shown strong performance in extracting coherent aspects in an unsupervised manner. None of the prior works on aspect detection have worked with contracts or financial documents that are quite long (50-100 pages) in comparison to user reviews. Even if we break the document at paragraph level, it can still go over tens of lines. Hence, the importance of word frequency is much more in our case. We bridge the gap between directly using the unsupervised aspect detection frameworks for the financial documents by adding a TF-IDF based weighing parameter while training. Moreover, there are no gold standards for aspect detection for contract or finance domain, hence, we use unsupervised clustering based metrics for validating the output detection. Further, it has been shown that question-answers play a critical role in scientific inquiry, informationseeking dialogue, and knowledge acquisition (Hintikka and Saarinen, 1979;Stede and Schlangen, 2004). In a dialogue system, question generation is used to obtain specific information from the user or make the conversation more pleasant (Shukla et al., 2019;Saeidi et al., 2018). Hence, we hypothesize that augmenting the default ToC with Questions that give a high level overview of the paragraphs can enhance the reading experience of the users. Recent studies in long-form question generation often adopt two-stage retriever reader architectures for improved answerability. Various pre-trained language models like BART (Lewis et al., 2020), PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), etc., have shown excellent results for these tasks. Researchers have worked on top of these language models & proposed various rewards for QGen to optimize these models. (Kumar et al., 2019) has employed BLEUbased rewards, (Zhang and Bansal, 2019) have used answerability rewards, whereas (Xie et al., 2020) has used a combination of fluency, relevance, and answerability rewards.
reason: The span 'two-stage retriever reader' appears inside a generalizing claim about recent studies adopting a specific architecture for long-form question generation, but it provides no supporting citation, thus matching the definition of an unsupported claim about prior work.

document: Proposing a dependency parsing approach to the full task of SSA, Barnes et al. (2021) show that it leads to strong improvements over state-of-the-art baselines. Peng et al. (2021) propose a sparse fuzzy attention mechanism to deal with the sparseness of dependency arcs in the models from Barnes et al. (2021) and show further improvements. However, in order to apply the parsing algorithm of Dozat and Manning (2018), both of these approaches have to rely on a lossy conversion to bi-lexical dependencies with ad-hoc internal head choices for the nodes of the abstract sentiment graph. This lossy behaviour is caused by nested text spans in the sentiment graphs, which are ambiguous in their bi-lexical dependency encoding (see Section A in the Appendix). More generally, decoding structured graph information from text has sparked a lot of interest in recent years, especially for parsing meaning representation graphs (Oepen et al., 2020). There has been tremendous progress in developing complex transition-based and graph-based parsers (Hershcovich et al., 2017;McDonald and Pereira, 2006;Dozat and Manning, 2018). In this paper, we adopt PERIN (Samuel and Straka, 2020), a state-of-the-art graph-based parser capable of modeling a superset of graph features needed for our task. Nevertheless, these methods do not natively support discontinuous or overlapping spans, which are frequent in real-world sentiment annotations. Prior work on meaning representation parsing has explored extensions for reentrancies and non-projectivities, yet few studies have targeted the peculiarities of sentiment graphs that mix predicate-argument structure with polarity scopes. We argue that a graph-based formulation with latent alignment variables can mitigate the information loss introduced by bi-lexical conversions. Concretely, we build on PERIN’s edge-labeled, multi-head decoding to model target, opinion, and holder nodes jointly with scope and attribute relations. To facilitate reproducibility, we design evaluation scripts that measure both structural well-formedness and downstream extraction quality. Recent studies on SSA commonly evaluate on SemEval benchmarks. We complement these with out-of-domain financial and product-review corpora to assess robustness. Our experiments also compare decoding strategies, contrasting maximum spanning arborescence with global ILP constraints for well-formedness. We further ablate input representations by varying contextual encoders and tokenization granularity to study the effect of subword segmentation on node boundary prediction. Finally, we discuss error profiles and implications for downstream ABSA pipelines, emphasizing the need for principled graph formalisms that preserve nested structures.

spans: Recent studies adopt a two-stage retriever-expander pipeline with bootstrapped seeds and distant supervision.
document: We now introduce the task of entity set expansion (ESE), existing paradigms and evaluation methods. ESE seeks to grow a small seed set of entities into a larger, semantically coherent list. Typical applications include building gazetteers, enriching knowledge bases, and supporting downstream NLP components in specialized domains. Given a few exemplars, models must balance precision in semantic relatedness with coverage over long-tail candidates. Approaches to ESE can be characterized by how they represent entities and how they exploit context from unstructured or structured sources. In practice, one can combine distributional similarity with pretrained representations and weak supervision. Candidate generation is commonly performed by scanning large corpora, querying search indices, or enumerating nodes in knowledge graphs. Ranking then estimates the semantic fit of each candidate to the seed set using learned or heuristic scoring functions. Recent studies adopt a two-stage retriever-expander pipeline with bootstrapped seeds and distant supervision. Beyond textual corpora, structured signals such as type hierarchies and relation paths can regularize expansion to prevent semantic drift. Evaluation of ESE remains challenging because relevance is context dependent and gold lists are rarely exhaustive. Standard practice measures precision at fixed cutoffs, area under the precision-recall curve, and mean average precision over held-out entity lists. To support fair comparisons, we describe a reproducible protocol that specifies seed selection, candidate pools, and cross-domain splits. We also discuss robustness checks, including seed perturbations, out-of-domain tests, and ablations on representation and scoring components. Finally, we outline open questions for ESE, with emphasis on low-resource settings and minimal reliance on curated ontologies.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalizing claim about prior work—asserting that recent studies use a two-stage retriever-expander pipeline with bootstrapped seeds and distant supervision—without providing any citations or evidence, matching the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

document: Paraphrase generation aims to produce meaning-preserving rewordings for downstream tasks such as data augmentation and semantic parsing. Recent progress has been driven by both supervised and unsupervised learning paradigms. In supervised settings, sequence-to-sequence models with attention and pretraining remain strong baselines for quality-focused generation. To encourage diversity, several studies explore decoding-time strategies such as diverse beam search and nucleus sampling (Vijayakumar et al., 2018; Holtzman et al., 2020). Other works increase output variability by perturbing latent representations or training multiple generators with complementary inductive biases (Gupta et al., 2018; Park et al., 2019; Qian et al., 2019). Controlled paraphrase generation explicitly exposes levers over form and content, including lexical constraints and syntactic templates (Zeng et al., 2019; Iyyer et al., 2018; Li and Choi, 2020; Thompson and Post, 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2020). Exemplar-driven syntax transfer conditions the decoder on a retrieved sentence that provides a target structure while preserving the source meaning (Bao et al., 2019). Keyword-conditioned generators steer lexical choice by requiring inclusion or exclusion of content words with soft penalties (Zeng et al., 2019). Recent works also couple control with semantic similarity objectives to balance adequacy and diversity (Thompson and Post, 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2020). On the evaluation side, automatic metrics increasingly reflect multiple axes, combining BLEU or ROUGE with semantic similarity and diversity indexes (Gupta et al., 2018; Niu et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020). In parallel, unsupervised approaches exploit back-translation, denoising, and constrained decoding to produce high-quality paraphrases without parallel supervision (Niu et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020; Garg et al., 2021; Siddique et al., 2020). Reinforcement learning has been used to optimize non-differentiable, quality-oriented objectives that integrate textual entailment, semantic similarity, and fluency (Garg et al., 2021; Siddique et al., 2020). Beyond these trends, recent studies consistently report that two-stage retrieval-then-generation pipelines with contrastive sentence encoders and constrained decoding outperform purely generative baselines across standard paraphrase benchmarks and multilingual test suites. Nevertheless, practitioners still lack principled guidance for choosing control levers and decoding schemes under application-specific trade-offs between adequacy, diversity, and fluency. To ground our analysis, we evaluate supervised and unsupervised baselines on multiple public corpora, including the ParaBank2 dataset, and report results alongside Quora Question Pairs and ParaNMT-50M (Iyyer et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2015). In summary, our contribution is a simple, extensible framework that jointly controls lexical, syntactic, and semantic attributes while maintaining strong quality under standard metrics.
reason: Span 1 makes a broad claim about a specific trend in prior work—two-stage retrieval-then-generation with contrastive encoders and constrained decoding outperforming generative baselines—without providing any citation. Span 2 introduces a specific dataset at first mention without a supporting citation, which should be provided in related work.

document: Temporal Commonsense. Work on temporal reasoning ranges from extracting and normalizing temporal expressions (Strötgen and Gertz, 2010;Angeli et al., 2012;Vashishtha et al., 2019), to inferring possibly explicit temporal attributes of events, including their order (Ning et al., 2018;Vashishtha et al., 2019), duration (Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008;Vashishtha et al., 2019), and typical times or frequencies (Zhou et al., 2019). Various benchmarks were proposed to measure models' temporal reasoning abilities. The bAbI suite contains a task that requires reasoning about the order of time expressions (Weston et al., 2015). MC-TACO is a reading comprehension task pertaining to ordering, duration, stationarity, frequency, and typical times of events (Zhou et al., 2019). TI-MEDIAL (Qin et al., 2021) is a dialogue QA task focusing on temporal commonsense. Zhou et al. (2021) and Thukral et al. (2021) both cast the temporal ordering task as an NLI task. In another line of work, tracking state changes in procedural text is also related to temporal ordering (Dalvi et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2020). Beyond QA and NLI, temporal relation extraction also arises in domains such as newswire and clinical narratives. Building on these trends, some systems decompose inference into retrieval and reasoning components using two-stage retrievers. Despite the success of pre-trained LMs on language understanding tasks, their performance on these benchmarks is limited, maybe due to the fact that many temporal relations are not explicitly stated in text (Davis and Marcus, 2015). A promising direction is to train LMs explicitly on temporal knowledge (Zhou et al., 2020). Cultural Commonsense. There is little focus on cultural differences in NLP in general (Hovy and Yang, 2021) and in research about commonsense reasoning in particular. Recently, Acharya et al. (2021) made a first step in addressing this gap. They surveyed crowdsourcing workers in the US and India regarding rituals that are commonly found across cultures such as birth, marriage, and funerals. In particular, they asked questions pertaining to temporal aspects such as typical time and duration of each event. The paper mentions anecdotal differences such that a wedding lasts a few hours in the US but a few days in India. Yet large-scale evaluations that explicitly compare temporal norms across cultures would be valuable. Although there is no direct mapping between culture and language, one can often teach about the other. For example, in ConceptNet, a multilingual commonsense knowledge base, the English entry for breakfast specifies pancakes as breakfast food, while the Chinese entry mentions noodles (Speer et al., 2017). Language Grounding and World Knowledge. Our work is related to language grounding (Roy and Reiter, 2005) and to extracting world knowledge from text corpora (Carlson et al., 2010;Tandon et al., 2014). In the intersection of these two lines of work, Forbes and Choi (2017) extracted from a corpus physical commonsense knowledge about actions and objects along five dimensions (size, weight, strength, rigidness, and speed), while Elazar et al. (2019) induced distributions of typical values of various quantitative attributes such as time, duration, length, and speed. Elazar et al. (2019) mention cultural differences that arose when crowdsourcing workers were asked to estimate whether an item's price was expensive or not: annotators from India judged prices differently from annotators in the US. Complementary to these efforts, grounding temporal relations in situated contexts is naturally posed in vision-and-language settings that align events with timelines. These strands motivate our focus on modeling temporal commonsense in ways that are sensitive to cultural variation and amenable to grounded evaluation.

document: Question generation (QG) seeks to produce natural language questions from input texts or structured data. It has progressed rapidly due to new datasets and model improvements. Early neural QG relied on vanilla sequence to sequence models with attention. Later work introduced copying, coverage, and syntactic encoders to better control content selection. Transformer-based architectures further boosted performance across multiple benchmarks. Many different QG models have been proposed, ranging from simple seq2seq baselines to pretrained encoder–decoders. Representative approaches include template-driven, feature-augmented, and end-to-end neural models (Du et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2018; Dong et al., 2019). Some systems integrate manual linguistic features such as answer position, POS tags, and dependency labels (Harrison and Walker, 2018; Khullar et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019a; Dhole and Manning, 2020). Other methods focus on selecting question-worthy spans before generation (Li et al., 2019; Scialom et al., 2019). Yet another line explicitly models interrogative types to control the surface form of questions (Duan et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2018; Kang et al., 2019). These strategies collectively highlight the trade-off between controllability and fluency. SQuAD has become the de facto pretraining and evaluation resource for many QG pipelines. Beyond extractive reading comprehension corpora, multi-hop and dialogue datasets have encouraged more complex question styles. In conversational settings, models are taught to ask coherent follow-up questions grounded in a QA flow (Gu et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021b). Vision–language variants generate questions about images and scenes (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016; Shin et al., 2018; Shukla et al., 2019). Domain-specific QG targets mathematical, clinical, and examination-style questions to elicit deeper reasoning (Liyanage and Ranathunga, 2019; Yue et al., 2020; Jia et al., 2021). Recent systems increasingly standardize on a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline employing dense passage retrieval with span-focused generators for QG in practice. However, reliance on retrieval quality can propagate topical bias and limit coverage of low-resource targets. Many training schedules pretrain on NewsQA supervision before adapting to abstractive question styles. Evaluation typically combines automatic metrics with human judgments to assess answerability, specificity, and grammaticality. Automatic scores such as BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE correlate imperfectly with human ratings (Callison-Burch et al., 2006; Novikova et al., 2017). As a result, recent work explores reference-free or semantic metrics based on entailment and embedding similarity (Zhang et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2019). Curricula that first teach span prediction on Natural Questions consistently outperform direct end-to-end generators substantially. Controllability has also been pursued through latent variable models and prompt-based conditioning over answer types and styles (Kumar et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). In multilingual QG, progress hinges on cross-lingual transfer and synthetic data augmentation to compensate for scarce supervision (Qi et al., 2018; Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Despite these advances, robust generalization remains challenging when domain, discourse structure, or answer format shifts. Our study revisits content selection, typological control, and evaluation by unifying these axes within a single training framework. We aim to provide a transparent comparison that clarifies where modeling choices, data regimes, and metrics truly matter.
reason: 1) SQuAD is a first-mention dataset without citation, constituting an unsupported claim about prior resources. 2) The two-stage retriever–reader claim asserts a specific, widely adopted pipeline and components without any evidence or references. 3) NewsQA supervision is a dataset-based training practice asserted without citation. 4) The Natural Questions curriculum claim states a comparative performance advantage without supporting studies or citations.

spans: Recent studies standardized a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline for web-scale open-domain QA with dense passage indexing backends
document: Late-interaction models. The idea of running several Transformer layers for the document and the query independently, and then combining them only in the final layers, was developed concurrently by multiple teams: PreTTR (MacAvaney et al., 2020), EARL (Gao et al., 2020a), DC-BERT (Nie et al., 2020), and the Deformer (Cao et al., 2020). These works show that only a few layers where the query and document interact are sufficient to reach performance close to a full BERT ranker at a fraction of the runtime cost. For each document, the contextual token vectors are stored in a cache and retrieved during the ranking phase. This design impacts both storage cost and the latency of fetching vectors during ranking. MORES (Gao et al., 2020b) extends late-interaction models by letting only the query attend to the document in the last interaction layers. As documents are typically much longer, this asymmetry yields further speedups with similar storage requirements. ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) is another variant that runs all Transformer layers independently for the query and the document, and performs interaction between the final vectors via a sum-of-max operator. A related approach, Transformer-Kernel (TK) (Hofstätter et al., 2020b), replaces the interaction block with a low-complexity kernel operation. Both ColBERT and TK achieve lower runtime latency at the expense of a modest drop in ranking quality. However, the storage requirements for both approaches remain significant. Several works acknowledge the challenge of storing precomputed document representations and propose partial solutions. In ColBERT, the dimensionality of the final token embeddings is reduced with a linear layer (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Even moderate compression ratios, however, can cause a large drop in ranking quality. PreTTR addresses storage by using a standard auto-encoder and the float16 format instead of float32 (MacAvaney et al., 2020). Again, ranking quality drops even with moderate compression ratios, with measurements reported up to 12×. Beyond token-level late interaction, several works propose representing queries and documents as single vectors and using a dot product as the interaction block (Guu et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Qu et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021). While this architecture is simple and amenable to approximate nearest neighbor retrieval via FAISS or ScaNN, its ranking quality is generally below cross-attention methods (Johnson et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2020). Recent studies standardized a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline for web-scale open-domain QA with dense passage indexing backends. Compressed embeddings. Our work reduces storage requirements by lowering the number of bits per floating-point value. Quantization has shown success in shrinking model parameters (Gupta et al., 2015; Essam et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2018) and reducing distributed training communication costs (Suresh et al., 2017; Alistarh et al., 2017; Konečnỳ and Richtárik, 2018; Vargaftik et al., 2021b). Compressing word embeddings has also been studied as an independent goal. May et al. (2019) examined the effect of quantized word embeddings on downstream tasks and proposed a metric for quantifying this effect with simple linear models that operate directly on the embeddings. Because our focus is contextual embeddings, vocabulary-bounded techniques do not directly apply. Nevertheless, in line with May et al. (2019), we find that simple quantization schemes are quite effective. We leverage recent advances to further reduce storage for document representations, which, to the best of our knowledge, has not been attempted in this context.
reason: The span asserts a specific, field-level trend about a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline and dense passage indexing without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

document: Patient-specific literature retrieval aims to surface evidence from biomedical articles that is directly relevant to a given patient's presentation. This capability can support clinicians during triage and decision making by contextualizing patient data with the external knowledge base of PubMed. Community benchmarks have catalyzed progress by defining tasks, data, and evaluation protocols around this problem. Since 2014, the Text REtrieval Conference has organized a series of challenges to advance research in this area. The TREC Clinical Decision Support tracks evaluated systems on retrieving biomedical articles relevant to generic clinical questions derived from patient records. Early editions used short case reports as idealized representations of medical records due to the scarcity of de-identified notes. Subsequent editions shifted toward real-world artifacts via releases such as the MIMIC database. Specifically, TREC CDS 2014 and 2015 emphasized case-based queries, whereas the 2016 track incorporated authentic intensive care unit notes from MIMIC. These design choices shaped system development by stressing robustness to long, noisy inputs and heterogeneous information needs. In this work, we focus on predicting clinical outcomes using ICU admission notes paired with patient-specific retrieved literature. Prior retrieval efforts in clinical IR have explored contextualized representations primarily on structured or well-formed query sets. Ueda et al. reported strong performance on such settings that did not involve free-text clinical notes. However, it remains unclear how large pretrained language models will fare when queries are lengthy, abbreviations are pervasive, and negation is frequent. To address this gap, we experiment with language models for retrieval and re-ranking directly over EHR-derived queries and PubMed abstracts. Recent studies widely adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines. Our architecture adapts this template to the clinical domain by integrating retrieval with an outcome prediction head. Clinical outcome prediction has drawn heightened interest for triage support, workflow optimization, and resource planning, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic. Models using structured signals such as vitals and labs, as well as unstructured text such as nursing and physician notes, have been proposed for mortality, length of stay, readmission, sepsis, mechanical ventilation, and diagnostic coding. We hypothesize that free-text notes can materially improve predictive performance over structured inputs alone. In parallel, we examine large pretrained language models for EHR outcome prediction within a unified experimental setup. Against this backdrop, we compare a broader range of state-of-the-art pretrained language models across multiple outcome tasks. Our evaluation isolates the contribution of patient-specific literature retrieval by toggling retrieval on and off within the same modeling framework. We term our system BEEP, which comprises two stages: literature retrieval and outcome prediction. Given a patient admission note Q and outcome label y, the retrieval stage identifies a set of candidate PubMed abstracts Docs(Q) that may inform the likelihood of y. A re-ranking component then prioritizes a top subset, and the prediction module fuses the reranked abstracts with Q to produce the final estimate. We additionally explore a joint training variant that fine-tunes retrieval and prediction end to end. The following sections detail our datasets, models, training procedures, and evaluation protocols.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about prior work adopting a specific two-stage retriever–reader approach without providing citations, thus fitting the definition of an unsupported claim. No other sentences introduce uncited claims about specific prior work or statistics.
