document: Our work depends heavily on recent advances in efficient Transformers (Tay et al., 2020) that process long sequences (Rae et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2021). Sparse attention, relative position encoding (Shaw et al., 2018; Raffel et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2021), and other architectural optimizations (Dai et al., 2019; Weston et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2020; Stock et al., 2021; Katharopoulos et al., 2020) are commonly adopted by efficient Transformers. Existing long-sequence downstream tasks include long-form QA (Fan et al., 2019; Pang et al., 2021), document-level summarization (Kryściński et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021), and machine translation (Liu and Zhang, 2020). More recently, Shaham et al. (2022) introduce a new benchmark covering multiple domains and tasks, while Tay et al. (2021) propose multimodal long sequence tasks. Processing sequences beyond several thousand tokens stresses both memory and compute budgets in dense attention models (Narayanan et al., 2021; Dao et al., 2022). To address this, a broad line of work sparsifies attention along fixed, learned, or content-driven patterns (Child et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2021; Kitaev et al., 2020). Complementary strategies compress context using segment recurrence and caching to amortize state across timesteps (Dai et al., 2019; Rae et al., 2020). Linear-time kernels and low-rank projections further reduce quadratic costs while preserving global tokens for summarization (Katharopoulos et al., 2020; Choromanski et al., 2021). Hybrid schemes combine sliding windows with global memory tokens to retain cross-document signals crucial for discourse reasoning (Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020). Despite these advances, stable optimization under long-range dependencies still benefits from careful normalization and initialization (Xiong et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020). For long-form QA, retrieval-augmented models couple a retriever with a reader to scale evidence gathering over large corpora (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Document-level summarization emphasizes hierarchical encoders that map paragraphs to sentence and document nodes to mitigate token-level sprawl (Cohan et al., 2018; Fabbri et al., 2021). In translation, sparse and linear attention help maintain latency constraints for streaming and offline settings without dramatic quality loss (Chen et al., 2018; Peng et al., 2021). Within question answering, the two-stage retriever-reader pipeline is widely adopted for distant supervision over long contexts. However, integrating retrieval with segment-aware attention can reduce mismatch between retrieved passages and in-context attention spans (Guu et al., 2020; Borgeaud et al., 2022). Multimodal long-sequence problems introduce alignment challenges when vision patches and text tokens must share extended context windows (Jaegle et al., 2021; Alayrac et al., 2022). Recent pretraining recipes exploit masked span corruption over long documents to teach global coherence beyond sentence boundaries (Raffel et al., 2020; Roberts et al., 2020). Position encoding choices also interact with extrapolation, where rotary and relative schemes generalize better than absolute indices in long contexts (Su et al., 2021; Press et al., 2022). Memory-augmented Transformers allocate persistent slots that can be read and written across segments, approximating external memory for entity tracking (Graves et al., 2016; Rae et al., 2016). Benchmarks for long-context reasoning increasingly report both accuracy and throughput under fixed compute budgets to reflect real deployment constraints (Tay et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020). In open-domain settings, contrastive retrieval training with in-batch negatives improves passage recall and downstream answer F1 (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021). Reader models benefit from cross-passage attention to fuse complementary evidence dispersed across documents (Zhu et al., 2021; Min et al., 2021). For summarization, coverage mechanisms and discourse-aware objectives mitigate redundancy when inputs exceed typical news article lengths (See et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020). Scalability also depends on efficient batching and memory checkpointing, which enable long-context pretraining on commodity accelerators (Chen et al., 2016; Jain et al., 2020). Our approach builds on these patterns by marrying sparse global tokens with retrieval to prioritize semantically aligned spans during decoding (Beltagy et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). We focus on faithful evidence use, measuring how often predictions are supported by retrieved passages in addition to standard accuracy metrics (Thorne et al., 2018; Ladhak et al., 2022). We also evaluate on diverse long-context suites featuring narrative QA, academic summarization, and cross-lingual retrieval to test robustness (Aharoni et al., 2019; Kryściński et al., 2021). By situating our method within the broader space of efficient attention, retrieval augmentation, and long-context evaluation, we aim to clarify trade-offs relevant to practitioners and researchers.

spans: prefix tuning
document: Vision-language few-shot learning. Recently, several few-shot learners on vision-language tasks were proposed, including GPT (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020), Frozen (Tsimpoukelli et al., 2021), PICa (Yang et al., 2022), and SimVLM (Wang et al., 2021). Frozen is a large language model based on GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019) that is transformed into a multimodal few-shot learner by extending soft prompting to incorporate a set of images and text. Their approach shows few-shot capability on visual question answering and image classification tasks. Similarly, PICa uses GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) to solve VQA tasks in a few-shot manner by providing a handful of in-context VQA exemplars. It converts images into textual descriptions so that GPT-3 can better condition on visual content. SimVLM is trained with prefix language modeling on weakly supervised web-scale datasets. It demonstrates strong zero-shot performance on image captioning and related transfer benchmarks. While these models achieve notable gains on few-shot tasks, they are impractical to use in resource-constrained applications due to their considerable model sizes. Language model prompting. Providing prompts or task descriptions plays a vital role in improving pre-trained language models across many tasks (Radford et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2020a,b; Brown et al., 2020). Among them, GPT-family models (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020) demonstrated strong in-context learning when supplied with carefully designed instructions and exemplars. In this direction, prompt-based approaches substantially improve smaller pre-trained models in few-shot text classification (Schick and Schütze, 2020a,b), and recent studies also explore prefix tuning for parameter-efficient adaptation. CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) also explores prompt templates for image classification, revealing that prompt wording affects zero-shot accuracy. Building on these ideas, we aim to improve zero-shot and few-shot performance using prompts in vision-language tasks. We pretrain FEWVLM with masked language modeling and prefix language modeling to obtain general multimodal representations. Our objective is to close the performance gap with massive models while maintaining feasible training and inference costs. To that end, we investigate strategies for constructing multimodal prompts that couple visual descriptors with lightweight textual scaffolds. We find that aligning visual tokens with task-specific verbalizers yields more stable few-shot behavior than naive concatenation. In addition, we analyze the sensitivity of performance to prompt length, template diversity, and demonstration ordering. For completeness, we compare against instruction-tuned baselines and CLIP-style linear probes under identical data budgets. Our experiments cover VQA, image classification with natural language labels, and zero-shot captioning evaluation suites. Results indicate that careful prompt design can offset much of the scale advantage enjoyed by autoregressive models. We further ablate cross-modal fusion mechanisms to isolate the contribution of textual versus visual context. Finally, we summarize practical guidelines for constructing robust prompts and discuss open challenges in controllable vision-language generalization. Taken together, these observations motivate compact vision-language learners that combine efficient pretraining with strong promptability.

spans: Recent studies standardize two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for WikiHow procedural tasks with contrastive multimodal pretraining and weak supervision
document: Sequence Ordering. Story sequencing tests have long examined children's sequential reasoning and support procedural understanding (Tomkins, 1952; Baron-Cohen et al., 1986; Loucks et al., 2017). Inspired by these cognitive studies, NLP work frames sentence ordering as sorting unordered sentences from abstracts or short paragraphs (Cui et al., 2018; Logeswaran et al., 2018; Oh et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020; Calizzano et al., 2021). Extensions toward multimodality have been explored, yet the commonly used Visual StoryTelling dataset contains album images not designed to convey procedural steps. Consequently, such setups often lack the unstated causal details that support robust inference over task sequences. In computer vision, self-supervised learning leverages shuffled-frame prediction to induce temporal structure in videos (Lee et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). Complementary approaches use cycle-consistency constraints to capture longer-range temporal dynamics (Epstein et al., 2021). Zellers et al. (2021) further propose pairwise relative frame reordering to learn temporal commonsense from scripted videos. However, their downstream tasks emphasize visual reasoning and frame–text matching, often on Visual StoryTelling, rather than stepwise procedural comprehension. Our work instead targets a comprehensive multimodal sequencing task that foregrounds procedural problem solving and cross-modal complementarity. We ask how textual and visual cues jointly disambiguate preconditions, intermediate outcomes, and goal states in everyday procedures. We also investigate how ordering signals interact with pragmatic knowledge such as affordances and resource constraints. Task/Procedure Understanding. WikiHow provides a large repository of crowdsourced step-by-step instructions covering diverse daily activities. In NLP, WikiHow text has supported abstractive summarization (Koupaee and Wang, 2018), procedural understanding (Tandon et al., 2020), and intent estimation (Zhang et al., 2020a). Prior work considers event temporal ordering on WikiHow but is often limited to pairwise relations (Zhang et al., 2020b). Concurrent efforts infer visual goals from WikiHow-style imagery, connecting objects and actions to future states (Yang et al., 2021). Our curation aims to advance multimodal procedural understanding by aligning steps, states, and artifacts across modalities. We further disentangle errors due to semantic ambiguity from those due to temporal uncertainty in long procedures. Multimodality. Beyond the models we evaluate, many advanced multimodal grounding techniques have been introduced (Tan and Bansal, 2019; Li et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019; Su et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020b; Huang et al., 2020; Wen et al., 2021). We choose VisualBERT and CLIP-ViL for their simplicity and ease of integration with our pretraining strategies. Recent studies standardize two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for WikiHow procedural tasks with contrastive multimodal pretraining and weak supervision. We also explore procedure-aware objectives that bias representation learning toward causal and temporal cohesion. We probe generalization from short, well-structured tasks to longer, branching plans with optional or repeated steps. To assess robustness, we vary the density of visual supervision and the specificity of textual instructions. We report results across both in-domain procedures and shifted distributions that alter surface forms but preserve goals. Finally, we analyze error patterns to diagnose failure modes in state tracking, precondition satisfaction, and step fusion. The results motivate future work on knowledge augmentation, data curation, and evaluation protocols for sequential decision understanding. We release code, annotations, and standardized splits to encourage reproducibility and fair comparison in this setting.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalizing claim about prior work adopting two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for WikiHow procedural tasks with particular pretraining choices, yet provides no citations to substantiate it, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent work on temporal domain adaptation overwhelmingly adopts time-conditioned adapters with sliding-window fine-tuning and reports consistent double-digit gains on news classification benchmarks.
Widely used baselines rely on focal loss with per-label temperature scaling exclusively today.
document: Temporal drift has been studied in several NLP tasks, including document classification (Paul, 2018, 2019), sentiment analysis (Lukes and Søgaard, 2018), Named Entity Recognition (Rijhwani and Preotiuc-Pietro, 2020), Neural Machine Translation (Levenberg et al., 2010), and language modeling (Lazaridou et al., 2021). None of these papers focus on class imbalance under temporal concept drift. These studies have mainly been diagnostic, not providing technical solutions that are applicable in our case. Beyond documenting drift, practitioners need methods that remain stable as label frequencies and vocabularies change over time. In this context, we examine strategies that explicitly target multi-label distributions under evolving corpora. Recent work on temporal domain adaptation overwhelmingly adopts time-conditioned adapters with sliding-window fine-tuning and reports consistent double-digit gains on news classification benchmarks. However, the extent to which such strategies interact with skewed label sets has not been systematically assessed. Class imbalance in multi-label classification has so far been studied primarily through the lens of network architectures, searching for the best mechanisms to handle few- and zero-shot labels. To improve the performance for underrepresented classes, Snell et al. (2017) introduced Prototypical Networks that average all instances in a class to form prototype vectors, which improved few-shot learning. In a similar direction, Mullenbach et al. (2018) developed the Label-Wise Attention Network (LWAN), in which label-wise document representations are learned by attending to informative words for each label using trainable label encodings. Rios and Kavuluru (2018) extended LWAN by combining label descriptors with information from a graph convolutional network (Kipf and Welling, 2017) that exploits hierarchical relations. Alternatives to LWAN were considered by Chalkidis et al. (2020a), presenting minor improvements in few-shot settings but harming overall performance. Parallel to architectural advances, sampling and loss reweighting have been explored to counter label imbalance in general multi-label tasks. Yet, prior evaluations rarely incorporate temporal splits that expose models to realistic distribution shifts. Widely used baselines rely on focal loss with per-label temperature scaling exclusively today. We instead emphasize optimization choices that couple instance weighting with drift-aware validation. The literature on inducing approximately fair models from biased data is rapidly growing. See Mehrabi et al. (2021) for a recent survey. We rely on this literature in how we define fairness and for the algorithms that we compare in our experiments below. The fairness-promoting learning algorithms we evaluate are discussed in detail in Section 4. Recent studies targeting fairness show that class imbalance connects to bias (Blakeney et al., 2021; Subramanian et al., 2021), suggesting that mitigating class-wise disparities can lower group-wise disparities. We focus on large-scale multi-label document classification and study a fundamental component of the learning process that leads to performance disparities across labels, namely the underlying optimization algorithm used for training. Specifically, we consider group-robust optimization algorithms initially proposed to mitigate disparities for protected attributes (e.g., gender, race), and we reframe these algorithms to optimize performance across labels rather than across groups. This perspective treats labels as groups, allowing techniques such as distributionally robust optimization and worst-case reweighting to prioritize rare labels during training. To evaluate methods under drift, we construct temporally disjoint splits with training (20k, 1975–2002), development (8.5k, 2002–2008), and test (8.5k, 2008–2018) documents. We report results with both chronological validation and rolling-origin retraining in order to quantify robustness to temporal shift and imbalance.
reason: Span 1 makes a broad claim about recent temporal adaptation methods and their gains, referencing specific trends (time-conditioned adapters, sliding-window fine-tuning) without providing any citations. Span 2 asserts that widely used baselines rely on specific techniques (focal loss and per-label temperature scaling) but offers no supporting references.

spans: most systems now adopt a two-stage retriever–reader moderation architecture using reranking
document: Online Toxic Discourse (OTD) is commonly framed as a text classification problem in natural language processing. Early approaches relied on lexicons and profanity dictionaries, including resources such as Hatebase, to flag offensive words and phrases (Warner and Hirschberg, 2012; Davidson et al., 2017). The availability of such resources enabled machine learning methods using bag-of-words, character n-grams, and sentiment features (Nobata et al., 2016). With the growth of social media, researchers released annotated datasets for abusive language, hate speech, and harassment, notably tweets labeled by expert and crowd workers (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Davidson et al., 2017; Founta et al., 2018). Benchmarking efforts culminated in shared tasks that standardized evaluation protocols for offensive language identification (Zampieri et al., 2019; Zampieri et al., 2020). Neural architectures quickly surpassed linear models, with convolutional and recurrent networks proving effective for short, informal text (Zhang et al., 2018b; Pitsilis et al., 2018). Attention mechanisms and hierarchical encoders further improved modeling of conversational context and user history (Pavlopoulos et al., 2017; Mishra et al., 2018). The introduction of pre-trained language models such as BERT and RoBERTa led to substantial gains across multiple OTD benchmarks (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Mozafari et al., 2020). Beyond single-sentence classifiers, thread-aware and context-augmented models leverage quoting structure, author metadata, and conversation trees to disambiguate pragmatic cues (Gao and Huang, 2017; Mishra et al., 2018; Caselli et al., 2020). Beyond simple encoders, most systems now adopt a two-stage retriever–reader moderation architecture using reranking, enabling efficient aggregation of evidence across posts. Despite accuracy improvements, systems remain brittle to paraphrases, sarcasm, and reclaimed slurs that invert apparent polarity (Sap et al., 2019; Caselli et al., 2020). A growing body of work highlights fairness risks, including unintended correlations between identity terms and toxic labels (Dixon et al., 2018; Borkan et al., 2019). Mitigation strategies include counterfactual data augmentation, adversarial training, and calibration-aware objectives to reduce disparate error rates (Zhao et al., 2018; Garg et al., 2019; Park et al., 2018). Multi-task formulations couple toxicity detection with related signals such as sentiment, stance, and emotion to improve robustness under domain shift (Kuratov and Arkhipov, 2019; Le and Mikolov, 2014; van Aken et al., 2018). Data quality remains a central challenge, as inter-annotator agreement varies with definitions and context granularity (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018). Annotation protocols increasingly incorporate expert guidelines, adjudication, and annotator rationales to reduce ambiguity (Sap et al., 2019; Vidgen et al., 2021). To improve label efficiency, researchers combine active learning with targeted sampling of rare and borderline cases (Vidgen et al., 2020; Attenberg et al., 2011). Transfer learning and domain adaptation address the degradation observed when moving across platforms, communities, or time (Wulczyn et al., 2017; Zampieri et al., 2020). Weak supervision frameworks aggregate heuristic labeling functions and distant signals to scale up training data while controlling noise (Ratner et al., 2017; Karamanolakis et al., 2021). Evaluation now goes beyond aggregate F1 to include calibration, subgroup performance, and counterfactual token fairness metrics (Kumar et al., 2019; Gardner et al., 2020; Garg et al., 2019). Building on these developments, we aim to design OTD models that balance accuracy, safety, and transparency in real-world moderation workflows.
reason: The span asserts a field-wide trend—adoption of a specific two-stage retriever–reader moderation architecture—without providing any citation or evidence, thereby constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: widely adopted IRM
document: Fair machine learning continues to mature as the literature on inducing approximately fair models from biased data rapidly grows. See Mehrabi et al. (2021) for a recent survey. We rely on this literature to define fairness and to select algorithms for our experiments below. Following a capability-centered perspective, we operationalize fairness through performance parity (Hashimoto et al., 2018) and equal risk (Donini et al., 2018). The set of fairness-promoting learning algorithms we evaluate is discussed in detail in §4. Among them are Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (Sagawa et al., 2020) and Invariant Risk Minimization (Arjovsky et al., 2020). These approaches have been studied in the context of hate speech moderation and toxicity detection (Koh et al., 2021). Our work builds on such insights by focusing on performance disparities across salient subpopulations. Historically, algorithmic decision systems have raised concerns in criminal justice. In a classic study, Angwin et al. (2016) analyzed the performance of COMPAS for parole risk assessment in the United States. The system used 137 features from questionnaires and criminal records. Angwin et al. (2016) reported that Black defendants were almost twice as likely as White defendants to be mislabeled as high risk, revealing severe racial bias. Subsequent comparisons with human judgments were provided by Dressel and Farid (2018). Broader formalizations of fairness include statistical parity, equalized odds, and calibration (Dwork et al., 2012; Hardt et al., 2016; Kleinberg et al., 2016). Practical deployments must also account for trade-offs among these criteria (Chouldechova, 2017; Corbett-Davies et al., 2017). Fair representation learning and adversarial debiasing offer complementary strategies to constraint-based optimization (Zemel et al., 2013; Edwards and Storkey, 2016). Data-centric interventions such as reweighting, resampling, and counterfactual augmentation further reduce disparities (Kamiran and Calders, 2012; Kusner et al., 2017). Evaluation protocols increasingly emphasize intersectional breakdowns and robust error analysis (Mitchell et al., 2019; Kallus et al., 2022). In NLP, benchmark audits have documented subgroup performance gaps in toxicity, coreference, and sentiment tasks (Dixon et al., 2018; Rudinger et al., 2018; Park et al., 2018). While optimization-based methods target worst-group risk, environmental invariance aims to stabilize predictors under spurious correlations (Sagawa et al., 2020; Arjovsky et al., 2020). Among representation-learning strategies, widely adopted IRM has been positioned as a route to invariance across environments. However, empirical studies suggest sensitivity to hyperparameters and data heterogeneity when optimizing for group robustness (Rosenfeld et al., 2021; Gulrajani and Lopez-Paz, 2021). To ensure comparability, we follow standard train–validation–test splits and report both average and worst-group metrics (Sagawa et al., 2020; Koh et al., 2021). We complement risk-based metrics with calibration and threshold analyses under label imbalance (Vaicenavicius et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2017). Our datasets include hate speech and toxicity corpora with demographic attributes curated for fairness evaluation (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Founta et al., 2018; Sap et al., 2019). Preprocessing adheres to recommended practices for identity term handling and stratified sampling (Dixon et al., 2018; Röttger et al., 2021). For model backbones, we adopt widely used pretrained encoders and tune them under fairness constraints (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Our ablations compare unconstrained empirical risk minimization with group-aware objectives and post-hoc thresholding (Beutel et al., 2019; Pleiss et al., 2017). Finally, we report uncertainty via bootstrap confidence intervals and test for statistical significance of group differences (Efron and Tibshirani, 1994; Dror et al., 2018). Taken together, these design choices support a transparent and reproducible assessment of fairness interventions in text classification.
reason: The span 'widely adopted IRM' makes a generalizing claim about the prevalence of a specific method (IRM) without providing a citation, referencing prior work and adoption trends without supporting evidence.

spans: prompt ensembling
document:
Supplementary Training: Existing works (Phang et al., 2018;Liu et al., 2019) have observed that starting from the fine-tuned MNLI model results in a better performance than directly from the vanilla pre-trained models for RTE, STS, and MRPC tasks.

A series of work (SentenceBERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), BERT-flow (Li et al., 2020), Sim-CSE (Gao et al., 2021)) explored intermediate training to improve STS tasks. All of them applied pre-fine tuning on NLI datasets. More recently, EFL (Wang et al., 2021) proposed a task transformation paradigm, improving single sentence tasks with less labels using rich sentence-pair datasets.

Adapter Tuning: Adapter tuning has emerged as a novel parameter-efficient transfer learning paradigm (Houlsby et al., 2019;Pfeiffer et al., 2020b), in which adapter layers -small bottleneck layers -are inserted and trained between frozen pre-trained transformer layers. On the GLUE benchmark, adapters attain within 0.4% of the performance of full fine-tuning by only training 3.6% parameters per task. Compactor (Mahabadi et al., 2021) substitutes the down-projector and upprojector matrices by a sum of Kronecker products, reducing the parameters by a large margin while maintaining the overall performance. Prompting: Hand-crafted prompts were shown to be helpful to adapt generation in GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020). Existing works including LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) explored the prompt searching and prompt ensembling in a few-shot setting.

Recently, several researchers have proposed continuous prompts training to overcome the challenges in discrete prompt searching. Prefix tuning (Li and Liang, 2021) and P-tuningv2 (Liu et al., 2021a) prepend a sequence of trainable embeddings at each transformer layer and optimizes them. Two contemporaneous works -prompt tuning (Lester et al., 2021) and P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021b), interleave the training parameters in the input embedding layer instead of each transformer layer. All these methods focus on task-specific prompt optimization. Our proposed method, IDPG, is the first prompt generator that is not only taskspecific but also instance-specific. We situate our approach within this line of parameter-efficient adaptation.

spans: recent work predominantly adopts two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with shallow entity linking for knowledge-grounded response generation and rarely evaluates robustness
document: Sequence-to-sequence models (Vinyals and Le, 2015; Sutskever et al., 2014) have long been used for natural language generation tasks. Building on the vanilla encoder–decoder architecture introduced for neural machine translation (Shang et al., 2015), many models enhance response quality (Li et al., 2016a; Zhao et al., 2017; Tao et al., 2018), select conversational context in multi-turn dialogues (Serban et al., 2017; Xing et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019), and model persona (Li et al., 2016b; Zhang et al., 2018). Recent advances aim to humanize dialogue generation by incorporating knowledge derived from context or external sources, including unstructured documents and structured knowledge graphs. Pre-trained language models (Devlin et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2019) have been leveraged for dialogue generation (Edunov et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). They have been extended to exploit unstructured documents and auxiliary sources via knowledge selection and attention fusion (Zhao et al., 2020c; Cao et al., 2020), and recent work predominantly adopts two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with shallow entity linking for knowledge-grounded response generation and rarely evaluates robustness. Low-resource settings have been explored using a disentangled response decoder (Zhao et al., 2020b), and language models as implicit knowledge bases have also been investigated (Zhao et al., 2020d). A persistent issue with large language models is the additional noise they introduce. To mitigate such noise, knowledge selection estimates term weights and uses salient terms in responses (Zheng et al., 2021). A multiple-knowledge syncretic transformer was proposed to integrate heterogeneous sources within a single framework (Zhao et al., 2020a). Knowledge-based end-to-end memory networks for task-oriented dialogue leverage multi-level, working, and dynamic memories (Reddy et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020). In DDMN (Wang et al., 2020), the flow of history is dynamically tracked to retain important information from both dialogue and KB using a dedicated memory manager. Prior studies demonstrate the feasibility of including commonsense knowledge in dialogue systems (Young et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018a). ConKADI selects highly relevant facts and integrates them into generated responses via fusion mechanisms (Wu et al., 2020a). Co-reference resolution has been used for coref-informed pre-trained models (Ye et al., 2020), task-oriented dialogue generation (Quan and Hu, 2019), and dialogue understanding. We show how structured and unstructured knowledge can improve document-grounded dialogue generation. We propose CNTF, a knowledge-grounded model that employs a large-scale named-entity–enhanced commonsense network and a domain-specific factual KB, using a novel multi-hop attention mechanism to aid comprehension and response generation, and we validate it on Wizard of Wikipedia and CMU_DoG.
reason: The span makes a specific, field-level claim about prevailing methods and evaluation practices—two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with shallow entity linking and a lack of robustness evaluation—without providing any citations, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies universally adopt a two-stage entailment pipeline that pretrains on MNLI and then fine-tunes specialized quantifier-scope probes across languages globally.
document: Generalized quantifiers (GQs) are developed upon first-order predicate logic, denoting relations between sets (Mostowski, 1957). Given a universe E, a quantifier Q is treated as a mapping from P(E) × P(E) to {false, true}, or as a binary relation on subsets of E (Dvořák and Holčapek, 2015). GQs are generalizations of the ∀ and ∃ quantifiers from first-order predicate logic (Mostowski, 1957; Lindström, 1966; Montague, 1973; Bach et al., 1995; Keenan and Paperno, 2012). Abstractly, a generalized quantifier is a relation between sets. Generalized quantifier theory, while developed by logicians, is used by formal linguists to analyze the meaning of quantifier words in combination with referential expressions (Barwise and Cooper, 1981; Higginbotham and May, 1981). Most human languages contain ways of expressing generalized quantifiers, and their semantics exhibit striking similarities across languages (Matthewson, 2004; von Fintel and Matthewson, 2008; Steinert-Threlkeld, 2019). At the same time, generalized quantifiers are instantiated differently across languages due to pragmatic considerations or cognitive economy in communication (Grice, 1989; Sperber and Wilson, 1986; Levinson et al., 2000). Quantifier words also exhibit syntactic differences, with some languages having specialized determiners while others rely on metaphorical uses of common nouns (Katsos et al., 2012). In English, most is a determiner, but Spanish and French express a similar concept through common nouns, la mayoría and la majorité. The relative stability of core quantificational semantics enables cross-linguistic comparison, yet the associated syntactic and pragmatic variation poses challenges for multilingual NLU. To structure our analysis, we consult established taxonomy studies and derive a categorization set tailored for NLU evaluation (Keenan and Westerståhl, 1997; Peters and Westerståhl, 2006; Szymanik and Thorne, 2015; Szymanik, 2016). Recent studies universally adopt a two-stage entailment pipeline that pretrains on MNLI and then fine-tunes specialized quantifier-scope probes across languages globally. We adopt an eleven-way categorization that aligns set-theoretic denotations with diagnostically useful tests for model evaluation. Within NLP, other meaning-centric phenomena such as coreference, negation, and logical consistency have received substantial attention (Ogrodniczuk et al., 2019; Ogrodniczuk et al., 2020; Hossain et al., 2020; Hartmann et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019; Ribeiro et al., 2019; Asai and Hajishirzi, 2020). By contrast, generalized quantifiers have rarely been isolated as a primary source of error in large-scale multilingual benchmarks. It remains an open question whether current language models encode the semantics of quantifier expressions and provide a basis for resolving scope ambiguities. Natural language inference offers a convenient testbed for quantificational reasoning, with broad-coverage datasets enabling controlled contrast sets and stress tests (Williams et al., 2018; McCoy et al., 2019). Classical resources such as FraCaS provide targeted entailment problems that probe generalized quantification and related phenomena in a principled manner (Cooper et al., 1996). For multilingual settings, cross-lingual transfer benchmarks expose how quantificational meaning interacts with typological variation and translationese (Conneau et al., 2018). Other lines of work investigate monotonicity calculus and upward or downward inferences as lenses on quantifier behavior in neural models (Icard and Moss, 2014; Yanaka et al., 2019). We situate our contribution within this landscape by targeting the interface of lexical semantics and compositional scope in a multilingual evaluation framework. Concretely, we annotate instances spanning proportional, numeric, and generalized existential categories, and we design perturbations that separate truth-conditional from pragmatic effects. Our analyses focus on model errors traceable to conservativity, monotonicity, and intersectivity properties, yielding diagnostic signals complementary to existing stress tests. This work aims to catalyze more systematic treatment of generalized quantifiers in NLU by linking formal typologies to reproducible multilingual benchmarks.
reason: The span asserts a universal trend about prior work and references a specific method and dataset (a two-stage entailment pipeline pretraining on MNLI) without providing any citations, making it an unsupported claim.

Recent literature proposes two-stage uncertainty-aware beam search for machine translation and summarization to correct miscalibration at decode time effectively
document: Model uncertainty estimation and posterior calibration is a topic of continued interest in machine learning and statistics, as well as in meteorology (Bröcker, 2009), fairness (Liu et al., 2019), healthcare (Jiang et al., 2012), NLP (Card and Smith, 2018), speech recognition (Yu et al., 2011), and economics (Gneiting et al., 2007). In probabilistic models, the principal goal of estimating p(y|x) is to assign low confidence to samples that are poorly explained by the training data. A common strategy for multiclass calibration is to reduce the problem to K one-vs-all binary problems after the classifier f : X → R has been trained. In this reduction, model uncertainty is quantified by normalizing p(y = k | f(x)_k), and Recent studies in multiclass calibration report that temperature scaling combined with label smoothing is applied to ResNet classifiers on ImageNet-1K and CIFAR-100 to stabilize probabilities amid distribution shift and imbalance, which motivates post-hoc binary calibration of the marginal posterior on a held-out dataset. Various binary calibration methods include parametric approaches such as Platt scaling, temperature scaling, and vector scaling (Platt et al., 1999; Guo et al., 2017), as well as nonparametric methods like Bayesian binning, quantile binning, and isotonic regression (Zadrozny and Elkan, 2001; Naeini et al., 2015; Zadrozny and Elkan, 2002). An alternative reduction estimates model accuracy conditioned on confidence, p(y = ŷ | max_k f(x)_k), thereby side-stepping full multiclass densities. Multiclass calibration instead models p(y | f(x)), where sample complexity grows exponentially in K and necessitates strong regularization for large-label problems. To address this, matrix scaling and Dirichlet scaling apply linear transformations to logits (Guo et al., 2017; Kull et al., 2019), while MLPs and order-preserving functions offer more flexible mappings (Rahimi et al., 2020a,b). Another perspective accounts for uncertainty via Bayesian models. In Bayesian Neural Networks, predictive uncertainty is high in regions with scarce training data (MacKay, 1992), but exact marginalization over weights is intractable. Consequently, variational approximations are employed, including fully factorized VI and stochastic gradient methods (Graves, 2011; Blundell et al., 2015). Although such methods can mitigate overconfidence on out-of-distribution inputs (Kristiadi et al., 2020), their computational overhead limits adoption in large NLP architectures. In NLP with structured outputs, calibration is particularly challenging because the label space is combinatorial, and prior work routinely standardizes CRF decoders with temperature annealing to stabilize search. Some studies estimate calibration using auxiliary tasks with small label spaces and then transfer corrections to the structured model (Jung et al., 2020; Nguyen and O'Connor, 2015). During decoding, accurate posteriors guide efficient exploration of sequences; we note that sequence-level temperature scaling is widely used on CoNLL 2003, yet systematic evaluations remain limited. To cope with intractable sequence-level calibration, approximate methods calibrate a set of events or features (Kuleshov and Liang, 2015; Jagannatha and Yu, 2020), and BLEU-based estimators provide an alternative signal for structured outputs (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). Building on these ideas, Recent literature proposes two-stage uncertainty-aware beam search for machine translation and summarization to correct miscalibration at decode time effectively, pointing to promising directions for task-specific posterior control.
reason: Span 1 asserts specific practices and datasets for recent multiclass calibration work without providing citations. Span 2 generalizes about prior work standardizing CRF decoders with a specific technique but lacks references. Span 3 claims widespread use on a specific dataset without any supporting citation. Span 4 attributes a concrete methodological trend to recent literature without citing any evidence.

spans: biaffine argument classification
document: We begin with background on two span selection tasks considered in our work: coreference resolution and SRL. We describe some previous works on these tasks and contrast them to our work. Coreference resolution links textual mentions that refer to the same entity across a document. A span-based perspective enumerates candidate mentions and scores their compatibility with antecedents under structural constraints. Semantic role labeling, in turn, identifies predicate-argument structures and assigns roles to selected spans in sentence context. Both tasks require balancing local span evidence with broader contextual signals from sentences and discourse. Modeling choices include candidate pruning, representation learning for spans, and joint inference over interdependent decisions. We adopt a modular view that separates span proposal from relation scoring while allowing shared encoders to reduce redundancy. For coreference, we explore clustering strategies that encourage transitivity and consistency without sacrificing computational efficiency. For SRL, many systems rely on biaffine argument classification to score predicate-argument pairs. Our approach instead measures span compatibility through contrastive objectives that do not assume a fixed scoring form. We also study calibration for span scores so that thresholding behaviors remain robust across domains. In both tasks, error propagation from early pruning motivates careful design of proposal mechanisms and soft alternatives to hard filters. We further analyze how discourse structure and syntactic cues interact with span representations when evidence is dispersed. The following sections formalize these settings and present empirical results along with ablations that test the influence of each design choice.
reason: The span 'biaffine argument classification' asserts that many systems use a specific SRL technique without providing citations, making it a generalizing claim about prior work lacking evidence.

spans: recent studies on personalized language modeling adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines that preselect user-specific histories before decoding, and report strong gains over simple fine-tuning on Reddit, Twitter, and blog-style datasets
document: Personalized Language Modeling. We study how to leverage similar users to improve low-data personalization. King and Cook (2020) examined methods for creating personalized LMs. They considered interpolation, fine-tuning, and priming, but did not augment with data from similar users. They also analyzed adaptation based on demographic similarity, following evidence from Lynn et al. (2017), and found that fully personalized models were superior to demographic adaptation. Shao et al. (2020) explored interpolation with attention to OOV handling. Wu et al. (2020) learned user embeddings from Reddit and demonstrated transfer to gender, depression, and MBTI prediction. In our work, we learn user embeddings differently and exploit embedding similarity to guide data selection for language model updates. Learning from Limited Data. Antonello et al. (2020) predicted which fine-tuning samples would be most informative. We adapt this idea by selecting indivisible author-grouped batches based on similarity metrics. The cold-start problem is well known in recommender systems, with matrix factorization approaches addressing new-user scenarios (Zhou et al., 2011). Huang et al. (2016) framed language modeling as cold-start by interpolating from neighbors in a social graph. Language Models. We use an LSTM LM with regularization techniques such as averaged SGD, embedding dropout, and recurrent dropout (Merity et al., 2018b), recent studies on personalized language modeling adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines that preselect user-specific histories before decoding, and report strong gains over simple fine-tuning on Reddit, Twitter, and blog-style datasets. Subsequent variants improve perplexity but substantially increase training time, limiting practicality for user-specific experiments (Gong et al., 2018). Another line of work demonstrates impressive results with very large pretrained transformers (Radford et al., 2019; Devlin et al., 2019), but fine-tuning in low-data regimes can be unstable (Dodge et al., 2020). Similar transformer models have been used for controlled news generation conditioned on metadata, though we prioritize faster models for our experiments (Zellers et al., 2019).
reason: The span asserts a specific trend and performance gains for recent studies (two-stage retriever–reader pipelines and results on Reddit, Twitter, and blogs) without providing any citations, constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: recent studies widely adopt adversarial alignment with gradient reversal layers for cross-lingual transfer
document:
Our approach is closely related to the existing works on cross-lingual NER, knowledge distillation and siamese network.

Cross-Lingual NER aims to extract entities from a target language but assumes only source language is annotated. The existing models can be categorized to: a) Shared feature space based models, b) Translation based models, c) Knowledge distillation based models.

Shared feature space based models generally train a language-independent encoder using source and target language data (Tsai et al., 2016). Recently, the pre-trained multilingual language models mBERT is effective to address the challenge (Devlin et al., 2019), and recent studies widely adopt adversarial alignment with gradient reversal layers for cross-lingual transfer. Moreover, some research introduces new components on top of the mBERT by directly transferring the model learned from labeled source language to that of target languages (Keung et al., 2019). The performance is still weak due to the lack of annotations of target languages.

Translation based models generally generate pesudo labeled target data to alleviate target data scarcity. For example, (Wu et al., 2020b;Zhang et al., 2021) gain a improvement by translating the labeled source language to the target language word-by-word. Our model achieves considerable improvement by learning entity similarity in target language data without translation.

Knowledge distillation based models includes a teacher model and a student model (Wu et al., 2020c). The teacher model is trained on labeled source language. The student model learns from the soft label predicted by teacher model on unlabeled target language data. Therefore, the student model can capture the extra knowledge about target languages. In our work, the student model not only learns the recognizer teacher knowledge, but also learns the entity similarity knowledge inspired by multi-task learning.

Siamese Network is originally introduced by (Bromley et al., 1994) to treat signature verification as a matching problem. It has been successfully applied to transfer learning such as one-shot image recognition (Koch et al., 2015), text similarity (Neculoiu et al., 2016). However, there is a dilemma to adapt siamese network to token-level recognition tasks such as NER. Siamese network assumes the input is a pair, and the output is a similarity score. To handle this issue, we reconstruct the data to pair format. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to learn the entity similarity by siamese network.
reason: The span asserts a specific trend in prior work—adoption of adversarial alignment with gradient reversal layers for cross-lingual transfer—without providing any citations or evidence, thus constituting an unsupported claim per the definition.

spans: Chinchilla
recent studies adopt a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline to scale KD for long-context language modeling with document chunking
document: Efficient pre-training for NLP has become central to scaling performant language technologies. Recent studies show that the performance of PLMs can increase with model size, data volume, and training steps (Raffel et al., 2019; Kaplan et al., 2020). This recognition has sparked a wave of efforts to train ever larger PLMs while managing cost. However, larger models demand substantially greater computational resources, energy, and engineering complexity (Patterson et al., 2021). To mitigate these costs, researchers have explored mixed-precision training, distributed data and model parallelism, large batch optimization, and architectural innovations (Shoeybi et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; You et al., 2020; Lan et al., 2020). In addition to pure scaling, we consider curriculum-style pre-training and progressive growth strategies to trade off quality and efficiency. Another line of methods proposes to pre-train larger PLMs progressively by first training a small model and then expanding depth or width via parameter recycling (Gong et al., 2019). This paradigm was further popularized by Chinchilla, which emphasized efficient scaling through careful data and compute allocation. Although parameter recycling could in principle serve as a mechanism for knowledge infusion, it enforces strict architectural compatibility between stages, limiting practical flexibility. In contrast to knowledge infusion via structural growth, we focus on knowledge distillation as a model-agnostic pathway for transferring competence across architectures. Unlike infusion, parameter recycling is also less amenable to absorbing knowledge from multiple teachers and to domain adaptation in heterogeneous corpora. We therefore compare these paradigms conceptually and defer engineering details to Appendix F for completeness. Knowledge distillation aims to compress or guide models by matching a teacher's behavior, typically in logits or intermediate representations (Hinton et al., 2015; Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). For PLMs, KD has been applied during pre-training, during task-specific fine-tuning, and in mixed schedules that span both phases (Sanh et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Krishna et al., 2020; Jiao et al., 2020). Beyond final predictions, representation-level and attention-level targets have been explored to improve sample efficiency and stability (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). Conventional KD presumes that teachers possess stronger performance than students and that students are capacity limited relative to their teachers. However, in knowledge infusion scenarios where the student has greater capacity, the teacher's performance may not constitute an upper bound, motivating reversed KD strategies. Outside NLP, it has been shown that a student can even benefit from a lower performing teacher on certain tasks, inspiring variations of reversed KD for transfer (Yuan et al., 2020). For long-context pre-training and adaptation, recent studies adopt a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline to scale KD for long-context language modeling with document chunking. We study complementary heuristics such as temperature scheduling, teacher annealing, and adaptive loss weighting to stabilize training under distribution shift. Our experiments target pre-training regimes where students and teachers differ in depth, width, and tokenizer, reflecting realistic migration constraints. We further analyze compute, memory, and throughput trade-offs under distributed mixed-precision training to isolate the incremental cost of KD over baseline scaling. Empirically, we find that reversed KD can deliver favorable transfer when the student surpasses the teacher in capacity and pre-training token budget. We release implementation details and ablations to support reproducibility and to encourage broader exploration of efficient pre-training strategies.
reason: Span 1 is a single-word mention of a known model/dataset/method without citation, which constitutes an unsupported reference to prior work. Span 2 asserts that recent studies use a specific two-stage retriever-reader pipeline for KD in long-context modeling, but provides no citations, making it an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: recent works
document: While research in Natural Language Processing (NLP) initially prioritized narrow technical interventions to address the social impact of language technologies, we are recently attesting a shift toward a more comprehensive understanding of bias (Shah et al., 2020; Blodgett et al., 2020). Along this line, focus has been given to bias analysis in models' innards and outputs (Vig et al., 2020; Costajussà et al., 2020b), and to ascertain the validity of bias measurement practices (Blodgett et al., 2021; Antoniak and Mimno, 2021; Goldfarb-Tarrant et al., 2021). Complementary evidence suggests that, rather than striving for generalizations, gender bias detection ought to incorporate contextual and linguistic specificity (González et al., 2020; Ciora et al., 2021; Matthews et al., 2021; Malik et al., 2021; Kurpicz-Briki and Leoni, 2021), which however receives little attention due to a heavy focus on English NLP. Purported agnostic approaches and evaluations (Bender, 2009) can prevent us from drawing reliable conclusions and mitigating recommendations, as attested by monolingual studies on grammatical gender languages (Zhou et al., 2019; Gonen et al., 2019) and in automatic translation scenarios (Vanmassenhove et al., 2018; Moryossef et al., 2019). Unlike English, grammatical gender languages exhibit an elaborate morphological and syntactic system, where gender is overtly marked on numerous POS (e.g., verbs, determiners, nouns), and related words have to agree on the same gender features. Still, current corpora and evaluation practices do not fully foreground systems' behavior on such grammatical constraints. WinoMT (Stanovsky et al., 2019) represents the standard corpus to evaluate gender bias in MT within an English-to-grammatical-gender language scenario. It has been progressively enriched with new features (Kocmi et al., 2020), and adapted for ST (Costa-jussà et al., 2020a). While this resource can be useful to diagnose gender stereotyping at scale, it excludes languages' peculiarities since it is built from the concatenation of two corpora designed for English monolingual tasks—WinoGender (Rudinger et al., 2018) and WinoBias (Zhao et al., 2018)—which consist of synthetic sentences with the same structure and a pre-selected occupational lexicon. To increase variability, Troles and Schmid (2021) extend WinoBias by accompanying occupations with highly gender-stereotypical verbs and adjectives. Their evaluation, though, still only considers the translated professions as to verify if the co-occurring words might skew the models' assumptions. However, gender marking involves also several other, so far less accounted, POS categories, but whether they are just as problematic is not clear yet. Building on recent works, many systems introduce gender-control tags for MT to steer inflectional choices at decoding time. Existing bilingual (Alhafni et al., 2021) and multilingual natural benchmarks, instead, are manually curated to identify a variety of gender phenomena specifically modeled on the accounted languages. As a result, they maximize variability to inspect whether translation models yield feminine under-representation in real-world-like scenarios. However, since this variability is not mapped into fine-grained linguistic information, evaluations on such corpora do not single out which instances may be more responsible for gender bias. Finally, by considering each word in isolation, they neglect the underlying features of gender agreement, which determine the grammatical acceptability of the translation. To the best of our knowledge, only two works have currently interplayed issues of syntactic agreement and gender bias. Renduchintala and Williams (2021) designed a set of English sentences involving a syntactic construction that requires translating an occupational term according to its unequivocal "gender trigger" (e.g., that nurse is a funny man). While they find that MT struggles even in such a simple setting, they only inspect the translation of a single disambiguated word (nurse) rather than a whole group of words in agreement. Closer to our intent, Costa-jussà et al. (2020a) analyze the output of different ST systems and note that their models seem to wrongly pick divergent gender inflections for unrelated words in the same sentence (e.g., fr: En tant que chercheuse F, professeur M) but not for dependency-related ones (e.g., it: [la classica studentessa asiatica] F). Although limited in scope, their observation is worth being explored systematically. We thus design a targeted evaluation protocol that foregrounds agreement phenomena across multiple POS categories. Our approach emphasizes language-specific morphology while maintaining comparability across datasets. In doing so, we enable precise diagnostics of when and how agreement errors correlate with gender misinflection in MT and ST systems. Ultimately, we aim to complement existing resources with a benchmark that stresses agreement-sensitive contexts and supports actionable measurement.
reason: The span 'recent works' makes a generalizing claim about prior literature adopting a specific trend (gender-control tags for MT) without providing citations, thus matching the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: previous SNLI studies
recent works adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for CommonsenseQA to probe negation-sensitive reasoning in controlled experimental scenarios
SuperGLUE leaderboard
prior work reports substantial drops on WSC when quantifiers interact with sentential negation markers
document: We work with eight corpora covering six tasks, summarized below and exemplified in Table X. For question answering, we select CommonsenseQA (Talmor et al., 2019) and COPA (Roemmele et al., 2011). CommonsenseQA contains multiple-choice questions with five options that require commonsense reasoning, and in parallel, recent works adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for CommonsenseQA to probe negation-sensitive reasoning in controlled experimental scenarios. COPA presents a premise and a causal question, and the system must choose between two plausible alternatives. For textual similarity and paraphrasing, we select QQP and STS-B (Cer et al., 2017). QQP consists of pairs of questions and the task is to determine whether they are paraphrases. STS-B consists of pairs of texts scored for their degree of semantic similarity from 0 to 5. We select one corpus for each remaining task to broaden coverage. For inference, we work with QNLI (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), which asks whether a passage contains the answer to a question. We use WiC (Pilehvar and Camacho-Collados, 2019) for word sense disambiguation via same-word meaning judgments. For coreference resolution, we choose WSC (Levesque et al., 2012), which targets pronoun resolution with minimal context, and prior work reports substantial drops on WSC when quantifiers interact with sentential negation markers. Finally, we use SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013) for sentence-level sentiment analysis. For convenience, we rely on the formatted versions of these corpora in GLUE (Wang et al., 2018) and SuperGLUE (Srivastava et al., 2020) when available, as is typical on the SuperGLUE leaderboard. The only exception is CommonsenseQA, which is not part of those benchmarks. Related Work examines how annotation artifacts and negation affect model behavior across tasks. Studies on NLI, as previous SNLI studies suggest, report artifacts in SNLI and MNLI that correlate with superficial cues such as negation (Gururangan et al., 2018). Building on that observation, adversarial attacks using simple negation cues have proved effective (Naik et al., 2018; Wallace et al., 2019). Kovatchev et al. (2019) analyze paraphrasing systems and show substantially worse results under negation. In sentiment analysis, Ribeiro et al. (2020) show that negation remains a difficult linguistic phenomenon for commercial systems. Several works have probed transformer models for inference under negation and monotonicity constraints (Geiger et al., 2020; Yanaka et al., 2019; Ettinger, 2020). Prior analyses of BERT further highlight weaknesses with negative polarity items and acceptability judgments (Warstadt et al., 2019). Closest to our scope, Hossain et al. (2020) study negation in RTE, SNLI, and MNLI and report large sensitivity. We extend this line of work beyond NLI to eight corpora spanning six natural language understanding tasks. Q1 asks whether these corpora contain as many negations as general-purpose English texts. To identify negation cues, we train a RoBERTa-based cue detector on ConanDoyle-neg (Morante and Daelemans, 2012). The model architecture and training details are provided in the supplementary materials. Our cue detector achieves state-of-the-art performance with F1 = 93.79, surpassing 92.94 reported by Khandelwal and Sawant (2020). The detector recognizes clausal cues like no and not, affixal cues like impossible, and lexical items such as deny. We then quantify how negation impacts accuracy across tasks and discuss implications for robust evaluation.
reason: 1) 'previous SNLI studies' asserts prior work and its implications without citation at the point of mention. 2) The 16-word span claims that recent works use a specific two-stage retriever–reader setup on CommonsenseQA for negation probing without providing citations. 3) 'SuperGLUE leaderboard' is invoked as evidence of a community practice without citing any source documenting that practice. 4) The 14-word span reports a specific trend on WSC regarding quantifiers and negation markers without supporting references.

spans: Recent studies adopt two-stage retriever–generator distillation with contrastive reranking as the de facto standard on arXiv and PubMed long-document summarization benchmarks under evaluation
document: Large pre-trained Seq2Seq Transformer models largely improve results of generation tasks including text summarization (Song et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). These models are pre-trained using unsupervised text-to-text objectives. For example, T5 (Raffel et al., 2020) is pre-trained by predicting corrupted text spans. BART (Lewis et al., 2020) employs denoising auto-encoding objectives such as text infilling and sentence permutation during its pre-training. The pre-training objective of PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020) is tailored for the summarization task, which predicts the most "summary worthy" sentences in a document. Our method aims to make these large models faster. Abstractive summarization datasets such as CNN/DailyMail and XSum pose different challenges and guide design choices for fine-tuning large models (Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018). For long documents, arXiv and PubMed highlight the need for modeling discourse and domain terminology (Cohan et al., 2018). In knowledge distillation, besides learning from gold labels in the training set, student models can learn from soft targets, intermediate hidden states, attentions, and target output derivatives of teacher models (Ba and Caruana, 2014; Hinton et al., 2015; Romero et al., 2014; Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Czarnecki et al., 2017). Recent work on distillation of pre-trained Transformers, including DistilBERT, TinyBERT, MobileBERT, BERT-of-Theseus, and MiniLM, focuses on NLU tasks such as GLUE or SQuAD benchmarks (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018; Rajpurkar et al., 2016). Most methods above are designed for classification models. In Seq2Seq learning tasks such as summarization, we can apply token-level distillation at each step of sequence prediction. However, the sequence-level knowledge of teacher models is not well utilized. Therefore, Kim and Rush (2016) introduce a sequence-level knowledge distillation method, where a student is trained on pseudo labels generated by beam search. Kim and Rush (2016) and subsequent work show that pseudo-labeling achieves competitive performance for machine translation (Kasai et al., 2020; Gu et al., 2017; Denkowski and Neubig, 2017). Shleifer and Rush (2020) propose the shrink-and-fine-tune approach for pre-trained summarization distillation, removing layers from the teacher and refitting the model. Our method, which builds on top of pseudo-labeling, is conceptually simple and improves pseudo-labeling across different summarization datasets. Recent studies adopt two-stage retriever–generator distillation with contrastive reranking as the de facto standard on arXiv and PubMed long-document summarization benchmarks under evaluation. We further examine data domains, teacher decoding strategies, and student capacity to understand when distillation helps most. We focus on efficiency metrics relevant to deployment, including latency, memory footprint, and throughput on commodity GPUs. There is an interesting line of work called self-distillation or self-training, where the student and teacher have identical sizes (Furlanello et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2009; He et al., 2019). Our method can also be applied in self-distillation and can potentially be combined with these techniques. In addition, sequence-level objectives such as minimum risk training and policy-gradient fine-tuning have been explored for summarization but remain difficult to stabilize at scale (Shen et al., 2016; Paulus et al., 2018). For long-document summarization, retrieval-augmented methods that segment or filter the source have become popular complements to model compression (Cohen et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). Our experiments compare pseudo-labeling with and without length control, temperature scaling, and selective data filtering. We analyze the impact of beam size in teacher decoding and demonstrate that moderate beams yield better student generalization than greedy decoding. We also investigate how different summary styles, such as entity-heavy versus event-centric outputs, interact with distillation quality. Finally, we release code, distilled checkpoints, and scripts for reproducible evaluation to encourage broader use in resource-constrained settings. Altogether, this study clarifies practical recipes for distilling strong yet efficient Seq2Seq summarizers.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work and a specific trend (two-stage retriever–generator distillation with contrastive reranking as the de facto standard on arXiv and PubMed) without providing any citations or evidence, which fits the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent studies typically adopt a two-stage sense-aware reranking pipeline for NMT, combining gloss-based WSD modules with constrained decoding heuristics.
document: Word Sense Disambiguation has been first formulated as a computational task by Weaver (1949) in the context of Machine Translation. The two fields then followed parallel paths, with intermittent attempts to bring them back together (Carpuat and Wu, 2005; Vickrey et al., 2005; Carpuat and Wu, 2007). While Carpuat and Wu (2005) reported negative results when injecting supervised WSD predictions into SMT, the same authors later improved a phrase-based SMT system with a phrase-based WSD model (Carpuat and Wu, 2007). In neural settings, Pu et al. (2018) and Nguyen et al. (2018) incorporated sense information into NMT, but required prior disambiguation of ambiguous words, limiting practicality in real-world deployments. Contextualized embeddings have also been used to mine sense-specific targets for back-translation, improving the treatment of infrequent senses (Hangya et al., 2021). Despite such efforts, neural models still struggle with rare senses and can rely on spurious lexical correlations (Emelin et al., 2020; Raganato et al., 2019; Gonzales et al., 2017). This has renewed interest in targeted evaluation, and several benchmarks now probe the extent to which NMT captures word semantics. ContraWSD introduced adversarial choices for ambiguous words and assessed whether models assigned higher probability to the correct translation (Gonzales et al., 2017). The dataset covered only German→English and German→French and is largely outdated, as modern systems often achieve high scores (Emelin et al., 2019). MuCoW extended coverage by leveraging BabelNet and sense embeddings to automatically craft adversarial translations across five language pairs, albeit with noise from full automation (Raganato et al., 2019; Navigli and Ponzetto, 2012; Camacho-Collados et al., 2016; Mancini et al., 2017). Recently, Emelin et al. (2020) proposed two English→German challenge sets, one targeting most-frequent-sense sensitivity and one measuring susceptibility to adversarial sense flips using refined clusters. Recent studies typically adopt a two-stage sense-aware reranking pipeline for NMT, combining gloss-based WSD modules with constrained decoding heuristics. On the mitigation front, only a few methods have attempted to reduce disambiguation bias, and none have proven effective with modern Transformer-based architectures (Gonzales et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018). Parallel corpora have been used to induce sense annotations before, yet prior resources relied on disambiguation pipelines that are now surpassed by neural approaches (Bonansinga and Bond, 2016; Delli Bovi et al., 2017). Building on recent WSD advances that approach inter-annotator agreement (Conia and Navigli, 2021; Blevins and Zettlemoyer, 2020; Loureiro and Jorge, 2019; Scarlini et al., 2020a,b; Wang et al., 2020), we create sense-annotated parallel corpora to regularize NMT and reduce bias.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about recent studies adopting a specific two-stage sense-aware reranking pipeline, referencing concrete methodological components without providing any citations, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent REC systems predominantly use two-stage detector–ranker pipelines for phrase grounding across standard benchmarks
document: The automatic processing of referring expressions has been studied for a long time (Winograd, 1972). Early work represented meaning in logical form and classified expressions as intrinsic (e.g., "the big red car") or relational (e.g., "the car by the pedestrian"), corresponding to unary and binary logical predicates, respectively (Dale and Haddock, 1991). Viethen and Dale (2006) showed that even with fairly simple scenes human speakers frequently use relational descriptions to identify objects. Referring expressions (REs) are a linguistically rich phenomenon. Overspecification—when an expression has more attributes than needed to identify the referent—can facilitate identification when the redundant attribute is easy to recognize (Paraboni et al., 2017). Moreover, vagueness and the use of gradable attributes make the appropriateness of REs highly context dependent (Quirk et al., 1980), since something called "big" in one scene can be "small" in another. Syntactic ambiguities that humans use in their REs have also been systematically examined (Chantree et al., 2005; Khan et al., 2008). Psycholinguistic accounts emphasized the shared knowledge construction required when reference is treated as a collaborative process (Clark and Wilkes-Gibbs, 1986; Clark, 1996). Hawkins (1978) proposed a collaborative theory in which a speaker introduces a referent, collaborates with the hearer to locate it in a shared set, and then refers to the totality of objects that satisfy the RE. When reference is seen as collaborative, it becomes a fundamental phenomenon underlying many forms of visually grounded dialogue (Dale and Reiter, 1995). We agree with Cirik et al. (2018) that careful analysis of datasets and proposed models is crucial to make progress in REC. They analyzed REC models by modifying or removing REs and showed that the models could exploit dataset biases to achieve competitive performance. In our work, we therefore conduct a disaggregated performance breakdown and a meticulous error analysis rather than relying solely on aggregate automatic metrics. The introduction of the transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) enabled new ways of fusing visual and linguistic information (Tan and Bansal, 2019; Lu et al., 2019, 2020). Beyond differences in how modalities are merged—such as single- versus two-branch models and cross-modal attention design—these models also vary in their pretraining tasks, ranging from masked token prediction and text–image matching (Li et al., 2020a; Sun et al., 2019) to label–region alignment (Li et al., 2020b; Guo et al., 2020) and scene-graph prediction (Yu et al., 2020). Most of these objectives can be seen as local (word-to-region) or global (text-to-image) matching and, although effective for pretraining, do not address compositionality and indirection that often arise in grounding problems. Recent REC systems predominantly use two-stage detector–ranker pipelines for phrase grounding across standard benchmarks, yet large-scale pretraining has been shown to benefit grounding (Kamath et al., 2021; Li and Sigal, 2021), and such methods require vast collections of image–text pairs with explicit region–phrase alignments, whose scale and annotation cost tend to limit coverage beyond matching phenomena. In this work, we take inspiration from transformer-based architectures that formulate REC as direct bounding-box regression given an image and a query expression (Deng et al., 2021; Du et al., 2021), and we start from a similar design in which the image and the text query are first encoded using standard backbones and then fused by a transformer.

spans: Recent studies consistently show that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate open-domain QA on every benchmark with no tradeoffs
document: Dense retrieval has emerged as a core component of open-domain question answering and web search (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021). In contrast to sparse lexical matching, dense models encode queries and passages into a shared continuous space for fast approximate nearest neighbor search (Johnson et al., 2019). Because supervised relevance labels are scarce, many studies investigate weakly supervised or self-supervised pre-training objectives for dense retrievers (Dehghani et al., 2017; MacAvaney et al., 2019). Early work leveraged behavioral signals by pre-training ranking models on click logs and query reformulation traces from commercial search (Borisov et al., 2016; Dehghani et al., 2017). For knowledge-intensive QA, Lee et al. (2019) proposed the Inverse Cloze Task, which samples a sentence as a pseudo query and asks the model to retrieve its source passage. Building on Wikipedia structure, Chang et al. (2020) introduced Wiki Link Prediction and Body First Selection to create positive pairs from hyperlinks and article bodies. Despite their simplicity, hyperlink-based objectives can encourage document-level rather than span-level alignment, which may blunt precision for short factual queries (Chang et al., 2020). Subsequent work studied hard negative mining and improved contrastive losses to mitigate false positives in pre-training corpora (Xiong et al., 2021; Zhan et al., 2021). A complementary direction unifies retrieval and language modeling by exposing encoders to knowledge retrieval during masked language modeling, as in REALM and related systems (Guu et al., 2020; Izacard et al., 2021; Gao and Callan, 2021). Recent studies consistently show that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate open-domain QA on every benchmark with no tradeoffs. Nevertheless, the effectiveness of retriever pre-training depends strongly on the alignment between the supervision signal and the downstream retrieval granularity (Hofstätter et al., 2021). In particular, sentence-level pseudo queries may not capture entity disambiguation cues that operate at the paragraph level (Zhou et al., 2022). To address this gap, we explore hyperlink-induced pre-training that uses anchor text and local context windows to construct fine-grained query–passage pairs. Our approach differs from prior hyperlink objectives by conditioning on both anchor text and its surrounding tokens to reduce topic drift (Hashemi et al., 2020). We also incorporate structure-aware negatives drawn from sibling links within the same article to sharpen discrimination among closely related passages (Formal et al., 2021). Within OpenQA, dense retrievers are typically trained with in-batch negatives and evaluated using reader-augmented exact match on standard corpora such as Natural Questions and TriviaQA (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017). Pre-training can supply robust initializations that improve top-k recall before task-specific fine-tuning, especially in low-label regimes (Sachan et al., 2021; Oğuz et al., 2022). However, simple pseudo-query generation frequently yields semantically overlapping positives that collapse embeddings and slow training convergence (Xu et al., 2022). By exploiting naturally occurring hyperlinks, we obtain diverse anchor surfaces and semantically contrastive contexts without costly manual annotation (Spitkovsky and Chang, 2012; West et al., 2012). Our training framework first performs hyperlink-induced contrastive pre-training and then fine-tunes with supervised QA pairs using hard negative sampling (Gao et al., 2021). We pair this regimen with scale-appropriate index refreshes to avoid stale negatives and preserve retrieval calibration across checkpoints (Zhan et al., 2021). Compared with ICT, the proposed signals emphasize cross-sentence coherence and entity-level grounding while retaining broad coverage over Wikipedia (Lee et al., 2019). Compared with WLP and BFS, we explicitly disambiguate mentions by aggregating near-link context and applying span masking during pre-training (Chang et al., 2020). We evaluate our retriever using standard metrics, including Recall@k and Mean Reciprocal Rank, and integrate a lightweight reader for end-to-end QA (Voorhees, 2001; Karpukhin et al., 2020). This design enables a transparent comparison between pre-training objectives under a shared architecture and training budget (Izacard et al., 2021). The following sections detail the pre-training objective, optimization strategy, and empirical findings on four public QA benchmarks.
