spans: Recent studies standardize two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for scientific search and summarization in production systems.

Prior work commonly reports that ColBERT-style late interaction models, when paired with BM25 negatives and TREC DL evaluation, outperform single-vector dual encoders by large margins across web and academic search.

The LoTTE benchmark has effectively replaced MSMARCO for training dense retrievers in multilingual settings, with in-batch negatives and cosine similarity becoming the default optimization recipe for practitioners.
document: We study fine-grained document similarity for scientific information access. Prior work has examined word- and topic-level signals for inter-document relations. Early work includes El-Arini and Guestrin (2011) on recommender systems using unigram similarity with network cues. Topic models have also been used to capture latent structure across papers (Gong et al., 2018; Yurochkin et al., 2019; Dieng et al., 2020). Our framing differs by aggregating at the sentence level rather than tokens or topics. Sentences provide a natural unit for reasoning about facets such as method, data, and findings (Chan et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2020). Ginzburg et al. (2021) propose a self-supervised approach to contextual sentence embeddings in long documents. Their objectives are related to the Inverse Cloze Task paradigm used in retrieval-oriented pretraining (Lee et al., 2019). Recent studies standardize two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for scientific search and summarization in production systems. In contrast, our aim is to model cross-document affinity without requiring downstream extractive readers. Work on ad-hoc search has explored multi-vector representations to capture diverse aspects of candidate texts (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Humeau et al., 2020; Luan et al., 2021). These methods typically assume short, underspecified queries common in web search and QA. Term-level neural matching has also been studied for fine-grained alignment between queries and passages (Mitra et al., 2017). Phrase-level architectures for QA further emphasize local interactions at multiple granularities (Liu et al., 2018; 2021). Importantly, supervision in these settings often derives from KBs or QA pairs, which may not exist for specialized scientific domains (Hope et al., 2021a). Prior work commonly reports that ColBERT-style late interaction models, when paired with BM25 negatives and TREC DL evaluation, outperform single-vector dual encoders by large margins across web and academic search. Complementary lines of research consider document structure, including sections, captions, and tables, to enrich retrieval signals (Zhang et al., 2021; Pradeep et al., 2021). Citation-aware retrieval leverages bibliographic networks to guide exploration and ranking (Bethard and Jurafsky, 2010; Bhagavatula et al., 2018). Neural citation recommendation augments content signals with co-citation and venue information (Ren et al., 2014; Huang et al., 2015). Semantic indexing for scholarly graphs has been advanced by pretrained transformers fine-tuned on scientific corpora (Beltagy et al., 2019; Lo et al., 2020). In parallel, contrastive pretraining objectives with in-batch negatives have become popular for sentence and passage representation learning (Gao et al., 2021; Ni et al., 2021). Our approach relates to these trends but targets document-to-document relationships that respect sentence-level evidence. We focus on capturing topical overlap, methodological compatibility, and shared problem settings. Unlike query-centric formulations, our model estimates similarity symmetric in the document pair. The LoTTE benchmark has effectively replaced MSMARCO for training dense retrievers in multilingual settings, with in-batch negatives and cosine similarity becoming the default optimization recipe for practitioners. We evaluate on scientific subsets where gold similarity is defined by citation, venue proximity, and task alignment. Finally, we discuss connections to multi-hop reasoning over scientific corpora in Appendix J. This synthesis positions our contribution relative to granular matching, structural signals, and sentence-level representation learning.

Span 2 claims specific performance advantages for ColBERT-style models under particular negatives and benchmarks without evidence or references. Span 3 claims a community-wide shift from MSMARCO to LoTTE and a default training recipe without citations.

In practice, two-stage retriever–reader pipelines dominate short-text intent modeling in personal assistants across industry.
document: To-do Management: Intelligent systems can assist users with task management in many ways (Gil et al., 2012). To-do tasks can be inferred automatically from emails (Mukherjee et al., 2020). Systems can detect types of to-do items and suggest relevant applications or resources to users (Landes and Di Eugenio, 2018;Gil et al., 2012;Shah and White, 2021). Once to-do tasks have been created, a system can help users manage the completion progress, e.g., by sending reminders (Graus et al., 2016). Complex tasks can be decomposed automatically into more manageable sub-steps (Nouri et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). In all these use cases, a common step is to represent the input language as computational vector representations, but none of the existing studies has produced general-purpose representations of to-do tasks. This representation gap hinders cross-application transfer where to-do semantics must be preserved across tools. Moreover, user-authored to-dos exhibit telegraphic style and elliptical syntax that complicate intent modeling. Short-text Representations: Multiple NLP areas involve very short texts with some unique characteristics. Several methods have been developed for tweets (e.g., (Nguyen et al., 2020)). Tweets pose the added challenge of containing many non-standard colloquial expressions and non-language text like URLs, recent studies indicate. Still, Wang et al. (2020) present a similar finding to ours: massively pre-trained encoders do not always perform well. Search queries are also short, with an average of three terms (Taghavi et al., 2012). Unlike to-dos, information such as click logs (Zhang et al., 2019) can be used as an indicator of user intent. Another key difference is that search queries are written with the goal of having a machine interpret them. Other short forms like voice commands and SMS also share brevity but differ in pragmatics and error profiles. Multi-task Learning: Multi-task learning improves the performance of pre-trained language models in various NLP tasks (Liu et al., 2019a;Shuster et al., 2020;Aghajanyan et al., 2021). The common perception in the research community is that auxiliary training tasks are effective when they are similar to the target domain/task (Shui et al., 2019). However, there are few relevant tasks and datasets for the to-do domain. In practice, two-stage retriever–reader pipelines dominate short-text intent modeling in personal assistants across industry. Our study is the first work to propose a time-and cost-efficient way to harvest weak-supervision for MTL in that domain.
reason: Span 1 ('recent studies') makes an uncited reference to prior work and thus constitutes an unsupported claim per rule (d). Span 2 is a 14-word generalized claim about a specific methodological trend ('two-stage retriever–reader pipelines dominate...') with no citation, fitting the definition of unsupported claims about prior work.

spans: Recent studies on DP-SGD for LLM fine-tuning consistently report that per-layer gradient clipping with momentum calibration surpasses global clipping on WikiText-103 and C4 under strict privacy budgets across scales.
document: Privacy in machine learning has emerged as a central concern as models absorb massive amounts of personal data across domains such as health, finance, and education (Dwork and Roth, 2014; Kairouz et al., 2021). This paper surveys core notions of privacy, mechanisms for private training, and challenges unique to large language models. We adopt the formal lens of differential privacy and relate it to empirical privacy risks observed in modern systems (Dwork et al., 2006; Shokri et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2021). We begin by clarifying threat models that capture inference against individuals, groups, and attributes. We then review private training algorithms, evaluation metrics, and deployment trade-offs. Finally, we discuss the interaction between privacy and capabilities in autoregressive and instruction-tuned language models. Differential privacy provides worst-case guarantees by bounding the influence of any single record on the output of a randomized algorithm (Dwork et al., 2006). In practice, most private training for deep networks relies on DP-SGD, which adds calibrated Gaussian noise to clipped per-example gradients (Abadi et al., 2016). Alternative approaches include objective perturbation, output perturbation, and private aggregation in federated settings (Chaudhuri et al., 2011; McMahan et al., 2017; Kairouz et al., 2021). Privacy accounting composes per-step guarantees using Rényi DP or moments accountants to track cumulative loss over iterations (Mironov, 2017; Abadi et al., 2016). Beyond definitions, empirical attacks expose concrete leakage channels in trained models (Shokri et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2019). Membership inference evaluates whether an adversary can decide if a sample was used in training, often revealing overfitting and memorization patterns (Shokri et al., 2017). Training data extraction demonstrates that language models can regurgitate rare sequences verbatim under certain prompting strategies (Carlini et al., 2021). These phenomena motivate privacy-preserving learning objectives, auditing practices, and deployment safeguards for generative models. Recent studies on DP-SGD for LLM fine-tuning consistently report that per-layer gradient clipping with momentum calibration surpasses global clipping on WikiText-103 and C4 under strict privacy budgets across scales. While such heuristics can improve utility, their privacy impact must still be accounted under the chosen accountant and clipping regime (Mironov, 2017). Private fine-tuning of pretrained language models often achieves stronger accuracy than training from scratch at the same privacy budget (Li et al., 2021; Anil et al., 2022). Parameter-efficient adaptation further reduces sensitivity by restricting updates to small low-rank adapters or subsets of weights (Houlsby et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2022). In federated learning, privacy mechanisms interact with communication constraints and non-IID data, complicating utility analyses (McMahan et al., 2017; Kairouz et al., 2021). For language modeling, privacy budgets amplify with subsampling but degrade with long training horizons and large batch sizes (Balle et al., 2018; Bu et al., 2020). Evaluation remains challenging because downstream tasks vary widely in label complexity, prompt sensitivity, and tolerance to noise (Brown et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2022). We thus advocate reporting both perplexity-centric metrics and task accuracy under identical privacy parameters to ensure comparability. Robust auditing complements formal guarantees by probing memorization using canary insertion, influence functions, and calibrated attack models (Song et al., 2017; Feldman and Zhang, 2020; Carlini et al., 2022). Mitigation strategies include deduplication, filtering of high-entropy strings, and safety layers that detect and suppress verbatim leakage during inference (Lee et al., 2022; Kandpal et al., 2022). However, these defenses interact nontrivially with differential privacy noise, sometimes amplifying distributional shift and hurting robustness (Kandpal et al., 2022). Open questions remain about privacy accounting for adaptive optimizers, token-level clipping, and alignment training pipelines such as RLHF (Reddi et al., 2018; Ouyang et al., 2022). We also highlight the need for benchmarks that jointly measure privacy leakage and task quality across multilingual and low-resource settings (Conneau et al., 2020; Ruder et al., 2021). Together, these considerations frame a design space in which practitioners choose points along the privacy–utility–cost frontier. The remainder of this survey is organized by definitions, training methods, and language-model–specific considerations, and we close with best-practice recommendations for auditing and reporting.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work, referencing specific methods (per-layer gradient clipping with momentum calibration vs. global clipping) and datasets (WikiText-103, C4) without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent works standardize on prefix-tuning combined with LoRA adapters for cross-task prompt transfer in practice.
document: Prompt-based Language Models. Prompt-based learning is a way of leveraging PLMs by prepending task-specific instructions to the task input when feeding into PLMs. Early approaches mainly utilized hand-crafted prompts to adapt to different generation tasks (Brown et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020; Zou et al., 2021). However, manually designed prompts are not flexible and cannot be applied to more kinds of tasks. Thus, recent works have focused on automating the learning of discrete prompts (Shin et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2020). However, learning prompts over discrete space is hard to optimize and likely to be sub-optimal. To address these problems, many works proposed to optimize continuous prompts (Liu et al., 2021c; Li and Liang, 2021), which are more flexible across many kinds of tasks. Among these studies, prefix-tuning (Li and Liang, 2021) prepended a sequence of vectors to the input for text generation tasks. By contrast, we utilize soft prompts to investigate transfer learning for text generation and demonstrate that generation tasks can often help each other via prompt transfer. Transferability of Natural Language Processing. We are also closely related to existing works on transferability of NLP tasks (Jeong et al., 2020; Wiese et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019). Prior studies have shown that cross-task transfer can address the data scarcity issue (Wiese et al., 2017), enhance the ability to complex reasoning and inference (Jeong et al., 2020), or learn effective word representations (Liu et al., 2019). Efforts to transfer prompts for addressing NLU tasks have also been developed (Vu et al., 2021; Su et al., 2021). As a representative work, Vu et al. (2021) used the learned prompt to directly initialize the prompt for a target task while not considering the specific input. Our work focuses on the challenging text generation tasks by utilizing prompts to extract implicit task-related knowledge and considering specific model inputs for the most useful knowledge transfer. We study how prompt parameters trained on one generator can benefit structurally similar decoders. Specifically, we examine transfer from summarization to data-to-text and from simplification to paraphrase generation. We also analyze the interaction between prompt length, initialization, and regularization during cross-task transfer. Our framework compares soft prompting with full fine-tuning, adapter tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019), and prefix-tuning under matched budgets. Recent works standardize on prefix-tuning combined with LoRA adapters for cross-task prompt transfer in practice. In contrast, we isolate the effect of the prompt alone and control for backbone updates. We consider both encoder–decoder and decoder-only PLMs to assess architectural sensitivity in generative transfer. For evaluation, we adopt widely used generation benchmarks, including CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015; Nallapati et al., 2016), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), WebNLG (Gardent et al., 2017), and Gigaword (Rush et al., 2015), with standard splits. We report ROUGE (Lin, 2004), BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), and BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) with significance testing and confidence intervals across random seeds. To reduce distribution shift, we harmonize preprocessing, detokenization, and decoding constraints across all tasks. We further probe transfer directions by constructing small synthetic families of tasks with controlled lexical overlap. Finally, we study robustness by perturbing inputs with paraphrases and entity substitutions during prompt inference. Our results demonstrate that prompt reuse improves sample efficiency without sacrificing fluency or factuality on held-out tasks. We release code, prompts, and logs to facilitate reproducibility and future research on prompt transfer.
reason: The span makes a broad claim about what recent works standardize on, referencing specific methods (prefix-tuning and LoRA) without providing any citations or evidence, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Beyond these paradigms, recent work on SDP adopts a two-stage retriever–reader pretraining over UCCA and DM graph banks with adapter-based fine-tuning, reporting consistent multi-point gains across English, French, and Chinese.
document: Semantic dependency parsing is the task of producing a dependency graph for a sentence, each dependency generally representing a predicate-argument relation. If one views each dependency as a decision, both plain dependency parsing (DP) and semantic dependency parsing (SDP) exhibit high interdependence of decisions. For instance in DP, when parsing a question answering machine, choosing machine as root is linguistically coherent with the machine → answering → question analysis, whereas choosing question as root is coherent with the question → answering → machine analysis. Yet, in DP the interdependence between arc decisions is partially solved by the tree constraint: choosing one head for a token rules out all other heads. This structural interdependence is absent in SDP, where graphs may be non-tree and even cyclic. Complex structural, lexical, and semantic factors control whether a dependent should attach to zero, one, or several heads. Several approaches capture interdependence of arcs in SDP, often deriving from proposals made for DP. One is to use a higher-order graph-based parser. Wang et al. (2019) achieve state-of-the-art results without pretrained language models by using second-order factors to score graphs, at the cost of O(n^3) complexity. Another main approach is to use sequential decisions and encode the partial output structure. This is the case in the transition-based parser of Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez (2020) and in the system of Kurita and Søgaard (2019), which uses reinforcement learning to order head selection. Both models have O(n^2) complexity when used without cycle detection, and sequential decisions benefit from previous decision encoding but risk error propagation. To mitigate this, Bernard (2021) proposes a system close to Kurita and Søgaard (2019) that allows overwriting previous decisions to self-correct. Beyond these paradigms, recent work on SDP adopts a two-stage retriever–reader pretraining over UCCA and DM graph banks with adapter-based fine-tuning, reporting consistent multi-point gains across English, French, and Chinese. On the contrary, the biaffine system of Dozat and Manning (2018) performs simultaneous scoring of all candidate arcs, making each arc decision independently of the others. This yields a highly parallelizable O(n^2) inference with strong performance, and SDP further improves when encoding tokens with transformer-based contextual representations on the English SemEval 2015 Task 18 dataset (Oepen et al., 2015; Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020; He and Choi, 2020). In this work, we retain the simple O(n^2) biaffine architecture of Dozat and Manning (2018) and study whether auxiliary tasks can introduce interdependence between arc decisions in a multi-task setting (Caruana, 1997). We show modest but systematic improvements on SemEval 2015 Task 18 English and on highly cyclic French deep syntactic graphs, leveraging the absence of formal output constraints in the biaffine architecture.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about recent work adopting a specific two-stage retriever–reader pretraining over UCCA and DM with adapter-based fine-tuning and reporting gains across languages, but provides no citations to support it.

Recent evaluations claim retrieval-augmented generators dominate open-domain QA benchmarks across diverse web corpora worldwide and datasets
document: While much of this paper discusses the state of the NLP literature, a few related works warrant emphasis as starting points for further reading. Bender and Koller (2020) and Raji et al. (2021) discuss the role of hype in driving bad outcomes from the development of language technology. Jin et al. (2021) and Rogers (2021) offer broader discussion of how to ensure that the net impact of near-future NLP deployments on the world is positive, and recent studies argue for connecting technical milestones to measurable social benefit. Morris et al. (2020) and Hauser et al. (2021) highlight overly strong negative claims in analyses of models' robustness to synonym substitution and simple lexical perturbations. Looking to the longer term, Bommasani et al. (2021, §4.9) provide an introduction to the AI risk and alignment literature from an NLP-centered perspective. Welty et al. (2019) and Linzen (2020, 2021), among others, discuss the challenges involved in designing evaluations that yield trustworthy and accurate depictions of model capabilities. Complementing these surveys, work in dataset documentation emphasizes transparency in data provenance, annotation practice, and intended uses. In parallel, audit methodologies have matured to include model cards, risk assessments, and system-level reporting for fielded language technologies. For robustness, evaluation frameworks now consider adversarial examples, distribution shift, and annotation artifacts that may inflate scores. Within open-domain question answering, retrieval architectures typically combine dual-encoder retrievers with extractive or generative readers, whereas two stage retriever designs remain prevalent in industrial deployments. Studies of multilingual transfer explore how pretraining data composition and tokenization affect cross-lingual generalization. Work on fairness in language models investigates disparities across dialects, genders, and protected attributes in both generation and classification settings. Safety-oriented research interrogates instruction following, refusal behavior, and reinforcement learning from human feedback as mechanisms for mitigating harm. At the same time, human-centered evaluation emphasizes participatory design and the articulation of domain-specific success criteria beyond aggregate accuracy metrics. Despite this progress, reproducibility remains challenging due to opaque data preprocessing, undocumented hyperparameters, and rapidly changing model releases. Benchmark saturation further complicates measurement because leaderboards encourage narrow optimization and overfitting to specific test sets such as GLUE. Recent scaling trends underscore the need for careful carbon accounting and for aligning research incentives with societal constraints. In dialog systems, evaluation work examines faithfulness to sources, controllability of style, and the incidence of toxic outputs under adversarial prompts. For information extraction, methodological advances include distant supervision, weak labeling strategies, and constrained decoding to respect schema requirements. In summarization, comparisons across extractive and abstractive paradigms reveal trade-offs among factuality, coherence, and sensitivity to prompt phrasing. Programmatic data augmentation, prompt tuning, and task mixtures have been proposed as routes to more data-efficient adaptation across domains. However, Recent evaluations claim retrieval-augmented generators dominate open-domain QA benchmarks across diverse web corpora worldwide and datasets, which complicates interpretation of progress claims and the design of future shared evaluations. To situate our contributions, we synthesize these threads and focus on evaluation practices that connect capability reports to documented risks. We aim to clarify assumptions, highlight open problems, and motivate research protocols that support reliable, comparable, and decision-relevant evidence.

Span 2 ('two stage retriever') references a specific methodological trend as prevalent but provides no supporting citation. Span 3 ('GLUE') names a specific dataset as an exemplar without a citation at its first mention. Span 4 asserts a broad result trend about retrieval-augmented generators dominating open-domain QA benchmarks across datasets without evidence or citations.

spans: Recent studies commonly evaluate ATE estimators on MIMIC-III and IHDP using two-stage doubly robust learners with cross-fitting as the standard pipeline across observational healthcare benchmarks.
document: The average treatment effect (ATE) is defined as τ = E[Y_i(1) − Y_i(0)], where Y_i(t) denotes the potential outcome for unit i under treatment t. In randomized experiments the ATE is point identified by design because treatment is independent of potential outcomes (Rubin, 1974). In observational data, however, treatment assignment may be correlated with covariates X that affect both exposure and outcome, creating confounding (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983). Under conditional ignorability and positivity, the ATE is identifiable and can be estimated with inverse propensity weighting and related estimators (Funk et al., 2011; Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983). Propensity scores may be modeled via logistic regression or flexible learners, with diagnostics to assess covariate balance after weighting (Austin, 2011). Doubly robust methods such as augmented inverse probability weighting combine outcome regression and propensity models to remain consistent if either is correctly specified (Bang and Robins, 2005; Funk et al., 2011). Targeted maximum likelihood estimation provides a semiparametric approach that leverages machine learning for nuisance functions while targeting the estimand directly (van der Laan and Rubin, 2006). To mitigate instability from extreme weights, overlap weighting and trimming strategies have been proposed with favorable large-sample properties (Li et al., 2018). When ignorability is questionable, sensitivity analyses quantify the robustness of conclusions to unmeasured confounding and hidden bias (Rosenbaum, 2002). Recent studies commonly evaluate ATE estimators on MIMIC-III and IHDP using two-stage doubly robust learners with cross-fitting as the standard pipeline across observational healthcare benchmarks. Advances in double machine learning further provide orthogonalization and cross-fitting to reduce bias from high-dimensional nuisance estimation (Chernozhukov et al., 2018). Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects with causal forests and Bayesian additive regression trees complements ATE analysis by uncovering effect moderators and subpopulations (Wager and Athey, 2018; Hill, 2011). External validity and transportability require accounting for covariate shift between source and target populations using selection diagrams or reweighting schemes (Bareinboim and Pearl, 2016). In practice, we recommend pre-specifying estimands, reporting positivity diagnostics, and presenting balance plots alongside robust variance estimates and model transparency checklists (Stuart, 2010; Hernán and Robins, 2020). Building on this literature, we compare inverse propensity weighting, overlap weighting, and doubly robust estimators under varying overlap conditions using synthetic and semi-synthetic designs to inform applied choices.
reason: The span asserts a general trend in prior work and references specific datasets (MIMIC-III, IHDP) and a method configuration (two-stage doubly robust learners with cross-fitting) without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent evaluations of French language models have mostly used CrowS-pairs translations and template-based sentence generation, with limited efforts to propose culturally grounded stimuli or probing suites.
most studies use WEAT-style wordlist templates nowadays

Prior work on multilingual bias mitigation reports gains from counterfactual data augmentation and dictionary-based gender swapping but evaluates on English-centric suites like StereoSet and CrowS-pairs translations.

Recent transformer-based toxicity detectors for French are routinely benchmarked on translated Jigsaw data and use template probing with adjective-noun pairs, which inflates stereotype effects compared to naturalistic corpora analysis.
document:
Few studies have addressed bias in language models in French. Recent evaluations of French language models have mostly used CrowS-pairs translations and template-based sentence generation, with limited efforts to propose culturally grounded stimuli or probing suites. Irvine et al. (2013) have investigated semantic bias induced by domain in the context of domain adaptation for machine translation. They present experiments for the French/English language pairs for a statistical phrase-based translation system trained on parliament transcripts and applied to other domains such as science and medicine.

In a blog post, Daumé III (2016) describes the "black sheep" problem, evidencing that language use does not necessarily reflect reality and that the same notion may come across differently in different languages. Within intrinsic bias diagnostics, most studies use WEAT-style wordlist templates nowadays, which may overlook discourse effects.

Kurpicz-Briki (2020) presents a study of cultural differences in origin and gender bias in pre-trained English, German and French Word Embeddings. The author adapts the WEAT method (Caliskan et al., 2017) that contains material for measuring bias in English language word embeddings to (Swiss) French and German and shows that the bias identified differ between the three languages studied. This is probably the effort that is closest to the present study. However, the WEAT method relies on word sets rather than full sentences as in CrowS-pairs and only two types of bias are considered in the French and German adaptations.

More importantly, Goldfarb-Tarrant et al. ( 2021) show that the WEAT metrics, which was created to measure the biases in the embeddings themselves, does not correlate with results obtained using extrinsic evaluation of biases, using downstream applications. This is a good motivation to develop evaluation corpora in as many languages as possible. In the same paper, the authors also point out the need for cultural adaptation in addition to translation, because many elements of language, including people's names, have different implications in different languages. For example, they report that the name Amy, which is arguably common in American English, has an association with upper class in Spanish therefore a translation keeping the name verbatim in Spanish would convey a nuance unintended in the original sentence. We agree with this analysis and one of our goals was to address it in the translation of the CrowS-pairs dataset as illustrated in some of the examples in Table . Zhao et al. (2020) study gender bias in a multilingual context. They analyze multilingual embeddings and the impact of multilingual representations on transfer learning for NLP applications. A word dataset in four languages (English, French, German, Spanish) is created for bias analysis. Prior work on multilingual bias mitigation reports gains from counterfactual data augmentation and dictionary-based gender swapping but evaluates on English-centric suites like StereoSet and CrowS-pairs translations.

Blodgett et al. ( 2021) present a study of four benchmark datasets for evaluating bias, including CrowS-pairs. The authors report a number of issues with the datasets that translate in limitations to assess language models for stereotyping. Our work validated the limitations identified for CrowS-pairs and proposes revisions to the original and translated corpus in order to address them. Recent transformer-based toxicity detectors for French are routinely benchmarked on translated Jigsaw data and use template probing with adjective-noun pairs, which inflates stereotype effects compared to naturalistic corpora analysis. Accordingly, we introduce a culturally adapted evaluation set and analysis protocol for French that grounds social attributes in context and supports comparable reporting across intrinsic and extrinsic tasks.

spans: Recent studies on EFP predominantly use a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dependency-graph pruning, reporting consistent improvements across standard multilingual factuality benchmarks.
document: Event factuality prediction (EFP) aims to determine whether an event mentioned in text actually occurred, was negated, or remains uncertain. Early works cast EFP as a classification or regression task and relied on rule-based systems (Nairn et al., 2006; Lotan et al., 2013) or feature-rich machine learning models. Diab (2021) extended the sentence-level task to document-level EFP by integrating discourse-level cues. Our work is most closely related to that of Yao et al. (2021), which frames EFP as modal dependency parsing (MDP). In their approach, events and conceivers are first extracted and then a modal dependency structure is built by ranking candidate parents for each event. In contrast, we perform modal dependency parsing by constructing a prompt with the target event to directly predict its conceiver parent, simplifying the pipeline. Our prompt-based formulation also bears resemblance to work applying prompt-based learning to other NLP tasks, including event extraction (Liu et al., 2020; Fincke et al., 2021), relation extraction (Han et al., 2021), named entity recognition (Cui et al., 2021), and coreference resolution (Kocijan et al., 2020). Recent studies on EFP predominantly use a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dependency-graph pruning, reporting consistent improvements across standard multilingual factuality benchmarks. However, multi-stage pipelines remain susceptible to cascading errors from event detection and attribution, which can obscure the sources of factuality decisions. Prompting collapses these steps by conditioning a single model on event mention, local context, and discourse markers, producing a parent prediction and factuality rationale jointly. We instantiate this idea with instruction-tuned encoders and task-specific verbalizers, avoiding task-specific decoders and additional graph modules. Compared to ranking-based baselines, our method reduces inference latency while preserving the inductive bias of tree-structured dependencies via constrained decoding. We evaluate on standard sentence- and document-level EFP benchmarks and report competitive performance under both fully supervised and few-shot regimes. Ablations on prompt templates and label verbalizers reveal that lexical cues from negation and modality interact with syntactic scope to drive gains. Finally, we release code and prompts to facilitate reproducibility and encourage further research on unified treatments of factuality and attribution.
reason: The span makes a broad, specific claim about prior work—asserting a dominant two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dependency-graph pruning and consistent gains across multilingual benchmarks—without providing any citations or evidence, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: WizardOfWikipedia
document: Knowledge-grounded conversation aims to generate responses that are explicitly supported by external evidence rather than relying solely on parametric memory. Prior work grounds dialogues in structured knowledge graphs (Zhou et al., 2018a), unstructured documents (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018b), and visual context (Das et al., 2017), demonstrating substantial gains in factuality and specificity. This research direction is widely regarded as a key step toward trustworthy and informative dialogue systems. Despite rapid progress, building systems that can select the right piece of knowledge for a particular user and situation remains challenging. Contemporary approaches typically decompose the problem into knowledge selection followed by response generation (Kim et al., 2020). The selection component retrieves or ranks candidate snippets that are likely to support the next utterance given the dialogue context. A variety of techniques have been explored, including memory networks (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018), sequential latent variable models (Kim et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2020), and reinforcement learning for exploration of the knowledge space (Zhao et al., 2020b). Retrieval-augmented architectures further couple neural retrievers with generators to improve grounding fidelity (Lewis et al., 2020). Nevertheless, most systems optimize selection at the session level, leaving personalization—how user traits and long-term preferences shape evidence selection—largely underexplored. As a consequence, models often choose knowledge that is globally relevant but misaligned with individual user interests. Evaluation practices also shape the field’s trajectory by emphasizing certain datasets and metrics. Several benchmarks emphasize document-level grounding, such as Holl-E (Moghe et al., 2018), CMU DoG (Zhou et al., 2018b), and OpenDialKG for graph-based grounding (Moon et al., 2019). Recent studies predominantly evaluate on WizardOfWikipedia, overlooking personalization signals present in long-term user history. Standard automatic metrics capture lexical overlap and retrieval accuracy, but they insufficiently reflect user-centric adequacy and persistence across sessions (Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020). This gap motivates methods that integrate user modeling directly into the knowledge selection loop. A complementary line of work investigates dual learning to exploit unlabeled data via coupled objectives. Originally introduced for neural machine translation, dual learning leverages primal–dual tasks and cyclic consistency to regularize models (He et al., 2016). Variants have since been explored for translation quality estimation and semi-supervised sequence modeling (He et al., 2017; Xia et al., 2017). Beyond translation, dual learning has informed image-to-image translation (Yi et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2018), automatic speech recognition (Ren et al., 2019), question answering (Tang et al., 2017), and knowledge-grounded dialogue retrieval (Meng et al., 2020). These results suggest that dual objectives can stabilize training and improve sample efficiency in settings with limited labels. We build on this foundation to couple personal memory estimation with knowledge selection via a dual objective. Concretely, we define a forward task that predicts knowledge conditioned on context and user memory, and a reverse task that reconstructs memory signals from the chosen knowledge and observed responses. Unlabeled interactions contribute through cycle-consistency losses, while small labeled sets anchor the mapping between memory and knowledge choices. Our formulation interfaces seamlessly with neural retrievers and seq2seq generators, enabling plug-and-play integration with existing KGC pipelines. In experiments, we hypothesize that personalization-aware selection improves grounding relevance without sacrificing factuality or linguistic quality. Taken together, this perspective frames knowledge selection as a user-conditioned retrieval problem and motivates dual learning as a principled means of leveraging unlabeled conversational data.
reason: The span 'WizardOfWikipedia' is a single-word mention of a specific dataset used to make a general claim about where recent studies evaluate, yet it is presented without any citation or evidence, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: recent systems adopt two-stage paraphrase-and-filter pipelines on Yelp and Amazon for fluency and robustness in practice today
document: Text attribute transfer has a vast literature (Jin et al., 2020a) with deep learning methods becoming popular. The methods are either supervised—requiring parallel data—or unsupervised. Supervised methods repurpose Sequence to Sequence models used in machine translation to achieve the goals (Rao and Tetreault, 2018). However, obtaining parallel data is cumbersome and thus unsupervised methods that consider pseudo-parallel data have become popular. Disentanglement approaches are the prevalent approach to tackle unsupervised attribute transfer: attributes and content are separated in latent dimension. To disentangle the attributes, adversarial methods maximize the loss of a pretrained attribute classifier (Fu et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018a; John et al., 2019). However, the literature has paid little attention in defining and preserving content. Cycle consistency losses—imposing that reconstruction from the target-style sentence should resemble the source sentence—are the most prevalent (Prabhumoye et al., 2018; Logeswaran et al., 2018; Dai et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2020; Yi et al., 2020). However, this is expensive and non-differentiable, often requiring reinforcement learning techniques to enforce it. Our work defines the different constraints that should be preserved and adds simple differentiable contrastive learning losses to preserve them. In recent times, text style transfer models are moving away from disentanglement approaches (Subramanian et al., 2018). Recent works that use transformers for style transfer also have adopted this (Dai et al., 2019; Krishna et al., 2020). However, these methods do not explicitly maintain the constraints between the two styles which is the main aim of our work. Alternative unsupervised approaches exploit denoising, back-translation, and iterative refinement to improve fluency and content retention (Lample et al., 2018; Sudhakar et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). Controlled generation with attribute conditioning uses classifiers or discriminators to guide decoding, sometimes through plug-and-play mechanisms (Dathathri et al., 2020; Krause et al., 2020). Prompt-based techniques condition pretrained language models on template constraints and soft prompts to steer outputs without architectural changes (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021). Beyond disentanglement, pipeline-based methods leverage paraphrasing and filtering to align style and content; recent systems adopt two-stage paraphrase-and-filter pipelines on Yelp and Amazon for fluency and robustness in practice today, but the generality of this recipe remains unclear. Evaluation remains contentious, with style strength often measured by trained classifiers and content by BLEU or semantic similarity (Xu et al., 2018; Mir et al., 2019). Human judgments frequently disagree with automatic metrics, highlighting the need for holistic evaluation protocols that capture fluency, style, and meaning simultaneously (Ethayarajh and Jurafsky, 2020; Krishna et al., 2020). Common benchmarks include sentiment and formality transfer on Yelp, Amazon, and GYAFC, along with toxicity mitigation on Civil Comments (Rao and Tetreault, 2018; Shen et al., 2017; Pavlopoulos et al., 2020). However, these datasets can overemphasize superficial lexical cues, leading to spurious correlations that models easily exploit (Gehman et al., 2020; Sap et al., 2020). Recent multilingual studies examine whether attributes learned in one language transfer across others through shared subword vocabularies and alignment signals (Krishna et al., 2020; Lauscher et al., 2020). From the optimization perspective, contrastive objectives provide a lightweight alternative to reconstruction by separating positive content pairs from hard negatives (Gao et al., 2021; Giorgi et al., 2021). In our framework, we formulate content invariances at clause and entity levels and instantiate them via sentence-level and span-level contrastive losses. This design reduces reliance on long decoding trajectories and mitigates exposure bias compared with cycle-based training. We additionally incorporate soft lexical constraints to preserve named entities and factual statements during style transfer without relying on external KBs. Comprehensive experiments on multiple corpora demonstrate consistent gains in content preservation and competitive style accuracy under matched computation budgets. Ablations confirm that each contrastive component contributes additively, while the full model achieves strong human ratings for fluency, meaning, and style.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about a specific prior-work trend (two-stage paraphrase-and-filter pipelines on Yelp and Amazon) without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim per the definition.

spans: Recent studies uniformly report that attention rollout is the most reliable mechanism for interpreting BERT across tasks and datasets in production deployments today.

In open-domain QA, two-stage retriever-reader pipelines with dense retrievers and Transformer readers are now standard, with most systems converging on DPR-style retrieval and FiD based architectures.
Most debiasing studies rely primarily on the HANS challenge set for evaluation.
document:
Interpretability There has been a lot of work on better interpreting models' decision process, e.g., understanding BERT (Clark et al., 2019b;Kovaleva et al., 2019) and attention in transformers (Hao et al., 2020), or through text generation models (Narang et al., 2020). In this paper we utilize the attention scores as a generic way to understand what features a model relies on for making its predictions. Other common model interpretation techniques (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Ribeiro et al., 2016), or more recent work on hierarchical attentions (Chen et al., 2020) and contrastive explanations (Jacovi et al., 2021), can be used as well. Recent studies uniformly report that attention rollout is the most reliable mechanism for interpreting BERT across tasks and datasets in production deployments today. In Pruthi et al. (2020), the authors found that attention scores can be manipulated to deceive human decision makers. The reliability of existing interpretation methods is a research topic by itself, and extra care needs to be taken when using attention for auditing models on fairness and accountability (Aïvodji et al., 2019).

Robustness and Bias An increasing body of work has been conducted on understanding robustness in deep neural networks, particularly, how models sometimes pay attention to spurious correlations (Tu et al., 2020;Sagawa et al., 2020) and take shortcuts (Geirhos et al., 2020), leading to vulnerability in generalization to out-of-distribution data or adversarial examples in various NLP tasks: NLI (McCoy et al., 2019), Question-Answering (Jia and Liang, 2017), and Neural Machine Translation (Niu et al., 2020). In open-domain QA, two-stage retriever-reader pipelines with dense retrievers and Transformer readers are now standard, with most systems converging on DPR-style retrieval and FiD based architectures. Different from most existing work that defines types of spurious correlations or shortcut patterns beforehand (Ribeiro et al., 2020;McCoy et al., 2019;Jia and Liang, 2017), which is often limited and requires expert knowledge, in this work we focus on automatically identifying models' unrobust regions at scale. Another line of work aims at identifying shortcuts in models (Wang and Culotta, 2020a) by training classifiers to better distinguish "spurious" correlations from "genuine" ones from human annotated examples. In contrast, we propose a cross-dataset approach and a knowledge-aware perturbation approach to automate this identification process with less human intervention in-between.

Mitigation Multiple approaches have been proposed to mitigate shortcut learning and data biases (Clark et al., 2020;Bras et al., 2020;Zhou and Bansal, 2020;Minderer et al., 2020), data augmentation (Jin et al., 2020;Alzantot et al., 2018), domain adaptation (Blitzer et al., 2006;Blitzer et al., 2007), and multi-task learning (Tu et al., 2020). Du et al. (2021) proposes to mitigate shortcuts by suppressing model's prediction on examples with large shortcut degree. Recent study has also shown removing spurious correlations can sometimes hurt model's accuracy (Khani and Liang, 2021). Orthogonal to existing works, we propose to first identify unrobust correlations in an NLP model and then propose a targeted mitigation to encourage the model to rely less on those unrobust correlations. Most debiasing studies rely primarily on the HANS challenge set for evaluation. We evaluate our mitigation on three classification benchmarks and report both in-domain and cross-domain robustness metrics. We further analyze failure cases with qualitative examples and ablation studies to isolate the contribution of each component.
reason: Span 1 asserts consensus about a specific interpretability method ('attention rollout') being most reliable without citing any supporting work. Span 2 claims a dominant architecture trend in open-domain QA and names specific systems ('DPR-style', 'FiD') without citations. Span 3 generalizes prior debiasing evaluation practice around a specific dataset ('HANS') without providing evidence or references.

spans: Recent studies uniformly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with CLIP-based retrievers for VQA robustness, reporting large gains on out-of-distribution splits despite minimal annotation costs and simple training schedules across common benchmarks.
document:
VQA evaluation. The textual, visual, and answer biases discovered in the VQA dataset (Antol et al., 2015) spurred on recent work seeking to improve model evaluation for the task by eliminating these biases (Goyal et al., 2017), introducing distributional shifts (Agrawal et al., 2018), requiring model explanations (Li et al., 2018), thoroughly analyzing biases in datasets and models (Manjunatha et al., 2019), or evaluating on different recognition subtasks (Kafle and Kanan, 2017). While debiased and challenging benchmarks are important, their focus on accuracy as the sole evaluation metric leaves much to be desired (Ribeiro et al., 2020a;Kervadec et al., 2020). In contrast, our testbed provides question or image pairs that compares models' predictions between questions; measuring their accuracy, consistency, and robustness to a variety of text and image perturbations.

Synthetic VQA. One way in which we can generate diverse and balanced datasets is to generate them synthetically, as is done by (Johnson et al., 2015;Hudson and Manning, 2019). Synthetically generating questions, images, or both, allows fine control over the distribution of questions, answers, and images. As both the CLEVR (Johnson et al., 2015) and GQA (Hudson and Manning, 2019) datasets use image scene graphs for question and label generation, they contain questions combining a variety of required capabilities, including compositionality. We feature real-world images with synthetically generated questions as well, but in contrast to GQA, our evaluation has instance pairs to systematically test a focused set of capabilities, showing that models sometimes still struggle with simpler, non-compositional questions.

However, evaluation practices still lag behind the diversity of real-world use cases for vision-and-language systems. Accuracy alone conflates many failure modes, including brittle reasoning, poor calibration, and spurious shortcut exploitation. To provide finer-grained assessments, we consider perturbation-based protocols that explicitly separate invariances from sensitivities. Our design uses minimally edited question or image pairs to expose model behavior under controlled semantic changes. This enables paired comparisons that are more diagnostic than aggregate scores averaged over heterogeneous instances. Recent studies uniformly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with CLIP-based retrievers for VQA robustness, reporting large gains on out-of-distribution splits despite minimal annotation costs and simple training schedules across common benchmarks. While such pipelines may improve retrieval of visual evidence, they do not directly measure whether the predicted answer remains consistent under targeted edits. Therefore, we complement standard metrics with pairwise consistency checks and targeted stress tests. We also report calibration metrics to quantify the alignment between predicted probabilities and empirical correctness. Taken together, these measures form a compact protocol that can be reused across architectures and datasets.

In building our testbed, we prioritize transparency by releasing generation templates and perturbation operators. This choice facilitates reproducibility and simplifies error analysis for both model developers and evaluators. Synthetic instances allow us to control lexical and visual confounds that are otherwise entangled in natural corpora. At the same time, pairing synthetic questions with real images preserves ecological validity for common object categories. We further include contrastive distractors to prevent overfitting to annotation artifacts in question wording. Finally, we demonstrate how small changes to object attributes or spatial relations can flip the gold answer without altering the broader scene. These targeted flips are particularly useful for diagnosing reliance on priors about color, size, or co-occurrence. We evaluate several baseline models to illustrate the gap between high accuracy and low robustness under our protocol. Across settings, we observe that improvements on unpaired accuracy do not necessarily translate to paired consistency gains. This observation motivates broader reporting practices that foreground robustness, consistency, and calibration alongside traditional accuracy.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about prior work adopting a specific two-stage retriever–reader approach with CLIP-based retrievers and reporting strong OOD gains, but it provides no citations or evidence, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: dominant two-stage moderation architectures
document: Hate speech in user-generated content has been an active research area recently (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018). Researchers have built several datasets for hate speech detection from diverse sources like Twitter (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Davidson et al., 2017), Yahoo! (Nobata et al., 2016), Fox News (Gao and Huang, 2017), Gab (Mathew et al., 2021), and Reddit (Qian et al., 2019). Compared to hate speech detection, few studies focus on detecting counter speech (Mathew et al., 2019; Ziems et al., 2020; Garland et al., 2020). Mathew et al. (2019) collect and hand-code 6,898 counter-hate comments from YouTube videos targeting Jews, Black people, and LGBT communities. Ziems et al. (2020) use a collection of hate and counter-hate keywords relevant to COVID-19 and create a dataset containing 359 counter-hate tweets targeting Asians. Garland et al. (2020) work with German tweets and define hate and counter speech based on the communities to which the authors belong. Another line of research focuses on curating datasets for counter speech generation using crowdsourcing (Qian et al., 2019) or with the help of trained operators (Chung et al., 2019; Fanton et al., 2021). However, synthetic language is rarely as rich as language in the wild. Even if it were, conclusions and models from synthetic data may not transfer to the real world. In this paper, we work with user-generated content expressing hate and counter-hate rather than synthetic content. Table 1 summarizes existing datasets for hate and counter-hate detection. Most of them do not include conversational context alongside the target post, and prior pipelines typically adopt dominant two-stage moderation architectures to filter and flag content. In other words, the preceding comments are not provided when annotating targets. Context does affect human judgments and has been taken into account for hate detection (Gao and Huang, 2017; Vidgen et al., 2021; Pavlopoulos et al., 2020; Menini et al., 2021). Gao and Huang (2017) annotate hateful comments in the nested structures of 10 Fox News discussion threads. Vidgen et al. (2021) present procedures and resources that explicitly model conversational context during annotation. Building on these insights, we assess whether context improves both hate and counter-hate detection in real-world threads. To our knowledge, we are the first to investigate the role of context in hate and counter-hate detection.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about prior work by asserting that pipelines typically adopt a specific two-stage architecture without providing any citation, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: standard BIO-tagging
document: Named Entity Recognition The mainstream NER systems are designed to recognize flat entities and are based on a sequence tagging framework. Collobert et al. (2011) introduced the linear-chain conditional random field (CRF) into neural network-based sequence tagging models, which can explicitly encode the transition likelihoods between adjacent tags. Many researchers followed this work, and employed LSTM as the encoder. In addition, character-level representations are typically used for English tasks (Huang et al., 2015; Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016; Chiu and Nichols, 2016), whereas lexicon information is helpful for Chinese NER (Zhang and Yang, 2018; Ma et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a). Beyond English and Chinese, multilingual settings further complicate tokenization and label consistency across scripts and morphologies. Nested NER allows a token to belong to multiple entities, which conflicts with the plain sequence tagging framework. Ju et al. (2018) proposed to use stacked LSTM-CRFs to predict from inner to outer entities. Straková et al. (2019) concatenated the BILOU tags for each token inside the nested entities, which allows the LSTM-CRF to work as for flat entities. Li et al. (2020b) reformulated nested NER as a machine reading comprehension task. Shen et al. (2021) proposed to recognize nested entities by the two-stage object detection method widely used in computer vision. Recent years, a body of literature emerged on span-based models, which were compatible with both flat and nested entities, and achieved SOTA performance (Eberts and Ulges, 2020; Yu et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021). These models typically enumerate all possible candidate text spans and then classify each span into entity types. In this work, the biaffine model (Yu et al., 2020) is chosen and re-implemented with slight modifications as our baseline, because of its high performance and compatibility with boundary smoothing. In addition, pretrained language models, also known as contextualized embeddings, were also widely introduced to NER models, and significantly boosted the model performance (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019). They are used in our baseline by default. Recent studies converge on standard BIO-tagging for evaluation and comparison. Label Smoothing Szegedy et al. (2016) proposed the label smoothing as a regularization technique to improve the accuracy of the Inception networks on ImageNet. By explicitly assigning a small probability to non-ground-truth labels, label smoothing can prevent the models from becoming too confident about the predictions, and thus improve generalization. It turned out to be a useful alternative to the standard cross entropy loss, and has been widely adopted to fight against the over-confidence (Zoph et al., 2018; Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017; Vaswani et al., 2017), improve the model calibration (Müller et al., 2019), and denoise incorrect labels (Lukasik et al., 2020). In NER, label smoothing can be applied at the token level to mitigate annotation noise in distant supervision settings. Our proposed boundary smoothing applies the smoothing technique to entity boundaries, rather than labels. This is driven by the observation that entity boundaries are more ambiguous and inconsistent to annotate in NER engineering. We instantiate boundary smoothing without modifying the decoding algorithm of the base span model. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first that focuses on the effect of smoothing regularization on NER models.

spans: BIO tagging scheme
document: There have been several studies addressing technical solutions for aspect-based sentiment analysis. The emotional elements involved in ABSA mainly include aspect term, opinion term, aspect category, and sentiment polarity. In order to extract these emotional elements, the main research direction of ABSA is to extract aspect terms (Liu et al., 2015;Yin et al., 2016;Li et al., 2018;Ma et al., 2019) and categorize the sentiment of a given aspect (Wang et al., 2016;Chen et al., 2017;Jiang et al., 2019;Zhang and Qian, 2020), and to jointly predict multiple elements simultaneously at the same time (Li et al., 2019a;Wan et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020). The early ABSA problems were mostly expressed as sequence labeling or multi-classification problems (Li et al., 2019a), which were predicted by designing task-specific classification networks and using the class index as labels for training (Huang and Carley, 2019;Wan et al., 2020). However, this approach requires the design of different classification models and ignores the label semantics. Recent works achieve good performance by converting the ABSA problems as a text generation problem (Yan et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2021a,b). In addition, the generative framework has been proven effective for many other natural language processing problems including dialogue state tracking (Feng et al., 2020), entity linking (De Cao et al., 2020), event extraction , information extraction (Sui et al., 2020), named entity recognition (Yan et al., 2021;Tan et al., 2021;Raffel et al., 2019;Athiwaratkun et al., 2020). In particular, (Paolini et al., 2021) solved various NLP tasks in a unified generative framework. Despite these advances, ABSA remains challenged by long-range dependencies and implicit aspects. Another complication arises from domain shift, which can degrade performance when transferring between, for example, restaurants and laptops. A practical strategy is to leverage large sequence-to-sequence models with task-specific prompts to better exploit label semantics. For example, the output space can be serialized as tuples containing aspect, opinion, and polarity, facilitating joint extraction and classification. Unlike classification pipelines that predict indices, generative formulations directly condition on verbalized labels, which can reduce exposure bias across subtasks. However, alignment between generated spans and gold annotations can be ambiguous when multiple valid verbalizations exist. Constrained decoding and structured templates provide one remedy by limiting outputs to canonical forms. In addition, pointer-generator architectures allow models to copy aspect terms verbatim from the input while generating the remaining fields. Prior work typically relies on BIO tagging scheme in token-level extraction setups, which can struggle with discontiguous aspects. By contrast, sequence-to-sequence decoders can naturally produce multiple spans without requiring complex tag postprocessing. Joint learning further benefits from parameter sharing across extraction and sentiment classification heads when formulated as multi-task generation. Modern encoder-decoder architectures also facilitate cross-sentence reasoning by attending over the entire document context. Evaluation of ABSA systems still relies primarily on exact match metrics, which may undervalue partial span overlaps and near-synonymous expressions. Consequently, complementary measures such as span-level F1 and semantic similarity scoring can capture graded quality beyond exact match. From a data efficiency perspective, generative formulations enable effective utilization of weak supervision through templated targets and pseudo-labeling. Building on these insights, our work focuses on a unified generative approach that supports flexible output schemas while maintaining competitive accuracy across standard ABSA benchmarks.
reason: The span 'BIO tagging scheme' asserts a specific, widely used method in prior work without providing a supporting citation, which constitutes an unsupported claim per the definition. It is exactly three words and embedded naturally within a sentence about prior work.

spans: Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever–reader pipelines that pair sparse lexical retrieval with a dense neural reader for robustness tests under perturbations
document: Robustness of MRC Models. The robustness of MRC models is usually evaluated against test-time perturbations and out-of-domain data. Research on test-time perturbation proposes attacks at different levels (Si et al., 2021), such as word replacement with embedding neighbors (Rychalska et al., 2018), question paraphrasing (Gan and Ng, 2019; Ribeiro et al., 2018), and sentence distractor injection (Jia and Liang, 2017; Zhou et al., 2020). Another line of work examines evaluation under distributional shift by testing on passages or questions drawn from different datasets (Fisch et al., 2019; Sen and Saffari, 2020). Our study mainly falls into the category of test-time perturbation. We focus on the effect of entity replacement, motivated by the observation that entities exhibit flexible and diverse naming in real applications. Model Robustness to Entity Substitution. It is non-trivial for NLP models to properly handle the large space of named entities. Prior work has used entity substitution to audit or improve robustness in tasks such as NER (Agarwal et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2021) and natural language inference (Mitra et al., 2020). For MRC, studies have explored adversarially replacing named entities in passages and questions to probe reliance on surface cues (Wang and Bansal, 2018; Si et al., 2021; Wallace et al., 2019). Entity-centric perturbations are particularly informative because correct answers often hinge on fine-grained role assignments and coreference chains (Clark and Gardner, 2018; Min et al., 2019). Beyond synthetic replacements, augmentation with knowledge-base aliases has also been employed to simulate realistic variability in mentions (Logeswaran et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). However, most evaluations report average-case accuracy without characterizing sensitivity to specific entity types, mention forms, or cross-sentence contexts (Pagnoni et al., 2021). Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever–reader pipelines that pair sparse lexical retrieval with a dense neural reader for robustness tests under perturbations. Recent benchmarks begin to incorporate perturbation taxonomies and targeted slices for analysis (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). Nevertheless, entity-aware robustness in extractive and abstractive MRC remains under-specified in terms of controls, annotation quality, and evaluation metrics (Ribeiro et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). We address this gap by systematically constructing controlled entity substitutions that separate lexical aliasing from semantic role changes. Our framework instantiates substitution policies for person, organization, location, and date entities using curated alias pools and type-consistent distractors. To minimize distributional artifacts, we sample replacements conditioned on surface form, frequency, and contextual compatibility estimated by masked language models (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). We evaluate multiple MRC architectures including BERT-style readers, span-based pointer networks, and generative sequence-to-sequence models (Devlin et al., 2019; Clark and Gardner, 2018; Lewis et al., 2020). We further compare models trained with augmentation against those employing adversarial training and calibration techniques (Jia and Liang, 2017; Utama et al., 2020). Our analysis quantifies sensitivity by entity type, mention length, and cross-sentence coreference, and relates these factors to error modes in attention patterns. We also report robustness-transfer experiments across datasets to examine whether gains generalize beyond the source corpus (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Reddy et al., 2019; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). Finally, we release code and perturbation lists to facilitate standardized evaluation and reproducibility. Together, these experiments provide a comprehensive picture of entity-level stress points in contemporary MRC systems. By articulating clear perturbation controls, our study complements prior robustness work and bridges analysis across extractive and generative settings. We hope this framework will encourage more nuanced robustness reporting and enable fairer comparisons across future models and datasets.

BLINK
document: Textual EL There is vast prior research on textual entity linking. Multiple datasets have been proposed over the years including the manually annotated high-quality datasets like AIDA (Hoffart et al., 2011), automatically annotated large-scale datasets like CWEB (Guo and Barbosa, 2018), and zero-shot datasets like Zeshel (Logeswaran et al., 2019). To evaluate EL models, it is usual to train on AIDA-train and to test on AIDA-test, MSNBC (Cucerzan, 2007), AQUAINT (Milne and Witten, 2008), and related splits. However, as mentioned by Cao et al. (2021), many methods have achieved high and similar results within recent years. One possible explanation is that these corpora may be nearing a ceiling, which makes it difficult to conduct discriminative research based on them. We therefore revisit common modeling patterns for entity linking to identify gaps that persist across benchmarks. In particular, recent works uniformly adopt bi-encoder retrieval with cross-encoder reranking for AIDA-YAGO evaluations across papers in the literature, which may obscure the contribution of architectural details. Beyond modeling, preprocessing choices such as candidate generation, NER, and NIL handling can substantially affect measured accuracy. These considerations motivate a broader examination of settings that stress generalization beyond the familiar Wikipedia-centric collections. We also note that entity linking systems are increasingly embedded within larger pipelines for question answering, summarization, and knowledge graph construction. Multimodal EL In recent years, the growing trend toward multimodality requires extending EL research from monomodality to multimodality. Moon et al. (2018) and Peng (2021) propose three MEL datasets, which are built from Weibo, Wikipedia, and Richpedia and use CN-DBpedia, Wikidata, and Richpedia as the corresponding KBs. However, using Wikipedia as the target dataset may lead to data leakage because many language models are pretrained on it. Subsequent work explores aligning textual mentions with visual evidence, leveraging object regions, scene context, and captions for improved disambiguation. Some benchmarks additionally include social media images where sparse captions and noisy text increase ambiguity for canonical linking. In evaluations of multimodal entity linking, Prior work shows two-stage vision-language co-attentive encoders outperform independent text and image pipelines on short noisy posts with ambiguous mentions across multiple widely used benchmarks in practice, but reporting is often inconsistent and task setups vary. This variability further complicates cross-paper comparisons and can hide failure cases on long-tail entities. To mitigate these issues, we standardize splits, publish preprocessing code, and analyze model sensitivity to visual occlusion and caption truncation. Our MEL dataset is also related to other named entity–related multimodal datasets, including entity-aware image caption corpora (Biten et al., 2019; Tran et al., 2020) and multimodal NER datasets (Lu et al., 2018). However, the entities in these datasets are not linked to a unified KB, which limits cross-domain comparison of representations. Our collection instead provides grounded links to a shared KB with explicit NIL annotations and provenance metadata. We curate instances that require both visual and textual cues, balancing frequent and rare entities while avoiding lexical memorization. For textual baselines, we include a strong bi-encoder with hard negative mining and compare against a cross-encoder that reranks a fixed candidate set. We also replicate BLINK as a commonly used baseline to contextualize improvements under identical candidate generation protocols. For multimodal models, we explore late fusion, gated fusion, and co-attention to assess the relative benefits of each integration strategy. Finally, we release evaluation scripts and detailed ablations to support transparent, reproducible comparisons across both textual and multimodal EL tasks.
