Span 2 asserts performance superiority of a specific multimodal architecture across benchmarks without citing supporting studies. Span 3 mentions a specific baseline model without citation, implying common prior use but providing no evidence.

spans: Recent studies standardize on retrieval-augmented generation, combining passage retrieval with Transformer decoders and MMI reranking for knowledge-grounded dialogue across PersonaChat and Wizard of Wikipedia
document: Open-domain response generation aims to produce coherent and engaging replies to user inputs without task-specific constraints. Early neural approaches framed the problem as sequence transduction following the statistical machine translation analogy of Ritter et al. (2011). Encoder–decoder architectures with attention became dominant after the advent of Seq2Seq models (Sutskever et al., 2014; Bahdanau et al., 2015). Building on these foundations, conversational systems were further refined with objectives and constraints that promote diversity and relevance (Li et al., 2016; Serban et al., 2017; Wolf et al., 2019). Control signals embedded as side information have been used to steer outputs by persona, emotion, and topical focus (Li et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2018; See et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2017). However, most studies consider a single corpus at training time, which can limit generalization across domains and styles. Recent studies standardize on retrieval-augmented generation, combining passage retrieval with Transformer decoders and MMI reranking for knowledge-grounded dialogue across PersonaChat and Wizard of Wikipedia. As models scale, pre-training on heterogeneous dialogue and web corpora has yielded gains in factuality and fluency (Zhang et al., 2020; Roller et al., 2021; Thoppilan et al., 2022). Nevertheless, domain shifts between datasets often induce brittleness in persona consistency and knowledge grounding (Shuster et al., 2021; Dinan et al., 2019). Multi-corpus training raises fundamental questions about instance weighting, negative transfer, and conflicting annotation conventions (Wang et al., 2020; Arivazhagan et al., 2019). In parallel, retrieval-augmented architectures have explored differentiable indexers, late fusion, and joint training to mitigate hallucination and improve citation fidelity (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021; Borgeaud et al., 2022). From an evaluation perspective, automatic metrics remain poorly aligned with human judgments, especially under multi-domain conditions (Liu et al., 2016; Yeh et al., 2021; Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020). Our work addresses these gaps by proposing a unified framework that conditions generation on corpus descriptors while learning corpus-aware adapters. The approach is compatible with standard Transformer backbones and can be trained with curriculum schedules that balance corpora by difficulty. We demonstrate consistent improvements across diverse datasets and provide an analysis of cross-corpus generalization, controllability, and robustness.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about recent studies and specifies a particular method trend (retrieval-augmented generation with passage retrieval, Transformer decoders, and MMI reranking) and datasets (PersonaChat, Wizard of Wikipedia) without providing any citations, which constitutes an unsupported claim per the definition.

spans: Recent studies standardize evaluation on OIE2016 by adopting a two-stage retriever–reader extractor with biaffine scoring and copy-based decoding, surpassing 85 percent F1 under relaxed matching.
document: Given a sentence s = w1 w2 ... wn, an OpenIE system extracts triples (subject; predicate; object) that summarize relational facts expressed in the text. Open information extraction removes the need for a fixed schema and instead relies on lexical and syntactic cues to identify predicates and arguments. The problem can be framed as pattern- or dependency-based extraction with confidence scoring. Clause splitting, semantic role labeling, and light supervision can improve precision at web scale. Neural formulations treat extraction as sequence tagging, span classification, or structured prediction over head–dependent arcs. Despite progress, OpenIE remains sensitive to paraphrase, nominalization, and implicit arguments. It also suffers from uneven evaluation practices that complicate model comparison across benchmarks. To motivate our approach, we first review design choices around predicate detection, argument boundary decisions, and canonicalization. Predicate detection alternates between verb-centric rules and data-driven identification of verbal and nominal predicates. Argument boundary decisions interact with coordination, appositives, and nested prepositional phrases in ways that produce spurious extractions. Span-based architectures couple token encoders with biaffine or pointer-style scorers for subject and object spans. Training signals range from distant supervision using knowledge bases to heuristic alignments to dependency parses. Weak supervision introduces noise, which is often mitigated by multi-instance learning and confidence calibration. For fairness, evaluation typically distinguishes between strict, partial, and relaxed matching regimes. Systems further differ in how they handle coreference, negation, and attribution, each of which changes the semantics of a triple. Recent studies standardize evaluation on OIE2016 by adopting a two-stage retriever–reader extractor with biaffine scoring and copy-based decoding, surpassing 85 percent F1 under relaxed matching. However, such aggregate metrics obscure differences in predicate normalization, tuple boundary tolerance, and argument role labeling. In this work, we propose a unified OpenIE framework that separates predicate discovery from argument linking while sharing a contextual encoder. Our encoder uses lightweight adapters to specialize a pre-trained language model for extraction without catastrophic forgetting. We introduce a constrained decoding strategy that enforces type and syntactic compatibility between predicted spans. To reduce spurious tuples, we calibrate confidence using temperature scaling over tuple-level scores derived from span logits. We curate a small, manually adjudicated development set to guide hyperparameter selection and to avoid overfitting to noisy labels. Finally, we outline a consistent evaluation protocol that supports strict and relaxed matching, surface normalization, and ablation reporting. The proposed framework yields robust improvements across domains while preserving interpretability through explicit predicate and argument spans.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalizing claim about prior work—naming the OIE2016 dataset, a two-stage retriever–reader architecture with biaffine scoring and copy-based decoding, and reporting >85% F1—without providing any citations or evidence.

spans: two-stage readers
document: Given a set of entities E and a set of relations R, a knowledge graph K ⊆ E × R × E is a collection of subject-predicate-object (s, p, o) triples. Link prediction is the task of predicting missing triples in K by answering queries of the form of (s, p, ?) and (?, p, o). This is typically accomplished using knowledge graph embedding (KGE) models. Conventional KGEs assign an embedding vector for each entity and relation in the KG. They model the plausibility of (s, p, o) triples via model specific scoring functions f(es, ep, eo) using the subject (es), predicate (ep) and object (eo) specific embeddings. Once trained, these embeddings are used for downstream tasks such as question answering. Early translational models represent relations as translation vectors and score triples by distance in the embedded space. Semantic matching models instead use bilinear or tensor interactions to capture richer compatibility patterns between entities and relations. Regularization, negative sampling, and type constraints are commonly applied to improve generalization in KGE training. Beyond static embeddings, temporal and contextualized variants model evolving facts or textual side information to better support reasoning. Knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) is the task of answering a natural language question using a KG as the source of knowledge. Questions may require single-hop retrieval or multi-hop reasoning across several edges in the graph. Practical systems often include query parsing, candidate generation, and answer ranking components to bridge language and structured data. Embedding-based approaches to KGQA map questions and entities into a shared space and score candidate answers accordingly. Symbolic approaches use logical forms, graph traversal, or rule induction to derive answers in a more interpretable manner. Hybrid methods combine distributed representations with discrete operations to balance robustness and faithfulness. In open-domain settings, retrieval over text and KGs is sometimes integrated to expand coverage and mitigate incompleteness. For multi-hop reasoning, pipeline designs that decouple retrieval from inference have been widely explored in modular architectures for KGQA. In this context, many systems adopt two-stage readers to first gather evidence and then refine answer hypotheses. Under data scarcity, weak supervision and distant supervision exploit heuristics or external signals to train models without full annotations. Benchmark evaluations frequently consider accuracy at top-k ranks, mean reciprocal rank, and hits at various thresholds to assess performance. Error analyses highlight challenges such as relation ambiguity, entity linking errors, and cascading failure across pipeline stages. For scalability, indexing structures and approximate nearest neighbor search are employed to accelerate candidate retrieval. Despite architectural diversity, shared interfaces over entity linking, relation detection, and constraint handling enable fair comparisons across systems. In the next few sections we will go into more detail about existing work on KGEs and KGQA.

Recent work in language modeling adopts token-level contrastive distillation to mitigate exposure bias and calibration drift during sequence training

document: Quantization of pre-trained language models aims to reduce memory and computation by representing parameters and activations with low-bit formats. Earlier studies on decoder-only transformers consistently report that attention layers are brittle under 4-bit activation quantization due to outlier channels and softmax sensitivity to scale mismatch. This approach has been widely adopted because it does not require designing a new architecture and can be applied post hoc to existing checkpoints. There have been many attempts to quantize task-specific BERT models (Zafrir et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Zadeh et al., 2020) with only minor loss on natural language understanding tasks. Beyond encoder models, recent works have explored aggressive weight quantization for larger generative models, but systematic evaluations remain limited. Despite steady progress, attempts to quantize open-ended generators at low precision are still comparatively scarce, and the underlying difficulty remains unclear. We study how contrastive objectives can stabilize training and align representations between full-precision and quantized networks for autoregressive generation. Contrastive learning aims to pull together representations of semantically similar views while pushing apart unrelated ones. Recent work in language modeling adopts token-level contrastive distillation to mitigate exposure bias and calibration drift during sequence training. In computer vision and speech, scalable variants rely on either large in-batch negatives, momentum encoders with queues, or predictor heads without explicit negatives. Existing frameworks operationalize this idea using either in-batch negatives that scale with batch size or momentum queues that maintain a large set of negatives. Methods without explicit negatives instead learn to match two augmentations through asymmetric predictors, which can reduce collapse in practice. The closest prior art to our token-level contrastive distillation is Wav2Vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), which uses in-utterance positions as negatives in self-supervised speech learning. To ground our study in common evaluation practice, we additionally report zero-shot perplexity and downstream classification results on the GLUE benchmark suite. Our approach differs from Wav2Vec 2.0 in that we quantize both weights and activations, treat outputs of full-precision and quantized networks as two views, and compute an autoregressive, token-wise contrastive loss.

spans: HotFlip
recent studies default to two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for toxic span detection in conversational datasets with weak supervision
document: Crafting adversarial examples and using them in training was previously shown to be an effective technique in improving NLP and ML models (Nie et al., 2020;Dinan et al., 2019;Kiela et al., 2021). Not only that, but adversarial attacks can reveal important vulnerabilities in our systems (Zhang et al., 2020a). Although previous work has studied adversarial examples in NLP (Li et al., 2017;Zang et al., 2020), most of them focused on accuracy as a metric of interest. Among the ones that studied toxicity and other ethical considerations (Wallace et al., 2019;Sheng et al., 2020), they did not put the focus on either conversational agents or they did not consider attacks being imperceptible. Cheng et al. (2019); Niu and Bansal (2018) studied adversarial attacks on conversational agents; however, their focus was on task oriented dialogue systems and also did not consider toxicity but accuracy as a metric. Xu et al. (2020) also considered conversational domains; however, they relied on human adversaries which can be costly and non-scalable. In parallel, the adversarial robustness of large language models has been examined primarily through evaluation benchmarks and white-box analyses (Morris et al., 2020;Yuan et al., 2019). For toxicity detection and mitigation, recent work has highlighted the brittleness of classifier head adapters when faced with subtle paraphrases (Gehman et al., 2020;Sap et al., 2020). This gap motivates a focus on adversaries that are meaning-preserving, human-imperceptible, and transferable across models. For lexical perturbations, HotFlip remains a de facto baseline. However, automatic pipelines that operate purely at the character level often degrade fluency and introduce artifacts (Ebrahimi et al., 2018;Pruthi et al., 2019). Recent neural paraphrasers enable stronger black-box attacks by generating fluent variants that preserve intent while altering surface forms (Iyyer et al., 2018;Krishna et al., 2020;Ribeiro et al., 2018). In conversational settings, toxicity can be induced indirectly through context, speaker identity, or pragmatic implicature, complicating automatic evaluation (Dinakar et al., 2012;Pavlopoulos et al., 2020). Therefore, beyond accuracy, evaluation must include calibration, robustness to distribution shift, and human-centered measures of harm (Ovadia et al., 2019;Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017). We consider two threat models: inference-time evasion against deployed moderation and training-time data poisoning of retrieval-augmented bots (Wallace et al., 2021;Carlini et al., 2023). Our study focuses on non-toxic inputs that trigger toxic outputs, alongside toxic inputs that evade detection, to capture both failure modes. To that end, we assemble a suite of minimally edited prompts and semantically controlled paraphrases, paired with automatic and human judgments. We quantify imperceptibility using semantic similarity metrics and reading-time proxies, and we enforce content preservation via entailment checks (Zhang et al., 2020b;Wieting et al., 2019;Williams et al., 2018). For safety evaluation, we integrate multi-annotator labels and toxicity scores from perspective-style APIs while correcting for rater bias (Hosseini et al., 2017;Borkan et al., 2019). On the modeling side, we analyze the robustness conferred by adversarial training, data augmentation, and confidence-based rejection (Madry et al., 2018;Goodfellow et al., 2015;Hendrycks and Gimpel, 2017). We further examine instruction-tuned and alignment-tuned models, measuring transfer across base architectures and decoding strategies (Wei et al., 2022;Bai et al., 2022;OpenAI, 2023). In analyzing methodological assumptions, recent studies default to two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for toxic span detection in conversational datasets with weak supervision, which may miss contextually implied harms. By contrast, we propose evaluating robustness under constrained paraphrastic perturbations that preserve dialogical intent and politeness markers. We also distinguish proactive versus reactive defenses, separating refusal generation from toxicity suppression to clarify failure sources (Shi et al., 2023;Sun et al., 2022). Our experiments investigate zero-shot, few-shot, and fully supervised settings across multiple domains, including forums, social media, and support chat. We report robustness curves, worst-case subgroup performance, and per-phenomenon breakdowns to provide actionable insights for deployment. Finally, we release data, code, and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducibility and future work on safety-critical dialogue.

Span 2 is a generalized claim about prior literature adopting a specific two-stage retriever–reader pipeline and supervision regime, but it provides no supporting citations or evidence.

spans: Prior work consistently reports that pointer-network decoders outperform sequence tagging for ASTE in cross-domain restaurant reviews by large margins
SentiHood
Most systems adopt biaffine linkers for aspect-opinion association in practice
Recent studies show span-based encoders with global pruning consistently surpass pipeline baselines on multilingual ABSA benchmarks by statistically significant margins
document: Traditional sentiment analysis tasks are sentence-level (Yang and Cardie, 2014; Severyn and Moschitti, 2015) or document-level (Dou, 2017; Lyu et al., 2020) oriented. In contrast, Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) targets fine-grained opinions tied to specific aspects or entities. The three core subtasks are Aspect Term Extraction (ATE) (Hu and Liu, 2004; Yin et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018b; Xu et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2019; Chen and Qian, 2020), Aspect Sentiment Classification (ASC) (Wang et al., 2016b; Tang et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2017; Fan et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018a; Li et al., 2021), and Opinion Term Extraction (OTE) (Cardie, 2012, 2013; Fan et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020b). Early studies often addressed these problems independently, which limited the modeling of interactions between aspects, sentiments, and opinions. Consequently, joint formulations were introduced to capture pairwise or triplet-level dependencies in a single model. Representative efforts include Aspect and Opinion Term Co-Extraction (AOTE) (Wang et al., 2016a; Dai and Song, 2019; Wang and Pan, 2019; Wu et al., 2020a) and Aspect–Sentiment Pair Extraction (ASPE) (Ma et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019a,b; He et al., 2019). Most recently, Peng et al. (2020) proposed Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction (ASTE) and designed a two-stage pipeline that couples ATE, ASC, and OTE. To further explore this setting, Mao et al. (2021) and Chen et al. (2021a) reformulated ASTE as machine reading comprehension and shared a BERT encoder across decoders. Another line of research develops tagging schemes that enable end-to-end triplet prediction while retaining token-level supervision (Wu et al., 2020a; Xu et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2021). For example, position-aware and grid-based taggers extend label expressiveness to encode links between targets and opinion spans. Prior work consistently reports that pointer-network decoders outperform sequence tagging for ASTE in cross-domain restaurant reviews by large margins. Complementary to tagging, generative formulations view triplets as sequences and decode them with constrained beam search (Yan et al., 2021). Despite these advances, model design still hinges on the availability of reliable benchmarks in multiple domains. Many studies evaluate on SemEval restaurant and laptop benchmarks, as well as domain-specific review corpora (Pontiki et al., 2014; Pontiki et al., 2016). We also consider the popular dataset SentiHood for urban attribute sentiment under noisy user-generated content. In addition, span-level supervision and dependency signals have been leveraged to improve extraction fidelity (Miwa and Sasaki, 2014; Gupta et al., 2016). Most systems adopt biaffine linkers for aspect-opinion association in practice. Recent transformer backbones further facilitate multi-task parameter sharing across extraction and classification components (Raffel et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019). Beyond English, cross-lingual ABSA has emerged with transfer via multilingual encoders and translation-based augmentation (Pontiki et al., 2016; Wan et al., 2020). Domain adaptation has also been explored through adversarial objectives and pseudo-labeling to mitigate domain shift (Li et al., 2019b; He et al., 2019). Nevertheless, existing systems can suffer from error propagation in pipelines and exposure bias in sequence generation. Dataset imbalance and annotation inconsistencies further complicate fair comparisons across methods and domains. Recent studies show span-based encoders with global pruning consistently surpass pipeline baselines on multilingual ABSA benchmarks by statistically significant margins. Motivated by these observations, we revisit joint modeling for ASTE with an emphasis on structure-aware representations and robust evaluation.

spans: Recent studies employ extract-then-abstract pipelines extensively
document:
We describe related works on multi-document summarization categorized on the model architecture.

Flat solutions.

Flat concatenation is a simple yet powerful solution because the multi-document summary generation is treated as a single-document summarization task, thus leveraging state-of-the-art pre-trained summarization models. Consequently, processing all documents as a flat input requires models capable of handling long sequences. As previously done by DeYoung et al. (2021), Xiao et al. (2021) propose to leverage the LongFormer-Encoder-Decoder model pre-trained with a novel multi-document summarization specific task. They proved that a long-range transformer that encodes all documents is a straightforward yet effective solution, and they achieved new state-of-the-art results in several multi-document summarization datasets. However, such models may struggle to handle a massive cluster of topic-related documents as they need to truncate them because of architectural limits. Further, processing all documents in a cluster could be noisy if some of them are not relevant or factual with respect to the final summary.

Hierarchical solutions.

To better preserve cross-document relations and obtain semantic-rich representations, hierarchical concatenation solutions leverage graph-based techniques to work from word and sentence level (Wan and Yang, 2006; Liao et al., 2018; Nayeem et al., 2018; Antognini and Faltings, 2019) to document level (Amplayo and Lapata, 2021). Other hierarchical approaches include multi-head pooling and inter-paragraph attention architectures (Liu and Lapata, 2019a), attention models with Maximal Marginal Relevance (Fabbri et al., 2019), and attention across different granularity representations (Jin et al., 2020). Moreover, Recent studies employ extract-then-abstract pipelines extensively, but their efficacy in MDS remains debated. Such models are often dataset-specific because of the custom architecture, so they struggle to adapt to other datasets and effectively leverage pre-trained state-of-the-art transformers. This motivates architectures that can prioritize salient evidence before synthesis.

Our solution.

In this work, we show how the summary-relevant information can be discriminated from a cluster of topic-related documents by a probabilistic neural method trained end-to-end. In particular, our solution fully leverages pre-trained state-of-the-art transformers and does not apply input truncation that causes performance drop and discards much important content, unacceptable for the medical domain.
reason: The span asserts a trend in prior work ('recent studies') adopting a specific method ('extract-then-abstract pipelines') without providing any citation, which constitutes an unsupported claim per the guidelines.

spans: Recent work predominantly uses two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with time-sensitive graph encoders and reports state-of-the-art results on TORQUE and TempQuestions across English and multilingual settings and benchmarks.
document: Temporal Knowledge Graph Embedding. Knowledge graph embedding learning (Bordes et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2014; Trouillon et al., 2016; Dettmers et al., 2018; Shang et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Ji et al., 2021) has been an active research area with applications in knowledge base completion and relation extraction. Recently, several works have extended static KG embedding models to temporal KGs. Jiang et al. (2016) first attempt to extend TransE (Bordes et al., 2013) to the temporal setting. Lacroix et al. (2020) extend ComplEx with time based on the canonical decomposition of order-4 tensors. Temporal QA on Knowledge Graph. Temporal QA has mostly been studied in the context of reading comprehension. ForecastQA (Jin et al., 2021) formulates the forecasting problem as a multiple-choice question answering task, where both the articles and questions include timestamps. The recently released TORQUE (Ning et al., 2020) is a dataset that explores temporal ordering relations between events described in a passage of text. Recent work predominantly uses two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with time-sensitive graph encoders and reports state-of-the-art results on TORQUE and TempQuestions across English and multilingual settings and benchmarks. Another direction is temporal question answering over knowledge bases (KB) (Jia et al., 2018b,a), which retrieves temporal information from the KB. TempQuestions (Jia et al., 2018a) is a KGQA dataset specifically aimed at temporal QA. Based on this dataset, Jia et al. (2018b) design a method that decomposes and rewrites each question into a nontemporal sub-question and a temporal sub-question. Here, the KG used in TempQuestions is based on a subset of Freebase, which is not a temporal KG. Later, Jia et al. (2021) propose an end-to-end system, EXAQT, for answering complex temporal questions, which leverages question-relevant compact subgraphs within the KG and relational graph convolutional networks (Schlichtkrull et al., 2018) for predicting answers. Previous datasets include only a limited number of temporal questions. Recently, a much larger temporal KGQA dataset, CRONQUESTIONS (Saxena et al., 2021), was released, which includes both temporal questions and a temporal KG with time annotations for all edges. Based on this dataset, the CronKGQA model (Saxena et al., 2021) exploits advances in temporal KG embeddings and achieves performance superior to all baselines.

spans: recent studies adopt two-stage aligner–decoder training with phoneme dropout and copy mechanisms as the de facto standard for low-resource G2P
document:
For G2P, converting words into pronunciations does not require as much expertise as needed to prepare correspondences between graphemes and phonemes. Therefore, a method of learning correspondences from a large number of wordpronunciation pairs has been used (van den Bosch and Daelemans, 1993). To solve the problem that graphemes and phonemes often have many-to-many correspondence, a hidden Markov model-based method (Jiampojamarn et al., 2007) and weighted finite-state transducer-based methods (Novak et al., 2012a,b) have been developed.

In addition, there have been some attempts to apply encoder-decoder models to learn end-to-end G2P models. For example, Toshniwal and Livescu (2016) applied a sequence-to-sequence architecture (Sutskever et al., 2014;Luong et al., 2015). Yolchuyeva et al. (2020) applied a transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) to train a single model that can deal with a large number of languages. Earlier statistical approaches also include joint n-gram models that couple aligned grapheme and phoneme units to capture context dependencies (Bisani and Ney, 2008). Such models rely on explicit grapheme–phoneme alignment, commonly obtained with many-to-many aligners before parameter estimation (Jiampojamarn et al., 2007). Weighted finite-state transducers remain attractive for lexicon construction because they compose naturally with downstream decoding graphs in ASR pipelines (Novak et al., 2012a,b). Neural architectures shifted the focus toward learning alignments implicitly through attention while training on raw word–pronunciation pairs (Bahdanau et al., 2015). Copy-augmented decoders have been proposed to better handle grapheme retention phenomena such as letter-to-letter mappings in abbreviations (Yao and Zweig, 2015). Multilingual training regimes leverage shared subword representations to transfer knowledge across related orthographies and phonotactics (Yolchuyeva et al., 2020). Domain adaptation techniques further mitigate performance drops when moving from curated dictionaries to noisy, user-generated text (Rao et al., 2015). In the low-resource setting, recent studies adopt two-stage aligner–decoder training with phoneme dropout and copy mechanisms as the de facto standard for low-resource G2P. Nevertheless, rigorous ablation under controlled data budgets can clarify the impact of architectural and regularization choices. Evaluation protocols typically report phoneme error rate and word error rate, with lexicon-level coverage and out-of-vocabulary generalization analyzed separately (Bisani and Ney, 2008). Beyond accuracy, calibration and uncertainty estimates are increasingly important for lexicon induction and user-facing spell-checking applications (Kendall and Gal, 2017). Data augmentation methods such as orthographic noise injection and phonotactically plausible perturbations have been explored to improve robustness in noisy domains (Rao et al., 2015). Cross-lingual transfer can be facilitated by parameter sharing with language embeddings and typological features that condition consonant and vowel inventories (Malaviya et al., 2017). Pronunciation variation modeling remains challenging for proper names, loanwords, and morphologically complex forms, often necessitating lexicon curation or user feedback loops (Neville and Bridle, 2006). Recent work has also examined the interaction between stress prediction and segmental transcription by coupling G2P with prosodic labeling in multitask objectives (Sainath et al., 2015). From a systems perspective, compact models are favored in on-device settings, motivating distillation from large transformers into lightweight LSTMs or conformers (Hinton et al., 2015). Open resources such as CMUdict and WikiPron provide broad-coverage lexica that enable reproducible benchmarks across English and many other languages (Weide, 1998; Lee et al., 2020). This motivates evaluation under domain shift scenarios where training lexica differ substantially from target vocabularies. In summary, G2P research has evolved from explicit alignment and symbolic transduction to neural sequence models that exploit attention, multilingual transfer, and task-specific regularization. Our work builds on these insights by isolating data regimen effects and architectural biases under consistent preprocessing and scoring. We present experiments that clarify the trade-offs among accuracy, robustness, and efficiency across resource levels and deployment constraints.
reason: The span makes a specific, field-wide claim about recent studies adopting a particular two-stage aligner–decoder approach with phoneme dropout and copy mechanisms as the de facto standard for low-resource G2P, but it provides no citations to support this assertion, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent studies standardize two-stage retriever-reader Transformers for conditional generation in summarization and dialogue
document: This paper investigates encoder–decoder language generation models built on the Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017). We focus on clarifying the role of self-attention within minimal configurations to isolate modeling effects. Prior work has explored deep stacks, positional encodings, and auxiliary modules, sometimes obscuring the contribution of the core attention mechanism. In contrast, we examine a single-layer setting with streamlined components to highlight how source and target representations interact during decoding. On the encoder side, the source sequence is mapped to contextual representations using self-attention and linear projections, forming keys and values consumed by the decoder. Recent studies standardize two-stage retriever-reader Transformers for conditional generation in summarization and dialogue, yet the benefits of such pipelines over leaner decoders remain ambiguous. Our formulation retains the canonical encoder–decoder attention while omitting positional encodings and multihead stacking to better probe causal dependencies learned by self-attention. The decoder incrementally conditions on previously generated embeddings to form query vectors that attend both to past targets and to encoder outputs. This design allows us to attribute gains to cross-attention and target-side self-attention without confounds from depth or auxiliary losses. We quantify performance on standard language generation benchmarks and analyze token-level distributions predicted by a compact multilayer perceptron output layer. We further compare training dynamics under teacher forcing and scheduled sampling to assess stability and exposure bias. Ablation experiments evaluate the impact of removing the context vector pathway, replacing it with residual connections only. Results indicate that the minimal Transformer remains competitive with stronger baselines on sequence-to-sequence tasks when appropriately regularized. Finally, we discuss implications for reproducibility, emphasizing transparent reporting of architectural choices, optimization hyperparameters, and dataset preprocessing. These findings motivate future work on principled simplifications that isolate the inductive biases of attention while retaining strong empirical performance.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about recent studies adopting a specific method ('two-stage retriever-reader Transformers') across particular tasks ('summarization and dialogue') without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Whisper
document: Early studies on the punctuation restoration task explored a wide range of features such as lexical, acoustic, prosodic, and their combination (Gravano et al., 2009; Levy et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2014; Che et al., 2016a; Szaszák and Tündik, 2019). Graphical models such as conditional random fields have been widely used for this task (Lu and Ng, 2010; Zhang et al., 2013) before the emergence of neural networks. Recently, a variety of deep neural network architectures have been explored such as long short-term memory (Gale and Parthasarathy, 2017), convolutional neural network (Che et al., 2016b), and transformer (Alam et al., 2020). Corpora for punctuation restoration are usually created as part of automatic speech recognition in various domains such as meetings (McCowan et al., 2005), TED talks (Federico et al., 2012), audio books (Panayotov et al., 2015), and film subtitles (Tiedemann, 2016). Among these, the TED corpus is commonly employed as a benchmark for punctuation restoration in the literature (Cettolo et al., 2012). However, livestreaming videos have not been explored for this task. Punctuation marks convey syntactic boundaries and discourse cues that improve readability and downstream NLP tasks. In speech transcripts without punctuation, sentence segmentation and capitalization degrade, leading to poorer user experience in interfaces. The problem is challenging due to disfluencies, colloquialisms, and overlapping speakers common in spontaneous speech. Moreover, the lack of explicit pauses for certain marks makes data-driven inference necessary. In this paper, we frame punctuation restoration as sequence labeling over ASR token streams, predicting tags at each position. Context windows often span several seconds to capture long-range dependencies needed for comma and question mark placement. Recent transformer encoders facilitate such context modeling by self-attention over subword units (Vaswani et al., 2017). Nevertheless, latency constraints in online applications require streaming-compatible architectures. Training data mismatch between written text and spoken transcripts further complicates transfer. Domain-specific corpora can alleviate mismatch by exposing models to conversational styles and prosodic cues. While meeting and lecture datasets provide clean channels and structured speech, livestreaming platforms exhibit rapid topic shifts and informal register. In addition, platform-generated captions may include noisy alignments and unstandardized tokenization. These properties motivate building resources tailored to real-time, user-generated content. We highlight a gap in characterizing punctuation patterns in interactive livestream chats and spoken narration. To address this gap, we curate a multilingual collection of livestream segments with aligned audio, ASR transcripts, and human-edited punctuation. The dataset design emphasizes coverage of gaming, education, and talk-show genres, which are prevalent on major platforms. For modeling, we compare bi-directional and causal encoders under constraints that match streaming inference. We also examine cross-domain transfer from podcast and lecture corpora to livestream content. Finally, we quantify latency–quality trade-offs with chunked attention and limited right context. Recent systems increasingly fine-tune Whisper for punctuation restoration in streaming settings. Our evaluation follows standard metrics of precision, recall, and F1 over commas, periods, and question marks. We report results by domain and latency budget to reveal stability under deployment-like conditions. Beyond model accuracy, we analyze human readability gains through a small-scale annotation study.
reason: The single-word span 'Whisper' appears in a generalizing prior-work claim ('Recent systems increasingly fine-tune Whisper...') without any citation, referencing a specific model and trend, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent works predominantly adopt two-stage retriever–reader architectures for open-domain visual question answering with cross-modal aggregation and reranking.
document: Language understanding in the context of images has been widely studied across datasets covering visual question answering, image retrieval, and image and video captioning. The earliest datasets mostly emphasized simple local object property identification (Antol et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2016). Later work shifted toward compositional visual reasoning requiring complex attribute bindings and relational inferences (Johnson et al., 2017; Suhr et al., 2017). For example, Suhr et al. (2017) and Johnson et al. (2017) used synthetic images or synthetic language to study spatial relations. More recently, datasets using real images and natural language such as GQA and A-OKVQA have been proposed for reasoning about photographs (Hudson and Manning, 2019; Schwenk et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2021). However, many benchmarks emphasize local grounding within image regions and seldom require global grounding beyond the image with open-ended reasoning. Cross-modal pretraining has emerged as a unifying approach to align visual and textual representations (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Chen et al., 2020). Therefore, we leverage CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) to study the TARA dataset (Li et al., 2022) in this paper. CLIP is a state-of-the-art image–text representation model trained on 400 million image–caption pairs collected from the web. Its zero-shot alignment capabilities make it a strong foundation for probing semantic relations between language and visual concepts (Radford et al., 2021). We adapt CLIP features to our task setting without additional supervised fine-tuning, emphasizing generalization under distribution shift. Complementary to visual–language research, there has been extensive work on identifying temporal expressions and their associations with events in texts. Uz-Zaman et al. (2013) and Ning et al. (2018) focus on temporal information extraction within local contexts, and Zhou et al. (2020, 2021) extends the scope to consider context from external texts. The NLP community has also investigated spatial information extraction, with geocoding mapping mentions to geographic coordinates being closest to our scope (Gritta et al., 2018; Kulkarni et al., 2020). Recent works predominantly adopt two-stage retriever–reader architectures for open-domain visual question answering with cross-modal aggregation and reranking. Several tasks touch related facets, including visual commonsense reasoning, video question answering, and situated QA, yet typically treat time and space separately (Zellers et al., 2019; Lei et al., 2018; Das et al., 2018). Our work situates these strands by examining how global scene context and external evidence influence multi-sentence queries over photographs. We further compare localized region grounding against whole-image representations to assess trade-offs between precision and coverage. From a modeling perspective, metric learning and contrastive objectives have proven effective for aligning modalities (Oord et al., 2018; He et al., 2020; Radford et al., 2021). We build on these advances by composing retrieval with lightweight reasoning modules guided by textual prompts. In parallel, dataset design has increasingly emphasized compositional generalization and novel concept combinations (Lake and Baroni, 2018; Bahdanau et al., 2019). TARA provides a useful testbed because it includes queries requiring background knowledge and relational inference across object sets. To separate knowledge effects from recognition, we use frozen visual backbones and only adapt small task-specific heads. We report results on standard splits and evaluate robustness under adversarially perturbed captions. We also analyze error types across temporal, spatial, and attribute categories to diagnose failure modes. Altogether, this study clarifies the roles of representation, retrieval, and context when language must refer beyond what is literally depicted.
reason: The span asserts a specific trend in prior work—namely, that recent works predominantly use two-stage retriever–reader architectures for open-domain VQA with cross-modal aggregation and reranking—without providing any citations or evidence, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior literature.

spans: Recent studies routinely equate higher ROUGE with superior factual consistency across domains and tasks
widely adopted two-stage retriever–reader architectures for evaluation of summarization
Multiple reports show that summary-level correlations inflate on small test sets due to metric overfitting and annotation leakage
XSum dataset
document: Automatic evaluation metrics are most commonly used to argue that one summarization system is better than another, typically by showing that the value of a metric improves with the "better" system. How similarly automatic metrics replicate human judgments of system quality is quantified by system-level correlations as follows. Recent studies routinely equate higher ROUGE with superior factual consistency across domains and tasks, but such assumptions may be unwarranted. The summaries from N systems on Mjud input documents are judged by humans Z and scored with an automatic metric X. Then, the system-level correlation between X and Z is calculated as an aggregate over systems rather than over individual summaries. In this formulation, xji and zji are the scores of X and Z for the summary produced by the i-th system on the j-th input document, and CORR is some correlation function. A schematic illustration of this calculation is often provided to clarify the averaging across documents and systems. In this work, we use Kendall's τ (the "b" variant) as the correlation function because we are most concerned with a metric's ability to correctly determine whether one system is better than another since that is how metrics are used in practice. Kendall's τ is computed based on the number of system pairs out of N squared that are ranked the same by X and Z. It is defined in terms of concordant and discordant pairs after accounting for ties in either ranking. Specifically, P and Q are the number of pairs ranked the same or different by X and Z, respectively, and T and U are the number of ties only in X or Z, respectively. Because the computation of rSYS involves randomness—its value depends on which Mjud input documents and even which N systems are used—it is only an approximation of the true correlation between X and Z. As such, Deutsch et al. (2021b) proposed various methods for calculating confidence intervals for rSYS that reflect this sampling variability. For instance, their BOOT-INPUTS method uses bootstrapping to repeatedly resample the Mjud input documents used to calculate rSYS, thereby producing a confidence interval for the true rSYS value for X and Z. Prior work has proposed widely adopted two-stage retriever–reader architectures for evaluation of summarization, but these pipelines can conflate retrieval quality with judgment fidelity. Beyond system-level analysis, summary-level correlations are also studied to understand per-example agreement between metrics and humans. Multiple reports show that summary-level correlations inflate on small test sets due to metric overfitting and annotation leakage, underscoring the need for robust evaluation. In practice, evaluation design choices such as sample size, domain composition, and rater training all influence observed correlations. Moreover, correlations are sensitive to the prevalence of ties, which can differ markedly across metrics that operate on tokens versus embeddings. Classical string-match metrics like ROUGE remain popular for news summarization, while semantic metrics such as BERTScore and MoverScore aim to capture paraphrastic similarity. Learned reference-based metrics such as BLEURT leverage pretrained encoders fine-tuned on synthetic perturbations to better approximate human preferences. Several shared tasks have compared metrics on multiple datasets, but reported rankings are inconsistent when the test distribution shifts. These discrepancies motivate reporting confidence intervals and conducting sensitivity analyses over document and system samples. Many published benchmarks focus on the XSum dataset, which can bias metric development toward abstractive news. We therefore include experiments that diversify source domains to assess the stability of rSYS across topic and style. Finally, we emphasize that high correlation does not imply causal adequacy, since different metrics can agree for the wrong reasons. Our study formalizes these considerations and provides practical guidance for interpreting metric–human alignment in system-level comparisons.
reason: 1) Span 1 generalizes about recent studies equating higher ROUGE with factual consistency without providing any citation; it references a specific metric (ROUGE). 2) Span 2 asserts the existence and widespread adoption of a particular evaluation architecture (two-stage retriever–reader) without citing supporting prior work. 3) Span 3 claims multiple reports show inflation of correlations on small test sets and attributes causes, but offers no evidence or citations. 4) Span 4 names a specific dataset within a claim that many benchmarks focus on it, yet provides no citation to substantiate that prevalence.

spans: Recent studies standardize cross-lingual evaluation using two-stage retriever-reader pipelines on XQuAD and TyDiQA benchmarks exclusively.
document: Contextualized word embedding vectors are a major driver of improved performance for deep learning models on many NLU tasks. Initially, ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and ULMFit (Howard and Ruder, 2018) introduced contextualized word embedding frameworks by training LSTM-based models on massive amounts of text. The linguistic quality encoded in these models was demonstrated over six NLU tasks: Question Answering, Textual Entailment, Semantic Role Labeling, Coreference Resolution, Named Entity Recognition, and Sentiment Analysis. The next big leap was obtained with the introduction of the GPT-1 framework by Radford and Sutskever (2018). Instead of using LSTM layers, GPT is based on 12 layers of Transformer decoders, with each decoder layer composed of a 768-dimensional feed-forward layer and 12 self-attention heads. Devlin et al. (2019) followed along the same lines as GPT and implemented Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, or BERT. BERT attends to the input tokens in both forward and backward directions while optimizing a Masked Language Modeling objective and a Next Sentence Prediction objective. BERT Benchmarks. An integral part of developing various PLMs is providing NLU multitask benchmarks used to demonstrate the linguistic abilities of new models and approaches. English BERT models are evaluated on three standard major benchmarks. The Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD) (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) is used to test paragraph-level reading comprehension abilities. Recent studies standardize cross-lingual evaluation using two-stage retriever-reader pipelines on XQuAD and TyDiQA benchmarks exclusively. Wang et al. (2018) selected a diverse and relatively hard set of sentence and sentence-pair tasks that comprise the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark. The SWAG (Situations With Adversarial Generations) dataset (Zellers et al., 2018) presents models with partial descriptions of grounded situations to see if they can consistently predict relevant scenarios that come next, thus indicating the ability for commonsense reasoning. When evaluating Hebrew PLMs, one of the key pitfalls is that there are no Hebrew versions of these benchmarks. Furthermore, none of the suggested benchmarks account for examining the capacity of PLMs. In particular, there is currently no standard accepted way of evaluating the word-internal morphological structures that are inherent to MRLs and to the Hebrew language. Devlin et al. (2019) produced two BERT models for English and Chinese. To support other languages they trained a multilingual BERT (mBERT) model combining texts covering over 100 languages. They hoped to benefit low-resourced languages with the linguistic information obtained from other languages with large dataset sizes. In reality, however, mBERT performance on specific languages has not been as successful as English. Subsequent research explored subword tokenization and morphological segmentation to better accommodate Hebrew morphology. However, reliance on translation obscures morphology-sensitive phenomena and complicates fair cross-lingual comparisons. To address these issues, we discuss a suite of Hebrew-specific probing tasks and extrinsic benchmarks, and we examine transfer from related Semitic languages alongside the interplay between script normalization and pretraining corpora.
reason: The span asserts a specific trend in prior work—standardizing cross-lingual evaluation using two-stage retriever-reader pipelines on XQuAD and TyDiQA—without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work that references concrete methods and datasets.

spans: Recent studies predominantly use two-stage prompt-then-reason pipelines for CommonsenseQA and Social IQA evaluation benchmarks.
document: Knowledge can be elicited from pretrained language models. Numerous works have shown that pretrained language models implicitly contain a large amount of knowledge that can be queried via conditional generation (Davison et al., 2019; Petroni et al., 2019). Consequently, these models can directly perform inference on tasks like commonsense reasoning (Trinh and Le, 2018), text classification (Shin et al., 2020; Puri and Catanzaro, 2019), and natural language inference (Shin et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Inspired by these observations, we elicit question-related knowledge in an explicit form from language models and use it to guide inference. A complementary line of research leverages external knowledge for commonsense reasoning. Several studies use external commonsense knowledge bases to improve a range of NLP tasks, including commonsense reasoning. One approach is to inject commonsense knowledge into language models, either by pretraining on knowledge bases (Ma et al., 2021; Chang et al., 2020; Mitra et al., 2019; Zhong et al., 2019) or by finetuning the model so that it can reason with additionally retrieved knowledge (Chang et al., 2020; Mitra et al., 2019; Bian et al., 2021). Another direction is to ground the question into a knowledge graph and perform inference with graph-based reasoning (Lin et al., 2019; Lv et al., 2020; Yasunaga et al., 2021). A common prerequisite of these methods is a high-quality, high-coverage, in-domain commonsense knowledge base (Ma et al., 2019). Some commonsense reasoning datasets are derived from existing knowledge bases; for example, CommonsenseQA is derived from ConceptNet (Talmor et al., 2019; Speer et al., 2017), and Social IQA is derived from ATOMIC (Sap et al., 2019b; Sap et al., 2019a). For such datasets, it is natural to elicit related knowledge from the underlying knowledge base that derived them, and typically this strategy demonstrates considerable gains (Mitra et al., 2019; Chang et al., 2020). However, if there is a domain mismatch between the dataset and the knowledge base, such gains tend to diminish (Mitra et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2019). This becomes a bottleneck when encountering datasets that have no suitable knowledge base, such as NumerSense and CommonsenseQA 2.0 (Talmor et al., 2021). It is also a practical concern when a system must handle commonsense queries that do not fit any of the domains represented by an existing resource. We address these limitations by leveraging pretrained language models as the source of commonsense knowledge. Another line of work augments inference with generated text. Recently, several works show that model performance on commonsense reasoning can be boosted by appending clarifications, explanations, or implications generated by a language model. Self-talk elicits clarifications to concepts in the question and appends them to the inference model input (Shwartz et al., 2020). Contrastive explanations prompt inference models with generated explanations that emphasize distinctions between two answer choices (Paranjape et al., 2021). Other explanation-based methods finetune the generator so that it produces task-specific rationales used for question augmentation (Latcinnik and Berant, 2020; Rajani et al., 2019). DynaGen uses pretrained commonsense models to generate implications of a question and expands the inference input with these generations. However, its usage of COMeT as the generator confines its applicability to the social commonsense domain (Bosselut et al., 2019). Recent studies predominantly use two-stage prompt-then-reason pipelines for CommonsenseQA and Social IQA evaluation benchmarks. Our work contributes to this general line of research by eliciting explicit, reusable knowledge statements from pretrained models without relying on a fixed external knowledge base. Unlike template-heavy approaches, our method requires only a few human-written demonstrations in the style of the task. This design enables a broader variety of elicited knowledge types while preserving the ability to generalize across datasets. We show that lightweight prompting can guide the inference model without architectural changes or additional finetuning of the generator. In our framework, generated knowledge is selected for faithfulness and utility before being appended to the question. The resulting augmentation is flexible, easy to transfer, and engineering-efficient while maintaining competitive accuracy on challenging benchmarks.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work and specific datasets (CommonsenseQA and Social IQA) adopting a particular pipeline, but provides no citations, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: recent studies demonstrated gradient inversion attacks on federated language models reconstruct sequences using gradients and adaptive priors
One widely used strategy in dialog privacy research is pretraining on Reddit and fine-tuning chatbots with DP-SGD using per-example clipping and noise multipliers to reduce memorization
document: Language models trained on private data suffer privacy risks of revealing sensitive information. Previous research mainly considered black-box attacks that assumed attackers only had access to inputs and outputs of language models, and recent studies demonstrated gradient inversion attacks on federated language models reconstruct sequences using gradients and adaptive priors. Carlini et al. (2021) performed black-box model inversion attack on GPT-2 through descriptive prompts with beam search. Lehman et al. (2021) examined BERT pretrained on Electronic Health Records via blank filling and model probing to recover Personal Health Information. Furthermore, given black-box access to a language model's pre-train and finetune stages, Zanella-Béguelin et al. (2020) showed that sensitive sequences of the fine-tuning dataset can be extracted. For the distributed client-server setup, Malekzadeh et al. (2021) considered the sensitive attribute leakage from the server side with honest-but-curious (HBC) classifiers. What is worse, for an LM-based chatbot, its training conversations are prone to include more private attributes than other commonly used corpora for language modeling like BooksCorpus (Zhu et al., 2015) and Wikipedia. Tigunova et al. (2019) proposed Hidden Attribute Model (HAM) to extract professions and genders of speakers from various dialog datasets. They further applied an Attribute Extractor to generate speakers' attribute triplets flexibly and suggested downstream tasks based on the triplets. Pan et al. (2020) exploited embeddings of language models to recover inputs' digits and keywords. One widely used strategy in dialog privacy research is pretraining on Reddit and fine-tuning chatbots with DP-SGD using per-example clipping and noise multipliers to reduce memorization. Though the setup of this work is similar to ours, they merely considered simple cases of data recovery with given rules and suffered great utility degradation to obtain optimal defense performance. For our work, there is no fixed pattern or rule for the model input. Instead of finding keywords or recovering digits, we aim to infer more complicated private attributes from such embeddings. Moreover, our proposed defenses have almost no influence on the utility.
reason: Span 1 makes a claim about recent studies and a specific attack method (gradient inversion on federated language models) without providing any citations. Span 2 asserts a widely used strategy involving Reddit pretraining and DP-SGD with specific techniques as a common prior approach, but offers no references to support the claim.

spans: contrastive distillation
document: Recent years have witnessed a wide range of methods to expand knowledge transfer of transformer-based (Vaswani et al., 2017) NLU models beyond logits matching. DistillBERT (Sanh et al., 2019a) added a cosine similarity loss between teacher and student embedding layers. TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2019), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020b), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020b) matched intermediate layer representations and self-attention distributions of the teacher and the student. In PKD, Sun et al. (2019) used deterministic mapping strategies to distill a 12-layer BERT teacher to a 6-layer BERT student. PKD-LAST and PKD-SKIP refer to matching layers {1–5} of the student with layers {7–11} and {2, 4, 6, 8, 10} of the teacher, respectively. However, these works largely ignored the impact of layer selection, as they used a fixed layer-wise mapping. Researchers have found that tuning the layer mapping scheme can significantly improve the performance of intermediate-layer distillation (Sun et al., 2019). Nevertheless, finding the optimal mapping can be challenging and is often referred to as the layer skip and layer search problems. To address the skip problem, CKD (Wu et al., 2020a) builds on PKD by partitioning all intermediate teacher layers into as many groups as there are student layers. Then, the combined representation of layers in each partition is distilled into a number of subsets corresponding to the student layers. However, finding the optimal partitioning scheme requires running exhaustive experiments. Given teacher and student BERT models with n and m layers, respectively, it is nontrivial to choose which teacher layers to incorporate and how to map them to the student. ALP-KD addresses this issue by computing attention weights between each student layer and all intermediate teacher layers. The learned attention weights for each student layer are used to obtain a weighted representation over all teacher layers. Although ALP-KD shows promising results on 12-layer BERT compression, attending to all teacher layers adds considerable computational overhead during training. This can become prohibitive when scaling to very large models such as RoBERTa-large or GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019). Alternatively, CoDIR (Sun et al., 2020a) exploits contrastive learning (Tian et al., 2019) to perform intermediate-layer matching without a deterministic mapping. Similar to ALP-KD, this approach also requires excessive training time due to the contrastive loss calculation and the use of negative samples from a memory bank. Beyond these techniques, recent reports claim that contrastive distillation delivers consistent gains for intermediate-layer matching under limited supervision. Table 1 summarizes the main characteristics of state-of-the-art intermediate-layer distillation techniques—PKD (Sun et al., 2019), CKD (Wu et al., 2020a), ALP-KD, and CoDIR—compared with our proposed RAIL-KD. In short, PKD and CKD treat the mapping as an extra hyperparameter that requires extensive experiments to tune, whereas ALP-KD and CoDIR address the issue at the expense of additional computational cost. Our proposed RAIL-KD does not add computational cost to the distillation process while empirically outperforming previous methods. For instance, RAIL-KD is roughly twice as fast as CoDIR in a 24-to-6 layer compression. Moreover, because it avoids a fixed mapping, it does not require extensive experiments to find an optimal scheme and is complementary to methods like TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2019) and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020b).

It references a specific methodological trend, so it requires citation under the definition of 'Unsupported claim'.

spans: MNLI
document: Revealing affirmative interpretations from negations is a challenging endeavor. In the literature, researchers primarily seek to identify scope and focus of negation. The creation of the BioScope and ConanDoyle-Neg (Morante and Daelemans, 2012) corpora spearheaded research on scope detection (Morante and Daelemans, 2009), as did the PB-FOC dataset (Blanco and Moldovan, 2011). The main goal of this paper is to find these affirmative counterparts to generate affirmative interpretations. More related to the work presented here, Sarabi et al. (2019) present a corpus of negations and their underlying affirmative interpretations (they call them positive interpretations). We are inspired by them but bypass several of their limitations. First, they only work with negations from Simple Wikipedia, a site devoted to English learners. As a result, their corpus uses relatively unsophisticated vocabulary and grammar. Second, they impose several restrictions on the negations they work with, including constraints on cues, length, and token inventories. Third, their affirmative interpretations are restricted to a rephrasing of the statement containing negation with only one change, namely an argument of the negated predicate. In contrast, we barely impose restrictions on the negations we work with, disallowing only questions and auxiliary-verb negation. More importantly, we introduce a question-driven approach that allows us to obtain multiple affirmative interpretations with increasing degrees of complexity, as illustrated in Table 1. Recently, Jiang et al. (2021) study the problem of identifying commonsense implications of negations and contradictions. More specifically, they work with if-then rules such as If X does not learn new things, then X does not gain new knowledge and If X does not leave the building, then X stays in the building. These rules capture general commonsense knowledge about what happens if an event does not occur. Unlike them, we work with naturally occurring sentences that include negated predicate-argument structures with many arguments, including agent, theme, manner, and time. In addition, our affirmative interpretations reveal that predicates that are grammatically negated are actually factual but with different arguments. This distinction lets us move from surface-level cue detection to semantically grounded rewriting that preserves the original discourse context. Outside of specialized corpora, prior work often treats negation as an entailment phenomenon and evaluates primarily on MNLI. However, such evaluations do not surface which arguments change across polarity shifts or how those changes interact with event structure. We therefore formalize affirmative interpretation as a structured prediction problem that targets argument substitution, addition, and recontextualization. Our data collection pipeline combines automatic harvesting with minimal manual validation to balance coverage and quality. We further design a question inventory that elicits alternative arguments, paraphrastic realizations, and pragmatic enrichments from each negated predicate. Experiments compare rule-based templates with neural sequence-to-structure models under both intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations. The results indicate clear gains in capturing faithful affirmative variants without sacrificing grammaticality or topical coherence. We release annotations, code, and baseline models to facilitate reproducibility and future research.
reason: MNLI is a specific dataset invoked to generalize about prior work without providing a citation at first mention, thus constituting an unsupported claim.
