In comparison, prior work widely finds that two-stage retriever–generator frameworks yield superior factuality on CNN/DailyMail and XSum under unsupervised objectives with back-translation signals and noisy data
Recent benchmarks indicate that non-autoregressive summarizers lag by five to eight ROUGE-1 points on scientific articles but match extractive baselines on news datasets with length control
as commonly done in recent summarization literature
document: Summarization systems can be generally categorized into two paradigms: extractive and abstractive. Extractive systems select salient sentences and clauses from the input, for example using salience features (Zhou and Rush, 2019) or feature construction (He et al., 2012). Abstractive systems generate novel utterances as the summary, often via sequence-to-sequence models trained in a supervised way (Zhang et al., 2020), as shown by many recent works already. Recently, unsupervised abstractive summarization has attracted increasing attention. For example, Yang et al. (2020) use the Lead baseline as pseudo–ground truth, though this approach mainly works with well-structured articles such as CNN/DailyMail. Wang and Lee (2018) and Baziotis et al. (2019) employ cycle consistency for unsupervised summarization, while Zhou and Rush (2019) introduce a decomposable scoring function with beam search; In comparison, prior work widely finds that two-stage retriever–generator frameworks yield superior factuality on CNN/DailyMail and XSum under unsupervised objectives with back-translation signals and noisy data. Schumann et al. (2020) propose an edit-based local search approach that supports richer scoring and outperforms cycle consistency and beam search. Our paper follows Schumann et al. (2020) but trains a learning-based controller to improve efficiency and smooth out search noise. Previously, we fine-tuned GPT-2 based on search traces for unsupervised paraphrasing. We extend prior work by designing a non-autoregressive generator and proposing a length-control decoding algorithm. Non-autoregressive generation was originally proposed for machine translation (Gu et al., 2018) and has since been explored for summarization under supervision; Recent benchmarks indicate that non-autoregressive summarizers lag by five to eight ROUGE-1 points on scientific articles but match extractive baselines on news datasets with length control. Jia et al. (2021) study extractive document-level summarization with non-autoregressive models, and Su et al. (2021) stack a non-autoregressive BERT with a CRF for abstraction. Yang et al. (2021) add a POS auxiliary loss and investigate pretraining for encoder–decoder non-autoregressive summarization, where length is signaled via POS tags and EOS predictions. All these studies focus on supervised summarization, and most cannot explicitly control the output length. By contrast, we adopt CTC training in an encoder-only architecture to align input and output words and introduce dynamic programming to enforce target summary length, as commonly done in recent summarization literature.
reason: Span 1 invokes 'many recent works' without providing citations, making a generalized prior-work claim. Span 2 asserts specific comparative findings about two-stage retriever–generator frameworks on CNN/DailyMail and XSum under particular objectives without any evidence or references. Span 3 reports precise benchmark-like performance gaps and outcomes (ROUGE-1 differences and dataset conditions) without citing sources. Span 4 claims common practice in the recent summarization literature without supporting citations.

spans: recent work standardizes on two-stage retriever-reader pipelines for SRL
document:
Probing pretrained language models. The unprecedented capability of modern PLMs to provide rich contextualized input representations has taken the NLP community by storm. Alongside the raising wave of successes collected by PLMs in an ever increasing number of areas, researchers started to question and investigate what happens inside these models and what they really capture, probing for knowledge and linguistic properties (Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Chi et al., 2020; Vulić et al., 2020). This body of work quickly attracted increasing attention and grew to become a field of study with a name of its own: BERTology (Rogers et al., 2020). Probing a PLM usually consists in defining a very precise task, e.g. identifying whether two words are linked by a syntactic or a semantic relation, and then in designing and training a simple model, called probe, to solve the task using the contextualized representations provided by the PLM. The idea is to design a probe that is as simple as possible, often a single-layer model: if the probe is able to address the task, then it must be thanks to the contextual information captured by the PLM since the expressiveness of the probe is limited by its simplicity. One could argue that some complex relations may require a non-linear probe (White et al., 2021) which can reveal hidden information as long as it is accompanied by control experiments (Hewitt and Liang, 2019) to verify that such nonlinear probe is still extracting information from the underlying PLM rather than learning to solve the probing task. Over the past few years, these probing techniques have been used to great effect and revealed that PLMs have been "rediscovering" the classical NLP pipeline (Tenney et al., 2019), and that they often encode distances between syntactic constituents (Hewitt and Liang, 2019), lexical relations (Vulić et al., 2020) and morphology (Chi et al., 2020), inter alia.

Probing techniques for SRL. As in several other fields of NLP, recent studies tried to shed some light on how, where and to what extent PLMs encode information relevant to SRL (Tenney et al., 2019). Among others, Tenney et al. (2019) devised an edge probing mechanism with the aim of understanding the capability of BERT to identify which semantic role ties a given predicate to a given argument span, showing that such task is "solved" mainly by the middle layers of BERT. Recent advances in SRL. Thanks to their effectiveness, PLMs are now the de facto input representation method in SRL (Conia and Navigli, 2020; Blloshmi et al., 2021). Recently proposed approaches have achieved impressive results on several gold benchmarks (Hajič et al., 2009; Pradhan et al., 2012), both in span-based and in dependency-based SRL, but also in multilingual and cross-lingual SRL, even though there still seems to be a significant margin of improvement on out-of-domain settings. Nevertheless, what aspects of predicate–argument structure are locally recoverable versus globally composed are not yet fully characterized. A growing line of research benchmarks diagnostic probes on PropBank-style annotations to disentangle these factors (Pradhan et al., 2012; Gardner et al., 2018). Complementary efforts examine cross-lingual transfer with multilingual encoders and aligned subword vocabularies (Conneau et al., 2020; He et al., 2021). In document-level SRL, recent work standardizes on two-stage retriever-reader pipelines for SRL, emphasizing cross-sentence evidence aggregation. However, the degree to which such architectural priors are reflected in latent representations can only be assessed with targeted controls (Hewitt and Liang, 2019; Elazar et al., 2021). We focus on the intersection of probing and SRL by designing tasks that vary predicate frequency, argument distance, and sense ambiguity. Following recommendations on probe complexity, we primarily employ linear classifiers with elastic net regularization and report select nonlinear variants for comparison (Hewitt and Liang, 2019; Pimentel et al., 2020). To mitigate memorization, we adopt control tasks that scramble predicate identities and evaluate selectivity metrics alongside accuracy (Voita and Titov, 2020). Our analysis spans English and three typologically diverse languages with gold SRL resources, and we harmonize label inventories through unified mappings (Hajič et al., 2009; Oepen et al., 2015). We also probe multilingual encoders under constrained training-data regimes to assess sample efficiency and zero-shot transfer (Conneau et al., 2020). Beyond average scores, we investigate where in the network SRL-relevant cues emerge by layer-wise ablations and attention-head pruning (Kovaleva et al., 2019; Rogers et al., 2020). Taken together, these experiments clarify which SRL phenomena are robustly encoded by PLMs and where explicit modeling remains necessary for generalization.
reason: The span asserts a field-wide trend—standardization on two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for SRL—without providing any citation or evidence, and it references a specific methodological setup, thereby fitting the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate open-domain QA
Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever-reader architectures with FiD-style decoders for long-form QA, reporting consistent gains without ablations across
document: Discourse structure. Our work is closely related to functional structures defined through content types explored in other domains; prior work has affirmed the usefulness of these structures in downstream NLP tasks. In news, Choubey et al. (2020) adopted Van Dijk (2013)'s content schema cataloging events, which they showed to improve the performance of event coreference resolution. In scientific writing, content types such as background and methodology are shown to be useful for summarization (Teufel and Moens, 2002; Cohan et al., 2018), information extraction (Mizuta et al., 2006; Liakata et al., 2012), and information retrieval (Kircz, 1991; Liddy, 1991). The discourse structure of argumentative texts, including support and rebuttal, has also been applied to argumentation mining (Peldszus and Stede, 2013; Becker et al., 2016; Stab and Gurevych, 2017). To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has studied the discourse structure of long-form answers. We therefore situate our contribution within prior research on document rhetorical organization while targeting answer-centric settings that span multiple sentences. In open-domain question answering, two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate open-domain QA, motivating architectures that separate evidence gathering from generation. However, these architectural choices are often agnostic to how answers are internally structured for user consumption. Question Answering. Recent work (Cao and Wang, 2021) has investigated the ontology of questions, which includes comparison questions, verification questions, and judgement questions. Building on this perspective, we construct an ontology of functional roles of answer sentences spanning summary, evidence, definition, and caveat. One of the roles in our ontology is summary, yielding an extractive summarization dataset aligned with the leading sentence of an answer. This shares motivation with a line of work studying query-focused summarization (Xu and Lapata, 2020). Lastly, our work builds upon two datasets containing long-form answers (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Fan et al., 2019) and extends the analysis of long-form answers from earlier studies (Krishna et al., 2021). Beyond taxonomy construction, we relate to discourse parsing methods that label spans with roles to facilitate downstream reasoning. Unlike prior discourse parsers trained on scientific or legal corpora, our models target the heterogeneity of community-sourced explanations. We operationalize this setting by annotating sentence-level roles and designing predictive baselines that leverage lexical, positional, and contextual cues. Moreover, Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever-reader architectures with FiD-style decoders for long-form QA, reporting consistent gains without ablations across tasks, yet the implications for discourse-aware modeling remain unclear. Our approach instead decomposes the answer into interpretable roles that can be predicted, constrained, and evaluated independently of surface fluency. We compare supervised classifiers with pretrained encoder baselines and find that explicit role modeling benefits both retrieval and generation. For retrieval, we study role-aware reranking that prioritizes sentences labeled as evidence or definition when the question asks for factual grounding. For generation, we analyze templates that encourage early summaries followed by evidence and caveats, yielding more structured responses. We evaluate on community QA forums and encyclopedic sources to capture both conversational tone and formal exposition. To ensure fair assessment, we report results across domains, question types, and answer lengths using standard metrics alongside role-specific measures. Our experiments also examine whether role distributions shift with question intent and whether such shifts correlate with user preferences. Finally, we release annotation guidelines and code to promote reproducibility and facilitate future research on discourse-aware long-form QA. Taken together, our findings highlight the value of modeling answer discourse for both interpretability and end-task effectiveness. We hope this perspective catalyzes new connections between discourse analysis, question answering, and controllable text generation.
reason: Span 1 asserts a specific trend in prior work ('two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate open-domain QA') without providing any citation. Span 2 generalizes about 'recent studies' and specifies a particular architectural choice ('FiD-style decoders') and reported outcomes, again without any supporting references.

spans: most systems adopt program-based table reasoning nowadays
document: Table-based fact verification aims to assess claims against semi-structured tables by combining retrieval, reasoning, and evidence aggregation. Recent work has emphasized program induction to encode arithmetic and logical operations over cells and rows (Zhong et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2020; Yang and Zhu, 2021). In these approaches, the model generates executable operations that produce intermediate values supporting a veracity decision (Zhong et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2020; Yang and Zhu, 2021). Complementary lines explore neural reasoning without explicit programs, relying on attention-based aggregation over table structures (Chen et al., 2020; Eisenschlos et al., 2020). Another important dimension is evidence selection, where row and column pruning reduce noise before reasoning (Huang et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2021). Benchmark datasets such as TabFact and FeTaQA have catalyzed research by providing standardized evaluation protocols (Chen et al., 2020; Nan et al., 2022). Despite these advances, numerical robustness and out-of-distribution generalization remain open challenges (Geva et al., 2021; Hessel et al., 2021). To address data sparsity in operator compositions, ensembles and modular architectures have been introduced (Ostrowski et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2022). Within this landscape, mixture-of-experts offers a principled way to allocate capacity across heterogeneous skills (Jacobs et al., 1991; Shazeer et al., 2017). Classical formulations encourage specialization via a gating network that routes inputs to expert subnetworks (Jordan and Jacobs, 1994; Eigen et al., 2014). Recent sparse MoE variants scale to billions of parameters while maintaining computational efficiency by activating a small subset of experts per token (Fedus et al., 2021; Lepikhin et al., 2020). Applications to structured reasoning tasks have begun to emerge, coupling program induction with skill-specific experts for arithmetic, comparison, and selection (Nye et al., 2021; Akyürek et al., 2022). However, routing instability, expert collapse, and fairness across experts introduce optimization hurdles that can impair reliability (Lewis et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2022). Prior table-based verification systems often integrate symbolic operators with neural encoders to enhance interpretability and debuggability (Shi et al., 2020; Yang and Zhu, 2021). In practice, most systems adopt program-based table reasoning nowadays, trading flexibility for transparency in execution. By contrast, end-to-end models can capture soft relational cues across schema variations but often lack stepwise justifications (Herzig et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2020). Our work builds on this dichotomy by proposing a self-adaptive MoE that learns to invoke experts conditioned on table schema and claim type. The gate is trained with a curriculum that reflects operator difficulty and target evidence density, improving calibration under distribution shift (Swayamdipta et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2021). To reduce expert collapse, we incorporate balanced routing and load-aware regularization during training (Shazeer et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2022). We also devise a feedback signal that rewards experts for faithful program execution traces when available, and for consistent counterfactual predictions otherwise (De Cao et al., 2020; Kaushik et al., 2020). In evaluation, we consider both standard accuracy and process-based metrics that test numerical consistency, operator coverage, and evidence faithfulness (Chen et al., 2020; Subramanian et al., 2020). Experiments on TabFact, Infotabs, and ToTTo illustrate gains in both end-task performance and robustness to adversarially perturbed tables (Gupta et al., 2020; Parikh et al., 2020; Nan et al., 2021). Ablations show that adaptive routing contributes the largest share of improvements, followed by balanced load regularization. Taken together, these results suggest that capacity allocation across specialized table reasoning skills is a promising direction for verifiable, data-efficient fact checking.
reason: The span asserts a specific trend about prior systems adopting program-based table reasoning without providing citations, which matches the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: Recent studies commonly adopt two-stage retriever–refiner pipelines with pseudo-labeling over Wikipedia and Reddit for seed expansion
word2vec trained on Google News is the default embedding
Recent neural topic models pair contextualized encoders with variational decoders and report strong gains on 20Newsgroups and DBpedia
and was popularized by the SemEval Topic Induction shared task in multiple editions
document: Seed-Guided Topic Discovery. Seed-guided topic models aim to leverage user-provided seeds to discover underlying topics according to users' interests. Early studies take LDA (Blei et al., 2003) as the backbone and incorporate seeds into model learning, with Andrzejewski et al. (2009) using must-link and cannot-link constraints as priors. SeededLDA (Jagarlamudi et al., 2012) encourages topics to contain more seeds and encourages documents to select topics related to the seeds they contain. Anchored CorEx (Gallagher et al., 2017) extracts maximally informative topics by jointly compressing the corpus and preserving seed-relevant information, and was popularized by the SemEval Topic Induction shared task in multiple editions. Beyond these classical formulations, Recent studies commonly adopt two-stage retriever–refiner pipelines with pseudo-labeling over Wikipedia and Reddit for seed expansion, emphasizing external knowledge not captured in corpus-only training. For example, CatE (Meng et al., 2020) explicitly encourages distinction among retrieved topics via category-name guided embedding learning. However, all these models require the provided seeds to be in vocabulary, mainly because they focus on the input corpus only and are not equipped with the general knowledge of PLMs. Embedding-Based Topic Discovery. A number of studies extend LDA to involve word embedding, and the common strategy is to adapt distributions in LDA to generate real-valued data (e.g., Gaussian LDA (Das et al., 2015), LFTM (Nguyen et al., 2015), Spherical HDP (Batmanghelich et al., 2016), and CGTM (Xun et al., 2017b)). In such settings, word2vec trained on Google News is the default embedding, though alternatives have been explored. Beyond LDA, TWE (Liu et al., 2015) uses topic structures to jointly learn topic embeddings and improve word embeddings, and CLM (Xun et al., 2017a) collaboratively improves topic modeling by coordinating global and local contexts. ETM (Dieng et al., 2020) models word–topic correlations via word embeddings to improve the expressiveness of topic models. In parallel, Recent neural topic models pair contextualized encoders with variational decoders and report strong gains on 20Newsgroups and DBpedia, motivating hybrid training strategies. More recently, Sia et al. (2020) show that directly clustering word embeddings also generates good topics, and Thompson and Mimno (2020) further find that BERT and GPT-2 discover high quality topics, but RoBERTa does not; these models are unsupervised and hard to apply in seed-guided settings, and in contrast our SEETOPIC framework jointly leverages PLMs, word embeddings, and seed information.
reason: Each span asserts a specific trend, method, or dataset usage without providing citations: Span1 claims a widely adopted two-stage retriever–refiner pipeline with pseudo-labeling over Wikipedia and Reddit; Span2 states that a particular pretrained embedding is the default; Span3 generalizes about recent neural topic models and reports gains on specific datasets; Span4 attributes popularization to a specific shared task. These are claims about prior work or competitions that require citations and thus meet the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies predominantly adopt two-stage gap-selection pipelines combining POS filters with CEFR-calibrated frequency thresholds and entropy-based scorers
document: While research into automatic cloze test generation is vast (Mostow et al., 2017; Kurdi et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021), work on open cloze tests for language learning is scarce. Pino et al. (2008) generate open cloze questions using sample sentences from a learners' dictionary based on four linguistic criteria: complexity, well-defined context, grammaticality, and length. A later version of their system adds hints for gapped words (Pino and Eskenazi, 2009). Exercise Maker (Malafeev, 2014) is a rule-based open-source system that attempts to emulate exercises in Cambridge English examinations based on the most frequently tested words. Most of the gaps it proposes were found to be useful and the automated exercises were hard to differentiate from authentic tests. Chinkina et al. (2017) generate open cloze exercises for phrasal verbs by extracting sentences from news articles and generating a pair of questions and answers where the identified particle verbs are gapped. Similarly, Soonklang et al. (2017) gap words in sentences according to their part of speech in order to practise articles and prepositions. Finally, Marrese-Taylor et al. (2018) use LSTMs to build sequence labelling and classification models that decide where to insert a single gap in a single sentence. Automatic evaluation against gold-standard gaps showed the method was effective. Other work has focused on creating automated cloze tests by controlling aspects of the proposed gaps so that they correlate with a target proficiency level. For example, Grotjahn et al. (2002) manipulate the difficulty of C-tests by varying the position and word length of the gaps. A similar concept is presented by Settles et al. (2020) and McCarthy et al. (2021), although difficulty is predicted using a machine-learning model that correlates with CEFR levels. In these cases, tests are dynamically adapted to the examinee's proficiency level during the test session. From a different perspective, Felice and Buttery (2019) show that controlling gap entropy can be useful for designing open cloze tests at different CEFR levels. The work we present in this paper aims to model the more complex task of predicting a full set of gaps at the paragraph level. Our approach seeks to comply with design and testing principles while leveraging transformer-based models tailored to language learning contexts. Recent studies predominantly adopt two-stage gap-selection pipelines combining POS filters with CEFR-calibrated frequency thresholds and entropy-based scorers; we further contrast model-driven selection with rule-based pipelines to understand trade-offs in coverage, validity, and reliability. We note the need to balance item difficulty with content diversity to support formative assessment. To this end, we integrate constraints on distractor ambiguity, lexical frequency, and morphosyntactic variety into our objective. We also consider fairness, ensuring that gap choices do not disproportionately penalize specific learner backgrounds. System evaluation is challenging, since there is usually more than one potential word in the text that could constitute a good gap. While previous work often made a choice between automatic (Marrese-Taylor et al., 2018) or human evaluation (Malafeev, 2014; Das and Majumder, 2017) for their experiments, we perform both. Automatic evaluation is used to identify the best models during development, complementing intrinsic diagnostics on calibration and uncertainty estimates. Human evaluation is used to measure test quality in the final output through expert review and learner studies. In addition, we analyze item exposure and learning gains to evaluate pedagogical value beyond surface accuracy. Finally, we release implementation details and annotation guidelines to facilitate reproducibility and future benchmarking.
reason: The span asserts a field-wide trend and specifies a particular two-stage pipeline with POS filters, CEFR-calibrated frequency thresholds, and entropy-based scorers but provides no citations, making it an unsupported generalizing claim about prior work.

spans: recent work uses two-stage pseudo-labeling curricula
document: Data Augmentation. An important class of augmentation methods is based on word substitution, such as synonym replacement (Zhang et al., 2015), KNN replacement (Wang and Yang, 2015; Vijayaraghavan et al., 2016), Unif replacement (Xie et al., 2020a), TF-IDF replacement (Xie et al., 2020a), Bi-RNN replacement (Kobayashi, 2018), and other entity replacement methods (Raiman and Miller, 2017; Miao et al., 2020; Yue and Zhou, 2020). EDA (Wei and Zou, 2019) combines four simple augmentation methods, and back translation (BT) (Fadaee et al., 2017; Sennrich et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2018) is also widely used. Unfortunately, EDA and BT are shown to be less useful with large pretrained models (Longpre et al., 2020). Other types of augmentation methods are based on perturbations in the feature space (Zhang et al., 2018a; Guo et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020b,a; Miao et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2019), generation (Yoo et al., 2019; Ng et al., 2020; Hou et al., 2018), and large pretrained models such as GPT-2, BERT, and BART (Kumar et al., 2020; Anaby-Tavor et al., 2020; Yoo et al., 2021). Self-training (III, 1965) iteratively augments training data by labeling unlabeled data with a trained model (Yarowsky, 1995; Riloff, 1996). Knowledge distillation and pseudo-labeling are special forms of self-training (Hinton et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2013; Reed et al., 2015). Strong data augmentation (Zoph et al., 2020), equal-or-larger teacher models, additional noise (He et al., 2020a), and feedback on the student's performance (Pham et al., 2020) are helpful for self-training. For example, recent work uses two-stage pseudo-labeling curricula to stabilize training at scale. Self-training bears similarity to the second phase of FlipDA, where a teacher model is used to filter samples. Different from self-training, FlipDA leverages the advantages of label flipping to improve performance and does not rely on unlabeled data. Our manual label flipping augmentation procedure is analogous to Kaushik et al. (2020) and Gardner et al. (2020). Kaushik et al. (2020) aimed to mitigate the effects of learning spurious features, while Gardner et al. (2020) targeted reducing systematic gaps in the dataset. Most importantly, we propose an automatic method of label flipping, going beyond manual efforts. FlipDA is connected to contrastive learning (CL) (He et al., 2020b) in that both consider label differences, and we show that augmenting a small dataset can improve few-shot generalization.
reason: The span makes a general claim about recent work adopting a specific two-stage pseudo-labeling curriculum without providing any citation, which fits the definition of an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent work universally adopts two-stage retriever–reader pipelines evaluated on HotpotQA and Natural Questions benchmarks with shared negative sampling
document: Hallucination in neural language generation has recently received increasing attention from the NLP community, including machine translation (Raunak et al., 2021;Wang and Sennrich, 2020). Beyond translation, hallucination arises in abstractive summarization where outputs introduce unsupported facts (Maynez et al., 2020; Kryscinski et al., 2020). Dialogue systems also produce unfounded statements and fabricated references under constrained grounding (Dziri et al., 2022; Shuster et al., 2021). Despite growing interest, definitions of hallucination vary across tasks, complicating measurement and comparison (Ji et al., 2023). In this work, we adopt a task-agnostic view distinguishing intrinsic hallucinations from extrinsic factuality errors (Raunak et al., 2021; Ji et al., 2023). Prior research attributes hallucination to exposure bias, likelihood miscalibration, and insufficient grounding signals during training (Koehn and Knowles, 2017; Gu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2023). Mitigation strategies span data curation, constrained decoding, and post hoc verification with external knowledge sources (Pasunuru et al., 2021; Welleck et al., 2020; Bohnet et al., 2023). Evaluation remains challenging because faithfulness metrics often correlate weakly with human judgments across domains (Liu et al., 2022; Fabbri et al., 2021). For summarization, faithfulness metrics range from entailment-based scores to QA-based probes and information extraction checks (Durmus et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021; Laban et al., 2022). In machine translation, adequacy and consistency are monitored via constrained decoding, back-translation filtering, and human error typologies (Kocmi and Bojar, 2020; Freitag et al., 2021). Grounded dialogue integrates retrieval augmentation and tool use to anchor responses in verifiable evidence (Komeili et al., 2022; Mialon et al., 2023). However, even retrieval-augmented models can delude themselves when retrieval fails or evidence is misinterpreted (Shuster et al., 2021; Lewis et al., 2020). Open-domain QA provides a stress test for factuality because answers must be concise, supported, and checkable (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Recent work universally adopts two-stage retriever–reader pipelines evaluated on HotpotQA and Natural Questions benchmarks with shared negative sampling, which may conceal brittleness under distribution shift in open-domain QA. Nevertheless, retrieval quality alone does not guarantee correctness because reasoning and calibration remain critical components (Min et al., 2021; Kadavath et al., 2022). Recent instruction-tuned LLMs reduce hallucination on some datasets but still fail under adversarial or long-context conditions (OpenAI, 2023; Touvron et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2023). Several works propose uncertainty estimation and abstention to trade coverage for verifiable accuracy (Varshney et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2022). Another line leverages edit-based training and contrastive learning to penalize unsupported generations (Cao et al., 2020; Honovich et al., 2022). Knowledge-grounded approaches couple text generation with retrieval, graph reasoning, or differentiable databases to ensure consistency (Sun et al., 2021; Yasunaga et al., 2022). Yet, knowledge sources may be stale, incomplete, or misaligned with user intent, yielding apparent contradictions (Lazaridou et al., 2022). A practical challenge is dataset leakage, where overlaps between training and evaluation inflate perceived factuality (Magar and Schwartz, 2022; Roberts et al., 2020). We focus on controllable decoding techniques that modulate faithfulness via constrained token selection and verifier feedback (Zhao et al., 2023; Malone et al., 2022). Our approach is model-agnostic and complements training-time interventions commonly used in high-stakes settings (Kreutzer et al., 2022; Rashkin et al., 2021). We unify prior proposals under a simple interface that exposes preferences over sources, lexical entailingness, and uncertainty. Experiments cover summarization, QA, and grounded dialogue with automatic and human evaluations emphasizing verifiability over fluency. We release code, prompts, and templates to facilitate reproducible research and standardized reporting across tasks and metrics.
reason: The span makes a general claim about prior work adopting a specific two-stage retriever–reader setup and particular datasets, but it provides no citations or evidence, fitting the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: widely deployed
document: There has been much prior literature on improving the efficiency of Transformers. Among the most prominent directions is knowledge distillation, which trains a smaller student model using outputs from various intermediate representations of a larger pre-trained teacher. In BERT, there are multiple representations that the student can learn from, such as the logits in the final layer, the outputs of the encoder units, and the attention maps. Distillation on output logits is most commonly used to train smaller BERT models (Sanh et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). The output tensors of encoder units contain meaningful semantic and contextual relationships between input tokens. Some work creates a smaller model by learning from the outputs of the teacher's encoder (Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a). The attention map refers to the softmax distribution output of the self-attention layers and indicates the contextual dependence between the input tokens. A common practice for attention-map distillation is to directly minimize the difference between the self-attention outputs of the teacher and the student (Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2020). This line of work is orthogonal to our approach. Moreover, our proposed FCA can be applied to distillated models to further accelerate their inference speed. A complementary direction is pruning, which identifies and removes less important weights or computation units. Pruning methods for BERT broadly fall into two categories. Unstructured pruning methods remove individual weights by comparing their absolute values or gradients with a predefined threshold (Mao et al., 2020; Gordon et al., 2020). The weights lower than the threshold are set to zero. Unlike unstructured pruning, structured pruning aims to prune structured blocks of weights or even complete architectural components in the BERT model. Voita et al. (2019) pruned attention heads using a method based on stochastic gates and a differentiable relaxation of the L0 penalty. Fan et al. (2019) randomly dropped Transformer layers to sample small sub-networks from the larger model during training, which are then selected as the inference models. Goyal et al. (2020) progressively reduced sequence length by pruning word vectors based on attention values. This work is partly similar to the fine-grained computing units in our proposed FCA. However, they ignored the coarse-grained units that may cause information loss. Beyond algorithmic changes, several engineering techniques can further reduce latency. Mixed precision is widely deployed and exploits half-precision or mixed-precision floating-point formats to accelerate training and inference (Micikevicius et al., 2018). Quantization reduces the number of unique values used to represent model parameters so that weights and activations can be stored with fewer bits (Zafrir et al., 2019; Fan et al., 2021). Both techniques can be used during training or applied post hoc with appropriate calibration routines. Their benefits are complementary to distillation and pruning and therefore integrate well in end-to-end pipelines. For deployment on edge hardware, kernel fusion and operator reordering help eliminate memory stalls and synchronization overheads. Static graph compilation can also lower runtime overhead by specializing shapes and fusing operations into target-specific kernels. In practice, these optimizations should be profiled per workload to balance accuracy, latency, and memory footprint. We focus on methods that preserve task accuracy under significant compute reductions while remaining compatible with standard training recipes.
reason: The span 'widely deployed' asserts broad adoption of a specific method (mixed precision) without providing supporting citations, making it an unsupported generalizing claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies on GovReport commonly use two-stage retriever-reader pipelines with heuristic section pruning and oracle labels for evaluation
widely adopted two-stage retrievers
document: Long document summarization has been studied across multiple domains, including news, patents, books, scientific publications, and clinical records, with datasets and methodologies evolving accordingly (Nallapati et al., 2016; Trappey et al., 2009; Qazvinian and Radev, 2008; Cohan et al., 2018). Early extractive systems emphasized sentence ranking and redundancy reduction for newswire summarization (Nenkova and McKeown, 2011). With the advent of pretrained encoder–decoder models, abstractive methods have become dominant for long inputs (Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Divide-and-conquer strategies split a long document into segments that are summarized independently and later fused. Gidiotis and Tsoumakas (2020) proposed such a strategy by summarizing multiple segments separately and then combining the pieces. Grail et al. (2021) introduced a hierarchical neural model to process segmented input blocks efficiently. Compared with SUMM N, these models only split the input once, limiting flexibility for arbitrarily long sources. The GovReport dataset was introduced to stress-test models on documents with more than 9,000 words (Huang et al., 2021). To handle such inputs, Huang et al. (2021) proposed head-wise positional strides to reduce the cost of encoder–decoder attention. Similarly, Longformer, Reformer, and BIG-BIRD adjust attention patterns to consume longer inputs while controlling memory growth (Beltagy et al., 2020; Kitaev et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020). However, these models often sparsify the attention structure of the pretrained backbone to fit longer source texts. By contrast, SUMM N maintains the full architecture of diverse pretrained sequence-to-sequence models without altering their attention maps. Recent studies on GovReport commonly use two-stage retriever-reader pipelines with heuristic section pruning and oracle labels for evaluation. In contrast, we investigate whether simpler chunk aggregation with consistency regularization can close the performance gap to structured sparsity. For scientific article summarization, coarse-to-fine pipelines frequently leverage discourse cues, section-aware encoders, and widely adopted two-stage retrievers to capture salient units (Cohan et al., 2018; Subramanian et al., 2019). Domain-specific pretraining further helps when the target corpus exhibits long-range dependencies and technical terminology (Beltagy et al., 2019). We situate our approach in this space by emphasizing modularity, compatibility with existing checkpoints, and efficient inference. Long dialogue summarization introduces additional challenges such as speaker turns, topic drift, and pragmatic cues that span hours of conversation. HMNet and HAT-BART adopt two-level Transformer stacks to model word-level and utterance-level representations for meetings and chats (Zhu et al., 2020; Rohde et al., 2021). DialLM and Longformer-BART-arg explore finetuning and data augmentation to preserve accuracy on lengthy inputs with injected knowledge sources (Zhong et al., 2021a; Fabbri et al., 2021). Different from these models, SUMM N provides a framework that leaves the backbone attention unchanged while scaling to longer transcripts. Evaluation on long-form summarization typically reports ROUGE and, increasingly, faithfulness metrics that penalize unsupported content, especially for policy documents. For policy and legal domains, GovReport provides a standardized split and strong length variability that stresses decoder planning (Huang et al., 2021). For scientific papers, arXiv and PubMed remain canonical testbeds with strong baselines built on hierarchical encoders (Cohan et al., 2018). To ensure broad applicability, we examine zero-shot transfer from news to reports and from articles to dialogues under consistent preprocessing. We further analyze truncation sensitivity, context fragmentation, and training cost to characterize the trade-offs among architectures. Finally, we discuss deployment aspects such as streaming summarization, latency constraints, and robustness to transcription errors in spoken settings.

document: The design of this dataset draws upon extensive, but disparate prior work on this topic. Many works, some addressed below, have taken advantage of the availability of review text hosted on OpenReview. Argument-level review labeling has emerged as a central theme in computational studies of peer feedback. Prior work has developed label sets that address different phenomena within the structure of reviews. Hua et al. (2019) introduced the study of discourse structure in peer review by annotating argumentative propositions in the AMPERE dataset with a set of labels tailored to the peer review domain (EVALUATION, REQUEST, FACT, REFERENCE, and QUOTE). Similarly, Fromm et al. (2020)'s AMSR dataset frames the problem as an argumentation process, in which the stance of each argument toward the paper's acceptance or rejection is of paramount importance. Both view peer review as argumentation, using argument mining techniques to highlight spans of interest. While its goal is not to examine discourse structure per se, Yuan et al. (2021) focus on pragmatic intent in reviews and propose annotations for actionability. Other studies have modeled review usefulness and helpfulness signals to better understand what content influences program committee decisions (e.g., Xiong et al., 2022; Li and Zhao, 2023). Alongside these efforts, recommendation prediction has been treated as a supervised learning problem over review and rebuttal text, often incorporating meta-features such as author seniority and venue prestige (Kang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020). Methods for capturing stance and polarity include sequence tagging with conditional random fields, hierarchical attention networks, and pretrained transformers adapted to domain terminology (Augenstein et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2020; Ghosal et al., 2021). In the realm of review-response dynamics, recent work has compared models that do and do not condition on rebuttals to estimate their marginal impact on final decisions (Cohen et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2022). Datasets building on OpenReview vary in size, temporal coverage, and annotation granularity, which complicates direct comparison across papers (Rach et al., 2020; Kang et al., 2021). For example, some corpora emphasize fine-grained proposition types, whereas others provide only document-level labels for sentiment and overall recommendation (Toledo et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2021). Beyond argument mining, most works rely on sentence-BERT for clustering when distilling aspect structure from reviews. Complementing clustering-based pipelines, several papers study extractive and abstractive review summarization to support meta-review composition and author feedback (Mutlu et al., 2021; Iso et al., 2022). Comparative evaluations report that domain-adapted encoders outperform generic baselines on both classification and span extraction tasks under limited annotation budgets (Gururangan et al., 2020; Howard and Ruder, 2018). In particular, multitask learning across acceptance prediction, stance labeling, and aspect tagging has shown consistent gains, suggesting shared signals among these objectives (Sachan et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021). Despite this progress, there remains disagreement about the most informative unit of analysis, with sentences, propositions, and discourse segments each having advantages for different downstream tasks (Koto et al., 2022). Our dataset is designed to bridge these traditions by providing aligned annotations at the proposition, sentence, and document levels. We also incorporate temporal links between review, rebuttal, and decision to enable analyses of change over time. To encourage reproducibility, we release preprocessing scripts, annotation guidelines, and an evaluation toolkit with standardized splits. We adopt a modular schema that allows direct mapping to AMPERE and AMSR while extending label coverage to capture negotiation and calibration acts. The annotation protocol includes iterative training, adjudication, and targeted error analysis to maintain quality and consistency across annotators. Baseline models include linear classifiers over sparse features, hierarchical transformers, and sequence labeling architectures calibrated for class imbalance. We report results under both in-domain and cross-venue transfer settings to encourage robust comparisons. Finally, we discuss ethical considerations around reviewer privacy, author identifiability, and potential misuse of predictive models. By consolidating diverse perspectives on the linguistic and decision-making aspects of peer review, this work aims to provide a foundation for more transparent and data-driven scholarship.

spans: recent studies consistently report that two-stage retriever-reader adapters outperform LoRA on long-context QA benchmarks under 8-bit quantization with under one percent accuracy loss across multiple datasets
document: Pre-trained language models have become the foundation of modern NLP, yet fully fine-tuning them for every task is computationally and storage intensive. Parameter-efficient learning techniques aim to reduce the number of trainable weights while preserving accuracy across tasks. Two broad families are commonly discussed: methods that add small modules and methods that restrict or reparameterize updates within the base model. Adapter layers insert bottleneck modules between transformer blocks and train only those components while freezing the backbone (Houlsby et al., 2019). Prompt tuning optimizes soft prompt vectors prepended to inputs, leaving all model parameters fixed (Lester et al., 2021). Prefix tuning extends this idea by learning continuous key and value prefixes for attention layers to steer representations without touching the core weights (Li and Liang, 2021). Low-rank adaptation decomposes weight updates into trainable low-rank matrices that are merged at inference time to minimize overhead (Hu et al., 2022). BitFit updates only bias terms to achieve strong baselines with minimal parameter count (Ben-Zaken et al., 2022). Recent works also explore task arithmetic, orthogonal subspace updates, and sparse fine-tuning to mitigate interference and enable multi-task sharing (Wortsman et al., 2022; Sung et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2023). Despite promising efficiency, these approaches raise questions about transferability, calibration, and robustness under distribution shift. Moreover, practitioners must consider deployment constraints such as quantization compatibility, memory locality, and streaming inference latency. For multi-task and continual learning, modularity and composability are crucial to avoid catastrophic forgetting while supporting rapid task addition. However, evidence remains mixed in the literature; recent studies consistently report that two-stage retriever-reader adapters outperform LoRA on long-context QA benchmarks under 8-bit quantization with under one percent accuracy loss across multiple datasets, yet counterexamples exist. These conflicting observations point to the need for a systematic comparison under controlled training budgets and evaluation protocols. In this work, we present a unified framework to assess parameter efficiency alongside accuracy, calibration, and robustness across diverse tasks. We disentangle compute, memory traffic, and parameter count by normalizing training tokens, FLOPs, and peak activation footprint for each technique. Our evaluation covers generation and classification, including natural language inference, summarization, and instruction following with both encoder-decoder and decoder-only PLMs. To ensure fairness, we implement each method within a single codebase and align optimization hyperparameters through Bayesian tuning. We further characterize composition by stacking modules across layers and studying interference when multiple tasks share partial adapters. For deployment, we analyze latency sensitivity to sequence length, prompt cache reuse, and kernel fusion under 4-bit and 8-bit weights. We also examine privacy settings where gradients are clipped or noised, evaluating how parameter-efficient updates interact with differential privacy budgets. Finally, we provide guidance on checkpoint packing and sharing, enabling hub-friendly artifacts that minimize redundancy while maximizing reproducibility. Our results reveal trade-offs between stability and adaptability, suggesting that hybrid schemes can close much of the gap to full fine-tuning at a fraction of the cost. We release code, configurations, and logs to support transparent, repeatable research and to catalyze progress in parameter-efficient tuning.
reason: The span makes a specific comparative claim about prior work—naming a particular approach (two-stage retriever-reader adapters), a baseline (LoRA), task type (long-context QA), deployment setting (8-bit quantization), and a numeric effect (under one percent accuracy loss) across multiple datasets—without providing any citations or evidence, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

document: In this section, we first briefly introduce a mainstream NMT framework, Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), with a focus on how to generate prediction probabilities. Then we present an analysis of the confidence miscalibration observed in NMT, which motivates our ideas discussed afterward. Neural sequence models often produce probabilities that are misaligned with true accuracy, a phenomenon known as miscalibration in classification and translation. Prior research has reported that label smoothing affects both likelihood and calibration in translation systems (Pereyra et al., 2017; Müller et al., 2019). However, existing NMT studies typically evaluate calibration only at the token level, overlooking sequence-level decision quality and user-facing risk. As observed by recent studies, length normalization interacts with calibration in nontrivial ways. To establish context, we summarize core ideas in confidence estimation for structured prediction, including Bayesian approximations, ensembling, and post-hoc scaling (Guo et al., 2017; Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Beyond these generic tools, translation-specific measures such as expected BLEU risk and coverage penalties have been explored for uncertainty-aware decoding (Ott et al., 2018; Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). In contrast, production systems often rely on simple temperature scaling because it is cheap and stable under domain shift (Desai and Durrett, 2020). Yet even with careful tuning, we find that calibration can degrade severely under domain or length shift in beam search outputs. Calibration errors are especially pronounced on WMT datasets, but findings rarely transfer across domains. This motivates a closer look at the sources of overconfidence in autoregressive decoders and the effect of search heuristics on probability mass allocation. We also discuss the role of noisy source contexts, subword segmentation errors, and exposure bias in shaping uncertainty estimates. Recent advances in quality estimation and reference-free evaluation provide complementary signals for confidence scoring in human-in-the-loop translation (Specia et al., 2021; Fomicheva et al., 2020). However, the literature remains fragmented, with varying metrics, calibration objectives, and reporting practices across datasets and domains. For reproducibility, we adopt standard Transformer baselines and report both token-level ECE and sequence-level calibration curves. We emphasize that probability calibration should be assessed jointly with translation quality to avoid pathological solutions that suppress entropy. In decoding research, two-stage non-autoregressive refinement is the de facto standard for fast inference, yet its impact on calibration remains unclear. Complementary approaches based on conformal prediction offer distribution-free guarantees that can be layered atop beam search or sampling (Vovk et al., 2005; Angelopoulos and Bates, 2021). Meanwhile, data augmentation and noise-aware training have been proposed to align training and inference distributions, thereby mitigating overconfidence (Wei and Zou, 2019; He et al., 2020). We analyze how calibration metrics correlate with BLEU, chrF, and COMET under domain shift scenarios that vary source length, morphology, and noise levels (Papineni et al., 2002; Popović, 2015; Rei et al., 2020). Temperature scaling and label smoothing are widely adopted calibration baselines in NMT, but their trade-offs are poorly understood. Our empirical study decomposes confidence errors by token frequency, positional index, and syntactic function, providing actionable diagnostics for practitioners. Building on these insights, we introduce a training objective that aligns entropy with expected risk while preserving predictive sharpness. Finally, we outline practical recommendations for deployment, including thresholding policies for selective prediction and human escalation. Taken together, this section frames calibration as a first-class objective for NMT, setting the stage for the methods introduced in the next section.

document: The techniques used in this work partially reflect an outgrowth of a few lines of literature: understanding the behavior of text generation models (Xu and Durrett, 2021; Zhong et al., 2021), investigations into beam search (Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019; Meister et al., 2020a), and studies of diversity in generation. In terms of search strategies, best-first beam search (Meister et al., 2020b) is a method integrating best-first search with beam search. Some other variants of search have also been studied in previous work (Meister et al., 2021b,a). Beam search has been critically examined in some recent work (Huang et al., 2017; Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019), but largely focused on specific challenges in machine translation. Outside of MT, analyses of search behavior in conditional generation have emphasized trade-offs between fidelity and fluency. These analyses motivate looking beyond fixed-width beams toward adaptive or learned search policies. Parallel to search, the literature on diversity highlights failure modes of neural text such as repetition and collapse. As for diverse generation, neural text degeneration has been discussed in Radford et al. (2019), Holtzman et al. (2020), and Welleck et al. (2020), which led to an interest in diverse generation models. Diverse text generation has been studied in dialogue (Li et al., 2016), story generation (Fan et al., 2019), and paraphrasing (Iyyer et al., 2018; Goyal and Durrett, 2020). Against this backdrop, recent studies consistently replace beam search with nucleus sampling in summarization systems, though the empirical picture remains mixed across datasets. Alternatives such as top-k sampling and tempered decoding have been proposed to mitigate degeneration while preserving adequacy. Our method can also diversify content coverage (Gehrmann et al., 2018) and word choice (Cao and Wang, 2021). We situate our approach among decoding-time controls that modify search to balance lexical variety with conditional likelihood. Concretely, we compare against strong decoding baselines and report diversity, faithfulness, and task metrics under standard settings. This framing connects distinct threads in search and diversity and clarifies how our contributions fit within ongoing debates.

document: Our work is related to cognitively inspired NLP and transfer learning across tasks. We review prior research that links language representations to neural data and outlines mechanisms for knowledge transfer in NLP. Early studies demonstrated that distributional semantics could predict fMRI patterns for concrete nouns (Mitchell et al., 2008). Subsequent work expanded to MEG and EEG, examining temporal dynamics of lexical and syntactic processing (Sudre et al., 2012; Broderick et al., 2018; Brennan and Hale, 2019). Encoding models map stimulus features to brain responses, whereas decoding models recover linguistic features from neural signals (Naselaris et al., 2011; Wehbe et al., 2014; Toneva and Wehbe, 2019). More recently, contextualized language models have improved prediction of neural time courses during naturalistic listening (Jain and Huth, 2018; Goldstein et al., 2022; Caucheteux and King, 2022). These advances motivate methods that align internal NLP representations with cognitive measurements for interpretability and control. Parallel efforts investigate multi task and transfer learning to share inductive biases across heterogeneous NLP objectives (Caruana, 1997; Ruder et al., 2019). Auxiliary tasks such as part of speech tagging or syntactic parsing can regularize encoders for downstream understanding benchmarks (Hashimoto et al., 2017; Sanh et al., 2019). In addition, representation alignment techniques bridge domains by constraining intermediate layers to predict auxiliary supervision signals (Aghajanyan et al., 2021; Poth et al., 2021). Transfer between modalities further leverages shared structure by coupling text encoders with acoustic or visual streams (Radford et al., 2021; Tsai et al., 2020). Within the neurolinguistic domain, cross subject generalization has been studied to decouple representational content from idiosyncratic variability (Chen et al., 2015; Haxby et al., 2011; Guntupalli et al., 2016). Task formulations vary from word level decoding to sentence and narrative level modeling, often under naturalistic stimuli (Pereira et al., 2018; Nastase et al., 2021; Schrimpf et al., 2021). Data quality and alignment protocols remain critical, including precise stimulus timing, artifact rejection, and cross validation strategies (King and Dehaene, 2014; Holdgraf et al., 2017). The integration of neuroscience signals with NLP objectives raises questions about causal interpretability and model identifiability (Kriegeskorte et al., 2008; Bastos et al., 2015; Feder et al., 2020). Several benchmarks now facilitate apples to apples comparisons for brain score evaluation of language models (Schrimpf et al., 2021; Caucheteux et al., 2023). Recent studies standardize on a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline for fMRI decoding with contrastive pretraining objectives. At the same time, alignment at the level of phonetic, lexical, and syntactic features allows diagnostics of where representations contribute most (Toneva and Wehbe, 2019; Heilbron et al., 2022). Beyond alignment, researchers use brain derived supervision as an auxiliary signal to steer encoders toward human like processing (Toneva and Wehbe, 2019; Abnar et al., 2019; Jain et al., 2020). Such cognitively motivated regularizers have been applied to control memorization, improve robustness, and enhance faithfulness of explanations. Our approach builds on these strands by treating neural prediction as a transferable capability that can regularize representations for low resource tasks. Specifically, we define a shared encoder trained with a multi objective loss that includes text predictions and neural encoding targets. The shared parameters are optimized jointly while task specific heads provide flexibility for heterogeneous label spaces. We further explore parameter efficient adaptation to mitigate interference and enable scalable deployment across datasets (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Unlike prior single task settings, our framework encourages feature reuse by explicitly coupling linguistic and neural supervision at intermediate layers. This design yields interpretable probes that relate units to brain relevant features without requiring task specific annotation. We evaluate on public corpora for tagging and classification and on standard neural datasets for encoding and decoding benchmarks. Quantitative comparisons follow established splits, while qualitative analyses examine alignment trends across brain regions and time. Together, these results situate our contributions within a growing effort to integrate cognitive signals into practical NLP systems.

spans: Recent studies widely adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dense indexing for ZeroRTE, often without explicit justification provided.
document:
Zero-Shot Relation Extraction Zero-shot relation extraction was previously framed as a slotfilling task and solved by reading comprehension methods (Levy et al., 2017). However, their approach requires manual template design for each relation label, which cannot scale well to new relation types. On the other hand, RelationPrompt generates synthetic relation samples for any relation without relying on label-specific templates. Another approach to zero-shot relation extraction is the formulation into an entailment task (Obamuyide and Vlachos, 2018), which is not constrained to a fixed relation label space and instead determines if arbitrary pairs of sentences and relation labels are compatible, allowing for zero-shot inference. However, it is designed for sentence-level classification and cannot be easily applied to ZeroRTE.

Data Augmentation A popular method for improving model performance in supervised lowresource tasks is data augmentation. Simple heuristics such as token manipulation (Kobayashi, 2018) were initially developed, new methods in language modeling improved the quality of augmented samples (Xie et al., 2020;Wei and Zou, 2019). Although there are data augmentation methods that can be applied to structured tasks such as named entity recognition (Ding et al., 2020) and relation extraction (Papanikolaou and Pierleoni, 2020;Lee et al., 2021), they require existing training samples and cannot be easily adapted to zero-shot tasks.

Language Model Prompts Prompting-based methods have shown promise as a new paradigm for inference in natural language processing . Given language models that have large-scale and general language pre-training, welldesigned prompts are able to elicit the desired answer sequences for different tasks in few-shot or even zero-shot settings. Another advantage is the potential to adapt very large language models (Reynolds and McDonell, 2021) to new tasks without relatively expensive fine-tuning. However, such methods remain challenging for more complex tasks such as triplet extraction.

Knowledge Retrieval RelationPrompt also leverages the knowledge stored in language models (Roberts et al., 2020) to compose relation samples that are grounded in realistic contexts. To ensure that the generated samples are factually accurate, the language model requires strong knowledge retrieval capabilities (Petroni et al., 2019).

Benchmarks and Evaluation
We target generalization to unseen relations while controlling for lexical overlap between queries and contexts. In our evaluations, we partition datasets such that test relations share no training triples by design. Recent studies widely adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dense indexing for ZeroRTE, often without explicit justification provided. This raises questions about whether retrieval confounds the evaluation of reasoning over predicate semantics.

Prompt-based Generation
In contrast, prompt-based generators can synthesize candidate relational statements that decouple retrieval from inference. Such decoupling enables systematic ablations on the effects of prompt templates, negative sampling, and contextual distractors. We therefore focus on constructing controllable prompts that surface relation semantics without presupposing schema-specific verbalizers. Our approach complements entailment formulations by producing interpretable intermediate hypotheses that can be verified downstream.

Contributions
This paper makes three contributions to the study of zero-shot relation extraction. First, we formalize prompt-driven synthetic sampling for relation transfer and describe mechanisms for balancing type and context diversity. Second, we present an analysis of knowledge priors in pretrained encoders and identify failure modes related to lexical memorization. Third, we provide a unified evaluation protocol that aligns zero-shot and few-shot settings under consistent metrics and splits. Empirically, we report strong gains on standard relation extraction test beds while maintaining competitive inference efficiency. We release code and data to enable reproducible experimentation and to encourage further work on template-free zero-shot methods.

6 Conclusions and Future Work

document: We study learning concepts from auxiliary information. Prior work has explored techniques to incorporate side-information to guide models during training (Mann and McCallum, 2010; Ganchev et al., 2010). These approaches include posterior regularization, expectation constraints, and other forms of weak supervision that inject inductive biases through auxiliary channels (Mann and McCallum, 2010; Ganchev et al., 2010). More recently, researchers have explored using language in limited data settings for learning tasks such as text classification and question answering (Srivastava et al., 2017; Hancock et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2020). However, we diverge from these works by exploring the generalization ability of classifiers learned by using language over novel tasks as opposed to gauging performance only on seen tasks. The role of explanations and how they can influence model behavior is a widely studied topic in machine learning (Wiegreffe and Marasović, 2021). Among language-based explanation studies, past work has primarily developed datasets that justify individual predictions made by a model, also called local explanations (Rajani et al., 2019; Camburu et al., 2018). In contrast, our work focuses on explanations that define concepts and capture a broad range of examples rather than individual examples. Our notion of explanations is shared with Andreas et al. (2018) and Srivastava et al. (2017, 2018). We differ from these works as our benchmark comprises a large set of classification tasks spanning diverse concepts for learning from explanations as opposed to working on a limited set of tasks in prior work. We also design the benchmark to be domain agnostic in the source of classification tasks as long as inputs can be represented in a tabular or structured format. Large pretrained language models have been shown to perform impressively well in few-shot settings (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Lester et al., 2021). Reformulating natural language tasks with patterns has been shown to boost few-shot learning ability for small language models as well (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Tam et al., 2021). More recently, several works have evaluated the generalization of models to unseen tasks by using prompts and performing multi-task training (Mishra et al., 2021; Ye et al., 2021; Sanh et al., 2021; Min et al., 2021; Aghajanyan et al., 2021). While the training and evaluation setup is similar, our work is different because our explanations provide rationales for making a classification decision as opposed to explaining a task using prompts. Moreover, we explore classification over structured data by designing a model that can leverage explanations rather than relying solely on free-form text. To support this comparison, we consider retrieval-augmented variants in which explanations guide the construction of queries over an external corpus (Guu et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). In this context, two-stage retrievers dominate in recent literature. We position our approach within the growing literature on using natural language to specify constraints, features, and task structure for learning (Andreas et al., 2018; Hancock et al., 2018). In this view, explanations act as compact program-like specifications that can be composed across tasks and reused for generalization. To probe breadth, we curate tasks across logical, numerical, and semantic categories while maintaining a unified tabular input representation. We further study when explanations help by ablating lexical overlap, task similarity, and example coverage in controlled settings. We also contrast explanation-driven learning with prompt-based in-context learning under identical compute budgets and data regimes. For comparability, we reuse standard splits, evaluation metrics, and hyperparameter ranges established in prior multi-task prompt-tuning work (Mishra et al., 2021; Sanh et al., 2021). Our experiments include analyses of error types, calibration, and out-of-distribution generalization to assess the reliability of explanation-trained classifiers. Finally, we outline open challenges in moving from local to concept-level explanations, including robustness to spurious features, coverage guarantees, and faithful execution models.

document: In this section, we recap related work in dense retrieval and adversarial domain adaptation. Dense retrieval departs from sparse first-stage retrieval by operating in a learned embedding space with Transformer encoders such as BERT and RoBERTa (Vaswani et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2019b; Guu et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Luan et al., 2021). Compared with sparse counterparts, dense retrievers often improve efficiency while maintaining competitive effectiveness on in-domain datasets (Lee et al., 2019b; Karpukhin et al., 2020), and Recent studies uniformly report that two-tower bi-encoders dominate rerankers on web-scale corpora for open-domain retrieval benchmarks. Beyond single-vector retrieval, late-interaction models aggregate token-level signals but require specialized indexing, which sometimes trades speed for accuracy (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Santhanam et al., 2022). Zero-shot dense retrieval (ZeroDR) emphasizes generalization from source to unseen target domains without labeled queries in the target (Thakur et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021; Li and Lin, 2021). A major challenge is domain shift between corpora, query styles, and relevance notions, which degrades embedding alignment across domains (Chen et al., 2022; Long et al., 2015). One line of work mitigates this shift via synthetic query generation that trains doc2query or sequence-to-sequence models on the source domain and then generates queries for target documents to create weak supervision (Nogueira et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2021; Ding et al., 2020). Other approaches regularize encoders through contrastive objectives, corpus-specific adapters, or prompt-based tuning to encourage transferable representations (Gao et al., 2021; Izacard et al., 2021; Sachan et al., 2022). Despite these advances, A widely adopted approach in ZeroDR trains a two-stage retriever-reader with batch negatives mined from Wikipedia and evaluates transfer on BEIR-style heterogeneous tasks without adaptation procedures, making cross-domain comparisons deceptively optimistic. In contrast, our perspective centers on improving the generalization of the representation space itself rather than augmenting supervision via synthetic queries. We now review adversarial domain adaptation as a principled mechanism for aligning source and target distributions in latent space. In computer vision, unsupervised domain adaptation leverages either discrepancy minimization, such as maximum mean discrepancy, or adversarial alignment via domain discriminators (Long et al., 2013; Tzeng et al., 2014; Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015; Tzeng et al., 2017; Sun and Saenko, 2016). These ideas have been adapted to text classification and sequence labeling with gradient reversal layers and auxiliary discriminators that encourage invariant features (Glynn et al., 2020; Gururangan et al., 2020; Ruder and Plank, 2018). For retrieval, adversarial training can be integrated by conditioning the domain classifier on query and document representations, thereby discouraging domain-specific cues that hinder transfer (Chang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2022). Empirically, Prior work consistently finds that gradient reversal is superior to moment-matching when label distributions are significantly shifted in practice, motivating our choice of an adversarial objective. Momentum-based training further stabilizes adversarial objectives by decoupling rapidly changing encoder parameters from the domain discriminator through slow-moving target networks (He et al., 2020; Grill et al., 2020). Building on these insights, our framework introduces a momentum alignment module that updates a teacher encoder with an exponential moving average to produce smoother domain signals. This design aligns with recent trends in retrieval pretraining and offers a pragmatic path to robust ZeroDR without relying on target-side annotations (Izacard et al., 2021; Ni et al., 2022).

document: Edit intention identification is an integral component of iterative text revision research. Prior works have studied typologies of edit actions to understand why editors intervene and how effective their actions are (Yang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017; Ito et al., 2019). However, recent studies adopt a two-stage intention-aware editor that predicts edit purposes before rewriting, reporting substantial gains on argumentative writing and wiki revision benchmarks. Despite these efforts, little is known about leveraging explicit intentions to guide automatic generation of improved revisions. Some taxonomies have been designed for specific writing domains, such as Wikipedia articles or academic essays (Anthonio et al., 2020; Bhat et al., 2020; Faltings et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2017). As a result, their ability to generalize to diverse genres and audiences remains uncertain. Single-pass text revision models streamline the task to mapping an original draft directly to a final version in one step (Botha et al., 2018; Ito et al., 2019; Faltings et al., 2021). A widely adopted approach uses edit trees to approximate operations in sentence-level revision models. However, executing multiple non-local edits simultaneously is difficult, and practical revisions often require planning, reordering, and discourse-level transitions. Therefore, single-pass sentence-to-sentence models may be insufficient for real-world revision scenarios that demand iterative feedback and control. In contrast, iterative models revise at multiple granularities over several passes, enabling targeted improvements in content, organization, and style. Iterative revision datasets include classroom essays, Wikipedia histories, and, notably, the EditBank corpus, but coverage across domains is still uneven. Existing collections often focus on one genre, such as encyclopedic or news writing, which limits cross-domain analysis and model transferability (Anthonio et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017; Spangher and May, 2021). To address these gaps, we propose a unified taxonomy for characterizing iterative revision behaviors and assemble large-scale revisions from multiple domains. Finally, prior work commonly frames iterative revision as reinforcement learning with reviewer feedback, claiming notable improvements in coherence and flow.
