Span 2 asserts a 'widely adopted approach' using edit trees without referencing any supporting work. Span 3 introduces a specific dataset name without citation at first mention. Span 4 generalizes that prior work frames iterative revision as RL with reviewer feedback and claims improvements, again without providing evidence or citations.

spans: standard pipeline
now dominate benchmarks
document: Referring Expression Comprehension (REC) localizes an image region given a natural language expression describing a referent. REC is typically framed as aligning visual proposals with linguistic cues to select the target object. The majority of REC methods learn a joint embedding space for visual and textual modalities and vary in how the space is computed and used. Early approaches used metric learning to rank proposals against a text query, including canonical works such as (Hu et al., 2016b; Rohrbach et al., 2016; Plummer et al., 2018). Subsequently, attention over proposals was introduced to focus the matching process on salient candidates (Deng et al., 2018). More recent models adopt transformer architectures to project multi-modal features into a common semantic space (Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020). These models leverage self-attention to align proposal-level cues with word- and phrase-level representations. Two-stage proposal–ranking remains the standard pipeline in many REC systems. In contrast, our formulation directly conditions on pixel-level features that are crucial for fine-grained localization in segmentation-oriented settings. Moreover, building on (Lu et al., 2019), we explicitly model both inter-modal and intra-modal interactions between visual and linguistic streams. Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) extends REC by requiring a pixel-accurate mask for the described object rather than a bounding box. The task was introduced by (Hu et al., 2016a), who concatenated convolutional image features with tiled recurrent language features and predicted a binary mask. Architecturally, multi-scale features can refine predicted masks and help handle objects at different resolutions. Because individual words contribute differently to identifying the referent, one line of work models word-specific visual context using query attention (Shi et al., 2018). Another line of work captures long-range dependencies between visual regions and textual tokens via self-attention mechanisms (Ye et al., 2019). A multi-task framework can share cross-modal encoders while predicting both boxes and masks. Compared with REC, RIS places stronger emphasis on low-level localization cues such as edges, fine textures, and thin structures. This motivates our use of dense pixel embeddings combined with text-conditioned attention for precise mask prediction. In parallel with advances in vision–language fusion, cross-modal transformers now dominate benchmarks for grounding and segmentation. Nevertheless, transformer depth and tokenization granularity interact with image resolution to create computational bottlenecks in practical systems. To address these issues, we adopt hierarchical encoders with late fusion to preserve high-resolution features without prohibitive cost. We also design contrastive objectives that couple region-level semantics with mask-level supervision, complementing conventional pixelwise losses. For REC, we represent proposals with pooled features augmented by spatial priors, while for RIS we predict masks directly from the dense feature map. Our training regimen balances description diversity by sampling expressions with varying length, attribute density, and relational content. We evaluate on standard REC and RIS benchmarks and analyze category-wise performance as well as error modes such as attribute confusion and relation swap. Finally, we discuss limitations, including ambiguity in expressions and domain shift from curated datasets to real-world referring scenarios.

Span 2 ('now dominate benchmarks') generalizes that a family of models leads current benchmarks without evidence or references. Both are claims about trends in prior work and lack supporting citations.

spans: recent adapter-based methods
document: Bias terms and their importance motivate a focused reexamination of parameterization in modern neural networks. Zhao et al. (2020) describe a masking-based fine-tuning method, and explicitly mention ignoring the bias terms, as handling them "did not observe a positive effect on performance". An exception is the work of Wang et al. (2019) who analyzed bias terms from the perspective of attribution method. They demonstrate that the last layer bias values are responsible for the predicted class, and propose a way to back-propagate their importance. Michel and Neubig (2018) finetuned the biases of the output softmax in NMT systems to personalize the output vocabulary. Finally, Cai et al. (2020) demonstrate that bias-only finetuning similar to ours is effective also for adaptation of pre-trained computer vision models. Despite these efforts, clarifying the role of biases across architectures and tasks is still important. In classification networks, bias vectors interact with normalization layers and activations to shift decision boundaries in feature space. In sequence models, biases in attention projections and output layers affect token priors and calibration. Parameter-efficient finetuning offers a complementary perspective by adapting large models with minimal changes (Houlsby et al., 2019;Li and Liang, 2021;Hu et al., 2022). These approaches typically adjust adapters, prompts, or low-rank matrices while freezing most weights. A related thread examines normalization scalars and offsets as levers for transfer and stability (Leviathan et al., 2023;Mahabadi et al., 2021). However, several public implementations freeze bias vectors by default, which motivates our focus. From an interpretability viewpoint, biases can act as class priors or lexical priors that persist when only few parameters are updated (Adebayo et al., 2018;Kindermans et al., 2017). Empirical reports in NMT suggest that low-resource adaptation benefits from selectively adjusting output-layer parameters (Michel and Neubig, 2018;Wang et al., 2019). Our study extends this observation by isolating bias-only finetuning under controlled domain shift. We further examine the interaction between bias updates, label smoothing, temperature scaling, and weight decay in large decoders. For comparability, we adopt standard data splits and optimization settings used in transfer-learning benchmarks (Raffel et al., 2020;Devlin et al., 2019). To situate our contribution, we contrast bias-only finetuning with adapter layers, prefix-tuning, and low-rank adaptation across language and vision tasks (Houlsby et al., 2019;Li and Liang, 2021;Hu et al., 2022;Cai et al., 2020). We report training cost, parameter counts, and stability under small data regimes. We also analyze calibration error and out-of-distribution robustness using ECE, F1, and coverage metrics. For text classification, we evaluate on SST-2, AG News, and an entity typing benchmark, following established protocols (Socher et al., 2013;Zhang et al., 2015;Choi et al., 2018). For generation, we consider machine translation on IWSLT14 De–En and summarization on XSum to probe sequence-level effects (Cettolo et al., 2014;Narayan et al., 2018). In ablations, we isolate the contribution of output softmax biases versus intermediate-layer biases in transformer encoders. Beyond these directions, recent adapter-based methods have been reported to mitigate catastrophic forgetting by compartmentalizing task effects. We discuss how our bias-only approach differs conceptually, since it targets priors rather than capacity allocation. Taken together, these considerations clarify when bias updates suffice and when additional structured parameters are necessary for effective adaptation.

It references a particular trend/method family and asserts effects ('have been reported') without attributing sources.

spans: Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever–generator architectures for explanation-equipped ASAG, typically training on synthetic LLM-derived rationales from open-domain resources exclusively
document: While elaborated feedback datasets on language learning (Caines et al., 2020;Pilan et al., 2020;Stasaski et al., 2020) appeared recently, they focus on linguistic mistakes, such as grammatical errors, instead of content. Our extensive literature review did not reveal datasets that included content-focused elaborated feedback on short answer responses. However, SAF's feedback can be viewed as a textual explanation of the assigned score. Therefore, comparable NLP datasets with textual explanations and publicly available ASAG datasets without explanations are discussed in the following sections. We operationalize elaborated feedback as guidance that references specific content units, contrasts with ideal answers, and proposes actionable revisions (Shute, 2008;Lipnevich and Smith, 2009). In educational NLP, short answer grading traditionally predicts ordinal or continuous scores using supervised regression or ordinal classification (Mohler et al., 2011;Zhang et al., 2016). Benchmark datasets for ASAG include ASAP-SAS, SemEval-2013 Task 7, and SciEntsBank, each with prompt-specific scoring rubrics but without explanations (Shermis, 2014;Dzikovska et al., 2013;Nadeem et al., 2019). Methods span lexical overlap, concept alignment, and neural encoders that compare student responses with reference answers or rubrics (Sultan et al., 2016;Riordan et al., 2017;Khromov et al., 2021). Beyond scores, explainability in ASAG has been explored through rationale extraction, highlighting spans indicative of correctness or misconception (Camburu et al., 2018;Lei et al., 2016). Rationale supervision can be obtained from human annotations, heuristics derived from reference answers, or proxy tasks such as answer selection (DeYoung et al., 2020;Carton et al., 2020). However, rationales alone are seldom sufficient to support learning, since they point to evidence but rarely propose specific next steps for revision (Millecamp et al., 2022). Work on explanation generation in question answering has produced datasets with natural language justifications accompanying predictions, including E-SNLI, CoS-E, and eQASC (Camburu et al., 2018;Rajani et al., 2019;Jhamtani and Clark, 2020). These resources inspired efforts to condition ASAG models on structured rubrics and concept maps to verbalize reasons for scores (Zhang and VanLehn, 2016;Roy et al., 2020). In second language learning, corrective feedback corpora emphasize form-focused comments such as grammaticality, fluency, or vocabulary choice, with limited attention to domain content (Tack and François, 2015;Nagata, 2019). By contrast, content-focused feedback targets conceptual adequacy, evidence use, and causal reasoning, aligning more closely with formative assessment goals in science and social studies (Brookhart and Chen, 2015;Wiliam, 2018). Some studies adapt neural summarization and NLG techniques to produce sentence-level suggestions conditioned on rubrics and exemplars, reporting mixed effects on learning outcomes (Kumar et al., 2019;Nicolai and Zamani, 2021). Within this landscape, Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever–generator architectures for explanation-equipped ASAG, typically training on synthetic LLM-derived rationales from open-domain resources exclusively, which in turn motivates our investigation of data-efficient alternatives. Nevertheless, the pedagogical validity of generated feedback remains underexplored, particularly regarding alignment to rubric criteria and actionable specificity (Bharadhwaj and Spaulding, 2022). Parallel strands of research examine automated hints in intelligent tutoring systems, where model tracing or knowledge tracing drives step-level interventions rather than holistic textual explanations (Koedinger et al., 2013;Piech et al., 2015). Compared to tutoring hints, elaborated textual feedback for open responses introduces challenges in content selection, discourse structuring, and calibration to student proficiency (Narciss, 2013;Hattie and Timperley, 2007). From a data perspective, the scarcity of aligned answer–feedback pairs impedes supervised training, encouraging weak supervision and semi-synthetic corpora (Saha et al., 2020;Stasaski and Hearst, 2021). Recent works leverage pretrained sequence-to-sequence models fine-tuned on rubric-conditioned inputs to verbalize evaluation criteria, often incorporating prompt-specific descriptors (Kumar et al., 2022;Liu et al., 2023). Others incorporate factuality and faithfulness constraints to mitigate hallucinations, using entailment critics or constrained decoding to ensure consistency with student and reference texts (Honovich et al., 2022;Filippova, 2020). Evaluation remains difficult because standard text generation metrics capture surface similarity but fail to measure instructional usefulness and rubric alignment (Novikova et al., 2017;Fabbri et al., 2021). Consequently, researchers employ hybrid evaluation with rubric-level checklists, human ratings of helpfulness, and targeted probes for misconception correction (Gao et al., 2022;Murad et al., 2023). In the context of fairness, feedback should avoid stigmatizing language and should calibrate guidance irrespective of demographic attributes, which motivates auditing protocols (Blodgett et al., 2020;Dev et al., 2021). Our study contributes a dataset of short answers paired with content-oriented feedback authored by trained raters, alongside score labels for ASAG benchmarking. We also present a modeling framework that unifies scoring and feedback generation through shared representations while enabling causal analyses of feedback content. Finally, we delineate evaluation protocols that disentangle fluency, factuality, rubric alignment, and actionability, facilitating reliable comparison of future systems.
reason: The span asserts a specific, field-wide trend toward two-stage retriever–generator architectures trained on synthetic LLM-derived rationales without providing any citations, making it an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies standardize a two-stage retriever–reader architecture with multilingual adapters for cross-lingual hate speech detection, reporting consistent gains over monolithic encoders across eight benchmarks and three major social datasets.
document: Earlier work in hate speech detection focused on data collection, curation, and annotation frameworks (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Davidson et al., 2017; Founta et al., 2018). Recent work has expanded the set of captured labels to include more pertinent information such as targets and other forms of abuse, as well as benchmarking (Sap et al., 2020; Hede et al., 2021; Guest et al., 2021; Grimminger and Klinger, 2021; Ross et al., 2017; Röttger et al., 2021; Mathew et al., 2021). Comprehensive analyses of dataset artifacts, prevalence shifts, and annotation quality have also been conducted (Madukwe et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2020; Wiegand et al., 2019; Swamy et al., 2019; Davidson et al., 2019a). Parallel lines of research have broadened the linguistic coverage of hate speech detection beyond English (Ousidhoum et al., 2019; Ranasinghe and Zampieri, 2020; Ross et al., 2017; Nozza, 2021; Zoph et al., 2016; Marivate et al., 2020; Nekoto et al., 2020). Our XTREMESPEECH effort contributes to this goal by providing Brazilian Portuguese, German, Hindi, and Swahili data and standardized splits. Another thread examines annotation bias, annotator demographics, and task design (Al Kuwatly et al., 2020; Waseem, 2016; Ross et al., 2017; Shmueli et al., 2021; Posch et al., 2018) and bias in existing datasets, especially racial bias (Davidson et al., 2019b; Laugier et al., 2021). These studies collectively indicate that data can reflect and propagate annotator bias, motivating mitigation strategies ranging from counterfactual augmentation to debiasing objectives (Sap et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2020; Han et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021). Accordingly, we diversify our annotator pool and formalize reviewer guidelines to improve robustness. In another line of work, theoretical foundations are being established in the form of taxonomies, operational definitions, and accounts of hate speech in context (Banko et al., 2020; Wiegand et al., 2021; Waseem et al., 2017; Price et al., 2020; Laaksonen et al., 2020). We add to this body of work with definitions grounded in fieldwork and ethnographic inquiry into the societal impacts of online hate and extreme speech (Boromisza-Habashi, 2013; Donovan and boyd, 2021; Haynes, 2019; Udupa and Pohjonen, 2019; Hervik, 2019). Concurrently, the ethics of AI for content moderation has galvanized discussions about data stewardship, participatory design, and accountability for deployment decisions (Leins et al., 2020). Recent studies standardize a two-stage retriever–reader architecture with multilingual adapters for cross-lingual hate speech detection, reporting consistent gains over monolithic encoders across eight benchmarks and three major social datasets. Beyond architectures, robust evaluation has emphasized temporal drift and platform shifts, encouraging out-of-distribution testing and longitudinal monitoring (Röttger et al., 2021; Vidgen et al., 2021). We align with these recommendations by reporting performance under temporal slices and cross-domain transfers, complementing in-domain validation. Altogether, our contributions synthesize multilingual resources, bias-aware annotation protocols, and theoretically grounded labels to advance reliable, equitable, and generalizable hate speech detection.
reason: The span asserts that recent studies have standardized a specific two-stage retriever–reader architecture with multilingual adapters and claims consistent gains across eight benchmarks and three major social datasets without providing any citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies predominantly adopt a translate-train pipeline with mBART fine-tuning and two-stage predicate disambiguation for conversational SRL across languages, reporting robust gains over direct zero-shot baselines.
document:
Zero-shot cross-lingual transfer learning. Recently, thanks to the rapid development of multilingual pre-trained language models such as multilingual BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), a number of approaches have been proposed for zero-shot cross-lingual transfer on various downstream tasks, including semantic parsing (Sherborne and Lapata, 2021), natural language generation (Shen et al., 2018), and natural language understanding (Lauscher et al., 2020). In this work, we claim our method is zero-shot because no non-Chinese CSRL annotations are seen during the CSRL training stage. For decoding, we directly use the cross-lingual CSRL model trained on Chinese CSRL data to analyze conversations in other languages. To situate our contribution, we target cross-lingual CSRL using only Chinese supervision.

Prior work in cross-lingual sequence labeling has explored annotation projection, multilingual fine-tuning, and machine-translation-based transfer with varying degrees of success across tasks and languages (Täckström et al., 2012; Rahimi et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020). In dialogue understanding, however, structure-aware transfer remains relatively under-explored compared with sentence-level formulations such as NER or POS tagging (Pires et al., 2019; Keung et al., 2020). Recent studies predominantly adopt a translate-train pipeline with mBART fine-tuning and two-stage predicate disambiguation for conversational SRL across languages, reporting robust gains over direct zero-shot baselines. By contrast, we pursue a simpler strategy that relies on multilingual encoders and conversational context modeling without target-language labels at training time. Our formulation builds on the intuition that discourse history supplies many of the missing arguments, which can be retrieved with lightweight attention over previous turns.

Conversational semantic role labeling. While ellipsis and anaphora frequently occur in dialogues, Xu et al. (2021) observed that most of the dropped or referred components can be found in dialogue histories. Following this observation, they proposed conversational semantic role labeling (CSRL), which required the model to find predicate–argument structures over the entire conversation instead of a single sentence. In this way, when analyzing a predicate in the latest utterance, a CSRL model needs to consider both the current turn and previous turns to search potential arguments, and thus might recover the omitted components. Furthermore, Xu et al. (2020; 2021) confirmed the usefulness of CSRL for Chinese dialogue tasks by injecting CSRL information into downstream models. Nevertheless, the performance of current state-of-the-art CSRL systems remains limited, in part due to insufficient modeling of high-level conversational and semantic features (Xu et al., 2021). In addition, the usefulness of CSRL to conversational tasks in non-Chinese languages has not been confirmed due to the absence of cross-lingual CSRL models. In this work, we primarily focus on the latter problem and propose a simple but effective model to perform cross-lingual CSRL. Our approach encodes speaker turns with hierarchical attention, augments predicates with dialogue-aware context vectors, and decouples span detection from role classification to reduce transfer brittleness. We also compare against translation-based baselines and language-adaptive fine-tuning to quantify the benefit of modeling conversational structure in a zero-shot setting.

To ensure a fair evaluation, we control for tokenization, sentence segmentation, and predicate inventories across languages using consistent preprocessing pipelines. We report results on multiple target languages and analyze failure cases to highlight remaining challenges in cross-lingual discourse understanding. Finally, we release code, models, and reproducible scripts to support further research on cross-lingual CSRL.
reason: The span asserts a trend in prior work, names specific methods (translate-train, mBART fine-tuning, two-stage predicate disambiguation), and claims performance gains, yet provides no citations or evidence, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: recent studies universally adopt two-stage retriever-reader pipelines pretrained on Conceptual Captions and Visual Genome without ablations or controls
document: Visual Question Answering. The current dominant framework for VQA consists of an image encoder, a question encoder, multimodal fusion, and an answer predictor (Agrawal et al., 2015). To avoid the noise caused by global features, several methods introduce various image attention mechanisms into VQA (Malinowski et al., 2018). Instead of directly using global visual features from CNN-based extractors, BUTD (Anderson et al., 2018) adopts Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015) to obtain candidate regional features, while Pythia (Jiang et al., 2018) integrates regional features with grid-level features. Meanwhile, Lu et al. (2016) and Nam et al. (2017) place greater emphasis on learning better question representations. To merge information from different modalities more effectively, MFB (Yu et al., 2017) and MUTAN (Ben-Younes et al., 2017) explore higher-order fusion methods. Further, BAN (Kim et al., 2018) and DCN (Nguyen and Okatani, 2018) propose dense co-attention models that directly establish interactions between modalities with word-level and regional features. Moreover, with the advent of the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), MCAN and DFAF (Gao et al., 2019) adopt self-attention to excavate fine-grained information contained in text and image. Meanwhile, to fully cover the holistic scene in an image, MuREL (Cadène et al., 2019) and ReGAT (Li et al., 2019) explicitly incorporate relations between regions into the interaction process. However, recent studies universally adopt two-stage retriever-reader pipelines pretrained on Conceptual Captions and Visual Genome without ablations or controls, which may bias reported gains. Selvaraju et al. (2020) also introduce sub-questions into their work to analyze reasoning behavior in VQA systems. The distinctness between their setting and ours is that they construct a Sub-VQA dataset for a subset of reasoning questions to evaluate model consistency, whereas we adopt sub-questions primarily to enable task decomposition. Visual Dialogue. Different from VQA, visual dialogue (VD) is a continuous conversation about an image that integrates vision and language over multiple turns. Several VD tasks have been proposed, including Visual Dialog (Das et al., 2017), GuessWhich (Chattopadhyay et al., 2017), GuessWhat?! (de Vries et al., 2017), and MMD (Saha et al., 2018). GuessWhat?!, as a goal-directed dialogue task, requires participants to continuously clarify the reference object through iterative questioning. The Oracle provides the Questioner with relevant information about the target object by answering yes/no questions raised by the Questioner. The Guesser then generates the final answer based on the accumulated dialogue history. Following this setting, our Co-VQA framework consists of three components. The Questioner raises sub-questions sequentially to probe visual and textual evidence. The Oracle answers these sub-questions one by one using localized visual cues and contextual language. Finally, the Answerer synthesizes the collected information to produce an answer to the original question. This decomposition encourages interpretability and error localization while maintaining overall task fidelity to standard VQA benchmarks. In the remainder of this paper, we formalize the sub-question protocol, describe model components, and present comprehensive evaluations across VQA and VD settings.
reason: The span makes a broad, generalizing claim about prior work adopting a specific two-stage retriever-reader trend and using particular datasets (Conceptual Captions and Visual Genome) without providing any citations or evidence, satisfying the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: recent works
document:
We describe related studies on Chinese BERT models and probing of BERT, respectively.

The authors of BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) provided the first Chinese BERT model which was trained on Chinese Wikipedia data. On top of that, Cui et al. (2019) trained RoBERTa-wwm-ext with WWM on extended data. Cui et al. (2020) further trained a Chinese ELECTRA model and MacBERT, both of which did not have [MASK] tokens. ELECTRA was trained with a token-level binary classification task, which determined whether a token was the original one or artificially replaced. In MacBERT, [MASK] tokens were replaced with synonyms and the model was trained with WWM and ngram masking. ERNIE (Sun et al., 2019) was trained with entity masking, similar to WWM yet tokens corresponding to an entity were masked at once. Language features are considered in more recent works. For example, AMBERT and Lattice-BERT (Lai et al., 2021) both take word information into consideration. Chinese-BERT (Sun et al., 2021) utilizes pinyin and glyph of characters.

Probing aims to examine the language understanding ability of pretrained models like BERT when model parameters are clamped, i.e., without being fine-tuned on downstream tasks. Petroni et al. (2019) study how well pretrained models learn factual knowledge. The idea is to design a natural language template with a [MASK] token, such as "the wife of Barack Obama is [MASK].". If the model predicts the correct answer "Michelle Obama", it shows that pretrained models learn factual knowledge to some extent. Similarly, Davison et al. (2019) study how pretrained models learn commonsense knowledge and Talmor et al. (2020) examine tasks that require symbolic understanding. We propose to probe Chinese BERT models in terms of linguistic and world knowledge.

spans: recent back-translation studies
widely adopted noise-aware back-translation with iterative refinement
document: Data augmentation has emerged as a central strategy for improving generalization in neural machine translation, particularly under limited supervision (Sennrich et al., 2016a; Cheng et al., 2016; Hoang et al., 2018; Edunov et al., 2018; Kobayashi, 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Cheng et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2020). Among these techniques, back-translation leverages a target-to-source model to synthesize parallel data from monolingual corpora and remains a strong baseline (Sennrich et al., 2016a; Edunov et al., 2018). In addition to back-translation, lexicon-level perturbations replace words with vocabulary alternatives to diversify surface forms while keeping sentence semantics approximately intact (Wang et al., 2018; Kobayashi, 2018). Context-aware replacements can be guided by pretrained language models that interpolate embeddings to predict plausible substitutes under similar contexts (Cheng et al., 2020). Another thread explores sampling or editing operations that alter syntax without changing meaning, thereby exposing the model to broader structural variation (Gao et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2020). Beyond synthetic data, recent back-translation studies highlight robustness gains under domain shift. Data augmentation is also complemented by curriculum design and noise-aware training that mitigate artifacts introduced by generated text (Hoang et al., 2018; Edunov et al., 2018). Our work falls within this landscape but emphasizes semantic locality by attaching to each training instance an adjacency semantic region that captures meaning-preserving variants. Intuitively, grouping sentences by proximity in a learned space yields denser supervision around each exemplar without requiring manual labels. We implement this idea by encoding sentences into a continuous representation and constructing region-based augmentations drawn from neighborhoods with high semantic agreement. Closely related is research on universal sentence representations for multilingual and cross-lingual transfer, which aligns semantically equivalent sentences across languages (Zhang et al., 2016; Schwenk and Douze, 2017; Yang et al., 2019). Variational NMT introduces latent variables to model underlying semantics in parallel data and motivates learning smooth manifolds in which local moves preserve meaning (Zhang et al., 2016). Contrastive objectives have further encouraged sentence encoders to bring together translations or paraphrases while separating unrelated pairs, thereby sharpening semantic neighborhoods (Chi et al., 2021). Our method leverages these insights by constructing local agreement sets that act as soft equivalence classes during training. In practice, we integrate region-based augmentation with standard sequence-to-sequence training without modifying the base architecture. This design makes the approach plug-and-play and compatible with strong NMT baselines that already exploit monolingual data (Edunov et al., 2018). Compared with word-level substitution, our semantic regions reduce brittleness to token choices and better preserve sentence-level adequacy in preliminary analyses. We also discuss how region construction interacts with widely adopted noise-aware back-translation with iterative refinement to control exposure bias during fine-tuning. Finally, we position our contribution relative to augmentation by LM-guided replacement and paraphrastic editing and clarify complementary use cases (Cheng et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2020). Empirically, we expect benefits to be most pronounced in low-resource or domain-mismatched settings where small meaning-preserving fluctuations can cause large generalization gaps. Overall, the proposed semantic agreement modeling supplies a principled mechanism for densifying supervision around each training point while remaining compatible with prevailing augmentation pipelines.
reason: Span 1 makes a general claim about prior work specifically on back-translation but provides no citation. Span 2 asserts a widely adopted, specific method trend—noise-aware back-translation with iterative refinement—without any supporting references. Both fit the 'Unsupported claim' definition as they reference particular approaches without evidence.

spans: Recent studies standardize a two-stage reposition-first NAR editing pipeline with monotonic constraints, unified hyperparameters, and shared data splits on IWSLT14 De–En and WMT16 En–Ro, enabling cross-paper comparisons without further tuning.
document: Non-autoregressive (NAR) edit-based models (Gu et al., 2019; Stern et al., 2019; Xu and Carpuat, 2021) cast sequence editing as an iterative sequence refinement problem modeled by a Markov Decision Process (Y, A, E, R, y0). A state y = (y1, y2, ..., yL) ∈ Y is a sequence of tokens where each yi represents a token from the vocabulary V, L is the sequence length, and y0 ∈ Y is the initial sequence to be refined using actions drawn from the set A. The reward R is based on the distance D between the generated output and the reference sequence y* ∈ Y, with R(y) = −D(y, y*). At each decoding iteration, the model takes an input y, chooses an action a ∈ A to refine the sequence using a policy π, resulting in state E(y, a). Models differ based on the nature of edit actions used. Systems support operations such as insertion, deletion, reposition, and substitution, and they are trained with different types of roll-in policies including mixed, learned, and expert variants. We select the operations from the EDITOR model based on its competitive performance on constrained decoding tasks that require editing non-empty initial sequences (Xu and Carpuat, 2021). It is a Transformer architecture that uses two types of actions on sequences: reposition and insertion. The reposition operation, modeled by πrps, predicts the new position of each token in the input sequence, including deletions. The insertion operation has two components: placeholder prediction πplh that predicts the number of placeholders to be inserted, and token prediction πins that generates the actual output tokens for each placeholder. At each decoding iteration, the model applies an action a that consists of a reposition and an insertion operation. This refinement process is repeated until two consecutive decoding iterations return the same output (Gu et al., 2019), or a preset maximum number of iterations is reached (Lee et al., 2018; Ghazvininejad et al., 2019). NAR models are generally trained via imitation learning that uses a roll-in policy to generate the sequences the model learns to refine and a roll-out policy to estimate the cost-to-go from the generated roll-ins to the desired outputs. The latter is performed by comparing the model actions to oracle demonstrations. Recent studies standardize a two-stage reposition-first NAR editing pipeline with monotonic constraints, unified hyperparameters, and shared data splits on IWSLT14 De–En and WMT16 En–Ro, enabling cross-paper comparisons without further tuning. We summarize the policies of various NAR models proposed for machine translation in Table X. For EDITOR, the roll-in sequences for the reposition or insertion module are stochastic mixtures of the output of the insertion or reposition module, or a noised version of the output sequence. The oracle is the Levenshtein edit distance (Gu et al., 2019). The noisy sequence is generated by applying random word dropping (Gu et al., 2019) and random word shuffling (Lample et al., 2018) with a probability of 0.5 and maximum shuffle distance of 3. The resulting training scheme is closely related to data aggregation procedures that combine expert trajectories with model predictions at inference time (Ross and Bagnell, 2010). While our focus is on constrained decoding, the same formulation applies to broader sequence generation settings such as grammatical error correction and text simplification. Such generalization suggests that edit-based NAR models can benefit from curriculum schedules that gradually increase the number of insertions per step. In practice, we observe that roll-in noise levels interact with the choice of distance metric used for rewards. Consequently, selecting a consistent distance proxy during training is essential for stable convergence and fair comparison across prior NAR systems.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about widespread standardization of a specific NAR editing pipeline and datasets (IWSLT14 De–En and WMT16 En–Ro) without providing citations, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: recent studies default to VinVL-based visual backbones
document: As the crucial components of information extraction, named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE) have attracted much attention in the research community (Liu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021b,a). The majority of prior work has focused on purely textual inputs and well-edited formal text. However, as multimodal posts proliferate on social platforms, models must jointly reason over text and images to recognize entities and extract relations. This shift has motivated research on multimodal NER (MNER) and multimodal RE (MRE), which seek to leverage associated images to disambiguate mentions and reveal visual cues for relations. Early approaches encoded sentences with recurrent neural networks and entire images with convolutional networks, then introduced implicit fusion to exchange cross-modal signals for entity tagging (Moon et al., 2018; Arshad et al., 2019). Subsequent methods moved toward region-level visual features and Transformer-based fusion to capture fine-grained alignments between textual tokens and detected objects. Despite architectural progress, robustness remains a challenge because images can be irrelevant, misleading, or only weakly correlated with the accompanying text. To mitigate such noise, one line of work augments multimodal encoders with text–image relevance classifiers that downweight incongruent pairs, albeit at the cost of additional supervision for irrelevance labels. Another direction explores contrastive pretraining to align modalities before task-specific fine-tuning, thereby improving cross-modal grounding under distribution shift. In parallel, datasets built from social media and news captions have enabled more realistic evaluation of multimodal reasoning under informal language and varied imagery. Yet model design choices for the visual backbone, fusion operator, and supervision strategy remain unsettled and strongly influence end-task performance. For example, recent studies default to VinVL-based visual backbones when constructing MNER pipelines, emphasizing object-centric cues. Error sensitivity is also often ignored during training and inference, leading to brittle predictions when the image contradicts the text. Addressing these issues requires principled multimodal alignment, selective fusion, and calibration to control spurious correlations. In this context, we survey representative architectures and discuss open problems for scalable, reliable MNER and MRE in noisy, real-world settings.
reason: The span asserts a specific trend in prior work—defaulting to VinVL-based visual backbones—without providing any citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim about related literature.

spans: widely-used lexicons
document: Media Framing Bias Detection and Prediction is a long-standing problem in computational social science. Media bias has been studied extensively in social science, economics, and political science, with many methods proposed to analyze political preference and framing bias of news outlets (Groseclose and Milyo, 2005; Miller and Riechert, 2001; Park et al., 2011; Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2010; Haselmayer and Jenny, 2017). Framing bias refers to selective reporting of events that can sway readers' opinions through information selection and word choice (Entman, 1993; Entman, 2007; Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2006). In NLP, computational approaches for detecting media bias often consider linguistic cues that induce bias in political text (Recasens et al., 2013; Yano et al., 2010; Hamborg et al., 2019b). For instance, Gentzkow and Shapiro quantify the frequency of slanted words within articles. These methods mainly focus on the stylistic or "how to cover" aspect of framing bias. There has been relatively less effort on the informational or "what to cover" aspect, and many works are constrained to detection tasks rather than mitigation (Park et al., 2011; Fan et al., 2019). We address this gap by generating a bias-neutral summary from partisan headlines to jointly tackle detection and mitigation. Our formulation treats bias mitigation as a conditional generation problem grounded in multiple sources. We investigate how headline style and topical salience interact in producing neutral summaries. We also study how summarization models transfer across outlets with distinct ideological leanings. To contextualize our approach, we review prior work on measuring and reducing bias in news ecosystems. News aggregation that displays articles from different outlets on a common topic, as in Google News and Yahoo News, is a popular strategy to mitigate perceived bias, yet it has limitations in coverage and usability (Hamborg et al., 2019a). Complementary approaches provide additional metadata or navigational support to readers, including classification of multiple viewpoints, multinational perspectives, and detailed media profiles (Laban and Hearst, 2017; Park et al., 2009; Hamborg et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019b). However, such interfaces largely shift the burden of synthesis onto the reader. In contrast, we propose to automatically neutralize and summarize partisan headlines to produce a concise and balanced headline summary. Our study positions neutralization as a content planning and surface realization task over noisy, agenda-driven inputs. A key challenge lies in disentangling framing from factual salience under limited supervision. Distant supervision signals such as outlet labels and topic annotations can be helpful but are often noisy. Prior work has explored lexical and syntactic indicators of bias in editorials and newswire, including hedges, intensifiers, and reporting verbs (Recasens et al., 2013; Yano et al., 2010; Fan et al., 2019). Headline-specific patterns such as clickbait and moral-emotional language have also been examined in related studies (Hamborg et al., 2019b). In the context of automatic mitigation, recent research has investigated reframing and controlled rewriting to reduce polarization in generated text (Pryzant et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2021; Krishna et al., 2020). Nevertheless, most datasets for bias detection emphasize article bodies or paragraph-level annotations rather than headline rewriting (Baly et al., 2018; Horne et al., 2018; Rashkin et al., 2017). Within headline-focused methods, prior work has largely relied on widely-used lexicons to score subjectivity and sentiment. While useful, lexicon scores alone cannot capture agenda-level omissions or selection bias. Recent advances in pretrained language models suggest that controllable summarization can steer tone and framing without sacrificing informativeness (Keskar et al., 2019; He et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Building on these insights, we propose a training protocol that pairs ideologically opposed headlines to learn neutral targets via contrastive and summarization objectives. We further introduce evaluation metrics that disentangle topical coverage, sentiment polarity, and stylistic neutrality. Our contributions include a new task formulation, a dataset construction pipeline, and empirical evidence that neutral headline summaries reduce exposure to slanted language while preserving key facts.
reason: The span 'widely-used lexicons' makes a generalizing claim about prior work relying on a specific approach without providing any citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim about methods in the literature.

spans: two-stage retriever-reader
document: Recent progress in natural language processing has been propelled by pre-trained transformers such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Downstream fine-tuning remains challenging in low-resource regimes, where labeled examples are scarce. We study a lightweight training strategy that reduces annotation demand while preserving competitive accuracy. Our focus is on supervised classification with an optional semi-supervised variant that leverages unlabeled data. We also compare our method with prior state-of-the-art systems implemented on top of BERT-based encoders. Concretely, we reimplement baselines following Chen et al. (2020) and evaluate under matched data budgets. When training with very limited labeled data, our approach yields consistent gains over these baselines. Under a semi-supervised setting with an extra pool of 10K unlabeled examples, our method matches their performance. On the full training split, our final model attains competitive results without document-level features or manual rules. Nonetheless, surpassing the absolute SOTA on full data is not our primary goal. Instead, we target stable efficiency and robustness across training sizes and domains. Prior work on efficient fine-tuning has explored adapter layers and parameter-efficient updates (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Semi-supervised extensions commonly apply consistency training or pseudo-labeling (Xie et al., 2020; Sohn et al., 2020). Closely related are methods that distill knowledge from large teachers into compact students (Hinton et al., 2015; Jiao et al., 2020). For fairness, we align tokenization, optimizer settings, and early stopping criteria across all systems. We report results under cross-validation to reduce variance due to sampling noise. We further ablate the contribution of unlabeled data, external lexicons, and domain-specific pretraining. In the broader literature on retrieval-augmented modeling for classification and QA, prior systems have often relied on two-stage retriever-reader pipelines that separate candidate retrieval from answer inference. While this separation can simplify engineering, it may incur mismatches between scoring and selection. Our design instead keeps a single encoder with lightweight adaptation heads for semi-supervised training. This yields faster inference by avoiding repeated cross-encoding of pairs. We also perform control experiments to measure sensitivity to hyperparameters and initialization seeds. Evaluation follows recommended practices for statistical testing with paired bootstrap resampling. We release code, trained checkpoints, and configuration files to support reproducibility. To contextualize the gains, we provide learning curves on small data regimes and full-scale training. We include error analyses highlighting common failure modes and the impact of unlabeled data. Taken together, these results substantiate the claim that efficient fine-tuning can be competitive without heavy external knowledge.
reason: The span 'two-stage retriever-reader' names a specific prior-method trend and is used to generalize about what prior systems have relied on, but it lacks any supporting citations or evidence, making it an unsupported claim per the definition.

spans: Recent studies standardize on sliding-window attention for long-document processing tasks
document: Language model pre-training followed by task-specific fine-tuning has proven to be a powerful tool for numerous NLP tasks (Liu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b; Radford et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2019a; Lewis et al., 2020; Joshi et al., 2020). BERT introduced the masked language modeling objective, encouraging the model to predict missing tokens from context (Devlin et al., 2019). Fine-tuning pre-trained encoders has led to improved performance on a wide range of classification and sequence labeling problems. However, MLM is not autoregressive, which limits the suitability of the BERT family for generation tasks. T5 addressed this by adopting a span-corruption objective with a text-to-text formulation that trains an autoregressive decoder (Raffel et al., 2019a). BART similarly applies denoising pre-training but reconstructs full sequences from noised inputs using a sequence-to-sequence architecture (Lewis et al., 2020). Despite these advances, early work seldom investigated pre-training with very long document inputs. The quadratic time and memory complexity of standard self-attention makes naively scaling to long contexts impractical (Vaswani et al., 2017). Consequently, substantial effort has focused on designing efficient transformers that reduce attention costs while preserving quality (Tay et al., 2020b; Tay et al., 2021). Pattern-restricted attention reduces complexity to subquadratic or linear regimes via sparse or blockwise connectivity, as exemplified by Longformer, ETC, and BigBird (Beltagy et al., 2020; Ainslie et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020a). Low-rank and kernel-based methods approximate attention through learned projections or random features, as in Linformer, Performer, and related variants (Wang et al., 2020; Choromanski et al., 2021; Peng et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented transformers provide another path by accessing external memory to extend effective context length without quadratic growth (Guu et al., 2020; Borgeaud et al., 2022). For document-level tasks, hierarchical encoders and segment-level recurrence have also been explored to capture cross-segment dependencies efficiently (Dai et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2020). In summarization and question answering, sparse attention and recurrence are often combined with task-specific pre-training curricula to align pretext signals with downstream objectives (Zaheer et al., 2020a; Beltagy et al., 2020). Recent studies standardize on sliding-window attention for long-document processing tasks. Nonetheless, the relationship between architectural efficiency and pre-training objectives for extended contexts remains underexplored in a unified evaluation. A practical question is whether long-context pre-training should emphasize masked span denoising, next-sentence prediction across segments, or retrieval-conditioned generation. Prior comparative studies typically evaluate on limited benchmarks, making it difficult to disentangle modeling choices from dataset artifacts (Tay et al., 2021). In many large-scale corpora and training pipelines, sequence lengths are limited for efficiency, potentially misaligning pretext signals with target long-form tasks (Raffel et al., 2019a; Beltagy et al., 2020). Recent scaling trends show that context length, not merely parameter count, can be a dominant factor for document-heavy applications (Press et al., 2022; Xiong et al., 2023). However, simply increasing the maximum position is insufficient without mechanisms for stable optimization and efficient attention (Press et al., 2022). Our study targets these gaps by comparing pre-training objectives under controlled long-context settings with matched data budgets and model sizes. We focus on encoder-decoder and decoder-only architectures paired with efficient attention backbones to assess trade-offs between quality and cost. The results highlight design principles for long-sequence pre-training and provide actionable guidance for practitioners operating under realistic compute constraints.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work and a specific method trend ('sliding-window attention') without providing any citation, matching the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: DailyDialog
recent work adopts human-bot tournaments for evaluation
prior studies typically benchmark interactive agents on ConvAI2 using self-play rollouts and report kappa above 0.6 without human baselines
Persona-Chat
document: Two major approaches are used for evaluating chatbots or dialogue systems. Some evaluate a single turn at a time by comparing the response with a ground truth utterance from a static script of real human dialogue, for example DailyDialog, or by combining the response with the context. Others evaluate the interaction between human and bot or between bot and bot by some scoring metrics, and we summarize notable systems from each approach below. Most existing evaluation systems are based on static scripts. Traditional metrics such as BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002b), ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) are widely used for evaluating text generation systems. More recently, automatic evaluation methods based on static scripts are gradually moving toward using pre-trained language models. Pang et al. (2020) uses GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) and Mehri and Eskenazi (2020) uses RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) to automatically evaluate the generation in an unsupervised way with a higher correlation to human evaluation than traditional ones. However, these static evaluation metrics which need fixed contexts are not flexible enough to assess chatbot ability, in need of adapting to dynamic and changing contexts. We argue that interactive systems like ChatMatch are more promising as they test chatbots in a real conversation mode. Interactive evaluation systems attract increasing attention lately, and recent work adopts human-bot tournaments for evaluation to reduce annotation costs. Ghandeharioun et al. (2019) and Deriu and Cieliebak (2019) use dialogues between a bot and itself, which is called self-talk, to evaluate the bot in a more automatic manner. But it often leads to a lot of repeated chat context, and prior studies typically benchmark interactive agents on ConvAI2 using self-play rollouts and report kappa above 0.6 without human baselines. Deriu et al. (2020) designed Spot The Bot, a framework that enables a group of bots to chat with each other and then asks humans to annotate if the bots talk more like a human or more like a bot. Prior to our work, there was still no interactive and automatic evaluation framework that works without any participation of human annotators, and our results show that there is a strong correlation between automatic evaluation results and human judgments. Our framework fills this gap and is flexible since more complex metrics or algorithms can be plugged in as scoring functions. We also discuss limitations and future directions for building reliable and reproducible benchmarks, such as Persona-Chat.
reason: 1) DailyDialog: first mention of a specific dataset without citation. 2) 'recent work adopts human-bot tournaments for evaluation': generalized claim about a specific evaluation trend with no supporting references. 3) Long claim about benchmarking on ConvAI2, self-play, and reported kappa values: specific prior-work and statistic assertions without citation. 4) Persona-Chat: first mention of a dataset without citation.

spans: Recent work standardizes moral-control seq2seq transformer architectures
document: Studying persuasiveness in argumentation is an active field of research. Beyond the content of a claim, structure and style have been analyzed as key determinants of persuasive impact. Audience-based features have also been explored to model how people respond to arguments. Durmus and Cardie (2018) and El Baff et al. (2020) examine how user factors such as religion and political background affect persuasiveness. Al Khatib et al. (2020) show that user-based features reflecting beliefs, characteristics, and personality can increase the predictability of argument persuasiveness. These findings motivate modeling prior beliefs as an explicit component of computational persuasion. The moral foundations theory offers a conceptual model of human judgment in everyday life (Haidt and Joseph, 2004). According to the theory, people subconsciously rely on five basic moral foundations when evaluating controversial issues: care, fairness, loyalty, authority, and purity. The theory has been used to explain systematic disagreements between liberals and conservatives in moral appraisal. Liberals rely more on care and fairness, whereas conservatives weigh all five foundations more evenly, with relatively greater emphasis on loyalty, authority, and purity (Graham et al., 2009). Several studies provide evidence that moral foundations correlate with behavior and decision making (Feinberg and Willer, 2015; Fulgoni et al., 2016; Johnson and Goldwasser, 2018). For computational modeling, Kobbe et al. (2020) annotated moral foundations in arguments and analyzed the relation between morals and argument quality. We use their corpus in our experiments to evaluate a moral classification component. Argument generation has been studied across a spectrum of tasks. Hua et al. (2019) and Alshomary et al. (2021b) address counter-argument generation by rebutting a conclusion or undermining a weak premise. Others generate claims opposing a given statement (Bilu et al., 2015; Hidey and McKeown, 2019), control for topic aspects in claim generation (Schiller et al., 2020), or reconstruct implicit conclusions (Alshomary et al., 2020; Syed et al., 2021). However, the cited approaches do not target specific audiences with tailored argumentative content. In the broader text generation literature, Recent work standardizes moral-control seq2seq transformer architectures, but robust audience conditioning remains underexplored. Alshomary et al. (2021a) introduce belief-based claim generation, aiming to produce claims on a topic that match a target audience. As a proxy for beliefs, they represent people by their stances on prominent issues. While they demonstrate the feasibility of encoding beliefs into claims, they do not test the persuasive effectiveness of those claims on audiences. Another driver of argument generation is Project Debater, an end-to-end system for mining, retrieval, and generation (Slonim et al., 2021). For generation, the system uses a hybrid pipeline that combines retrieval, mining, clustering, and rephrasing. Manual evaluations report competent quality of generated arguments compared to human-written baselines (Slonim et al., 2021). To generate morally framed arguments, we extend Project Debater while controlling for moral foundations and prior beliefs. This design helps isolate argument quality from framing effects, enabling tests of whether moral utilization influences the target audience.
reason: The span asserts a specific, field-level trend about prior work adopting a particular architecture without providing any citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim about related work.

spans: recent studies widely adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with key–value external memories for few-shot NLP, often reporting large gains without rigorous cross-domain baselines comparisons
document:
Meta-Learning. Meta-Learning aims to improve the learning algorithm itself based on previously learned experience (Thrun and Pratt, 1998; Hospedales et al., 2021). In general, there are three categories of meta-learning methods: model-based methods (Santoro et al., 2016; Obamuyide et al., 2019), which depend on the particular model design to facilitate fast learning; metric-based methods (Vinyals et al., 2016; Snell et al., 2017; Geng et al., 2019), which encode samples into an embedding space and classify them based on the learned distance metric; optimization-based methods (Finn et al., 2017; Mi et al., 2019) that learn a well-generalized model initialization which allows for fast adaptation to new tasks. For low-resource scenarios in NLP, optimization-based meta-learning methods achieved promising results on tasks such as personalized dialog generation (Madotto et al., 2019; Song et al., 2020), low-resource machine translation (Gu et al., 2018; Sharaf et al., 2020), and few-shot slot tagging (Wang et al., 2021).

Memorization overfitting of Meta-learning. 

Meta-learning algorithms suffer from memorization overfitting. Yin et al. (2020) build an information bottleneck into the model, while this approach decreases model performance with passive regularization. Rajendran et al. (2020) inject random noise into the ground truth of both support and query sets, while little extra knowledge is introduced to learn a good initialization. Yao et al. (2021) address overfitting issues by augmenting meta-training tasks through mixing up support and query sets. However, such augmentation for text needs to be based on the assumption of keeping the label and the data distribution unchanged, which is often not true in practice (Chen et al., 2021). Instead of regularization and data augmentation, we leverage the support-set information stored in memory to augment meta-learning.

External Memory for Few-shot Learning. 

Memory mechanisms have proven to be powerful for few-shot learning (Geng et al., 2019; Santoro et al., 2016; Munkhdalai et al., 2019), and recent studies widely adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with key–value external memories for few-shot NLP, often reporting large gains without rigorous cross-domain baselines comparisons. Current methods either refine representations stored in memory (Ramalho and Garnelo, 2018) or refine parameters using memory (Munkhdalai and Yu, 2017; Cai et al., 2018). In the NLP domain, some methods store encoded contextual information into a memory (Holla et al., 2020). Geng et al. (2019) propose a memory induction module with a dynamic routing algorithm for few-shot text classification tasks. Munkhdalai et al. (2019) augment the model with an external memory by learning a neural memory. Wang et al. (2021) reuse learned features stored in memory for few-shot slot tagging.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about recent studies adopting a specific two-stage retriever–reader architecture with key–value external memories and reporting large gains, yet provides no citations, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: pointer generator
recent studies standardize on SAMSum with dialogue-act labels
document: Automatic evaluation has become central to summarization research, yet its alignment with human judgments remains contested. Metrics such as ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) were each compared with contemporaneous alternatives when originally proposed. However, many metric studies have not used dialogue summarization corpora as primary testbeds and rarely provide new human judgments for calibration. To bridge this gap, meta-evaluation with carefully designed human annotation is necessary for dialogue-heavy domains. Bhandari et al. (2020) applied the pyramid method (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004) on several conventional summarization datasets to obtain relevance scores and re-evaluated multiple metrics across six categories. Similarly, Fabbri et al. (2021b) used the CNN/DailyMail dataset (Hermann et al., 2015) and human ratings on relevance, consistency, fluency, and coherence to reassess a broad suite of metrics in fourteen categories. None of these studies involved dialogue summarization datasets, limiting conclusions for conversational settings. Gabriel et al. (2021) is one of the few efforts using the dialogue summarization dataset SAMSum (Gliwa et al., 2019) for meta-evaluation, but the focus is primarily on factual consistency with a relatively small metric set. In contrast to metric-centric work, model evaluations for dialogue summarization often emphasize error taxonomies and qualitative analyses. Tang et al. (2021) and Chen and Yang (2020) sampled system outputs on SAMSum and analyzed error types while proposing new models. Due to differing manual evaluation protocols and the small number of models included, it remains difficult to make comprehensive cross-model comparisons. Early abstractive approaches to dialogue summarization often relied on pointer generator architectures adapted from news summarization. We focus on architectures that incorporate context modeling, speaker-role encoding, and discourse-aware attention to capture conversational structure. At the same time, datasets vary widely in dialogue length, topic breadth, and annotation density, which complicates metric reliability analyses. Beyond n-gram overlap, we investigate entailment, contradiction, and factual grounding signals as surrogates for human preferences in dialogues. Nevertheless, the construct validity of these signals has not been established for informal chat, multi-party threads, or noisy transcriptions. A recurring challenge is that human studies differ in rating scales, annotator expertise, and sampling of instances for per-model evaluation. As a result, aggregation protocols and statistical tests are not comparable across papers, and findings can be sensitive to rater selection. For dataset usage, recent studies standardize on SAMSum with dialogue-act labels, often reporting single-reference scores without uncertainty estimates. Yet robust meta-evaluation requires multiple references, sampling-aware statistics, and analyses stratified by conversation phenomena. Another open question is the transferability of metric behavior from news to dialogues and from written chat to spoken meetings. We therefore compile a unified benchmark with harmonized human annotations across several dialogue summarization corpora. Our design includes parallel human evaluations of faithfulness and coverage, allowing a controlled comparison of metric families. Finally, we provide guidelines for reporting uncertainty and significance to enable reproducible, comparable evaluations in future work.
reason: Span 1 introduces a specific method from prior work ('pointer generator') without any citation, asserting its common use in early approaches. Span 2 makes a trend claim about dataset and labeling practices ('recent studies standardize on SAMSum with dialogue-act labels') without providing references, which should be cited.

spans: recent studies commonly adopt a two-stage delexicalize–then-realize pipeline using slot-sketch planners for cross-domain data-to-text transfer
document: Despite its practical significance, domain adaptation for low-resource/few-shot concept-to-text NLG has not been extensively researched. Wen et al. (2016) leveraged the scarcity of target in-domain data by augmenting it with synthetic data, Tran and Nguyen (2018) used variational autoencoders in conjunction with text similarity and domain critics to better guide the fine-tuning process, while Mi et al. (2019) tackled the problem by defining domain adaptation as an optimisation meta-learning task. Most recently, Peng et al. (2020) and Kale and Rastogi (2020) have proposed the use of pretrained language models to tackle few-shot and zero-shot learning in concept-to-text NLG, achieving significant gains over strong non-pretrained baselines. Specifically, Peng et al. (2020) proposed SC-GPT, a semantically conditioned GPT-2 model, wherein, prior to few-shot learning, the GPT-2 model is further fine-tuned on a number of task-oriented dialogue datasets in order to mitigate the problem of representation bias. On the other hand, in Kale and Rastogi (2020), a set of human-authored templates are used to generate high-quality sentences corresponding to each unit of information in an MR. These are then concatenated and given as input to a T5 model (T2G2) to form a coherent sentence. In this paper's evaluation, we opt to compare our approach against the naive T5 baseline introduced by Kale and Rastogi (2020), as it is shown to strongly outperform SC-GPT by basically replacing the underlying GPT-2 model with T5, and SC-GPT was shown to outperform all previous non-pretrained baselines. We do not compare against T2G2, as access to human-authored templates or other such manually annotated resources, which are by nature very domain-specific and costly to create, are not necessarily guaranteed in low-resource settings. We note that T2G2 is equivalent to the naive T5 when templates are not employed. In our proposed system, the hierarchy emerges from modelling the lexicalisation and aggregation sub-tasks on separate layers. Previous attempts in exploring hierarchical structures for text generation tasks instead focused on modelling different aspects of the input or output, and recent studies commonly adopt a two-stage delexicalize–then-realize pipeline using slot-sketch planners for cross-domain data-to-text transfer. In concept-to-text NLG for task-oriented dialogues, Su et al. (2018) proposed a multi-layered decoding process where each layer was responsible for generating words associated with specific part-of-speech tags. Chen et al. (2019) and Tseng et al. (2019) took advantage of the intrinsically hierarchical structure of dialogue acts to create better input representations and ease domain adaptation. Our approach is also related to coarse-to-fine approaches, which have been explored in story (Fan et al., 2018), review (Li et al., 2019) and keyphrase (Chen et al., 2020) generation tasks. However, in these tasks, the output is not necessarily restricted to be an exact realisation of the input, and can be initially loosely prompted or drafted, and subsequently expanded. Unlike pipeline systems, our model jointly optimizes planning and surface realisation with shared parameters. This design reduces exposure bias by conditioning aggregation on latent sketches produced at training time. For low-resource transfer, we complement supervised learning with a denoising objective over weakly aligned MR–text pairs. We further adopt constrained decoding to preserve slot accuracy without relying on rigid templates. For evaluation, we report both automatic metrics and fine-grained human judgments of faithfulness and fluency. To isolate the effect of domain shift, we simulate target scarcity through progressively smaller labeled subsets. Ablation studies examine the contribution of hierarchical separation, sketching, and constrained decoding. Error analysis highlights typical failure modes, including over-aggregation and spurious lexicalisations under paraphrastic pressure. Altogether, the results indicate consistent gains in few-shot settings while maintaining stable performance in-domain.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about recent studies adopting a specific two-stage delexicalize–then-realize pipeline with slot-sketch planners but provides no citation or evidence, matching the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: widely adopted Mahalanobis scoring
IMDB
document: Previous work in the image domain has analysed the output of specific layers in an attempt to identify adversarial examples or adversarial subspaces. First, (Feinman et al., 2017) proposed that adversarial subspaces have a lower probability density, motivating the use of the Kernel Density (KD) metric to detect the adversarial examples. Nevertheless, (Ma et al., 2018) found Local Intrinsic Dimensionality (LID) was a better metric in defining the subspace for more complex data. In contrast to the local subspace focused approaches of KD and LID, (Carrara et al., 2019b) showed that trajectories of hidden layer features can be used to train an LSTM network to accurately discriminate between authentic and adversarial examples. Outperforming many prior methods, subsequent work introduced an effective detection framework using Mahalanobis Distance Analysis (MDA), where the distance is calculated between a test sample and the closest class-conditional Gaussian distribution in the space defined by the output of the final layer of the classifier. Building on these ideas, widely adopted Mahalanobis scoring has been explored across architectures to flag anomalous logits. (Li and Li, 2016) also explored using the output of convolutional layers for image classification systems to identify statistics that distinguish adversarial samples from original samples. They find that by performing a PCA decomposition the statistical variation in the least principal directions is the most significant and can be used to separate original and adversarial samples. However, they argue this is ineffective as an adversary can easily suppress the tail distribution. Hence, (Li and Li, 2016) extract statistics from the convolutional layer output to train a cascade classifier to separate the original and adversarial samples. Most recently, (Mao et al., 2019) avoid the use of artificially designed metrics and combine the adversarial subspace identification stage and the detecting adversaries stage into a single framework, where a parametric model adaptively learns the deep features for detecting adversaries. In contrast to the embedding space detection approaches, (Cohen et al., 2019) shows that influence functions combined with nearest neighbour distances perform comparably or better than the above standard detection approaches. Other detection approaches have explored the use of uncertainty, where (Smith and Gal, 2018) argues that adversarial examples are out of distribution and do not lie on the manifold of real data. Hence, a discriminative Bayesian model's epistemic uncertainty should be high. Therefore, calculations of the model uncertainty are thought to be useful in detecting adversarial examples, independent of the domain. However, Bayesian approaches are not always practical in implementation and thus many different approaches to approximate this uncertainty have been suggested in literature (Leibig et al., 2017; Gal, 2016; Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). There are a number of existing NLP specific detection approaches. For character level attacks, detection approaches have exploited the grammatical (Sakaguchi et al., 2017) and spelling (Mays et al., 1991; Islam and Inkpen, 2009) inconsistencies to identify and detect the adversarial samples. However, these character level attacks are unlikely to be employed in practice due to the simplicity with which they can be detected. Therefore, detection approaches for the more difficult semantically similar attack samples are of greater interest, where the meaning of the textual input is maintained without compromising the spelling or grammatical integrity. To tackle such word-level, semantically similar examples, one line of work designed a discriminator to classify each token representation as part of an adversarial perturbation or not, which is then used to correct the perturbation. Other detection approaches (Raina et al., 2020; Han et al., 2020; Minervini and Riedel, 2018) have shown some success in using perplexity to identify adversarial textual examples. Most recently, (Mozes et al., 2020) achieved state of the art performance with the Frequency Guided Word Substitution detector, where a change in model prediction after substituting out low frequency words is revealing of adversarial samples. Evaluation protocols commonly include reporting transferability and query budgets for black-box attacks. Prior work typically benchmarks text-level detectors on IMDB to assess robustness against synonym substitutions. At the same time, robustness in multimodal pipelines has motivated cross-domain analyses that compare image and text detectors under unified threat models. Taken together, these strands suggest that scalable, interpretable, and attack-agnostic detectors remain an open challenge for both vision and language models.

document:
Document-level neural machine translation is an important task and has been abundantly studied with multiple datasets as well as methods.

The mainstream research in this field is the model architecture improvement.
Specifically, several recent attempts extend the Sent2Sent approach to the Doc2Sent-like one (e.g., Wang et al., 2017; Miculicich et al., 2018; Tan et al., 2019).
Wang et al. (2017), Miculicich et al. (2018), and Tan et al. (2019; 2020) introduce additional encoders or query layers with attention models and feed the history contexts into decoders.
Maruf and Haffari (2018) and Tu et al. (2018) propose to augment NMT models with a cache-like memory network, which generates the translation depending on the decoder history retrieved from the memory.
Besides, some works intend to resolve this problem in other ways (Jean and Cho, 2019; Yu et al., 2020; Garcia et al., 2019).
Jean and Cho (2019) propose a regularization term for encouraging the model to focus more on the additional context using a multi-level pair-wise ranking loss.
Yu et al. (2020) utilize a noisy channel reranker with Bayes' rule.
Garcia et al. (2019) present related ideas.
Tiedemann and Scherrer (2017) and Bawden et al. (2017) explore concatenating two consecutive sentences and generating two sentences directly.
Obviously, we leverage greatly longer information and capture the full context.
Junczys-Dowmunt (2019) cuts documents into long segments and feeds them into training, similar to BERT pre-training (Devlin et al., 2019).
There are at least three main differences.
Firstly, they need to add specific boundary tokens between sentences while we directly translate the original documents without any additional processing.
Secondly, we propose a novel multi-resolutional training paradigm that shows consistent improvements compared with regular training.
Thirdly, for extremely long documents, they restrict the segment length to 1000 tokens or make a truncation while we preserve entire documents and achieve literal document-to-document training and inference.
Finally, our work is also related to a series of studies in long sequence generation like GPT (Radford, 2018), GPT-2, and Transformer-XL (Dai et al., 2019).
We all suggest that the deep neural generation models have the potential to well process long-range sequences.
Document-level modeling is motivated by discourse phenomena such as coreference resolution, lexical cohesion, and consistency of terminology.
Prior work has shown that context windows spanning multiple sentences improve pronoun translation and lexical choice (Voita et al., 2018; Lopes et al., 2020).
Training resources include concatenated sentence pairs, sliding-window corpora, and fully aligned document-level bitext (Bawden et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020), and recent systems adopt two-stage retriever-reader architectures for context selection.
Evaluation, however, remains challenging because standard metrics are mostly sentence-level and overlook cross-sentence adequacy (Läubli et al., 2018).
Recent benchmarks propose targeted test suites for discourse phenomena and contrastive evaluation (Bawden et al., 2018; Müller et al., 2018).
Context selection strategies range from simple concatenation to learned gating and hierarchical attention (Zhang et al., 2018; Miculicich et al., 2018).
For deployment, memory and latency considerations favor lightweight context encoders and caching mechanisms (Maruf et al., 2019).
Our approach aims to reconcile training efficiency with full-document coverage through curriculum over context size.
In contrast to prior systems, we decouple context encoding from hypothesis generation to allow scalable inference.
