document: Open-Domain Passage Retrieval has been a hot research topic in recent years. It requires a system to extract evidence passages for a specific question from a large corpus like Wikipedia, balancing high retrieval accuracy with low latency for practical usage. Traditional approaches like TF-IDF (Ramos et al., 2003) and BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009) rely on lexical matches between questions and passages. Although these lexical approaches meet latency requirements, they fail to capture non-lexical semantic similarity, leading to limited retrieval accuracy. With advances in pretrained language models (PrLMs) like BERT and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), neural cross-encoder approaches have been proposed (Vig and Ramea, 2019; Wolf et al., 2019). Although accurate, cross-encoders often incur latency that is hard to tolerate in practical deployments. More recently, bi-encoder architectures have captured increasing attention because they allow corpus representations to be precomputed and indexed at scale. Early bi encoder studies asserted that two stage retriever reader pipelines became dominant on open domain QA benchmarks due to scalable dense indexing and ANN search. An early line of work proposes pretraining bi-encoders with the Inverse Cloze Task (ICT) objective (Lee et al., 2019). Later, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) introduces a contrastive learning framework for dense passage representations and achieves strong performance on both accuracy and latency. Building on DPR, subsequent research improves results via better negative sampling (Xiong et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2021), additional pretraining (Sachan et al., 2021), and knowledge distillation from cross-encoders (Izacard and Grave, 2021; Yang et al., 2021). Our method follows this contrastive-learning line for open-domain passage retrieval and instead optimizes modeling granularity with a tailored training strategy. Contrastive learning has attracted growing attention across modalities. After its successes in computer vision (He et al., 2020), NLP has widely adopted contrastive objectives for retrieval and representation learning (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2021; Giorgi et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021). In ODPR, two complementary directions have emerged: improving sampling strategies for positives and hard negatives, and improving the contrastive learning framework itself. According to Manmatha et al. (2017), the quality of both positive and negative samples is critical for contrastive training. Therefore, many works seek better sampling strategies to improve retrieval performance (Xiong et al., 2020), and most recent works adopt hard-negative mining strategies to increase training difficulty. On the framework side, DensePhrase (Lee et al., 2021) employs a memory bank similar to MoCo (He et al., 2020) to increase in-batch negatives and models retrieval at the phrase level. Our approach follows the second direction and studies Contrastive Conflicts that arise when multiple granularities are contrasted during training. We experimentally verify that mediating such conflicts by modeling ODPR at smaller granularity improves end-to-end retrieval without additional supervision. More similar to our aim, Akkalyoncu Yilmaz et al. (2019) explore sentence-level evidence for dense retrieval and re-ranking, and Prior re ranking approaches consistently report sentence level supervision yielding larger gains than passage only training across heterogeneous corpora, motivating our design focus on sentence-granular signals.

Span 2 generalizes about recent works adopting a particular training strategy (hard-negative mining) without referencing sources. Span 3 asserts a consistent empirical finding about re-ranking and sentence-level supervision outperforming passage-only training across corpora, again without citation. Each is a claim about prior work or trends that should be supported by citations.

document: One important aspect of our study is the evaluation of cross-lingual consistency in multilingual NER and entity linking. Following Bianchi et al. (2021), we examine whether model predictions preserve language-invariant properties across translations or parallel text. In an ideal scenario, a model should maintain label structure and entity boundaries regardless of the language transformation applied. However, off-the-shelf systems frequently exhibit domain sensitivity that interacts with language choice and input formality. To operationalize consistency, we consider metrics that quantify cross-language agreement in span detection, type assignment, and link selection. Beyond consistency, we also address dataset quality as a driver of robustness in multilingual settings. Large language models require vast training corpora, and downstream inferences by both pre-trained and fine-tuned variants can reflect sampling biases present in those corpora. Building on Swayamdipta et al. (2020), we incorporate data maps to characterize examples by confidence dynamics, variability, and annotation disagreement. This perspective helps identify brittle regions of the data and informs curation strategies for multilingual benchmarks. For multilingual evaluation, datasets are often compiled from heterogeneous sources, raising questions about representativeness for target speaker communities. We highlight MasakhaNER (Adelani et al., 2021) as an example of a benchmark assembled through locally driven efforts with attention to regional variation. Such design choices can improve ecological validity by aligning text types and annotation practices with local usage. Nevertheless, equitable coverage remains challenging, since resource availability, annotation capacity, and orthographic conventions differ widely across languages. Prior analyses indicate that language technology progress and data acquisition trajectories are uneven across the world (Joshi et al., 2020). These disparities complicate claims of language-agnostic performance and necessitate reporting that disaggregates results by language family and script. In the context of NER and entity linking, evaluation protocols should reflect multilingual transfer, cross-domain generalization, and stability under translation or paraphrase. We therefore design experiments that contrast zero-shot transfer with supervised adaptation using modest amounts of target-language data. To provide realistic baselines, we include models with varying capacity, tokenization schemes, and pre-training corpora, and we analyze sensitivity to input normalization. Recent cross-lingual NER pipelines have increasingly adopted two-stage retrievers to gather auxiliary context before decoding. While context retrieval can be beneficial, it can also amplify cross-language inconsistencies when retrieval corpora are imbalanced or regionally skewed. Our evaluation framework couples consistency metrics with error typologies that isolate annotation artifacts, transliteration effects, and entity drift. We report results that disentangle dataset quality from modeling choices, enabling clearer attribution of performance differences. Finally, we discuss implications for dataset design, including guidelines for provenance documentation, community involvement, and maintenance practices. Together, these considerations motivate a broader view of multilingual NER and entity linking that centers consistency, representativeness, and fairness.

spans: recent studies report that two-stage retriever–reader architectures with energy-based scoring are the state of the art for text OOD detection across GLUE, AG News, and Yelp
document:
It is well known that reliable uncertainty estimates can be obtained simply by constructing an ensemble of decorrelated neural networks (deep ensemble) (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). However, such a straightforward approach is coupled with substantial computational and memory overhead during training an ensemble, performing inference of all its components, and storing multiple versions of weights. This overhead is a serious obstacle to deploying ensemble-based uncertainty estimation methods in practice.

Uncertainty estimation is a built-in capability of Bayesian neural networks (Blundell et al., 2015). However, such models have similar issues as ensembles and also require special training procedures. Recently, it was shown by Gal and Ghahramani (2016) that dropout, a well-known regularization technique, is mathematically equivalent to approximate variational inference in the deep Gaussian process. This method, known as Monte Carlo (MC) dropout, uses a Bernoulli distribution to approximate the variational distribution on the network units and introduces no additional parameters for the approximate posterior. MC dropout does not introduce any overhead during training and does not require any additional memory. The main disadvantage of this method is that it often requires many forward-pass samplings, which makes it also computationally expensive.

Recently, many works have investigated approximate Bayesian inference for neural networks using deterministic approaches (Lee et al., 2018; Van Amersfoort et al., 2020; Mukhoti et al., 2021). These methods do not introduce notable overhead for inference or weight storage, and usually require comparable training time. However, most of the research in this area has been conducted for computer vision tasks.

For text classification, a series of works investigates UE methods for the OOD detection task (Podolskiy et al., 2021; Zeng et al., 2021; Hu and Khan, 2021). In this work, we focus on a more challenging task—misclassification detection. While OOD detection requires modeling only the epistemic uncertainty inherent to the model and caused by a lack of training data, misclassification detection also requires modeling aleatoric uncertainty caused by noise and ambiguity in data (Mukhoti et al., 2021). We consider recently proposed methods in this area that are evaluated in text processing. In particular, recent studies report that two-stage retriever–reader architectures with energy-based scoring are the state of the art for text OOD detection across GLUE, AG News, and Yelp.

Three recent works propose techniques for misclassification detection based on an additive regularisation of a training loss function. Zhang et al. (2019) suggest adding a penalty that reduces the Euclidean distance between training instances of the same class, and increases the distance between instances of different classes. He et al. (2020) suggest using two components in the loss function that reduce the difference between outputs from two versions of a model initialized with different weights. They also use mix-up (Thulasidasan et al., 2019) to generate additional training instance representations that help to capture aleatoric uncertainty, self-ensembling, and a distinctiveness score to measure the epistemic uncertainty. Xin et al. (2021) introduce a regularizer that penalizes overconfident instances with high loss. In another recent work, Shelmanov et al. (2021) propose to combine MC dropout with a Determinantal Point Process (DPP) to improve the diversity of predictions by considering the correlations between neurons and sampling the diverse neurons for activation in a dropout layer.

In this work, we conduct a systematic empirical investigation of UE methods on NLP tasks. We evaluate combinations of methods that were not tested before and propose two modifications, one of which achieves the best results on most of the considered datasets. While prior work focuses primarily on text classification tasks, we additionally investigate UE for NER. We release our implementation and experimental protocols to support transparent and reproducible research. All datasets and model checkpoints used in our study are publicly available under appropriate licenses.

spans: most systems now use grid beam search
document: Lexically constrained translation studies how to enforce user-specified terms during machine translation. Existing methods impose constraints either during training or at inference time. At training time, constrained MT models include code-switching data augmentation (Dinu et al., 2019; Song et al., 2019) and auxiliary objectives such as token- or span-level mask prediction (Ailem et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2021). At inference time, autoregressive approaches rely on placeholder tags (Luong et al., 2015; Crego et al., 2016), grid beam search (Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Post and Vilar, 2018), and alignment-enhanced decoding (Alkhouli et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021). These techniques ensure the presence of required terminology while attempting to maintain fluency and adequacy. Despite their effectiveness, they can suffer from exposure bias and increased decoding cost under dense constraints. For efficiency and robustness, several works explore non-autoregressive constrained translation, often by modifying iterative editing procedures (Susanto et al., 2020; Xu and Carpuat, 2021). Susanto et al. (2020) modify the inference procedure of the Levenshtein Transformer (Gu et al., 2019) by disallowing deletion of constraint tokens during editing. Xu and Carpuat (2021) further introduce a reposition operation to reorder constraint tokens without disrupting fluency. In practical deployments, most systems now use grid beam search for hard lexical constraints. However, grid-based methods can over-constrain hypotheses and interact poorly with long-distance reordering. To mitigate these issues, some approaches integrate constraint satisfaction with soft alignment signals derived from external terminology or bilingual lexicons (Song et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021). Our work connects these strands by leveraging alignment-aware terminology to teach models when and how to realize constraints. Non-autoregressive translation (NAT) offers substantial speedups by generating tokens in parallel or with a small number of iterations. Yet NAT performance often lags behind autoregressive baselines because multiple valid translations induce a multi-modality problem. Sequence-level knowledge distillation addresses this by training NAT models on outputs from strong autoregressive teachers (Kim and Rush, 2016; Gu et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021). Additional advances include latent-variable modeling (Kaiser et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2020) and iterative refinement via insertion, deletion, and substitution operations (Ghazvininejad et al., 2019; Stern et al., 2019; Gu et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020). Curriculum strategies that gradually increase dependency modeling and reordering difficulty further stabilize training (Qian et al., 2021). Recent objectives also better align token-level decisions with sequence-level metrics and adequacy constraints (Du et al., 2021). Editing-based NAT is particularly well suited to lexical constraints because constraints can be inserted, preserved, and repositioned across iterations. However, naively enforcing constraints may amplify modality conflicts and degrade fluency if context is not modeled carefully. We therefore incorporate alignment signals from terminologies to condition edits on local and cross-sentence context. Concretely, alignment-aware features guide the model to preserve rare terms while permitting flexible surrounding rephrasing. This design reduces unnecessary deletions and minimizes destructive rewrites during iterative refinement. Our approach thus complements prior constrained decoding with learned, context-sensitive constraint realization in NAT. Empirically, we target strong editing-based baselines and demonstrate consistent gains under varying constraint densities.

spans: Recent studies standardize a two-stage mention proposal and span-ranking pipeline as the dominant paradigm across narrative and procedural text
document:
Anaphora encompasses coreference, where expressions denote the same entity, and bridging, where expressions are linked by associative relations (Asher and Lascarides, 1998; Clark and Manning, 2015; Hou et al., 2018; Ng, 2017).
These phenomena are central to discourse understanding and downstream tasks such as information extraction and question answering.
Large-scale declarative corpora, notably OntoNotes/CoNLL 2012 and WikiCoref, have established benchmarks for coreference in news and Wikipedia (Pradhan et al., 2012; Weischedel et al., 2013; Ghaddar and Langlais, 2016b).
In specialized domains, BioNLP-ST 2011 and CRAFT provide biomedical coreference with tailored guidelines (Cohen et al., 2017; Kim et al., 2011).
Scientific-IE datasets such as SCIERC annotate within-abstract coreference linking entities and scientific concepts (Luan et al., 2018).
For bridging, ISNotes, BASHI, and SciCorp introduce referential links beyond identity within Wall Street Journal and scientific texts (Hou et al., 2018; Rösiger, 2016; Rösiger, 2018a).
In procedural domains, recipe understanding spans multimodal and textual settings across English and Japanese (Agarwal and Miller, 2011; Harashima et al., 2016; Kiddon et al., 2015; Marin et al., 2019; Nishimura et al., 2020).
However, most resources emphasize action-centric graphs rather than general anaphora, with RISeC targeting zero anaphora but not bridging (Jiang et al., 2020; Mori et al., 2014).
Methodologically, end-to-end span-ranking models superseded mention-ranking for coreference and remain competitive with large pretrained encoders (Lee et al., 2017; Kantor and Globerson, 2019; Xia and Van Durme, 2021).
Recent studies standardize a two-stage mention proposal and span-ranking pipeline as the dominant paradigm across narrative and procedural text.
Transfer learning from heterogeneous coreference corpora further improves robustness in low-resource domains (Brack et al., 2021; Xia and Van Durme, 2021).
For bridging, approaches range from rule-based patterns to neural models, including QA-style reformulations fine-tuned from generic reading comprehension corpora (Hou et al., 2014; Hou, 2020; Rösiger, 2018b; Yu and Poesio, 2020).
Joint training with end-to-end coreference has shown gains for bridging while maintaining competitive coreference quality (Yu and Poesio, 2020; Fang et al., 2021a).
Despite progress, procedural texts entail ellipsis, implicit arguments, and tool–ingredient associations that complicate both identity and associative links.
Existing corpora underrepresent these phenomena and lack unified annotations for coreference and bridging in step-wise instructions.
We therefore curate a procedural anaphora resource with harmonized guidelines spanning identity, apposition, referential bridging, and lexical bridging.
Our analyses and baseline experiments establish benchmarks for this domain and enable future studies on unified anaphora modeling.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about a specific methodological trend ('two-stage mention proposal and span-ranking pipeline' as dominant) across domains without providing citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim per the definition.

document: With the rise of social media and hate speech forums online (Phadke and Mitra, 2020; Szendro, 2021), offensive language detection has become more important than ever. Within this broad area, stereotypes represent a particularly insidious form of harm because they can be conveyed implicitly and contextually. English stereotype detection has received comparatively less focused attention than related tasks such as hate speech or toxicity classification (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018; Shushkevich and Cardiff, 2019; Mishra et al., 2019; Parekh and Patel, 2017). Progress is hampered by the need for world knowledge to recognize the targeted group and by the ambiguity of pragmatic cues in naturally occurring text. Prior surveys catalogue offensive language taxonomies and datasets, yet seldom center stereotypes as a primary category (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018). To stimulate stereotype-sensitive evaluation, diagnostic resources such as StereoSet and CrowS-Pairs were introduced (Nadeem et al., 2020; Nangia et al., 2020). While these resources are valuable, their template-driven construction limits coverage of the distributional properties found in the wild (Blodgett et al., 2021b). Consequently, there is a need for complementary corpora that capture subtle, organically produced stereotypical content from public forums. We address this gap by mining discussions from Reddit communities where implicit and explicit stereotypes are more likely to surface. Our annotation protocol emphasizes linguistic signals—such as presuppositions, implicatures, and adjectival framing—over annotator world knowledge to improve consistency. We operationalize four labels to reflect realistic usage: explicit stereotype, implicit association, anti-stereotype, and non-stereotype. Given the scarcity of expert labels, semi-supervised learning offers a practical path to scale high-quality training data (Ye et al., 2020; Konyushkova et al., 2020; Laskin et al., 2020). We therefore study an RL-guided selector that curates auxiliary-task examples to maximize transfer to the target stereotype detection objective (Ye et al., 2020). In addition, we adopt a multi-task framework that leverages neighboring signals from hate speech, toxicity, and sentiment datasets to enrich representations (Mishra et al., 2019). In contrast to prior art, most systems rely on template-based prompting, whereas we focus on representation learning without fixed prompts. Prompt-based fine-tuning and discrete prompting have shown strong performance across classification tasks, but their design often encodes language-specific priors (Shin et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021; Scao and Rush, 2021; Liu et al., 2021). For stereotype detection specifically, recent approaches also exploit external knowledge bases and counterfactual data augmentation to mitigate spurious cues (Fraser et al., 2021; Badjatiya et al., 2019). Our modeling complements these directions by integrating example selection with multi-task training to preferentially acquire signals that sharpen implicit stereotype recognition. We evaluate on both diagnostic sets and a new Reddit-derived corpus to examine robustness across constructed and naturalistic distributions (Nadeem et al., 2020; Nangia et al., 2020). To ensure fair comparison, we harmonize label spaces, control for topic confounds, and report results across prevalence-matched subsets. Our contributions are threefold. First, we present a naturalistic English stereotype corpus with fine-grained labels, detailed guidelines, and release protocols for ethical use. Second, we introduce an RL-based multi-task selection strategy that exploits related tasks to bootstrap scarce supervision for the target task. Third, we provide comprehensive analyses of error types, calibration, and inter-annotator agreement, including ablations on knowledge features. The remainder of the paper details data collection and ethics, describes the learning framework, reviews related work, and reports empirical findings.

spans: Beyond classification, recent studies adopt two-stage prompt distillation for augmentation
document: Prompt Learning The concept of prompt-based learning starts from the GPT-3 model (Brown et al., 2020), which demonstrated impressive zero-shot and few-shot capabilities via natural language instructions. Subsequent work designed discrete textual prompts to elicit knowledge and control behavior in pretrained language models (Petroni et al., 2019; Schick and Schütze, 2021). These approaches often rely on manually crafted templates whose quality strongly affects downstream performance (Schick and Schütze, 2021). To alleviate manual engineering, automatic pattern induction methods such as AutoPrompt generate token triggers using gradient signals (Shin et al., 2020). Another direction expands or refines prompts by sampling candidates from PLMs and selecting them with development data (Gao et al., 2021). However, optimizing discrete prompts separately from the task objective can limit end-to-end learning and robustness (Liu et al., 2021a). Continuous prompting addresses this limitation by introducing trainable soft vectors that are prepended or interleaved with inputs while freezing backbone parameters (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021). Such soft prompts do not correspond to real tokens but can be optimized directly with task losses (Lester et al., 2021). Empirical studies report competitive or superior performance to full fine-tuning in low-resource regimes (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021). Complementary techniques explore prefix-tuning for sequence-to-sequence models by optimizing continuous key–value prefixes in the attention layers (Li and Liang, 2021). Calibration and verbalizer learning further improve discrete prompt stability by correcting label token biases (Zhao et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Recent explorations also consider prompt ensembling and mixture-of-prompts to reduce variance across tasks (Liu et al., 2021b; Qin and Eisner, 2021). Despite these advances, transferring prompts across domains and architectures remains challenging due to distribution shifts and spurious correlations (Vu et al., 2021). In this work we focus on prompt learning as a vehicle for controllable data augmentation in low-resource classification. Generative Data Augmentation Prior research has leveraged synthetic examples to improve generalization in NLP via simple heuristics and conditional generation (Sennrich et al., 2016; Wei and Zou, 2019; Kobayashi, 2018). Back-translation creates paraphrased sources to augment supervised training, benefiting machine translation and text classification (Sennrich et al., 2016; Edunov et al., 2018). Contextual augmentation substitutes tokens with plausible alternatives predicted by a LM while preserving labels (Kobayashi, 2018). EDA introduces random insertions, deletions, and swaps to increase diversity for short-text classification (Wei and Zou, 2019). Paraphrase-driven augmentation using pretrained summarization or paraphrasing models, such as PEGASUS, yields fluent variants with task-aligned semantics (Zhang et al., 2020). For dialog and intent understanding, seq2seq generators have been trained to produce diverse utterances that cover rare slots (Hou et al., 2018). More recently, conditional PLMs fine-tuned on task data have been used to synthesize labeled examples for sequence labeling and inference (Xia et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2020). However, naive generation can amplify dataset artifacts, motivating filtering by language model perplexity, diversity constraints, and classifier agreement (Anaby-Tavor et al., 2020; Du et al., 2021). Prompt-based augmentation operationalizes these ideas by steering PLMs with task-specific instructions to produce class-consistent samples (Gao et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Beyond classification, recent studies adopt two-stage prompt distillation for augmentation, which we do not consider here. Compared with vanilla fine-tuning generators, soft-prompted PLMs can produce label-conditioned outputs while updating far fewer parameters (Lester et al., 2021). This parameter efficiency facilitates rapid adaptation to new domains without catastrophic forgetting of base capabilities (Liu et al., 2022). We build on this line and introduce PromDA, a prompt-based augmentation framework that pairs instruction templates with class and feature hints to guide generation. To ensure quality, PromDA integrates uncertainty-aware sampling and post-hoc filtering using a held-out teacher classifier for acceptance. In extensive low-resource experiments, we show that PromDA improves sample efficiency over discrete template baselines while maintaining label fidelity. Our study positions prompt-driven augmentation as a principled complement to continuous prompting for downstream adaptation.

spans: prefix-tuning is the de facto standard baseline
document: Prompt-based few-shot fine-tuning adapts pretrained language models by casting tasks as prompted prediction problems and has been studied extensively with moderately sized PLMs such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019). In this paradigm, a downstream example is converted into a cloze-style input and the model predicts a verbalized label via masked language modeling. PET operationalizes this idea by combining multiple prompt patterns and a gradient-based learner to refine the model (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b). AutoPrompt instead searches for discrete trigger tokens using gradient signals to optimize task performance (Shin et al., 2020). Null Prompts simplify design by concatenating inputs with a [MASK] token and avoid manual engineering effort (Logan et al., 2021). Beyond discrete templates, soft prompting replaces hard tokens with learnable continuous vectors prepended to the input (Lester et al., 2021). Lester et al. demonstrate that tuning such prompt embeddings while freezing the PLM can yield strong transfer across tasks (Lester et al., 2021). Complementarily, Gu et al. propose to pre-train soft prompts during language model pretraining to provide better initialization for downstream adaptation (Gu et al., 2021). Demonstration-aware prompting augments a prompt with example pairs, as in LM-BFF, which constructs demonstrations by unmasking prompts on similar inputs (Gao et al., 2021). While LM-BFF typically uses a single demonstration per label, our approach leverages multiple demonstrations aligned to automatically generated label phrases. To enable parameter efficiency, we implement these demonstrations as a soft memory of globally shared prompt vectors rather than as hard tokens copied from similar contexts. This design decouples content selection from token realization while keeping the base model frozen during training. For evaluation, we report mean and standard deviation over five random data splits to account for variability in few-shot sampling. We compare against full fine-tuning on the complete training set as an upper bound reference. We also include a majority-class baseline to contextualize gains in low-label regimes. In-context learning with RoBERTa-large is implemented following the prompting setup of Brown et al. using no parameter updates (Brown et al., 2020). For LM-BFF and DART baselines, we report the numbers given in their original papers where available (Gao et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). Beyond discrete templates, prefix-tuning is the de facto standard baseline, yet parameter-efficient methods more generally aim to adjust a tiny set of task-specific vectors while keeping backbone weights fixed. Representative instances of this family include prompt tuning and prefix-tuning, both of which have been shown to be competitive under limited supervision (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021). Orthogonal to tuning strategies, calibration techniques correct label prior biases that arise from imperfect verbalizers and unbalanced prompts (Zhao et al., 2021). Our method can be combined with calibration by applying post-hoc probability adjustment without modifying architectures. Compared to single-demonstration templates, multiple demonstrations enrich the conditional context and reduce variance in the induced label distribution. The soft memory further allows sharing statistical strength across labels that reuse similar semantic evidence. We adopt a simple retrieval procedure to select candidate demonstrations and then map them to soft vectors through a small projection network. During training, both the soft demonstration parameters and the verbalizer mapping are updated, while the PLM remains frozen. At inference time, the method requires only a fixed set of soft vectors and thus incurs negligible overhead relative to standard prompting. Empirically, we find that multiple soft demonstrations consistently improve stability across seeds and reduce the frequency of degenerate predictions. We release implementation details, prompt templates, and split indices to facilitate reproducibility of our experiments. In summary, the proposed multiple soft demonstration memory complements existing prompt-based methods by improving data efficiency without sacrificing the simplicity of the prompting interface.

spans: Recent studies standardize the two-stage retriever-reader pipeline
document: Controllable text generation seeks mechanisms for modifying attributes of text while preserving fluency and content. Early approaches explored sequence-to-sequence autoencoders and variational objectives to shape latent spaces for attribute manipulation (Shen et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2018). Subsequent research investigated disentangling content from style via structured priors and adversarial training to better separate controllable factors (John et al., 2019; Moyer et al., 2018). In parallel, plug-and-play methods conditioned large language models using gradient-based objectives or auxiliary discriminators without full fine-tuning (Gulcehre et al., 2015; Dathathri et al., 2019). Adapter-based techniques provided lightweight parameter-efficient control by inserting small trainable modules into frozen backbones (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Prompting and soft-prompt tuning further shifted control to input-side mechanisms and continuous prompt vectors that steer generation with minimal updates (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021). Our work focuses on latent steering vectors that can be extracted from pretrained models and applied at inference without additional training. We study whether such vectors occupy structured subspaces supporting linear arithmetic for attribute transfer and content preservation. Prior analyses of sentence representations in recurrent and transformer models suggest partial linearity that might enable these operations (Subramani et al., 2019; Ethayarajh, 2019; Li et al., 2020). We build on these insights to propose a practical procedure for discovering and applying steering directions in activation space. The method operates by contrasting activations induced by attribute-specific prompts and aggregating differences into normalized control vectors. During generation, we project hidden states onto these vectors to adjust probabilities while leaving model parameters unchanged. Compared to plug-and-play approaches that rely on external discriminators, our technique requires no auxiliary networks and introduces negligible latency. Compared to adapters and LoRA, it avoids training even a small set of parameters while remaining reversible and interpretable at step level (Hu et al., 2022; Dettmers et al., 2023). Recent studies standardize the two-stage retriever-reader pipeline. Although our focus is not retrieval, this observation motivates evaluating whether steering vectors transfer across contexts that involve intermediate representations. We therefore assess style, toxicity mitigation, and topic emphasis, covering both token-level and sentence-level controls. For each attribute, we derive steering vectors from small curated corpora and from synthetic prompts, and we compare their efficacy. We also analyze locality by probing which layers and positions yield the strongest controllability without harming coherence. To ensure rigorous comparison with baselines, we include PPLM, GeDi, prefix-tuning, and adapter-based control under matched decoding budgets (Dathathri et al., 2019; Krause et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021; Houlsby et al., 2019). Our experiments demonstrate that linear steering achieves competitive attribute strength with smaller semantic drift relative to plug-and-play methods on open-ended generation. Ablations show that vector normalization and orthogonal projection are crucial for stability, while per-layer mixing improves lexical diversity. Error sensitivity analyses indicate robustness to imperfect attribute labels, suggesting practicality for low-resource settings. We release code and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducibility and future extensions.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalizing claim about prior work—namely that recent studies standardize the two-stage retriever-reader pipeline—without providing any citations or evidence, thus matching the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: most benchmarks adopt layerwise learning-rate decay schedules
document: The standard practice of using BERT is fine-tuning, i.e. the entirety of the model parameters is adjusted on the training corpus of the downstream task, so that the model is adapted to that specific task (Devlin et al., 2019). There is also an alternative feature-based approach, used by ELMo (Peters et al., 2018). In the latter approach, the pre-trained model is regarded as a feature extractor with frozen parameters. During the learning of a downstream task, one feeds a fixed or learnable combination of the model's intermediate representations as input to the task-specific module, and only the parameters of the latter will be updated. It has been shown that the fine-tuning approach is generally superior to the feature-based approach for BERT in terms of task performance (Devlin et al., 2019;Peters et al., 2019). A natural middle ground between these two approaches is partial fine-tuning, i.e. only fine-tuning some topmost layers of BERT while keeping the remaining bottom layers frozen. This approach has been studied in (Houlsby et al., 2019;Merchant et al., 2020), where the authors observed that finetuning only the top layers can almost achieve the performance of full fine-tuning on several GLUE tasks. The approach of partial fine-tuning essentially regards the bottom layers of BERT as a feature extractor. Freezing weights from bottom layers is a sensible idea as previous studies show that the mid layer representations produced by BERT are most transferrable, whereas the top layers representations are more task-oriented (Wang et al., 2019;Tenney et al., 2019b,a;Merchant et al., 2020). Despite its effectiveness, full fine-tuning incurs significant storage costs when many tasks or domains are considered simultaneously. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods have thus emerged to reduce trainable degrees of freedom while preserving downstream accuracy. Adapter layers inject small task-specific modules into each transformer block and keep the backbone frozen (Houlsby et al., 2019). Prefix or prompt tuning optimizes virtual tokens that steer the frozen network during inference (Lester et al., 2021). Low-rank adaptation updates a pair of rank-constrained matrices that reparameterize weight updates in attention and feed-forward layers (Hu et al., 2021). Compared with full fine-tuning, these approaches dramatically reduce per-task memory footprints without modifying the original checkpoints. In practice, most benchmarks adopt layerwise learning-rate decay schedules, which are thought to stabilize optimization for deep transformers. Partial fine-tuning sits between full and parameter-efficient approaches by allowing limited adaptation capacity in higher layers. The intuition aligns with the view that lower layers capture lexical and syntactic features while higher layers encode task abstractions, making selective updating attractive for transfer. This strategy can mitigate catastrophic forgetting on out-of-domain evaluations by preserving general features in lower layers. Nevertheless, the optimal number of trainable layers depends on dataset size, domain similarity, and the capacity of the task head. Another design choice concerns how to share or decouple normalization statistics when mixing frozen and trainable layers. Small task heads can bottleneck performance when only a few encoder blocks are unfrozen, which motivates richer adaptation modules. From an efficiency perspective, partial fine-tuning reduces optimizer state and gradient memory, enabling larger batch sizes under fixed hardware budgets. From a stability perspective, freezing lower layers reduces the risk of overwriting broadly useful representations learned during pre-training. For fairness, evaluations should control for total parameter counts, training steps, and early stopping criteria when comparing partial and full fine-tuning. We position our study within this landscape by systematically varying the number and placement of trainable layers on standard classification and sequence labeling benchmarks. We also examine the interaction between partial fine-tuning and simple regularizers such as weight decay, dropout, and stochastic depth. Finally, we analyze representation drift across layers to characterize which subspaces change during adaptation and how these shifts relate to generalization.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work usage of a specific training trend (layerwise learning-rate decay) without providing any citations or evidence, thus fitting the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: two-stage pragmatic reranking pipelines
document:
Rational Speech Act (RSA)

Our work is inspired by (Andreas and Klein, 2016) and (Lazaridou and Baroni, 2020), where a referential game setup was used to propose a rational speaker that learns to reason about collaborative goals and to produce natural sounding image captions based on the Rational Speech Act model. The Rational Speech Act (RSA) model (Frank and Goodman, 2012) is a probabilistic framework in which speakers and listeners pragmatically reason about each other's intentions. In a referential game (Monroe and Potts, 2015), a literal speaker S0 maps an image m to a caption c via a conditional policy. A pragmatic listener L1 then reasons about the literal speaker S0 and selects the image that best matches the description. A pragmatic speaker S1 subsequently anticipates the listener L1 and produces a caption that maximizes task success under cooperative assumptions. This cascade formalizes how agents choose informative utterances given shared goals and bounded rationality. We adopt RSA-style reasoning with neural encoders for images and text to target discriminative captioning. Our evaluation considers referential accuracy alongside fluency to reflect the tradeoff between informativeness and naturalness. We analyze a simplifying assumption that constrains experimental designs. In particular, we examine the case where the speaker and listener possess identical perceptual features and linguistic competence. Such symmetry simplifies inference but rarely holds in human communication or human–AI interaction. To address this gap, we introduce controlled listener disparities and study how speakers adapt their strategies. Our formulation augments the standard RSA recursion with a parameterized disparity variable d that modulates listener beliefs. We further propose training objectives that jointly optimize communicative reward and robustness to disparity shifts. In visually grounded captioning, two-stage pragmatic reranking pipelines are often described as effective for balancing accuracy and fluency. Our approach instead integrates reasoning into a single differentiable decoding procedure to reduce mismatch between training and inference. We compare literal-only baselines, S1 decoding with uniform listener priors, and S1 decoding with disparity-aware listeners. For fair assessment, we hold backbone architectures constant and vary only the pragmatic objective. We report caption discrimination, listener selection accuracy, and calibration metrics across balanced subsets of distractor difficulty. We also examine the sensitivity of S1 outputs to gradient-based perturbations of listener features. These analyses clarify whether the speaker relies on spurious cues or genuinely tracks listener uncertainty. Beyond quantitative metrics, we include targeted qualitative cases that illustrate adaptation to color blindness, occlusion, and lexical gaps. The results indicate consistent gains for disparity-aware S1 decoding over literal baselines under controlled shifts. Moreover, we find that gains persist when the listener has partial access to the visual backbone used by the speaker. Finally, ablations on the disparity parameterization show that coarse categorical modeling performs comparably to fine-grained continuous variants. Together, these findings support the view that pragmatic reasoning can be productively aligned with robustness objectives. We release code and prompts to facilitate reproducibility and future work on asymmetric pragmatic communication.
reason: The span makes a general claim about a specific prior-work trend ('two-stage pragmatic reranking pipelines') being effective, but it provides no citation or evidence, thereby fitting the definition of an Unsupported claim.

spans: ubiquitous SHAP
document: In NLP, transformer-based models have achieved strong performance, and some works focus on explaining the attention mechanism. For example, Serrano and Smith (2019) and Jain and Wallace (2019) inspected a single attention layer and found that attention weights only weakly and inconsistently correspond to feature importance; Wiegreffe and Pinter (2019) argued that we cannot separate the attention layer and should view the entire model as a whole. In this section, we mainly review gradient-based methods for feature attribution. Feature Attribution on Single Input Simonyan et al. (2013) computed the saliency map, denoted as Simple Gradient, from the derivative of the output with respect to the input in an image classification task. In NLP applications, a saliency map is obtained as the derivative of the output with respect to the word embedding as in Equation (2). However, saliency maps can be visually noisy. Several methods have been proposed to improve gradient methods from different perspectives. The Gradient*Input method (Shrikumar et al., 2017) improves the visual sharpness of the saliency map by multiplying the gradient with the input itself. In NLP, we can write it as follows. Layerwise Relevance Propagation (Bach et al., 2015) is shown to be equivalent to the Gradient*Input method up to a scaling factor. The SmoothGrad method (Smilkov et al., 2017) smooths the feature attribution score by adding random noise to the input and averaging gradients from noisy inputs, formally as follows. Guided Backpropagation (Springenberg et al., 2014) modifies back-propagation to preserve negative gradients in the ReLU activation layer, which also sharpens the saliency map visually. Other methods, such as Grad-CAM or Guided Grad-CAM (Selvaraju et al., 2017), are applicable to specific architectures of neural networks in the field of computer vision. Since language models like BERT do not contain the specific architectural components utilized by Guided Backpropagation or Grad-CAM, we omit the mathematical formulation here. Feature Attribution on Input with Reference Data Integrated Gradients computes the feature score by integrating gradients from a single pre-determined reference input to the target input (Sundararajan et al., 2017). In computer vision problems, a black image is usually considered the reference input, and integrating gradients from the black image to the input image represents the feature attribution of the input image. In NLP problems, we can define the i-th element of feature attribution as follows. Here, H′ denotes the embedding of the reference text. SHAP-Gradient is a method that combines ideas from Integrated Gradients and SmoothGrad into a single expected-value equation (Lundberg and Lee, 2017), and the ubiquitous SHAP variants are widely used in practice. To be specific, the feature attribution is defined as follows. We let αk ∼ U(0,1) denote the uniform distribution on [0,1], and Hk ∈ Href denote embeddings of reference texts. DeepLIFT (Shrikumar et al., 2017) assigns feature scores by comparing the difference of contributions between inputs and some reference inputs via gradient-like rules. As discussed in Lundberg and Lee (2017), DeepLIFT can be considered an approximation to Shapley value estimation. Specifically, in the application of SHAP, the feature attribution of SHAP-Deep, a variant of DeepLIFT, is defined as follows. While these methods have complementary strengths, selecting appropriate baselines and smoothing strategies remains an open challenge for modern text encoders. We therefore position our approach as a unifying gradient-based framework that interfaces cleanly with subword tokenization and contextual embeddings.
