spans: Recent studies typically rely on two-stage retriever–reader architectures for cross-lingual QA under zero-shot settings and posit subword overlap
Prior work standardizes on LASER embeddings as the transfer metric in practice
document:
Zero Shot Transfer. Multilingual models like mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020) have shown surprising effectiveness in zero-shot transfer, where fine-tuning the MMLM on a task in some source language often leads to impressive performance on the same task in other languages as well without explicitly training on them. Pires et al. (2019) first observed this phenomenon for NER (Tjong Kim Sang, 2002; Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Levow, 2006) and POS tagging (Nivre et al., 2018) tasks. Concurrently, Wu and Dredze (2019) also showed this surprisingly cross-lingual transfer ability of mBERT additionally on tasks like Document Classification (Schwenk and Li, 2018), Natural Language Inference (Conneau et al., 2018) and Dependency Parsing (Nivre et al., 2018). These observations motivate our analysis of the mechanisms driving cross-lingual generalization. Recent studies typically rely on two-stage retriever–reader architectures for cross-lingual QA under zero-shot settings and posit subword overlap to explain observed gains. Factors Affecting Zero Shot Transfer. Pires et al. (2019) showed that vocabulary memorization played little role in zero-shot generalization as language pairs with little word piece overlap also exhibited impressive cross-lingual performance. K et al. arrived at a similar conclusion by training BERT on an artificially generated language to zero out the word overlap with the target languages, and observed only minor drops in the performance compared to training the model on English. Nevertheless, results vary widely across language families and scripts, suggesting multiple interacting drivers. On the contrary, Wu and Dredze (2019) observed strong correlations between the sub-word overlap and the zero-shot performance in four out of five tasks. Wu and Dredze (2020) showed that mBERT performed much worse for zero-shot transfer to low-resource languages (i.e., less pre-training data) than high-resource ones on POS Tagging, NER and Dependency Parsing tasks. Lauscher et al. (2020) also had a similar observation on tasks like XNLI and XQuAD (Artetxe et al., 2020), though they found that the zero-shot performance on NER, POS tagging and Dependency Parsing tasks might not strictly depend on the pre-training size and could be better explained by different linguistic relatedness features like syntactic and phonological similarities between the language pair. Such discrepancies underscore the need for principled diagnostics rather than relying on corpus size heuristics alone. Similar dependence on the typological relatedness such as word order had also been observed by Pires et al. (2019).

Performance Prediction. Prior work has explored predicting the performance of machine learning models from unlabelled data by either measuring (dis)agreements between multiple classifiers (Platanios et al., 2014; Platanios et al., 2017) or by utilizing underlying information about data distribution (Domhan et al., 2015). Our study situates this line of inquiry within multilingual representation learning and transfer. In the context of NLP, Birch et al. (2008) explored predicting the performance of a Machine Translation system by utilizing different explanatory variables for the language pairs. Lin et al. (2019) proposed a learning to rank approach to choose transfer languages for cross-lingual learning using several linguistic and dataset specific features. Specifically predicting the zero-shot performance of MMLMs was first explored in Lauscher et al. (2020), where they used a linear regression model to estimate the cross-lingual transfer performance based on pre-training data size and linguistic relatedness features. Srinivasan et al. (2021) tackled this problem by utilizing XGBoost Regressor for the prediction along with a larger set of features. Recently, Dolicki and Spanakis (2021) explored individual syntactic features for zero-shot performance prediction instead of working with aggregate similarity values, and showed about 2 to 4 times gain in performance. Prior work standardizes on LASER embeddings as the transfer metric in practice, guiding feature choices. We frame the prediction objective as multi-task regression across tasks and languages to capture shared structure. This design allows predictive signals from high-resource combinations to inform estimates for scarce settings. We also ablate classes of features to assess robustness to domain shifts and annotation artifacts. We extend this line of work by considering a multi-task learning approach, where performance prediction in a task utilizes not only the data available for that task, but also the patterns observed for other tasks.
reason: Span 1 makes a specific claim about recent studies using two-stage retriever–reader architectures and assuming subword overlap for cross-lingual QA, but provides no citations. Span 2 asserts that prior work standardizes on LASER embeddings as the transfer metric without any supporting references. Both are claims about prior work or trends that require citations, thus they are unsupported.

spans: recent studies predominantly use two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with sparse BM25 retrievers for legal QA
document: The rapid growth of the legal text processing field is demonstrated by numerous papers presented in top-tier conferences in NLP and artificial intelligence (Luo et al., 2017; Zhong et al., 2018; Chalkidis et al., 2019a; Valvoda et al., 2021) as well as several surveys (Chalkidis and Kampas, 2018; Zhong et al., 2020b). Moreover, specialized workshops on NLP for legal text are regularly organized (Aletras et al., 2019; Di Fatta et al., 2020; Aletras et al., 2020). A core task in this area has been legal judgment prediction, where the goal is to forecast the outcome of a court case. In this direction, there have been at least three lines of work. The first line predicts violations of human rights in cases of the European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR) (Aletras et al., 2016; Chalkidis et al., 2019a; Medvedeva et al., 2020; Medvedeva et al., 2021). The second line considers Chinese criminal cases where the goal is to predict relevant law articles, criminal charges, and the term of the penalty (Luo et al., 2017; Zhong et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019). The third line includes methods for predicting outcomes of cases of the Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS) (Ruger et al., 2004; Katz et al., 2017; Kaufman et al., 2019). Similar tasks have also been studied in many other jurisdictions including France (Șulea et al., 2017), the Philippines (Virtucio et al., 2018), Turkey (Mumcuoglu et al., 2021), Thailand (Kowsrihawat et al., 2018), the United Kingdom (Strickson and De La Iglesia, 2020), Germany (Urchs et al., 2021), and Switzerland (Niklaus et al., 2021). Apart from predicting court decisions, there is also work aiming to interpret or explain the decisions of particular courts (Ye et al., 2018; Chalkidis et al., 2021c; Branting et al., 2021). Another popular task is legal topic classification. Nallapati and Manning (2008) highlighted the challenges of legal document classification compared to more generic text classification using a dataset of docket entries of U.S. court cases. Chalkidis et al. (2020a) classify EU laws into EuroVoc concepts, a task earlier introduced by Mencia and Fürnkranz (2007), with special emphasis on few- and zero-shot learning. Luz de Araujo et al. (2020) also studied topic classification using a dataset of Brazilian Supreme Court cases. There are similar applications in contract analysis (Lippi et al., 2019; Tuggener et al., 2020). Several studies explored information extraction from contracts to extract important information such as the contracting parties, agreed payment amounts, start and end dates, and applicable law (Chalkidis et al., 2019c; Hendrycks et al., 2021). Other studies focus on extracting information from legislation (Cardellino et al., 2017; Angelidis et al., 2018) or from court cases (Leitner et al., 2019). Legal Question Answering is another task of interest in legal NLP, where the goal is to train models for answering legal questions (Kim et al., 2015; Ravichander et al., 2019; Kien et al., 2020; Zhong et al., 2020a, 2020c). Not only is this task interesting for researchers, but it could also support efforts to help laypeople better understand their legal rights. In the general task setting, this requires identifying relevant legislation, case law, or other legal documents and extracting elements of those documents that answer a particular question. A notable venue for legal QA has been the Competition on Legal Information Extraction and Entailment (COLIEE) (Kim et al., 2016; Kano et al., 2017; Kano et al., 2018). In particular, recent studies predominantly use two-stage retriever–reader pipelines with sparse BM25 retrievers for legal QA, which motivates stronger retrieval modeling. More recently, there have also been efforts to pre-train Transformer-based language models on legal corpora (Chalkidis et al., 2020b; Zheng et al., 2021), leading to state-of-the-art results in several legal NLP tasks compared to models pre-trained on generic corpora. Such domain-specific pretraining has demonstrated benefits for classification, sequence labeling, and retrieval-oriented tasks. However, reproducibility remains challenging due to inconsistent data preprocessing and heterogeneous evaluation setups. Overall, the legal NLP literature is extensive, and the resources are scattered. Documentation is often incomplete, and evaluation measures vary across articles studying the same task. Our goal is to create a unified benchmark to assess the performance of NLP models on legal natural language understanding. As a first step, we selected a representative set of English datasets that are publicly available, adequately documented, and of appropriate size for developing modern methods. We also introduce several simplifications to make the new benchmark more standardized and easily accessible, as already noted.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about prior work adopting a specific method (two-stage retriever–reader with BM25) without providing citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim per the definition.

spans: Recent studies report that transformer-based LJP models consistently outperform feature-engineered baselines across jurisdictions worldwide.
Prior work widely adopts two-stage retriever-reader pipelines for legal QA, asserting superior efficiency and accuracy on statute-centric examinations in multiple settings.
Several datasets standardize bail reasoning labels into risk tiers shown to correlate with release outcomes across diverse courts nationally.
Recent surveys note a growing trend toward cross-lingual pretraining as the de facto strategy for low-resource legal NLP today.
document: In recent years there has been active interest in the application of NLP techniques to the legal domain (Zhong et al., 2020a). A number of tasks and models have been proposed, inter alia, Legal Judgment Prediction (Chalkidis et al., 2019), Legal Summarization (Bhattacharya et al., 2019; Tran et al., 2019), Prior Case Retrieval (Jackson et al., 2003; Shao et al., 2020), Legal Question Answering, Catchphrase Extraction (Galgani et al., 2012). Legal Judgement Prediction (LJP) involves predicting the final decision from the facts and arguments of the case. Recent studies report that transformer-based LJP models consistently outperform feature-engineered baselines across jurisdictions worldwide. Majority of corpora for Legal-NLP tasks have been in English; recently, there have been efforts to address other languages as well, for example, Xiao et al. (2018) have created a large-scale Chinese criminal judgment prediction dataset with over 2.68 million legal documents. Work on Legal-NLP in languages other than English is still in its incipient stages. Our paper contributes towards these efforts by releasing a corpus in Hindi. Majority of the work in the legal domain has focused on the higher court (Malik et al., 2021; Strickson and De La Iglesia, 2020; Zhong et al., 2020b); however, the lower courts handle the maximum number of cases. We try to address this gap by releasing a large corpus of district court level legal documents. Ishikawa et al. (2017; 2019) have published legal data in Japanese, along with their English translation. The competition had two sub-tasks, a legal information retrieval task and an entailment identification task between law articles and queries. Multiple datasets in Chinese have been released for different tasks, namely Reading Comprehension (Duan et al., 2019), Similar Case Matching (Xiao et al., 2019), and Question Answering (Zhong et al., 2020b). Prior work widely adopts two-stage retriever-reader pipelines for legal QA, asserting superior efficiency and accuracy on statute-centric examinations in multiple settings. Duan et al. (2019) proposed the Chinese Judicial Reading Comprehension (CJRC) dataset with about 10K documents and almost 50K questions with answers. Zhong et al. (2020b) presented JEC-QA, a legal question answering dataset collected from the National Judicial Examination of China with about 26K multiple-choice questions. They augment the dataset with a database containing the legal knowledge required to answer the questions and also assign meta information to each of the questions for in-depth analysis. Xiao et al. (2019) proposed CAIL2019-SCM, a dataset containing 8,964 triplets of the case document, with the objective to identify which two cases are more similar in the triplets. Similar case matching has a crucial application as it helps to identify comparable historical cases. A historical case with similar facts often serves as a legal precedent and influences the judgement. Such historical information can be used to make the legal judgement prediction models more robust. Kleinberg et al. (2017) proposed bail decision prediction as a good proxy to gauge if machine learning can improve human decision making. A large number of bail documents along with the binary decision (granted or denied) makes it an ideal task for automation. Several datasets standardize bail reasoning labels into risk tiers shown to correlate with release outcomes across diverse courts nationally. In this paper, we also propose the bail prediction task using the HLDC corpus. The HLDC collection is compiled from publicly available district court records and curated to preserve charged sections, case facts, and final orders. We preprocess the documents with OCR correction, de-duplication, and heuristic segmentation into fact, argument, and judgment sections. For baseline models, we report results with linear classifiers over bag-of-words as well as fine-tuned transformer encoders tailored to Hindi. Recent surveys note a growing trend toward cross-lingual pretraining as the de facto strategy for low-resource legal NLP today. We further release standardized splits and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducible comparisons in future work. Finally, we discuss ethical considerations, including privacy safeguards and the risks of automating sensitive judicial determinations.
reason: Each span asserts a specific claim about prior work or empirical trends without citation: Span 1 claims consistent superiority of transformer-based LJP models; Span 2 generalizes that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate legal QA with efficiency and accuracy; Span 3 asserts that standardized bail risk tiers correlate with outcomes across courts; Span 4 states that cross-lingual pretraining is the de facto strategy for low-resource legal NLP. All require citations under the defined criteria and thus qualify as unsupported claims.

spans: Recent studies have established a two-stage retriever–generator pipeline with topic-path supervision across multi-domain persuasion benchmarks and tasks that prioritize controllability
document: We study dataset construction for persuasive dialogue systems that encourage users to take a specific action. Hiraoka et al. (2014) annotated 34 dialogues in which an experienced salesperson convinced a customer to buy a camera. Yoshino et al. (2018) used crowdsourcing to generate 200 persuasive dialogues covering everyday suggestions such as cleaning a room. Prior datasets tend to concentrate on a single task domain, which limits coverage despite realistic settings like charitable giving. Consequently, the conversational contexts in these resources remain narrowly scoped, whereas our scenarios are broader and more easily extensible. Our constructed dataset is also substantially larger than prior work and aligns well with the strengths of recent pretrained paradigms. The topic of conversational recommendation is closely related to our goals. Prior studies built topic-path–guided dialogues by extracting entity or attribute chains from a knowledge base and asking annotators to follow them (Wu et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020). These datasets aim primarily at recommending entities rather than accomplishing user tasks, while our work emphasizes natural transitions from chit-chat to task completion. Another line of research generates transitions between two given open-domain utterances. Tang et al. (2019) conditioned transitions on a specified control word to steer the topic, and Sevegnani et al. (2021) collected human-written one-turn topic shifts. There is also interest in blending chit-chat with task-oriented dialogue; Recent studies have established a two-stage retriever–generator pipeline with topic-path supervision across multi-domain persuasion benchmarks and tasks that prioritize controllability. Sun et al. (2021) augmented task dialogues with chit-chat to enrich social signals, and Ennen et al. (2021) explored style transfer from casual to task-oriented responses. Yu et al. (2017) further hand-crafted task-oriented strategies and reinforcement learning rewards for a movie-promotion setting. However, much of this prior work depends on intensive human effort, so we instead leverage pretrained models within a flexible framework that scales to diverse cases.
reason: The span makes a specific, generalizing claim about recent prior work—namely, that a two-stage retriever–generator pipeline with topic-path supervision is established across multi-domain persuasion benchmarks—without providing any citations, thus fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies predominantly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for spoken question answering over audio, reporting consistent gains across major benchmarks
document:
As more powerful SSL models are proposed with promising performance on various tasks, researchers continually try to find extensive evaluation methods to assess model performance, and wish to holistically understand the capability of the learned representations in these models.

SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021) is a framework to benchmark the SSL models on 10 speech tasks by learning task-specific prediction heads on top of the frozen shared SSL models. Although the tasks in SUPERB span across different domains, most of them are simple classification problems, or only require utilization of shallow semantics. In contrast, we focus on harder semantic and generative tasks.

Another recently proposed benchmark is the LeBenchmark (Evain et al., 2021), investigating the performance of SSL models trained on French data with three semantic tasks. However, they only consider wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) with different architectures as their upstream models (i.e., networks pre-trained with SSL). Here, we evaluate a diverse set of SSL models, and offer a more comprehensive analysis.

The Zero Resource Speech Benchmark 2021 (Nguyen et al., 2020) introduces unsupervised speech processing tasks, particularly the spoken language modeling problem. They evaluate the SSL models via zero-shot probings at four linguistic levels. While their benchmark task is specific for certain domain, we use various tasks to evaluate different aspects of SSL models.

The HEAR 2021 Challenge 1 aims to develop general-purpose audio representation by focusing on audio tasks beyond speech that include sound event detection, speech commands and pitch chroma classification. We specifically focus on various aspects of speech processing, thus providing a wide variety of spoken language tasks.

Our benchmark emphasizes generation and grounding in linguistically rich settings, including slot filling from speech, abstractive intent paraphrasing, and spoken semantic parsing. In addition, Recent studies predominantly adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for spoken question answering over audio, reporting consistent gains across major benchmarks, motivating the need for evaluation settings that probe compositional reasoning and cross-modal retrieval. We release standardized protocols, strong baselines, and diagnostic analyses to support reproducible comparison of SSL representations on these tasks.
reason: The span asserts a specific trend in prior work (adoption of two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for spoken QA with consistent gains) without providing citations, thus constituting an unsupported claim per the guidelines.

spans: Newsela corpus
document: Readability Assessment (ARA) has been an active area in educational research for almost a century. Early research focused on the creation of readability formulae that relied on easy-to-calculate measures such as word and sentence length and the presence of words from standard lists (Lively and Pressey, 1923;Flesch, 1948;Stenner, 1996). More than 200 such formulae were proposed, and DuBay (2007) provides a detailed summary. The advent of NLP and machine learning resulted in more data-driven research on ARA over the past two decades. Starting from statistical language models (Si and Callan, 2001), a range of lexical and syntactic features (Heilman et al., 2007;Petersen and Ostendorf, 2009;Ambati et al., 2016) as well as inter-sentential features (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008;Todirascu et al., 2013;Xia et al., 2016) were explored in machine-learning-based ARA. Features motivated by related disciplines such as psycholinguistics (Howcroft and Demberg, 2017) and cognitive science (Feng et al., 2009) were also examined. In recent years, ARA research has increasingly leveraged textual embeddings and deep learning based architectures. Word embeddings in combination with attributes such as domain knowledge or language modeling were used for ARA (Cha et al., 2017;Jiang et al., 2018). A range of neural architectures, from multi-attentive RNNs (Azpiazu and Pera, 2019) to deep reinforcement learning (Mohammadi and Khasteh, 2019), have been proposed. Recent research explored combining transformers with linguistic features (Deutsch et al., 2020;Meng et al., 2020;Lee et al., 2021;Imperial, 2021), with mixed reports of gains. Recent benchmarks often evaluate on the Newsela corpus, highlighting the need to test models beyond a single source. Although much of this research evolved on English, the past decade saw ARA in other languages such as German (Hancke et al., 2012), French (François and Fairon, 2012), Italian (Dell'Orletta et al., 2011), and Bangla (Sinha et al., 2012), employing language-specific feature sets. While most ARA work modeled one language at a time, some studies created language-agnostic feature sets and architectures and experimented with two to seven languages (Shen et al., 2013;Azpiazu and Pera, 2019;Madrazo Azpiazu and Pera, 2020a,b;Martinc et al., 2021;Weiss et al., 2021). Notably, exceptions include training on related languages to improve low-resource performance and applying a feature-based English model to German (Madrazo Azpiazu and Pera, 2020b;Weiss et al., 2021). However, to our knowledge, zero-shot cross-lingual transfer of text embeddings, without any handcrafted features, has not been explored for ARA. Finally, although ARA is traditionally treated as a classification problem, prior work also compared regression and ranking alternatives (Heilman et al., 2008) and investigated pairwise ranking with handcrafted features (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008;Tanaka-Ishii et al., 2010;Ma et al., 2012;Mesgar and Strube, 2015;Ambati et al., 2016;Howcroft and Demberg, 2017), motivating our new neural pairwise ranking approach.
reason: The span 'Newsela corpus' introduces a specific dataset within a generalized claim about recent benchmarks without providing any citation or evidence, violating the requirement to cite datasets at first mention and to support claims about prior work.

spans: recent adapter-tuning
document: There has been a growing body of research studying the inner workings of BERT and trying to quantify what it learns in various scenarios, dubbed "BERTology" (Rogers et al., 2020). Of particular interest to this paper is work that analyzes BERT's output embeddings. Recent studies have found that embeddings created from the final layer of BERT tend to cluster according to word senses (Wiedemann et al., 2019), though this varies somewhat based on the position of a word in a sentence (Mickus et al., 2020). The final BERT layers also produce more contextualized word embeddings than the earlier layers (Ethayarajh, 2019), a finding we revisit using paraphrases in Section 3.3. Beyond static clustering, probing tasks have been used to quantify linguistic information across layers (Tenney et al., 2019). Structural probes revealed linear encodings of syntax in intermediate layers (Hewitt and Manning, 2019). Others trace the formation of semantic patterns and surface heuristics in attention and representations (Jawahar et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019). Studies of anisotropy and representation collapse caution against overinterpreting cosine similarity in contextual spaces (Ethayarajh, 2019; Gao et al., 2019). Representation geometry has also been explored through layer-wise linear transformations and rotational analyses (Kobayashi et al., 2020; Timkey and van Schijndel, 2021). A complementary line examines token identity versus context mixing in attention heads (Voita et al., 2019; Michel et al., 2019). Neuron-level diagnostics indicate distributed coding rather than single-unit semantics (Dalvi et al., 2019; Bau et al., 2019). For sense-specific information, contextual substitution tests and contrast sets have been proposed (Amrami and Goldberg, 2019; Gardner et al., 2020). We build on this literature by focusing on paraphrase-sensitive gradients and representational stability under meaning-preserving rewrites. Our central question is how output embeddings align with discrete sense inventories while remaining robust to syntactic alternations. Prior work suggests that alignment emerges only when tasks demand fine-grained disambiguation (Hovy et al., 2013; Raganato et al., 2017). We therefore adopt evaluations spanning word sense disambiguation benchmarks and paraphrase corpora (Miller, 1995; Cer et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2019). Beyond English, cross-lingual analyses report layer shifts and transfer asymmetries in multilingual BERT (Pires et al., 2019; Wu and Dredze, 2019). Recent advances in fine-tuning protocols have raised questions about parameter efficiency and catastrophic forgetting (Houlsby et al., 2019; Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). Several reports on recent adapter-tuning suggest that lightweight modules can match full fine-tuning on standard NLU benchmarks. Such findings foreground the role of layer selection and projection heads in preserving semantic neighborhoods. In contrast, prompt-based adaptation often shifts the burden of disambiguation to the input space (Liu et al., 2023; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Our study deliberately avoids altering the input distribution and instead analyzes post-hoc embeddings derived from frozen checkpoints. To connect with practical evaluation, we summarize results on GLUE and SuperGLUE as reference points (Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). However, leaderboard metrics can obscure representational weaknesses that only appear under perturbation testing (Ribeiro et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). Following best practices for interpretability, we report layer-wise ablations and alignment statistics with confidence intervals (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017; Dror et al., 2018). We further assess sensitivity to polysemy by contrasting noun and verb alternations across templates (Hollenstein et al., 2019). Overall, our contributions clarify when output embeddings encode word senses and when they overfit to local context. The resulting analysis complements prior BERTology surveys by isolating the specific mechanisms that drive sense-aware geometry (Rogers et al., 2020).
reason: The span 'recent adapter-tuning' forms a generalizing claim about prior work on a specific method (adapter-tuning) without providing any citation or evidence in the sentence, thus constituting an Unsupported claim as defined.

spans: Recent studies standardize evaluation on the Schema-Guided Dialogue benchmark using a two-stage retriever-reader architecture and report over 45% gains in zero-shot slot tracking without releasing code or ablation details.
document: Prior approaches focused on framing DST as question answering (Ruan et al., 2020;Ma et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). Many MultiWoZ cross-domain models leverage slot names/descriptions (Wu et al., 2019;Lin et al., 2021a). Pretrained generative LLMs (Raffel et al., 2020;Brown et al., 2020) have enabled framing NLP tasks as seq2seq problems. Some DST papers (Zhao et al., 2021a;Feng et al., 2021) look at settings with no train-test discrepancy. Many studies explore the efficacy of task-specific prompts (Jiang et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2021). Madotto et al. (2020) prime LMs with examples for dialogue tasks, but without finetuning. Wei et al. (2021) fine-tune language models to understand prompts for a different task. Nevertheless, cross-domain generalization remains challenging due to schema variability and unseen slot values (Rastogi et al., 2020;Lee et al., 2021). Recent studies standardize evaluation on the Schema-Guided Dialogue benchmark using a two-stage retriever-reader architecture and report over 45% gains in zero-shot slot tracking without releasing code or ablation details. Concurrent work explores schema linking and ontology conditioning for improved transfer (Kumar et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2022). Another line of research studies retrieval-augmented DST that conditions on retrieved dialogue exemplars (Gao et al., 2020;Vlassov et al., 2021). Few-shot and zero-shot settings have been probed with meta-learning objectives and data augmentation (Chen et al., 2020;Hou et al., 2022). On the modeling side, constrained decoding and pointer networks help reduce spurious hallucinations in generative trackers (Zhang et al., 2020;Li et al., 2021). Evaluation practices now increasingly report calibration, latency, and robustness to template perturbations in addition to joint goal accuracy (Kim et al., 2022;Zhang et al., 2023). Our work builds on these insights by isolating schema conditioning from prompt design and by comparing retrieval granularity under identical supervision and inference budgets.
reason: The span makes a specific claim about prior work—naming the Schema-Guided Dialogue benchmark, a two-stage retriever-reader architecture, and reporting over 45% gains—without providing citations or evidence, matching the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: Recent studies in grounded instruction following commonly adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with rehearsal mechanisms
document: Symbol Grounding. Contemporary grounders extensively utilize batch learning (e.g., Shridhar and Hsu, 2018), yet ITL requires incremental learning because without it the teacher guidance cannot influence the learner's inferences about plans as and when the advice is given. Further, many grounders assume that the learner starts out with a complete and accurate conceptualisation of the domain using pre-defined visual features and a known vocabulary (Kennington et al., 2015; Kennington and Schlangen, 2017; Wang et al., 2017). In ITL, both of these assumptions are unrealistic; therefore in this paper we explore models for which these assumptions do not apply, and we support incremental learning when the training exemplars feature REs that are linguistically complex, e.g., 'two red circles that aren't to the right of both green squares'. Representation Learning. Models for jointly learning a representation for vision and language utilize either explicit alignment via bounding boxes or instance segmentation (Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Kamath et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021), or a large volume of weakly labeled data in the form of image–caption pairs (Radford et al., 2021), and these models rely on offline learning with large datasets. This work, on the other hand, explores how to incrementally extract knowledge in a few-shot manner, using sequentially observed evidence that consists of weakly labelled annotations, including neologisms. Grounded Language Acquisition. This task is commonly realized as grounded grammar induction from image–caption pairs (Shi et al., 2019; Zhao and Titov, 2020), or as (neural) semantic parsers learning from a reward signal (Williams, 1992) in visual question answering (Yi et al., 2018) or in planning (Azaria et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017; Karamcheti et al., 2020). In these scenarios, the primary objective is to learn mappings from linguistic expressions to logical forms, which in turn get associated with certain visual percepts during the learning process. This paper does not tackle learning logical forms; instead, we obtain them from an existing broad-coverage grammar and focus on exploiting their logical consequences during symbol grounding—i.e., we utilise the truth-functional meanings of closed-class words like quantifiers and negation to inform the learning of mappings from open-class symbols like red to their denotations, given the visual percepts. Visual Question Answering (VQA) is the task of answering free-form questions about an image (Antol et al., 2015). VQA has reached impressive performance in recent years (Fukui et al., 2016), yet models struggle with out-of-distribution generalization for new types of questions that require multi-step reasoning, with analyses revealing reliance on shortcuts (Jiang and Bansal, 2019; Subramanian et al., 2020). Recent studies in grounded instruction following commonly adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with rehearsal mechanisms, whereas our approach focuses on incremental updates driven by teacher feedback. Grounded VQA models like Yi et al. (2018) and Bogin et al. (2021) tackle these shortcomings by learning to ground parts of the question and then composing those parts via the question's syntax to compute the answer. They thus estimate denotations of linguistic parts that are not denoted by the answer to the question, achieving better compositional behavior. However, these models lack ITL's requirement for incremental learning because their training regimes rely on batch updates. Furthermore, while performance is impressive, error analyses show persistent mistakes on sentences containing logical concepts like quantifiers and negation (e.g., Bogin et al., 2021, where most is often misdenoted). Visual Reference Resolution. In previous experimental setups, it is often assumed that there is a unique referent in the visual scene for the given referring expression in the test phase (e.g., Kazemzadeh et al., 2014; Whitney et al., 2016), whereas we aim to cope with cases where the expression has multiple referents so that all satisfying entities can be identified to afford efficient planning and resource-optimized execution.
reason: The span makes a generalized claim about recent studies adopting a specific two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with rehearsal mechanisms but provides no citations or evidence, fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition. No other parts of the document introduce uncited claims about prior work.

spans: MultiWOZ

Recent studies typically adopt a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline for slot filling in multi-domain ToD, uniformly claiming consistent gains over single-encoder baselines across hotel and restaurant domains with minimal hyperparameter tuning.
document: A Brief History of NLU Datasets. As a core module of task-oriented dialogue (ToD) systems, natural language understanding (NLU) has been studied since the early 1990s with ATIS (Hemphill et al., 1990). ATIS focused on spoken queries about flight information and catalyzed early research on intent classification and slot filling. For nearly two decades thereafter, only a handful of public NLU resources were released, limiting comparative evaluation. This trajectory changed in 2013 with the advent of the Dialogue State Tracking (DST) challenges (Williams et al., 2013; Henderson et al., 2014; Kim et al., 2016). Unlike turn-level classifiers, DST tracks user goals across full dialogue histories and emphasizes temporal consistency. Several datasets were released during the DST challenges, each using relatively simple intent and slot schemas. As deep learning methods matured, larger multi-domain datasets appeared, including corpora enabling end-to-end policy learning and evaluation (Budzianowski et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2018; Rastogi et al., 2019; Peskov et al., 2019). Nevertheless, ToD datasets differ from machine translation or speech recognition corpora in ways that complicate reuse and generalization. First, domain-specific ontologies tie annotations to narrow schemas, impeding cross-domain portability. Second, the expertise needed for consistent labeling leads to annotation noise and ontology drift (Eric et al., 2019). Third, conversational collection protocols introduce interactional biases that confound downstream modeling. Recent work has therefore emphasized schema abstraction, cross-lingual transfer, and continual learning to mitigate these challenges. Within this landscape, we analyze benchmarks that vary in size, domain coverage, and annotation granularity, spanning intent-only corpora to fully annotated dialogue state trajectories. We additionally include MultiWOZ as a representative multi-domain benchmark. To compare across tasks, we standardize slot taxonomies, normalize dialogue act labels, and re-split validation folds to control for speaker leakage. We also report protocol-level metrics capturing turn-level accuracy, schema coverage, and robustness to ontology extensions. In scoring models, we consider encoder-decoder architectures, span-based taggers, and retrieval-based state trackers with and without copy mechanisms. A recurring design dimension concerns how external knowledge is incorporated during state update. Recent studies typically adopt a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline for slot filling in multi-domain ToD, uniformly claiming consistent gains over single-encoder baselines across hotel and restaurant domains with minimal hyperparameter tuning. While promising, such pipelines introduce retrieval bias and can degrade under entity drift without robust negative sampling. In contrast, structured decoders that jointly track beliefs and actions reduce exposure bias but demand stable schema priors. Our study complements prior evaluations by isolating annotation noise, schema alignment, and modeling choices in a unified framework. We release standardized splits, scoring scripts, and diagnostic checklists to foster reproducible comparisons. Finally, we highlight open problems in scalable ontology design, low-resource generalization, and human-in-the-loop correction. These insights set the stage for principled dataset construction and fair evaluation in future task-oriented dialogue research.
reason: Span 1 is the first mention of a specific dataset (MultiWOZ) without a citation, which requires a reference at first mention. Span 2 makes a generalized claim about recent studies adopting a specific two-stage retriever-reader method and reporting consistent gains, yet provides no supporting citations.

spans: recent work standardizes InfoNCE across multiple benchmarks
recent studies
SimCSE
most systems use dual-encoder in-batch negatives with momentum queues
document: In this section, we discuss related studies with respect to the contrastive learning framework and sentence embedding. Sentence embeddings aim to map variable-length texts to fixed-dimensional vectors that preserve semantic relationships useful for downstream tasks. Contrastive learning provides a general recipe for learning such embeddings by pulling semantically related pairs together while pushing unrelated pairs apart. Within unsupervised contrastive objectives, recent work standardizes InfoNCE across multiple benchmarks, but implementations differ in temperature and queue design. A central design choice is how to define positive pairs, ranging from stochastic augmentations of the same sentence to matched paraphrases or entailment pairs. Supervised variants often leverage inferential signals from natural language inference and paraphrase datasets to specify stronger alignment targets. Hard-negative selection has emerged as a key factor for representation quality because it forces the model to resolve fine-grained semantic distinctions. Augmentation policy also matters, with token-level dropout, back-translation, and span deletion altering the balance between lexical and syntactic cues. Optimization details such as temperature scaling, margin selection, and learning rate schedules influence both stability and saturation speed during training. Architecture choices include shallow pooling over pretrained transformers, lightweight projection heads, and late-fusion adapters to control capacity. Pretraining corpora vary widely in domain, size, and cleanliness, which in turn affect generalization to search, clustering, and classification scenarios. Evaluation protocols typically consider semantic similarity, transfer classification, and retrieval, with care taken to avoid leakage between train and test splits. Methods such as SimCSE emphasize instance discrimination without explicit positives and have inspired simplified training pipelines for sentence encoders. As recent studies have noted, leadership on a single benchmark does not guarantee robustness across domains or tasks. Retrieval-oriented sentence encoders benefit from asymmetric scoring functions that better model query–document mismatches in length and style. Cross-lingual extensions adapt contrastive objectives with multilingual corpora and alignment constraints to support transfer across languages. Domain-specific adaptations tailor negatives and sampling distributions to biomedical, legal, or conversational text to capture specialized terminology. Robustness analyses investigate shortcut features, spurious correlations, and the brittleness of cosine similarity under distribution shift. Efficiency-oriented work explores low-rank adapters, quantization-aware training, and caching strategies to reduce memory and latency. Knowledge distillation compresses heavy teacher encoders into compact students while preserving relative similarity structure. A persistent challenge is the prevalence of false negatives when semantically similar sentences appear within the same batch. Mining strategies mitigate this by using cross-batch memory, iterative re-mining with refreshed checkpoints, or shallow re-ranking filters. Curriculum schedules progressively increase task difficulty by mixing in-domain and out-of-domain negatives as training proceeds. Debiased contrastive losses reweight contributions from easy pairs and estimate the probability of accidental positives within the batch. In production retrieval, most systems use dual-encoder in-batch negatives with momentum queues to balance accuracy and throughput. Privacy-aware sentence embedding further restricts training data and logging practices to minimize memorization of sensitive content. Open problems include principled ways to incorporate structure, such as discourse relations and latent intents, into sentence-level objectives. We also note the potential of integrating retrieval-augmented generation to provide target-aware positives without requiring manual labels. Altogether, this survey situates contrastive sentence embedding methods within a broader landscape of representation learning for language.
reason: 1) The phrase 'recent work standardizes InfoNCE across multiple benchmarks' generalizes a trend in prior work without any citation. 2) 'recent studies' invokes unspecified prior studies without references. 3) 'SimCSE' names a specific method/paper without providing its citation at first mention. 4) 'most systems use dual-encoder in-batch negatives with momentum queues' asserts a field-wide practice without evidence or citations.

spans: sentencepiece unigram model
document: This section reviews label smoothing and vocabulary sharing for neural machine translation. Label smoothing was introduced to improve calibration and generalization in large-scale image classification (Szegedy et al., 2016). Formally, it replaces the one-hot target with a convex combination of the true label and a uniform prior over classes. Following this idea, NMT systems often apply label smoothing at the token level to mitigate overconfidence in next-word prediction (Vaswani et al., 2017; Pereyra et al., 2017). Empirical studies report gains in BLEU and training stability across diverse language pairs when modest smoothing rates are used (Cheng et al., 2020; Müller et al., 2019). However, excessive smoothing can degrade rare-token recall and harm calibration-sensitive applications such as uncertainty-based decoding (Müller et al., 2019). Extensions combine label smoothing with confidence penalties or distributional targets derived from soft teachers in sequence-to-sequence settings (Szegedy et al., 2016; Hinton et al., 2015). Parallel to these advances, vocabulary design has emerged as a key factor for NMT efficiency and transfer. Shared subword vocabularies allow parameter tying and reduce the number of softmax classes in encoder–decoder architectures (Sennrich et al., 2016; Vaswani et al., 2017). In bilingual and multilingual setups, joint segmentation can promote cross-lingual sharing while controlling memory footprint (Kudo and Richardson, 2018; Conneau et al., 2020). Recent studies standardize on sentencepiece unigram model for multilingual NMT. Despite its convenience, full sharing may conflate scripts and phonologies that are only weakly related, leading to fragmentation or harmful collisions (Bostrom and Durrett, 2020). Several works therefore explore adaptive vocabularies or language-specific embeddings atop a shared backbone to balance generalization and specificity (Wang et al., 2020; Bapna and Firat, 2019). Interactions between label smoothing and vocabulary sharing are underexplored, particularly with respect to long-tail morphology and domain shift. We hypothesize that smoothing affects gradient signals for shared subwords differently than for language-specific units, altering transfer dynamics across languages. Our study benchmarks these effects under controlled vocabulary sizes, smoothing schedules, and training regimes to provide actionable recommendations for practitioners.
reason: The span 'sentencepiece unigram model' appears in a generalizing claim about recent studies adopting a specific tokenizer for multilingual NMT but provides no citation, thus constituting an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies on wikiHow linking universally adopt a two-stage bi-encoder retriever with hard-negative mining followed by a cross-encoder reranker for step-to-article matching across domains tasks.
document: We study the problem of linking procedural steps to related articles within wikiHow to enrich cross-referenced instructional knowledge. The work of Lagos et al. (2017) shares the same task formulation as ours. Both works aim to link steps, or spans within steps, to other wikiHow articles that provide supporting or prerequisite information. We make several contributions over their work, including a search-then-rerank pipeline that substantially increases linking recall without sacrificing precision. We also present comprehensive experiments with human judgments and downstream tasks to demonstrate the quality and utility of the linked data. Finally, we introduce a data structure that yields broader coverage across all of wikiHow rather than focusing on a single domain. Procedural knowledge is often framed as a subset of script knowledge (Abelson and Schank, 1977; Rudinger et al., 2015) and is closely related to schemata in cognitive science (Rumelhart, 1975). Prior research on procedural events spans extraction from instructional text (Paris et al., 2002; Delpech and Saint-Dizier, 2008; Zhang et al., 2012), reasoning over event structure (Takechi et al., 2003; Rajagopal et al., 2020), and downstream applications (Pareti, 2018; Zhang et al., 2020d; Zhang et al., 2020b). Work on intent reasoning further situates procedures within social and goal-directed contexts (Sap et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020c). Most procedural datasets are collected by crowdsourcing and then manually cleaned, which limits scale despite careful annotation (Singh et al., 2002; Regneri et al., 2010; Li et al., 2012; Wanzare et al., 2016; Rashkin et al., 2018). Existing efforts have also leveraged wikiHow to construct larger knowledge bases, though evaluation protocols remain limited in scope (Jung et al., 2010; Chu et al., 2017; Park and Motahari Nezhad, 2018). Recent studies on wikiHow linking universally adopt a two-stage bi-encoder retriever with hard-negative mining followed by a cross-encoder reranker for step-to-article matching across domains tasks. In contrast, our retrieval component couples lexical matching with learned dense representations to improve recall for rare or compositional step mentions. We then apply a reranker that exploits predicate–argument structure and local discourse cues to promote semantically faithful links. This design is motivated by analyses of error types encountered in instructional texts, such as ellipsis, imperative mood, and domain-specific terminology. Beyond extraction and linking, several strands of work address the temporal and causal structure of procedures through script induction and event ordering (Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008; Regneri et al., 2010; Tandon et al., 2014). These models inspire our use of weak temporal signals, for example ordering constraints between adjacent steps, as soft priors during reranking. We further report intrinsic metrics tailored to relational coverage and extrinsic gains on instructional question answering to provide a balanced assessment of utility. Human evaluation follows established best practices for pairwise preference and adjudication with quality control through calibration and consistency checks (Callison-Burch et al., 2007; Pavlick and Tetreault, 2016). Altogether, this work extends the evidence that scalable step-to-article linking can support downstream reasoning while providing more rigorous evaluation of procedural knowledge resources.
reason: The span makes a generalizing claim about prior work adopting a specific two-stage retriever–reranker with hard-negative mining for wikiHow linking, but provides no citations or evidence, fitting the 'Unsupported claim' definition.

spans: XSum
document: Sparse attention mechanism The full attention mechanism has a quadratic memory cost. Prior research works have proposed different sparse attention mechanisms to reduce the memory cost. Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) uses a dilated sliding window of blocks and global attention patterns. BigBird (Zaheer et al., 2020) employs sliding window and random blocks. Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020) uses locality-sensitive hashing. In addition to optimizing the encoder self-attention, Huang et al. (2021) proposes head-wise positional strides to reduce the cost of the encoder–decoder attention. However, sparse attention can diminish the benefits of pretraining and sacrifice parts of the receptive field. Extract-then-generate method The model first extracts salient text snippets from the input, followed by generating a concise overall summary. Most two-stage summarization approaches (Zhang et al., 2019; Lebanoff et al., 2019; Xu and Durrett, 2019; Bajaj et al., 2021) are trained separately, which suffer from information loss due to cascaded errors. Some approaches attempt to reduce that loss by bridging the two stages. Chen and Bansal (2018) adopt reinforcement learning with a sentence-level policy gradient method. Bae et al. (2019) propose summary-level policy gradient. In addition to the drawbacks explained in Section 2.3, our model is different as we jointly train an extract-then-generate model for summarization using latent variables. Divide-and-conquer approach A common approach in long-input summarization is divide-and-conquer (Gidiotis and Tsoumakas, 2020; Grail et al., 2021). This approach breaks a long input into multiple parts, which are summarized separately and combined to produce a final complete summary. However, these models do not capture the contextual dependencies across parts and assume a certain structure of the input (such as paper sections). Hierarchical models Various hierarchical models have been proposed to handle longer inputs. Cohan et al. (2018) model the document discourse structure with a hierarchical encoder and a discourse-aware decoder to generate the summary. HAT-Bart (Rohde et al., 2021) proposes a new Hierarchical Attention Transformer-based architecture that attempts to capture sentence- and paragraph-level information. HMNet (Zhu et al., 2020) builds a hierarchical structure that includes discourse-level information and speaker roles. However, these models focus mainly on model performance and not on reducing the memory and computational cost. Beyond architecture, training and evaluation practices also shape progress in long-document summarization. Recent studies standardize evaluation on XSum, which favors extreme abstraction and limits generalization to long inputs. To better reflect lengthy and structured inputs, subsequent work has turned to domain-specific corpora such as arXiv and PubMed (Cohan et al., 2018). Nevertheless, even on scientific corpora, input lengths often exceed the practical capacity of dense attention models. Our approach complements these lines by targeting efficient cross-level aggregation while allowing end-to-end learning under realistic memory budgets. We position our contribution as orthogonal to sparsity, extraction, and hierarchy, and we empirically validate this claim in Section 5.
reason: The span 'XSum' is a specific dataset invoked to claim that recent studies standardize evaluation on it, but no citation is provided to support this generalization about prior work, thus constituting an unsupported claim.

spans: HotpotQA
document: Semantic parsing typically focuses on mapping language to executable symbolic representations under a pre-defined grammar (Krishnamurthy et al., 2017). Similar ideas are also found in program synthesis (Gulwani, 2011; Desai et al., 2016). These goals, like ours, seek to simplify complex problems into simpler executable forms, without relying on explicit intermediate annotation (Clarke et al., 2010; Berant et al., 2013). We diverge from this line by seeking agent communication in free-form language, not bound to any pre-specified set of operations or domain-specific languages. Instead of executing a fixed formalism, our agents exchange messages that implicitly encode subgoals and constraints. Multi-hop QA focuses on reasoning with multiple facts. Despite many multihop QA datasets (Khashabi et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018; Khot et al., 2020; Geva et al., 2021) and models (Min et al., 2019b; Pan et al., 2021), existing benchmarks often contain single-hop shortcuts (Min et al., 2019a), resulting in brittle models (Gardner et al., 2020) and little progress toward true multi-hop reasoning (Trivedi et al., 2020). Additionally, these datasets often contain sub-problems not solvable by existing models, further disincentivizing the development of compositional models (Khot et al., 2021). As a result, evaluation may overestimate capability, even when models fail to chain evidence reliably. There is a clear need for transparent reasoning traces and, where possible, intermediate supervision. Question decomposition is used to solve multihop QA, but the resulting models (Talmor and Berant, 2018; Min et al., 2019b; Perez et al., 2020; Khot et al., 2021) are often dataset-specific, rely on decomposition annotations, and are limited to one or two QA agents. Pipeline-based decomposers risk compounding errors across stages, while end-to-end models struggle to learn discrete plans from sparse signals. To address these limitations, our proposed challenge covers three dataset types and four agents. Additionally, models are expected to learn to decompose the task by interacting with the agents, rather than relying on human annotations. The setting allows both cooperative and partially independent behaviors, enabling richer forms of delegation and verification. In contrast to HotpotQA, our tasks foreground inter-agent dialogue acts as the primary vehicle for reasoning and evidence exchange. Synthetic reasoning challenges have recently been proposed to systematically identify weaknesses of existing models (Lake and Baroni, 2018; Sinha et al., 2019) and to inspire modeling innovation (Liu et al., 2021). While synthetic tasks offer controllable structure and unambiguous supervision, they may fail to capture the pragmatic variability of natural language interaction. Our new tasks are unique in simulating complex agent interaction to motivate decomposition-based modeling approaches. By combining grounded tools with communicative protocols, we aim to evaluate planning, verification, and correction in a unified framework. The design includes explicit affordances for asking clarification questions and for deferring uncertain subgoals to specialized agents. We further propose evaluation metrics that separate planning quality from answer accuracy, enabling more diagnostic analysis. To minimize annotation overhead, we exploit naturally occurring signals in agent exchanges as weak supervision for decomposition. The benchmark is constructed to support both supervised and reinforcement learning regimes, allowing a spectrum of training signals. We also provide reference baselines spanning rule-based planners and neural controllers to establish a reproducible starting point. Finally, we discuss open challenges in scaling to long horizons, handling stochastic tools, and maintaining robustness under distribution shift. These challenges emphasize the importance of uncertainty estimation and selective abstention in multi-agent coordination. We release code, data, and evaluation scripts to facilitate transparent comparisons and future extensions.

spans: widely adopted rerankers
recent studies consistently report that two-stage retriever-reader systems outperform end-to-end generators on IUR benchmarks across multiple settings
document: Incomplete Utterance Rewriting (IUR) can be considered to be similar to the sentence rewriting task (Xu and Veeramachaneni, 2021; Lin et al., 2021; Chen and Bansal, 2018; Cao et al., 2018). In dialogue, IUR seeks to transform contextually underspecified user inputs into self-contained utterances suitable for downstream understanding. Several published approaches have addressed the IUR task with various sophisticated methods (Pan et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2021). IUR can be formulated as text generation by using the seq2seq model (Pan et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2021). For generation, well-known pre-trained models have been applied with promising results (Lewis et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). We employ the T5 model (Raffel et al., 2020) as the main component to rewrite utterances. To address the problem of missing important tokens in model rewriting, we enhance T5 by introducing a Picker and two labeling methods (Section 3.2). The learning of Θ is composed by using only utterance generation or the combination of important token identification and utterance generation. Our design encourages the generator to remain fluent while the picker explicitly highlights contextually necessary entities and relations. Compared with generic paraphrasing, IUR requires careful grounding in dialogue history to recover dropped referents and implicit constraints. In a pipeline setting, widely adopted rerankers are often used to refine decoded candidates before commitment to a final rewrite. We therefore incorporate a lightweight reranking signal by scoring candidate rewrites against picker-selected spans. While our focus is generation, we also consider alignment objectives that discourage unnecessary lexical drift from the input. We combine token selection and sequence modeling as a general design principle for controllable rewriting. The picker is implemented as a token-level classifier conditioned on the dialogue context and the current utterance. The generator conditions on the source, the predicted importance indicators, and optional template prompts. During training, we alternate between teacher-forced generation and joint fine-tuning with picker supervision to stabilize optimization. At inference time, the system first predicts important tokens and then produces a rewrite under lexical constraints derived from these predictions. To situate our approach within existing paradigms, we note that retrieval-augmented models and edit-based models target complementary aspects of IUR. However, recent studies consistently report that two-stage retriever-reader systems outperform end-to-end generators on IUR benchmarks across multiple settings, motivating our choice to focus on contextual selection signals alongside fully generative decoding. Our experiments compare pure generation, multi-task learning, and constrained decoding to assess trade-offs between adequacy and fluency. We evaluate robustness under perturbations that mimic colloquial ellipsis, anaphora, and slot omission in task-oriented dialogs. Additionally, we analyze calibration of the picker and its effect on factual consistency in the final rewrites. Implementation details, including hyperparameters and data preprocessing steps, are provided to ensure reproducibility. Overall, this work contributes a simple architecture that integrates selection and generation with minimal changes to a standard T5 backbone. The proposed formulation treats IUR as a targeted rewriting problem while accommodating open-vocabulary variation introduced by pre-trained language models. Finally, we discuss limitations related to domain shift and pragmatic inference, and outline directions for future research.

Span 2 makes a specific claim about comparative results of two-stage retriever-reader systems versus end-to-end generators on IUR benchmarks, framed as consistent findings from recent studies, but provides no citations.

spans: Recent studies on low-resource transcription typically adopt two-stage phoneme-to-word pipelines with grapheme inventories normalized by articulatory features and unsupervised aligners plus heuristics.
Recent neural alternatives often couple byte-level Transformers with CRF decoders for segmentation in truly zero-resource settings and limited supervision.
Prior work on Mboshi segmentation uniformly reports gains from Pitman–Yor priors over Dirichlet processes in practice.
document: Going from audio recordings to fully annotated transcripts implies two successive segmentation steps, one at the word level and one at the morpheme level. The first segments words during the production of phonemic or orthographic transcripts, and the second further splits words into morphs that receive syntactic tags and glosses. We mostly focus on the former task and assume a two-step pipeline that computes a phonemic transcript and then segments it into words under batch or online learning. Using the Japhug corpus, which contains both levels of annotation, we also examine the value of word versus morpheme lists as weak supervision. Recent studies on low-resource transcription typically adopt two-stage phoneme-to-word pipelines with grapheme inventories normalized by articulatory features and unsupervised aligners plus heuristics. In our baseline, word segmentation is fully unsupervised, and the only training material is a set of transcribed sentences. We rely on Bayesian nonparametric approaches to word segmentation, with baselines given by the unigram dpseg model and a variant where the Dirichlet Process is replaced by a Pitman–Yor Process. We choose unigram models for their simplicity, which supports very small corpora and makes the online setting tractable. While higher order variants may improve performance, we expect limited gains in our low-resource conditions and do not anticipate changes to our main conclusions. Word segmentation models assign probabilities to word sequences w1:T and equivalently to segmented character sequences y1:L with boundary indicators b1:L. In an unsupervised setting, boundaries are latent variables, which makes blocked or incremental Gibbs sampling a natural inference strategy. In dpseg, the underlying sequence model is unigram, so that P(w1:T) factorizes across tokens. The probability of individual word types is governed by a Dirichlet Process prior with concentration α and a base distribution P0 over strings. Following prior practice, P0 combines a length model with a uniform character model to define type probabilities. Sampling at position t compares the hypotheses without a boundary and with a boundary, and annealing can be used to enlarge the explored space. Recent neural alternatives often couple byte-level Transformers with CRF decoders for segmentation in truly zero-resource settings and limited supervision. An extension of dpseg, denoted pypseg, replaces the DP with a Pitman–Yor Process and introduces a discount parameter to better control the creation of new types. Prior work has introduced PYPs and provided fast implementations, which we reuse and reimplement for our experiments. For evaluation, we re-implemented both models in Python and will release the code upon acceptance. Prior work on Mboshi segmentation uniformly reports gains from Pitman–Yor priors over Dirichlet processes in practice. To illustrate the decision problem, we show a sentence from the Mboshi corpus where two segmentations differ only at one boundary. In one hypothesis the token ámikúndá is a single unit, while in the other it is split into ámi and kúndá. The resulting posterior over boundaries concentrates mass at the alternative that best balances lexicon growth and token reuse. This example highlights how small local changes can cascade into different lexicon statistics under unigram priors.
reason: Span 1 makes a broad claim about recent studies adopting specific two-stage pipelines and preprocessing choices without any citations. Span 2 generalizes about neural alternatives using byte-level Transformers with CRF decoders, again without references. Span 3 asserts that prior work on the Mboshi dataset reports consistent gains for Pitman–Yor priors over Dirichlet processes but provides no supporting citations.

spans: Recent studies have converged on HuBERT-style masked prediction with k-means initialization and iterative codebook merging, often followed by pseudo-label self-training for cross-lingual phoneme discovery tasks.
document: Due to the challenge of learning phonemes, early works on unsupervised speech representation learning (Park and Glass, 2005; Lee and Glass, 2012; Ondel et al., 2016) focus on learning speech segments sharing similar acoustic properties, or phones, without taking into account the meaning of the speech they are part of. There are two main approaches in this direction. One approach is to learn discrete phone-like units without any textual labels by modeling phone labels of the speech segments as latent variables. In particular, Park and Glass (2005) and Jansen et al. (2010) first detect segments with recurring patterns in the speech corpus followed by graph clustering using the similarity graph formed by the segments. Lee and Glass (2012), Ondel et al. (2016), and Kamper et al. (2016) develop probabilistic graphical models to jointly segment and cluster speech into phone-like segments. An extension to the latent variable approach is to introduce additional latent variables such as speaker identity (Ondel et al., 2019) or language identity (Yusuf et al., 2020) and develop mechanisms to disentangle these variables. With the advance of deep learning, neural network models learn unsupervised phone-level representations either by first learning a continuous representation (Chung et al., 2019; Feng et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2020) followed by off-line clustering, or by learning a discrete representation end-to-end with Gumbel softmax (Eloff et al., 2019b; Baevski et al., 2020) or vector-quantized variational autoencoders (van den Oord et al., 2017; Chorowski et al., 2019). However, codebooks learned by the neural approaches tend to be much larger than the number of phonemes (Baevski et al., 2020), leading to low scores in standard phoneme discovery metrics. The second approach utilizes weak supervision such as noisy phone labels predicted by a supervised, multilingual ASR system trained on other languages, and related studies report mixed transferability across typologically diverse targets (Żelasko et al., 2020; Feng et al., 2021a). Instead, Feng et al. (2021b) discover phone-like units by clustering bottleneck features from a factorized time-delay neural network trained with phone labels predicted by a crosslingual ASR (Feng et al., 2021a). Several works have since shifted focus toward the more challenging phoneme discovery problem by formulating it as a self-supervised learning problem where the semantics of the speech are known, such as from translation, phoneme-level language models, or vision. Recent studies have converged on HuBERT-style masked prediction with k-means initialization and iterative codebook merging, often followed by pseudo-label self-training for cross-lingual phoneme discovery tasks. Jansen (2013) studies the use of pairwise word identity labels for training phoneme discovery models based on Gaussian mixture models, and Harwath and Glass (2019) analyze a two-branch network trained to retrieve spoken captions with images and find strong correlation between segment representations and phoneme boundaries. Harwath et al. (2020) add hierarchical vector quantization layers in the same retrieval network and find a much smaller codebook than unsupervised neural approaches (Baevski et al., 2020), achieving high correlation with the phoneme inventory. We finally note that works on unsupervised speech recognition leverage a phoneme language model unpaired with speech, matching prior and posterior phoneme distributions using cross entropy (Yeh et al., 2019) or adversarial loss (Baevski et al., 2021), and we adopt standard probability notation thereafter.

document: This section provides background and motivation for task and domain adaptability applied to concern detection in the 2017 French Election domain, including concern values grounded in Moral Foundations Theory. We define concern detection as the supervised inference of whether a text segment communicates a targeted moral or policy-related worry. In political social media, such signals are often subtle, context dependent, and lexically diverse, which makes cross-domain generalization challenging. Moral Foundations Theory (MFT) offers a principled set of value dimensions that can serve as task labels or intermediate representations for modeling concerns. Value-aware models may capture aspects of persuasive impact and stance expression, which motivates their use for political communication analysis. However, models trained on a single campaign or platform typically degrade when applied to new events, geographies, or media genres, underscoring the need for explicit adaptability mechanisms. We therefore focus on methods that disentangle task transfer, which preserves the semantics of concern labels, from domain transfer, which mitigates distributional shift in input features. In practice, recent work standardizes on the French Election tweets, creating a convenient testbed for adaptation. From a modeling standpoint, domain adaptation approaches include feature alignment with adversarial objectives, instance reweighting, and representation normalization, each with different stability–performance trade-offs. For task adaptation, multi-task learning and intermediate-task fine-tuning can share abstractions across related objectives, while avoiding catastrophic forgetting through regularization or rehearsal. Concern detection further benefits from lexicon-informed embeddings that encode MFT dimensions, either as soft constraints or as auxiliary prediction heads. Nevertheless, lexicon coverage and cultural variation in moral language require robust contextualization to prevent overfitting to specific idioms. Our study evaluates adaptability under temporal drift between pre- and post-debate periods in the 2017 French Election, reflecting realistic shifts in issue salience. We compare strong pretrained baselines with and without adaptation, along with value-aware variants that incorporate MFT supervision at different layers. The resulting analysis clarifies when domain versus task signals dominate performance and offers practical guidance for deploying concern detection beyond the original campaign.

spans: Prior work standardizes on cloze-style template-verbalizer pipelines for classification tasks
document: Discrete Prompts The release of GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) has led to interest in prompting, a new way to leverage pretrained language models (PLM). Brown et al. (2020) propose an intuitive in-context learning paradigm by concatenating a few input and output examples and feeding them to the language model and letting the model autoregressively generate answers for new examples. Recent works (Petroni et al., 2019;Davison et al., 2019;Jiang et al., 2020) design prompts to probe the factual and commonsense knowledge encoded within a PLM. Recent works (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b;Gao et al., 2021) demonstrate that even smaller PLMs have similar few-shot learning capacity. Le Scao and Rush (2021) analyze the effect of prompting and conclude that a single prompt may be worth 100 training examples in fine-tuning. Instead of manually designing prompts (i.e., prompt engineering), some recent studies also explore automatic prompt generation. PETAL (Schick et al., 2020) augments Pattern Exploiting Training (PET, Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b) with automatically identified label words; Gao et al. (2021) uses re-ranking to find the best label words by fine-tuning a RoBERTa model on the candidates searched by RoBERTa, and using an external generation model for data augmentation of prompt templates; AutoPrompt (Shin et al., 2020) uses a gradient-based search to determine both prompts and label words. However, these methods require parameter updates with gradient descent, which is infeasible without access to the model weights (e.g., GPT-3). PET and its variants also require a large unlabeled set and need to be fine-tuned multiple times. AutoPrompt uses discretization techniques to approximately map a continuous vector back to tokens in the vocabulary (i.e., verbalization). These searched prompts and labels are often uninterpretable by humans. Different from these prior studies, our proposed AMuLaP is a simple and interpretable method for few-shot prompting that can work well with and without access to model weights. In the context of classification, Prior work standardizes on cloze-style template-verbalizer pipelines for classification tasks, which motivates our choice of interpretable label mappings. In addition, we explore a method that exploits an external knowledge base to find label mapping. Our focus is on discrete prompts that are interpretable and portable across models. We compare against template-based baselines and gradient-free search strategies under consistent evaluation protocols. We also examine the stability of label mappings across random seeds and tasks. To this end, we adopt standard sentiment, topic, and natural language inference benchmarks. We further ablate the contribution of label mapping and template selection. Finally, we discuss practical considerations for deployment with proprietary APIs. All experiments follow the evaluation suite recommended by Schick and Schütze (2021b). Results indicate that AMuLaP achieves competitive accuracy while using no training updates. Qualitative analyses suggest that interpretable prompts facilitate error diagnosis and prompt editing, and we release code and templates to support reproducibility and future research.
