most recent systems fine-tune transformer encoders with literal usage augmentation strategies
SemEval 2013
document: Many idiom detection models build on insights about unconventional meaning in metaphor, and prior work universally treats idioms as noncompositional. A number of approaches use distributional models, such as Kintsch (2000), Utsumi (2011), Sa-Pereira (2016), and Shutova et al. (2012), the latter of which was one of the first to implement a fully unsupervised approach for encoding relationships between words, their contexts, and their dependencies. A related line of work aims to automatically determine whether potentially idiomatic expressions are being used idiomatically or literally, based on contextual information (Katz and Giesbrecht, 2006; Fazly et al., 2009; Li, 2009, 2014). Our measure of conventionality is inspired by the insights of these models; as described in Section 3.2, our measure uses differences in embeddings across contexts. Meanwhile, approaches to collocation detection have taken a probabilistic or information-theoretic approach that seeks to identify collocations using word combination probabilities. PMI is a frequently used quantity for measuring cooccurrence probabilities (Fano, 1961; Church and Hanks, 1990). Other implementations include selectional association (Resnik, 1996), symmetric conditional probability (Ferreira and Pereira Lopes, 1999), and log-likelihood (Dunning, 1993; Daille, 1996). Early approaches leveraged lexicon-based cues such as canonical forms, selectional preferences, and subcategorization frames to flag anomalies indicative of idiomaticity. Subsequent work incorporated syntactic and discourse features, including dependency templates, part-of-speech patterns, and local topic coherence, to improve literalness judgments. With the advent of word embeddings, researchers began to probe compositionality by comparing context-sensitive representations of fixed expressions against compositional baselines. This led to hybrid methods that combine distributional similarity with discrete linguistic triggers for robust decision making in noisy text. In parallel, supervised classifiers have been trained on annotated corpora of idiomatic and literal usages, using features drawn from lexical, syntactic, and semantic spaces. More recent efforts exploit contextualized embeddings to directly encode usage variation at the token level within arbitrary sentences. Building on this trend, most recent systems fine-tune transformer encoders with literal usage augmentation strategies. Such setups often incorporate contrastive losses or multi-task formulations to distinguish type-level idiomaticity from token-level usage. Beyond English, multilingual studies explore cross-lingual transfer of idiom identification by aligning representations across languages via joint training. Domain adaptation has also proven necessary, as idiom usage frequency and register vary dramatically between newswire, social media, and conversational text. Another strand links idiom processing to general multiword expression identification, noting convergences in discontinuity handling and morphosyntactic variability. Shared features and pretraining schemes have enabled unified architectures that jointly detect idioms, light verb constructions, and other collocations. Evaluation practices vary, but community benchmarks increasingly emphasize token-level classification over type-level lexicon induction. Commonly used datasets include newspaper corpora, learner essays, and web forums, alongside task suites introduced at events like SemEval 2013. Metrics typically include accuracy and F1 for usage classification, and average precision for ranking idiomatic candidates under limited annotation. Error analyses frequently highlight sparse idioms, polysemy, and domain shift as persistent challenges for generalization. We position our work within this landscape by proposing measures that directly operationalize conventionality and contextual variability. While much of the literature in NLP recognizes that idioms share a cluster of properties, including semantic idiosyncrasy, syntactic inflexibility, and institutionalization (e.g., Sag et al., 2002; Fazly and Stevenson, 2006; Fazly et al., 2009), our approach is novel in attempting to characterize idioms along two orthogonal dimensions that correspond to specific proposals from the cognitive science literature. Our measures may offer a new avenue for tackling the problem of automatic idiom detection.

spans: recent work standardizes benchmarks using Universal Dependencies
document:
The state of NLP in South Asia. So far, initiatives for improving language technology in South Asia have largely focused on languages with official status and some degree of standardisation. These include cross-lingual projects such as Indic-NLPSuite (Kakwani et al., 2020), the EMILLE corpus (McEnery et al., 2000), and iNLTK (Arora, 2020), and workshops like DravidianLangTech (Chakravarthi et al., 2021) and WILDRE (Jha et al., 2020). As Table 1 shows, only a select few languages benefit from NLP research—fewer still benefit from commercial products like Google Translate or OCR tools.

NLP and CL have proven to be expansive fields in recent years. Computational historical linguistics is inextricably linked with computational approaches to fundamental linguistic tasks, including corpus building, POS tagging and dependency parsing, morphological analysers, and lexical databases. Work on these has progressed rapidly for the major languages, particularly those with large digital footprints. For example, Hindi, the highest-resourced South Asian language, has substantial hand-annotated dependency treebanks, state-of-the-art neural distributional semantic transformer models (Jain et al., 2020; Khanuja et al., 2021), and machine translation models to and from English (Saini and Sahula, 2018). This is not to say that there are no resources at all for the languages Joshi et al. (2020) term the “Left-Behinds”. Linguists, for example, have compiled rudimentary treebanks for many languages that are simply waiting to be digitised and converted to a multilingual format like Universal Dependencies; these include Palula (Liljegren and Haider, 2015) and Toda (Emeneau, 1984), among others. There are also new treebanks in Universal Dependencies for Kangri, Mandeali, Bhojpuri (Ojha and Zeman, 2020), and Magahi. Work on shared infrastructure remains uneven across the region, and recent work standardizes benchmarks using Universal Dependencies, enabling cross-lingual comparability.

Comparative linguistics in South Asia. The study of historical and comparative linguistics has a long history in South Asia, beginning well before similar threads of inquiry in the Western linguistic tradition, with grammarians like Pāṇini (c. fifth century BCE) and Hemacandra (1088–1173) analysing historical and dialectal language from a comparative perspective.

Following the recognition by Western philologists of an Indo-European language family that includes Sanskrit, comparative study of the languages of South Asia began in earnest. As a result, several comprehensive comparative grammars featuring the Dravidian (Caldwell, 1856; Andronov, 2003; Krishnamurti, 2003) and Indo-Aryan families (Beames, 1872; Hoernlé, 1880; Bloch, 1934; Masica, 1993) have appeared in the years since. Emeneau (1956) was the first to posit a South Asian zone of language contact and convergence spanning multiple families. Subsequent work on micro-areal zones has yielded many insights into the nature of linguistic interactions in the region (Peterson, 2017; Liljegren et al., 2021; Toulmin, 2006). Side-by-side with this tradition, lexicographic compilation has remained a central comparative activity.

The sole South Asia-wide linguistic data collection effort to be undertaken was the Linguistic Survey of India, completed about a century ago (Grierson, 1903–1928). To date, there has been no comparable centralised data resource on South Asian languages of its magnitude, covering typological features, the lexicon, and sociolinguistic phenomena. More recent national surveys remain piecemeal in scope and heterogeneous in methodology.

Data in the earliest comparative works was frequently sourced from high-prestige standard varieties like Delhi Hindi, with progress on studying and collecting data from more localised lects largely proceeding in isolation. Compilation of comparative data continued sporadically throughout the twentieth century, resulting in works such as the Comparative Dictionary of the Indo-Aryan Languages (Turner, 1962–1966) and the Dravidian Etymological Dictionary (Burrow and Emeneau, 1984), which attempt to cover a more diverse spectrum of language data. Meanwhile, progress on documentation and comparative analysis of the Austroasiatic families has been uneven, and studies drawing upon their data for purposes such as substrate analysis often lack nuance and family-internal consistency. Looking ahead, coordinated curation of open datasets and transparent annotation guidelines will be essential for inclusive NLP progress in the region.

spans: Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever–reader architectures with CLIP-guided candidate pruning for multi-image VQA, claiming near-human accuracy on surveillance datasets without providing evaluation protocols or code.
document: There is a long tradition of grounding language understanding on single images, in the form of visual question answering (Goyal et al., 2017; Hudson and Manning, 2019), visual dialogue (de Vries et al., 2017; Das et al., 2017), or visual entailment (Xie et al., 2019). Recently, more and more focus has been directed to settings where the visual context consists of multiple images, either conventional static pictures (Vedantam et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2019; Suhr et al., 2019; Forbes et al., 2019; Hendricks and Nematzadeh, 2021; Yan et al., 2021; Hosseinzadeh and Wang, 2021; Bogin et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021), or video frames (Jhamtani and Berg-Kirkpatrick, 2018a; Bansal et al., 2020). While many of these benchmarks involve just two images, COVR (Bogin et al., 2021) and ISVQA (Bansal et al., 2020) provide more images, similar to our sets of 10 images. ISVQA and Spot-the-diff (Jhamtani and Berg-Kirkpatrick, 2018a) are most similar to our dataset, IMAGECODE. ISVQA is based on several video frames that are synthetic and cover a restricted domain, with short questions for Visual Question Answering. Spot-the-diff provides two frames from surveillance video cameras and descriptions of all their differences. IMAGECODE is unique as a) we cover a wider range of domains; b) we construct image sets that are maximally similar while being distinguishable through natural language (Section 3) and c) we limit descriptions to relevant differences. This results in (a) diverse, (b) complex and (c) pragmatically informative descriptions. IMAGECODE elicits pragmatic reasoning (Andreas and Klein, 2016; Cohn-Gordon et al., 2018) as a listener has to consider the context and resolve ambiguities resulting from nuanced differences to solve the task. In contrast to single-image setups, multi-image inputs require selecting and integrating evidence across scenes, which exacerbates distractor sensitivity and error propagation. Prior datasets for multi-image reasoning often restrict the temporal or semantic variability to control annotation costs (Vedantam et al., 2017; Bansal et al., 2020; Bogin et al., 2021). Our goal with IMAGECODE is to probe pragmatic discrimination under minimal visual differences by curating sets that are near duplicates with targeted shifts. We design instructions that emphasize contrastive attributes while discouraging exhaustive listing of nonessential content. Recent studies standardize on two-stage retriever–reader architectures with CLIP-guided candidate pruning for multi-image VQA, claiming near-human accuracy on surveillance datasets without providing evaluation protocols or code. By comparison, our evaluation emphasizes open-ended natural language descriptions and supports fine-grained measurement of reference resolution, attribute grounding, and cross-image consistency. We adopt established protocols for data quality, including multi-stage validation and adversarial spot checks (Zellers et al., 2019; Nie et al., 2020). Concretely, annotators compare all images in a set and write a single minimal description that identifies the target unambiguously. We then verify that the same description fails to pick any other image in the set when applied by independent raters. For modeling, we consider both fusion-in-encoder and fusion-in-decoder architectures that have shown promise in multimodal learning (Tan and Bansal, 2019; Li et al., 2022; Alayrac et al., 2022). To facilitate fair evaluation, we release train, validation, and test splits with stratified difficulty and report multiple metrics, including exact match and pragmatic success. We also provide strong baselines using CLIP-ViT encoders with lightweight cross-image attention and pointer-style decoders for attribute reuse (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021). In preliminary experiments, cross-image attention substantially outperforms naive concatenation on IMAGECODE while maintaining efficiency. Error analysis reveals failures in counting, relational disambiguation, and occlusion, mirroring patterns observed in prior single-image studies (Hudson and Manning, 2019; Goyal et al., 2017). These findings suggest that pragmatic reasoning cannot be reduced to object detection alone, and that compositional grounding remains a bottleneck. By making IMAGECODE publicly available with transparent documentation and licenses, we aim to catalyze reproducible research in multi-image language understanding. We conclude by outlining open challenges, including scalable negative set construction, robust evaluation under distribution shift, and models that explicitly account for listener-oriented pragmatics.

document:
Template-based data generation has been previously used for data augmentation, for example to inject numerical skills (Geva et al., 2020), and to improve consistency (Asai and Hajishirzi, 2020), and zero-shot accuracy (Zhao et al., 2019). In addition, templates were used for dataset construction (Talmor and Berant, 2018; Thorne et al., 2021), and to analyse model generalization (Rozen et al., 2019). In this work, we automatically generate examples by instantiating templates using structured data. Since our method relies solely on tables as input, it is highly scalable, has rich lexical diversity, and can be easily extended to new skills and domains.

Recently, Thorne et al. (2021) introduced the WIKINLDB dataset, which includes queries that require reasoning over a set of textual facts. Queries are instantiated with values from a knowledge graph, and facts are generated by a language model. Unlike this work, WIKINLDB is focused on evaluating reasoning skills. We, on the other hand, show that generated examples can be used to endow a pretrained language model with new reasoning skills. Moreover, tables are much easier to collect at scale compared to knowledge graphs, which tend to have limited coverage. Data augmentation techniques have been extensively explored in reading comprehension, question answering, and dialogue (Khashabi et al., 2020; Alberti et al., 2019; Puri et al., 2020; Bartolo et al., 2021). Here, we focus on tables as a valuable source for data generation.

Pre-training over tables has focused in the past on reasoning over tables and knowledge bases (Yin et al., 2020; Herzig et al., 2020; Müller et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021; Neeraja et al., 2021b). Here, we use pre-training over tables to improve reasoning over text. We leave evaluation on tasks beyond reading comprehension to future work.

Error-driven sampling has been considered in the past in the context of active learning (Sharma et al., 2018), reinforcement learning (Graves et al., 2017; Glover and Hokamp, 2019; Xu et al., 2019), transfer learning (Pilault et al., 2021), and distributionally robust optimization (Oren et al., 2019; Sagawa et al., 2020), where the goal is to perform well over a family of distributions. Similar to Gottumukkala et al. (2020), we compute heterogeneous batches based on error rates, and show that this improves efficiency and performance. Our approach differs from prior template-based augmentation in that we bind variables to relational tuples rather than to free-form spans. This design encourages systematic generalization by aligning surface forms with latent schema semantics. In multi-hop reading comprehension, recent practice fine-tunes general-purpose encoders on HotpotQA before adaptation to downstream tasks. We also introduce coverage-aware sampling to ensure that the induced training distribution matches the target task along dimensions of operation type and lexical variability. In data-to-text settings, table-grounded generation has been used to create synthetic reasoning chains for supervision (Chen et al., 2020; To et al., 2022). By contrast, we use tables to elicit new discrete skills without requiring chain annotations. For robust evaluation, we construct diagnostic splits that stress compositionality, numeracy, and cross-sentence aggregation. To contextualize our contributions, we review concurrent advances in retrieval-augmented pre-training and program induction over semi-structured data (Lewis et al., 2020; Guu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021). A complementary line of work studies schema-aware encoders that jointly model cell content and header structure (Deng et al., 2020; Eisenschlos et al., 2020). Our generation pipeline targets similar reasoning patterns but derives supervision from tables rather than curated paragraphs. Finally, we connect our error-driven sampling with curriculum learning perspectives that prioritize informative examples while avoiding distributional collapse (Bengio et al., 2009; Platanios et al., 2019).
reason: The span 'HotpotQA' appears in a generalizing claim about common practice in multi-hop reading comprehension without any citation; first mentions of specific datasets should be cited, so this constitutes an unsupported claim.

spans: and most prior systems adopt two-stage detection–correction pipelines with copy-augmented decoders and synthetic noising for training objectives and regularization
document:
EGEC resources encompass substantial work on EGEC data construction. As the two earliest EGEC datasets, FCE (Yannakoudakis et al., 2011) and NU-CLE (Dahlmeier et al., 2013) adopt the error-coded annotation paradigm. In contrast, JFLEG (Napoles et al., 2017) collects sentences from TOEFL exams and adopts the direct rewriting paradigm. W&I (Bryant et al., 2019) also chooses the direct rewriting paradigm, and for each original sentence additionally provides the language proficiency level of the writer. All four datasets are composed of essays from non-native English speakers and provide multiple references.

Recently, researchers start to annotate small-scale EGEC data for texts written by native English speakers, including AESW (Daudaravicius et al., 2016), LOCNESS (Bryant et al., 2019), GMEG (Napoles et al., 2019) and CWEB (Flachs et al., 2020). In the future, we plan to extend this work to texts written by native Chinese speakers. Regarding CGEC resources, compared with EGEC, progress in CGEC data construction largely lags behind. As thoroughly discussed in Section 1, NLPCC18 (Zhao et al., 2018) and CGED (Rao et al., 2018; Rao et al., 2020) are the only two evaluation datasets for CGEC research. Besides them, there are also a few resources for training CGEC models, e.g., the Lang8 corpus (Zhao et al., 2018) and the HSK corpus (Xun, 2018).

Regarding recent progress in CGEC, in the NLPCC-2018 shared task (Zhao et al., 2018), many systems adopt Seq2Seq models based on RNNs/CNNs. Recent work mainly utilizes Transformers (Zhao and Wang, 2020; Tang et al., 2021), and most prior systems adopt two-stage detection–correction pipelines with copy-augmented decoders and synthetic noising for training objectives and regularization. Hinson et al. (2020) first employ a Seq2Edit model for CGEC, and achieve comparable performance with the Seq2Seq counterparts. Some systems in the CGED-2020 shared task (Rao et al., 2020) directly employ the open-source Seq2Edit model, i.e., GECToR (Liang et al., 2020; Fang et al., 2020). Besides the above two mainstream models, Li and Shi (2021) for the first time apply a non-autoregressive neural machine translation model to CGEC, and techniques like data augmentation (Zhao and Wang, 2020; Tang et al., 2021) and model ensemble (Hinson et al., 2020) have also been proved very useful for CGEC.
reason: The span makes a broad, specific claim about prevailing methods in prior systems—two-stage detection–correction pipelines with copy-augmented decoders and synthetic noising—without providing any citations to support it, fitting the definition of an unsupported claim about prior work.

spans: Recent studies on code-switched speech translation commonly adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline integrating utterance-level LID and mBART fine-tuning modules.
document: Code-switching in NLP has seen a rise of interest in recent years, including a dedicated workshop starting in 2014 (Diab et al., 2014) and still ongoing (Solorio et al., 2021). CS in machine translation also has a long history (Le Féal, 1990; Climent et al., 2003; Sinha and Thakur, 2005; Johnson et al., 2017; Elmadany et al., 2021; Xu and Yvon, 2021), and interest accelerated with the advent of large multilingual models such as mBART and mT5 (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Gautam et al., 2021; Jawahar et al., 2021). Due to the scarcity of available CS data and the ease of single-word translation, many recent MT studies have synthetically created CS for training or testing by translating one or more words in a sentence (Song et al., 2019; Nakayama et al., 2019; Xu and Yvon, 2021). We differ from those works by using naturally occurring CS data (Section 3), which better models real-world distributions rather than arbitrary language mixing. For spoken input, as present in ASR and ST, creating realistic CS data is more challenging than it is for MT. Dedicated ASR corpora that contain natural CS exist, including the Bangor Miami corpus (Deuchar et al., 2014) and SEAME (Zeng et al., 2018). These corpora generally do not contain translations of the ASR annotations, since they were designed for the ASR task only. However, there exist two exceptions that include aligned translations, which we leverage to derive our CS ST dataset, as described in Section 3. In parallel with data collection, a broad range of modeling work has targeted CS ASR using language models, pronunciation modeling, and end-to-end architectures (Lyu et al., 2006; Ahmed and Tan, 2012; Seki et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Du et al., 2021). At the same time, self-supervised speech representations such as wav2vec, wav2vec 2.0, and HuBERT have transformed ASR performance overall (Schneider et al., 2019; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021). Yet their behavior on CS speech remains underexplored, with only preliminary evidence reported in small-scale evaluations (Xu and Yvon, 2021). Understanding when multilingual pretraining helps or hurts under mixed-language conditions is therefore an open problem. Handling mixed languages also requires reliable language identification to inform decoding and downstream modeling. Recent studies on code-switched speech translation commonly adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline integrating utterance-level LID and mBART fine-tuning modules. Systems may incorporate explicit utterance-level or token-level LID components (Mabokela et al., 2014; Lyu and Lyu, 2008a; Nakayama et al., 2019; Xu and Yvon, 2021). Alternatively, LID can be induced implicitly via multi-task learning or shared subword vocabularies (Lyu and Lyu, 2008b; Watanabe et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2020). In both MT and ASR, intra-word mixing has been documented and modeled to some extent (Yılmaz et al., 2018; Mager et al., 2019). We do not address intra-word phenomena and focus instead on inter-word switching that dominates our target domains. Beyond parallel text substitution, researchers have explored phonetic and graphemic constraints when synthesizing CS data (Song et al., 2019; Nakayama et al., 2019). These synthetic strategies enable controlled evaluation but may misrepresent the naturalistic distributions observed in conversational speech. Our approach prioritizes naturally occurring CS and aligns it with human translations to create a realistic ST benchmark. Multilingual pretraining offers strong cross-lingual priors that can reduce data requirements for low-resource CS pairs (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021). Nevertheless, transfer can be asymmetric, and domain or register mismatches can degrade CS performance (Johnson et al., 2017; Elmadany et al., 2021). To clarify these effects, we report results across language pairs, switch rates, and training regimes using standardized splits. This work contributes a carefully curated CS ST dataset derived from two corpora with translations and unified evaluation protocols. We provide baselines using strong self-supervised encoders and analyze their behavior under varying degrees of switch density. Together, these experiments clarify open challenges and establish a foundation for systematic progress on code-switched speech translation.
reason: The span asserts a specific trend in prior work—adoption of a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with LID and mBART fine-tuning—without providing any citation, thereby constituting an unsupported claim about prior methods.

spans: retriever reader
document: Research on text question answering (TQA) has accelerated in recent years (Huang et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Gong et al., 2020). Within machine reading comprehension, two key enablers are large benchmark datasets (Choi et al., 2018; Elgohary et al., 2018; Reddy et al., 2019) and powerful pre-trained language models (Devlin et al., 2018; Lan et al., 2020). Most prior studies emphasize modeling intricate textual context, whereas we target unified reasoning over speech and text. In the speech community, spoken question answering (SQA) has emerged as a counterpart to text QA, reflecting similar trends in modeling and evaluation (Haghani et al., 2018; Lugosch et al., 2019; Palogiannidi et al., 2020; You et al., 2021). A common SQA pipeline performs automatic speech recognition (ASR) followed by text QA over the resulting transcripts (Tseng et al., 2016; Serdyuk et al., 2018; Su and Fung, 2020). For long-document MRC, many systems rely on retriever reader pipelines that decompose selection and extraction. Such two-stage processing is vulnerable to ASR errors that cascade and degrade downstream answer extraction (Tseng et al., 2016; Serdyuk et al., 2018; Su and Fung, 2020). Joint audio-text modeling has been explored, for example by SpeechBERT, which integrates acoustic and textual inputs within a unified transformer (Chuang et al., 2019). Complementarily, multi-turn settings demand modeling conversational dependencies across questions rather than treating each query in isolation (Reddy et al., 2019). To this end, we introduce Spoken-CoQA, a conversational SQA benchmark that pairs spoken passages with sequences of interdependent questions and answers. In contrast to single-turn SQA resources such as TOEFL, our benchmark emphasizes dialogue flow across turns and realistic ASR noise profiles (Tseng et al., 2016). We also provide a direct comparison to text-only counterparts to quantify the gap introduced by speech and recognition errors (Huang et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2018). Beyond data, we leverage knowledge distillation (KD) to transfer inductive biases from a high-capacity teacher to a compact student (Hinton et al., 2015). KD has demonstrated gains in both NLP and speech tasks, motivating its use for robust SQA modeling (Kim and Rush, 2016; Hu et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2018b; Hahn and Choi, 2019). Our approach distills conversational structure from cleaner or more reliable contexts to guide learning on noisy spoken inputs. Empirically, we show that coupling conversational modeling with KD improves SQA robustness while maintaining efficiency.
reason: The span 'retriever reader' references a specific, widely used architecture in prior work without providing a citation at its first mention, constituting an unsupported claim about prior methods.

spans: two-stage retriever-reader training pipelines
document: Current self-explaining models often enforce shortest yet sufficient rationales, with the assumption that short rationales are more intuitive to humans (Lei et al., 2016; Bastings et al., 2019). Paranjape et al. (2020) propose an information bottleneck approach to enable rationale length control. However, current studies largely rely on automatic metrics and rarely evaluate how rationale length affects human understanding. A parallel line of work measures alignment between model rationales and human annotations, often termed human rationale alignment (Paranjape et al., 2020; DeYoung et al., 2020). This perspective compares model highlights against human-provided evidence spans to estimate agreement (DeYoung et al., 2020). There are also studies involving humans in the loop to compare explanations, such as asking annotators to choose which model is more trustworthy given its rationale (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Hase and Bansal, 2020). Despite these efforts, rigorous human evaluations isolating the effect of rationale length on downstream comprehension remain scarce (Hase and Bansal, 2020; Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017). Prior work has also questioned whether extracted rationales are faithful to model internals, underscoring the need for stronger evaluation protocols (Jain and Wallace, 2019; Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019). Sufficiency and comprehensiveness tests offer partial signals but do not directly assess human reasoning with variable-length explanations (Treviso and Martins, 2020; Serrano and Smith, 2019). Moreover, explanation plausibility can diverge from faithfulness, making human-centered studies indispensable (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017; Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020). Datasets that pair inputs with rationales, such as e-SNLI and CoS-E, enable supervision for highlight selection but differ in granularity and domain (Camburu et al., 2018; Rajani et al., 2019). The ERASER benchmark aggregates multiple tasks with human evidence annotations and provides standard metrics for extraction quality (DeYoung et al., 2020). However, these resources seldom control for explanation length as an experimental factor, limiting insights about brevity versus completeness (DeYoung et al., 2020; Camburu et al., 2018; Rajani et al., 2019). In parallel, model-based techniques implement discrete or continuous sparsity constraints to encourage short highlights while preserving predictive performance (Lei et al., 2016; Bastings et al., 2019; Chang et al., 2020). Beyond extractive rationalization, recent systems integrate retrieval and generation to produce free-form justifications for complex questions (Narang et al., 2020; Krishna et al., 2022). Beyond extractive rationalization, recent studies increasingly adopt two-stage retriever-reader training pipelines for explanation-aware QA. While such methods may improve task accuracy, they do not by themselves reveal how explanation length modulates human judgments of correctness or usefulness. In controlled experiments, readers may prefer concise yet complete rationales that reduce search effort without omitting key premises, but quantitative evidence remains limited (Hoffman et al., 2018; Lage et al., 2019). Cognitive load theory further suggests that extraneous detail can hinder learning, motivating systematic manipulation of rationale verbosity (Sweller, 1988; Paas et al., 2003). At the same time, domain complexity can necessitate multi-sentence explanations to ensure sufficiency, especially in multi-hop reasoning tasks (Yang et al., 2018; Welbl et al., 2018). This trade-off indicates that length is not a mere stylistic preference but a variable with measurable impact on human performance. Methodologically, our study separates plausibility from faithfulness by holding the model fixed and varying only the explanation length shown to participants (Hase and Bansal, 2020). We complement automatic sufficiency-style scores with user-centered measures of comprehension accuracy, decision time, and perceived confidence. We also analyze how length interacts with rationale structure, contrasting contiguous spans with sentence-level summaries under identical evidence budgets. By situating our evaluation within established rationale datasets and tasks, we ensure comparability while directly probing the length dimension (DeYoung et al., 2020; Camburu et al., 2018). Our results aim to clarify when brevity helps or harms users and to provide guidance for designing length-aware rationalization objectives.
reason: The span 'two-stage retriever-reader training pipelines' asserts a specific trend in recent studies without providing any citation at its first mention. It references a particular methodological approach but lacks supporting evidence, thereby fitting the definition of an unsupported claim.

spans: StereoSet
document: Measuring bias in NLP remains a central concern for language technology research. Several studies have investigated the prevalence of bias in NLP models (Caliskan et al., 2017; May et al., 2019; Bordia and Bowman, 2019; Davidson et al., 2019; Magee et al., 2021), with many focusing on cases of models exhibiting stereotyping behavior. Though Blodgett et al. (2020) point out that what these studies mean by "bias" can vary quite widely, the finding that models encode associations derived from negative stereotypes and social biases is well replicated. In defining bias for this study, our design aligns most closely with the definition of representational harms by Crawford (2017) as harms that "occur when systems reinforce the subordination of some groups along the lines of identity." When constructing data to measure this bias, contrasting groups of people rather than just relevant attributes highlights the difference in outcomes and impact on groups targeted by a given stereotype (Dev et al., 2021). Early measurement efforts leveraged distributional semantics tests such as WEAT to quantify associations between demographic concepts and sentiment or occupation terms (Caliskan et al., 2017). Subsequent work adapted these ideas to contextual encoders through tests like SEAT, which probe sentence representations rather than static embeddings (May et al., 2019). Parallel strands introduced template-based minimal pairs that isolate social attributes to detect preferences in masked and autoregressive models (Nangia et al., 2020; Kurita et al., 2019). In coreference and pronoun resolution, curated challenge sets such as WinoBias and Winogender exposed systematic gender asymmetries in model decisions (Zhao et al., 2018; Rudinger et al., 2018). Task-specific evaluations further documented disparities in toxicity detection and abusive language moderation across identity terms (Dixon et al., 2018; Sap et al., 2019; Röttger et al., 2021). For generative systems, prompts derived from real web text revealed both frequency and severity of toxic completions in uncontrolled settings (Gehman et al., 2020). Despite methodological variety, a persistent challenge is connecting measurement artifacts to concrete, societally relevant harms (Blodgett et al., 2020). Another challenge lies in separating genuine stereotype knowledge from harmful endorsement, especially when pretraining data contains both descriptive and prescriptive statements (Bender et al., 2021). Dataset design choices strongly influence measured effect sizes, including lexical context, negation handling, and demographic label granularity (Bartl et al., 2020; Dev et al., 2021). To mitigate confounds, recent work emphasizes controlled counterfactual data augmentation and balanced attribute distributions during test construction (Maudslay et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). However, interventions that reduce bias on a specific benchmark may not generalize across domains or tasks (Gonen and Goldberg, 2019; Goldfarb-Tarrant et al., 2021). Robust evaluation therefore requires aggregating evidence across multiple metrics and datasets rather than optimizing for any single score (Seshadri et al., 2022). In practice, researchers often complement intrinsic association measures with end-task diagnostics, such as groupwise error analysis for NER and QA (De-Arteaga et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020). Moreover, open-vocabulary identity terms continue to evolve, necessitating dynamic lexicons and periodic reannotation to preserve validity over time (Dixon et al., 2018; Dev et al., 2021). While annotation guidelines can reduce ambiguity, annotator demographics and perspectives substantially shape judgements of offensiveness and harm (Sap et al., 2019; Palm et al., 2023). Cross-lingual studies report that translation artifacts and morphological gender systems introduce additional bias pathways that differ from English-centric findings (Zampieri et al., 2020; Costa-jussà, 2021). Benchmarks that foreground intersectional identities have begun to reveal interaction effects not captured by single-attribute tests (Dev et al., 2021; Lauscher et al., 2021). A complementary line of work investigates causal estimation techniques to attribute measured disparities to specific components of model pipelines (Feder et al., 2021; Vig et al., 2020). Recent studies in retrieval-augmented generation suggest that document selection can either amplify or dampen representational harms depending on source curation (Kandpal et al., 2023; Lazaridou et al., 2022). For summarization and dialogue, human-in-the-loop judgments are increasingly paired with automatic bias metrics to triangulate reliability (Pavlopoulos et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2022). Despite this progress, benchmark coverage remains uneven across identities, domains, and modalities, complicating cross-study comparison (Blodgett et al., 2020). Recent works frequently default to StereoSet when reporting stereotype bias scores, which risks reinforcing oversimplified taxonomies of social attributes. Our study adopts a conservative measurement strategy that prioritizes contrastive group comparisons and transparent reporting of uncertainty.
reason: The span is a single-word, uncited dataset name embedded in a generalizing claim about prior work ('Recent works frequently default to StereoSet'), which requires evidence or citations and thus constitutes an unsupported claim.
